questions.stem: What is the correct position for a sublingual tablet?

choices.label: A
choices.text: As far back on the top of the tongue as possible without swallowing it

choices.label: B
choices.text: Between the cheek and the gum of the upper teeth

choices.label: C
choices.text: Between the cheek and the gum of the lower teeth

choices.label: D
choices.text: Under the front of the tongue

questions.rationale: A drug given by the sublingual route, such as nitroglycerin, is placed under the tongue. The blood supply is very good in the mouth; therefore, these drugs dissolve and are absorbed quickly.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: pp. 39-40

questions.stem: When is it acceptable to take a verbal order from the prescriber before giving a drug to a patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: During the nightshift when the prescriber is not at the hospital

choices.label: B
choices.text: In an emergency situation such as a cardiac arrest

choices.label: C
choices.text: When a patient is experiencing severe pain

choices.label: D
choices.text: At any time it is convenient

questions.rationale: Verbal orders should be accepted only in emergency situations. As soon as the emergency has been resolved, verbal orders must be written and signed.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 28

questions.stem: What is the most important role of the health care worker in preventing drug errors?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Always checking the patient’s diagnosis before giving a drug

choices.label: B
choices.text: Always following the “eight rights” of drug administration

choices.label: C
choices.text: Being the one defense for detecting and preventing drug errors

choices.label: D
choices.text: Being most likely to detect a drug error that has occurred

questions.rationale: When administering drugs, always follow the “eight rights.” Many drug errors occur because one or more of the “rights” is not followed.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 27

questions.stem: Which statement accurately describes the correct technique for giving subcutaneous drugs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use a 3/4-inch, 25-gauge needle and a 15-degree angle for injection.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Use a 3/8-inch, 25-gauge needle and a 45-degree angle for injection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use a 1-inch, 22-gauge needle and a 90-degree angle for injection.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use a 2 inch, 25-gauge needle and a 45-degree angle for injection.

questions.rationale: For subcutaneous drug administration, small, short needles are used ({3/8} inch, 25 to 27 gauge). Insert the needle at a 45-degree angle for most patients. If the patient is obese, you may need to use a 90-degree angle. If the patient is thin, you may need an angle that is less than 45 degrees.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 35

questions.stem: What administration technique should you use when giving a 2-year-old child ear drops?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pull the earlobe down and back.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pull the earlobe up and out.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Keep the earlobe straight.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hang the patient’s head over the side of the bed.

questions.rationale: For children younger than 3 years, pull the earlobe (pinna) down and back. This straightens the ear canal. This helps to ensure that the ear drops are placed where they are needed to be effective.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 40

questions.stem: What must you have a patient do after a vaginal drug is administered?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Replace the drug in the refrigerator when not in use.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Give the drug while the patient is sitting on the toilet.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Have the patient empty her bladder after receiving this drug.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Keep the patient lying down for 10 to 15 minutes after receiving the drug.

questions.rationale: Be sure to have the patient lie down for 10 to 15 minutes after receiving vaginal drugs to ensure that the drugs are fully absorbed.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 40

questions.stem: When giving a drug to a patient who is awake but confused, what is the best way to identify that it is the right patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check the room and bed number that the patient occupies.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the patient to state his or her name and birth date.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check the name on the patient’s wristband.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient if he or she is Mr. or Ms. [name].

questions.rationale: When a patient is confused, he or she may not reply with his or her own name and birth date. Beds can be moved and rooms can be changed. In addition, sometimes patients are placed or get into the wrong bed. In this case, the patient’s wristband provides the most reliable identification information.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 27

questions.stem: What is the best way to make sure that the right patient is receiving a prescribed drug when the patient is alert and oriented?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the patient to state his or her social security number.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check the patient’s wrist band.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Look at the patient’s chart.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Have the patient state his or her name and birth date.

questions.rationale: To make sure that the right patient receives any drug that has been prescribed, The Joint Commission (TJC) recommends checking two unique patient identifiers (name and birth date) before medication administration. An alert and oriented patient can be asked directly.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 27

questions.stem: Why are nose drops or sprays most often given?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To treat dryness that may lead to nose bleeds.

choices.label: B
choices.text: For allergies to pets, pollen, and molds.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To treat congestion and infection.

choices.label: D
choices.text: For cold and flu symptoms.

questions.rationale: Nose drops or sprays are most often used to treat congestion or infection.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 40 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which technique is used with some intramuscular drug injections but not with subcutaneous or intradermal drug injections?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ensuring the site selected is appropriate for injection

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cleansing the selected site before inserting the needle

choices.label: C
choices.text: Aspirating the syringe before injecting the drug solution

choices.label: D
choices.text: Checking for allergic or sensitivity reactions to the injection

questions.rationale: Aspiration is not recommended for IM injection of vaccines or immunizations. For drugs such as penicillin, aspiration may be indicated. When indicated, aspirate the syringe (pull back on the plunger) to make sure that the needle is not in a vein. If the needle is in a vein, blood will appear in the syringe. Remove the needle and discard the drug if this happens. Get a new dose of the drug and a sterile needle and syringe and give the injection in another site.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 36

questions.stem: What is the proper way to prepare skin for a transdermal patch?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Shave the skin that will be underneath the patch.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Leave the old patch on and apply the new one next to it.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Clean and dry the skin where the patch will be applied.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Remove the old patch and apply the new one to the exact same spot.

questions.rationale: When administering a transdermal patch, first wash your hands and put on gloves. Clean the area of skin where the drug will be applied. Apply topical drugs in a smooth, thin layer, and cover the area. When administering transdermal drugs, remove old patches or doses of the drug. Be sure to remove all traces of the drug from the previous dosage site, and rotate sites to avoid skin irritation or breakdown.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 39

questions.stem: A patient is to receive nitroglycerin ointment, 1 inch STAT, for elevated blood pressure. What must be done before giving this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Shave the hair off the patient’s chest.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Place the patient on a heart monitor.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Put on a pair of disposable gloves.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Measure the dose directly on the patient’s skin.

questions.rationale: Wash your hands and put on gloves. Without gloves, if you come into contact with the ointment you may experience the same side effects as a patient (e.g., headache, drop in blood pressure).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 39

questions.stem: To prevent a drug overdose from receiving two doses too close together, what should you do immediately after giving a PRN pain drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask if the patient’s pain has been relieved.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check the patient’s vital signs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Notify the prescriber.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Document the action.

questions.rationale: It is important to document giving PRN (as needed) drugs as soon as possible before another health care worker gives a second dose in response to a patient’s statements about pain.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 28

questions.stem: The prescriber orders atenolol (Tenormin) 25 mg to be given orally once a day to control a patient’s high blood pressure. You check the patient’s vital signs and find that the blood pressure is 128/80 mm Hg and the heart rate is 60 beats/min. What should you do first before giving this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check the order for prescriber limitations on when the drug should be given.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Notify the prescriber and ask if the drug should be given.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reassess the blood pressure and heart rate in 30 minutes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Give the drug exactly as prescribed.

questions.rationale: Prescribers often include limitations about when a drug should or should not be given. You should first check the order for any limitations. Because a heart rate of 60 beats/min is borderline low, and unless there are no limitations, you should notify the prescriber and ask if the drug should be given to this patient.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 31

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed omeprazole (Prilosec) 60 mg once a day orally. The patient is having difficulty with swallowing and has a feeding tube in place. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Open the capsule and mix the contents with water, then give the drug through the feeding tube.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Raise the head of the bed 90 degrees and mix the capsule in applesauce for easier swallowing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Contact the prescriber and pharmacist about using another drug or another form of the drug.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hold the tube feeding for at least 30 minutes before giving the drug.

questions.rationale: Omeprazole comes in time-released capsules, which should not be opened to prevent rapid absorption of the drug and consequent side effects or adverse effects. Mixing the drug with applesauce and asking the patient to swallow it when the patient has difficulty swallowing puts the patient at high risk for aspiration.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 32

questions.stem: A patient with severe postoperative pain is ordered to receive morphine 2 mg intravenously. The patient asks if the drug could be taken by mouth instead. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Giving the drug intravenously will give you faster pain relief.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I will call your prescriber and ask if the order can be changed.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Your surgeon wants you to receive the drug intravenously.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “We can substitute the intravenous drug with an oral drug.”

questions.rationale: The intravenous route is used when a drug needs to enter the bloodstream rapidly or a large dose of a drug must be given. The rates of absorption and action are very rapid with this route and this route is best for a patient with severe postoperative pain.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 38

questions.stem: The prescriber orders a new drug over the telephone for a nursing home patient who has symptoms of a urinary tract infection. The order is for Gantanol, 2 g now and then 1 g every 12 hours for the next 10 days. What further information is most important for you to obtain from the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “How many refills are needed?”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Do you want the drug given orally or intravenously?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Which brand of drug should be given, or is this a generic drug?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Does this drug need to be given with a meal or on an empty stomach?”

questions.rationale: The prescriber must indicate the route of administration for the drug prescribed. Although this drug is available only as an oral drug, the actual drug order needs to include this information. Because this prescription is for an inpatient (nursing home resident) not for a patient taking the drug at home, the refill information is not important at this time. Whether or not the drug should be taken with food or on an empty stomach might be a special instruction, but is not as critical as the correct route.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 28

questions.stem: The prescriber orders all of the following drugs for a patient who had surgery 2 days ago. Which drug order should you administer first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alphamine (cyanocobalamin) 100 mcg intramuscularly once

choices.label: B
choices.text: Benadryl (diphenhydramine) 25 mg orally every 8 hours

choices.label: C
choices.text: Compazine (prochlorperazine) 10 mg orally STAT

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dalmane (flurazepam) 30 mg orally at night PRN

questions.rationale: STAT drugs are prescribed to correct or help an immediate problem; they are given as soon as they are available. If the drug is not available on the unit, you must call the pharmacy for an immediate drug dose. PRN drugs may be important but are given at the patient’s indication for a need of the drug. The Benadryl order is written as a standing order and does not indicate an immediate need. Although Alphamine is written as a single-dose drug order, there is no indication for immediate administration.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 29

questions.stem: You ask the patient to state his name and birth date, and the patient responds correctly. Then you give the patient the prescribed drug tablet. The patient says “I haven’t ever taken a green pill before.” What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Go ahead and take the drug. The same medications from different drug companies may have a different color.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Go ahead and take the drug. It is likely that your health care provider has prescribed a new . drug for you.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Don’t take this drug right now. It is probably not the one prescribed for you.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Don’t take this drug right now. Let me recheck everything to be sure.”

questions.rationale: When a patient does not recognize a drug that is being given, it is a “red flag” for a possible error. Even though the drug may be newly ordered or may be made by a different manufacturer than what the patient has had in the past, it is always best to recheck the order, the drug, and the patient before proceeding. Although withholding the drug entirely is not completely wrong, there may be no drug error and this response may frighten the patient unnecessarily.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 31

questions.stem: Which question is most important for you to ask a patient before administering a new drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Are you allergic to any drugs?”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Do you know what this drug is for?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “When was the last time you ate or drank?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “What other drugs have you taken in the last 24 hours?”

questions.rationale: All of these questions are important to know when giving a new drug. The information that is most critical, however, is whether the patient has an allergy to this drug or any other drug. A drug allergy can result in life-threatening effects.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 31

questions.stem: When you bring in the next dose of a drug that a patient first received 6 hours ago, the patient reports a “pounding” heart rate ever since taking the last dose. What is your best first action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Document the report as the only action.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check the patient’s vital signs for changes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hold the dose and notify the prescriber immediately.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reassure the patient that this is an expected response to the drug.

questions.rationale: Any side effect or response a patient has after starting a new drug should be investigated, even when it is an expected side effect of the drug. Some drugs may increase the strength of the heartbeat and heart rate either as the intended action or as a side effect. However, any drug that affects heart response can also cause adverse heart problems. Before giving the drug or notifying the prescriber, you should check the patient’s vital signs, especially heart rate and quality, heart rhythm, and blood pressure. These changes are important to know for you and the prescriber.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 31

questions.stem: A 1-year-old child is prescribed a transdermal drug patch for pain control. To which site should you apply the patch?

choices.label: A
choices.text: On the lower arm where changing the patch is easier

choices.label: B
choices.text: On the back between the shoulders so the child cannot reach it

choices.label: C
choices.text: On the upper chest so that any skin irritation can be seen quickly

choices.label: D
choices.text: On the leg between the knee and the thigh for fastest drug absorption

questions.rationale: When a transdermal patch is placed in an area visible to a small child, he or she usually picks at it and may remove it. Placing it between the shoulders on the back removes it from the child’s sight and attention. On a small child, circulation is not usually a problem and the drug would be as well absorbed from the back as from anywhere else.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 33

questions.stem: Which action is most important when you prepare to administer an oral drug to a patient of any age?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Asking the patient whether he or she prefers a tablet or a capsule

choices.label: B
choices.text: Determining when the patient last ate or drank

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assessing whether the patient has nausea

choices.label: D
choices.text: Checking the patient’s ability to swallow

questions.rationale: A patient who cannot swallow should not take any drug, drink, or food by the oral route. The risk for aspiration is very high and can lead to many serious complications, even death.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 32

questions.stem: Which condition requires that you withhold a drug dose for a patient with a feeding tube?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The drug is in the form of a tablet.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The drug volume is greater than 20 mL.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Carbon dioxide is detected from the feeding tube.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient is unconscious and unable to swallow.

questions.rationale: When carbon dioxide comes from the feeding tube, the tube is in the trachea rather than the stomach. Using this compromised feeding tube for drug administration would result in drug placement into the lungs instead of the stomach, which can cause serious complications.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 33

questions.stem: You prepare to administer an intravenous (IV) push drug, but the skin around the patient’s IV site is swollen and red. The patient states that the area hurts, and no blood return is obtained when you aspirate the IV setup. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continue IV administration of the drug.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Discontinue IV administration and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dilute the drug more before injecting it into the current IV site.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reassure the patient that this is an expected reaction and offer the prescribed pain medication.

questions.rationale: These symptoms indicate there has been IV infiltration and the needle is no longer in the vein. No further drugs can be delivered through this IV setup, even if they are well diluted. IV administration of the drug must be discontinued. The prescriber should be notified before restarting IV administration of the drug. The prescriber may change the drug to a different form or prescribe a different drug.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF:  p. 38

questions.stem: You are receiving a telephone order from a health care provider. After receiving all the information, what is the most important next action to perform?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Notify the supervisor about the verbal order.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer the drug as soon as it is available.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Read the order back to the prescriber and ask whether it is correct.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inform the patient and family that a new drug has been prescribed by the health care provider.

questions.rationale: For safety, when you contact the prescriber by telephone or follow a verbal order, be sure to write the order, read it back, and ask for confirmation that what you wrote is correct before administering any drug. Be sure to document that you read back the order to the prescriber.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 28

questions.stem: What is the most important action to take after giving a patient a newly prescribed drug for hypertension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Teach the patient to measure his or her pulse.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check the patient’s blood pressure an hour later.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the patient whether any other family members also have hypertension.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient whether he or she has ever taken a drug for hypertension in the past.

questions.rationale: You must be familiar with the patient’s medical diagnosis and the purpose of the drug. Assessing for drug effectiveness is important. After you give a drug, check the patient to make sure that the drug has the desired effect. For example, check the blood pressure for improvement after giving an antihypertensive drug. Be sure to document what you monitored and any other appropriate interventions.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 28

questions.stem: When you bring a sedative for sleep in to the patient, he asks you to leave the drug on his table and he will take it when his television show is over. What should you do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Insist that he take the drug right now.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Leave it at his bedside as he requests.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tell him you will bring it back later, after the show.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Document that he refused to take his prescribed drug.

questions.rationale: You are responsible for documenting that drugs have been taken and must witness that this has occurred. Most drugs, including sedatives, should never be left at the bedside. You have no idea whether it was actually taken by the patient or by someone else. It is possible for a patient to collect drugs left at the bedside and take them all at once.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 32 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Before administering any drug, what should you do? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check the order.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wash your hands.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Instruct the patient that he or she must take the drug.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Find out the patient’s family history.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Check the patient’s identification band.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Keep drug in container until at the bedside.

questions.rationale: Before giving any drug, always follow the “eight rights.” Always check the written order. Check the patient’s identification wristband and ask the patient’s name and birth date. Limit interruptions and distractions. Wash your hands and wear clean gloves when needed (e.g., parenteral, rectal routes). Keep drugs in their containers or wrappers until at the patient’s bedside. Avoid touching pills or capsules. Never give drugs prepared by someone else. Follow sterile technique when handling syringes and needles. Remain alert to drug names that sound or look alike. Giving the wrong drug can have serious adverse effects.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 27

questions.stem: The patient with an NG tube has orders for several enteral drugs (e.g., capsules, tablets, and liquids). What should you do before giving these drugs? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check with the pharmacist about crushing the tablets.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Follow the procedures of the “eight rights.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: Aspirate to check for stomach contents.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Open the extended-release capsules.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inject 150 mL of water to check tube patency.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Place the patient in a supine position.

questions.rationale: As with all oral drugs, check the drug orders, which may be written as PO or by feeding tube. Check your drug book or with the pharmacist before crushing tablets or opening capsules. Wash your hands and place the patient upright. Check to make sure that the tube is located in the stomach by withdrawing (aspirating) stomach contents with a syringe, or you can attach an end- tidal carbon dioxide (CO2) detector to the feeding tube. The presence of carbon dioxide indicates that the tube is in the trachea rather than the stomach. Additionally, if the patient is receiving a tube feeding, check the amount of tube feeding remaining in the stomach (residual). Some drugs are not well absorbed when food is in the stomach (e.g., phenytoin [Dilantin]), and the tube feeding must be stopped for a period before and after administration. Liquid drugs should be diluted and flushed through the tube. Crushed tablets and the contents of opened capsules are first dissolved in water before being given through the tube. To give the drugs, attach a large syringe to the tube, pour the liquid or dissolved drug into the syringe, and let it run in by gravity.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: pp. 32-34

questions.stem: You are teaching a patient about a prescribed sublingual drug. What will you be sure to tell the patient? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Keep the drug sterile to avoid infection.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Do not drink anything until the drug is completely dissolved.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Place this drug between your jaw and your molar teeth.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Notify your prescriber if you experience side effects.”

choices.label: E
choices.text: “Place the drug beside or below the tongue.”

choices.label: F
choices.text: “Don’t swallow this drug.”

questions.rationale: A drug given by the sublingual route, such as nitroglycerin, is placed under the tongue. The blood supply is very good in the mouth; therefore, these drugs dissolve and are absorbed quickly. The patient should not eat or drink until the drug is completely dissolved. Teach the patient not to swallow or chew while the drug is in the mouth because these drugs are not effective if absorbed through the GI tract. A patient should be taught to notify the prescriber for any side effects.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: pp. 39-40

questions.stem: You are giving morning medications to a patient who refuses to take an oral dose of docusate (Colace). What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Your prescriber ordered that you must take this drug twice a day.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Docusate will soften your bowel movements so that you do not strain.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “This drug will help prevent constipation while you are on bed rest.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Can you tell me why you do not want to take the docusate?”

questions.rationale: The patient may have a good reason for refusing to take this drug. For example, he or she may be experiencing diarrhea. Understanding the patient’s reason for refusal helps you to understand how to intervene for him or her. A patient has the right to refuse any drug. Be sure that he or she understands why the drug has been prescribed and the consequences of refusing to take it. When a patient refuses to take a drug, document the refusal, including the fact that the patient understands what may happen if the drug is not taken.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 28

questions.stem: Safe drug administration requires that the individual giving a drug be knowledgeable about which drug features? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Purpose

choices.label: B
choices.text: Actions

choices.label: C
choices.text: Brand name

choices.label: D
choices.text: Side effects

choices.label: E
choices.text: Abnormal reactions

choices.label: F
choices.text: Follow-up care

questions.rationale: You are responsible for providing competent, safe patient care, including giving drugs. To give drugs safely, you should be knowledgeable about the purpose of the drug, its actions, side effects, abnormal reactions, delivery methods, and any necessary follow-up.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 27

questions.stem: A patient is to receive an acetaminophen (Tylenol) suppository for an elevated temperature of 102.8° F. What actions must the nurse take? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask if the patient is having any diarrhea.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lubricate the blunt end of the suppository.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Put on a pair of sterile gloves.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Place the patient in the Sims’ position.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ask the patient to take a deep breath and bear down.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Push the suppository into the rectum about 1 inch.

questions.rationale: Diarrhea may make the rectal route of drug administration undesirable because the patient may be unable to hold the drug in the rectum long enough to be absorbed. Disposable gloves should be used, but they do not need to be sterile. The suppository is inserted pointed end first, not blunt end. The Sims’ position (with the patient turned to the side and one leg bent over the other) is the best position for giving a rectal suppository. The suppository should be pushed into the rectum about 1 inch for better absorption. Be sure to instruct the patient about how long the suppository should be held in the rectum.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 34

questions.stem: A patient with several chronic illnesses is being cared for in a hospital acute care setting. For which events should you expect to apply the principles of the process of medication reconciliation? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient is transferred from the ICU to a medical care unit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient is transported to radiology for a CT scan.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient’s care is transferred from the evening to the night nurse.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient is moved from an acute care to a long-term care facility.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The patient is relocated to a private room on the same medical care unit.

choices.label: F
choices.text: The patient is sent to a different hospital for specialized surgery.

questions.rationale: When a patient visits a health care provider, is admitted to the hospital, or is transferred from unit to unit in the hospital, it is common to receive new prescriptions or to have changes made in currently prescribed drugs. The process of medication reconciliation is used during these transitions of patient care to avoid drug errors such as omissions, duplications, dosing errors, and drug interactions.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 30 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: What does the term infiltration mean?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Leakage of intravenous fluid into the surrounding tissue

choices.label: B
choices.text: Infusion of intravenous fluids at a faster rate than was prescribed

choices.label: C
choices.text: Plugging up or clotting off the needle during intravenous infusion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Infusion of intravenous fluids without the use of a pump or controller

questions.rationale: The definition of infiltration is a condition in which an IV needle or catheter pulls from the vein and leaks IV fluids into the surrounding tissue, resulting in tissue swelling. An infusion of IV fluids faster than prescribed is a “runaway” IV. Plugging or clotting of a needle has no specific name. Infusion of fluids without a pump or controller is a manually controlled infusion.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 79

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: How many drops per milliliter (mL) does a microdrip chamber and tubing provide?
questions.rationale: The universal drop factor for a standard microdrip chamber is 60 gtt/mL by all manufacturers.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 77

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: Which term describes how much fluid is to be infused intravenously?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Infusion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Volume

choices.label: D
choices.text: Duration

questions.rationale: The volume is the amount to be infused. The rate is the speed of the infusion. The infusion is just the term used to describe the act of running IV fluids into a blood vessel. The duration is how long in minutes or hours an IV infusion is order to run.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 78

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: On an infusion pump, what does the abbreviation “VTBI” indicate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Amount of fluid already infused

choices.label: B
choices.text: Amount of fluid remaining in the bag or bottle

choices.label: C
choices.text: Total amount of fluid prescribed for the day

choices.label: D
choices.text: How fast the infusion should be run

questions.rationale: The abbreviation VTBI stands for volume to be infused. It is the amount remaining in an IV bag that has not yet been infused.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 78

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: If an IV scheduled to have a duration of 6 hours is started at 5:00 p.m., what is the expected stop time?
questions.rationale: Six hours from 5:00 p.m. is 11:00 p.m. 5:00 a.m. would be 24 hours; 11:00 a.m. would be 18 hours; and 1:00 a.m. would be 8 hours.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 78

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: What sign or symptom is an indication of IV extravasation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fever

choices.label: B
choices.text: Purulent drainage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pain at the infusion site

choices.label: D
choices.text: Strong blood return when the IV bag is positioned below the level of the IV site

questions.rationale: Fever and purulent drainage indicate infection. A strong blood return indicates the IV needle or catheter is in the blood vessel. With extravasation, the needle or catheter is not in the blood vessel. Fluid then leaks into the surrounding tissue and often causes pain in those tissues.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 79

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: Under which conditions should a microdrip setup for an IV infusion always be used?

choices.label: A
choices.text: When the patient is an infant

choices.label: B
choices.text: When whole blood is to be infused

choices.label: C
choices.text: When the patient has only a few veins available for infusion

choices.label: D
choices.text: When a very large amount of fluid needs to be infused quickly

questions.rationale: An infant has a small normal fluid volume and an IV that runs in too fast can cause fluid overload, sometimes with heart failure. Using a microdrip setup helps prevent a too rapid infusion rate.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Remembering    REF:  p. 77

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: What is the most important advantage for intravenous (IV) infusion of drugs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anyone can administer IV drugs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The drug reaches the bloodstream immediately.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Drugs given intravenously cost less than drugs given orally.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient is not required to be alert to swallow the drug.

questions.rationale: Drugs administered by IV infusion are immediately in the bloodstream and reach their target tissues fastest. This means that both the drug benefits (intended actions) and any adverse actions can happen more quickly. IV drugs tend to cost more than oral drugs. Although the patient does not have to be alert to receive IV drugs, it is not their main advantage. Administering IV drugs can only be performed by a skilled health care provider.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 76

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: How does the “drop factor” affect IV infusions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fluid with a larger drop factor infuses more slowly than fluid with a smaller drop factor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Smaller drop factors occur with smaller needles (or cannulas) and larger drop factors occur with larger needles.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The smaller the drop factor, the fewer the number of drops needed to administer 1 mL of infusion fluid.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The larger the drop factor, the fewer the number of drops needed to administer 1 mL of infusion fluid.

questions.rationale: The drop factor is the number of drops (gtt) needed to make 1 mL of IV fluid. The larger the drop, the fewer drops needed to make 1 mL.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 77

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: A patient is to receive 1000 mL intravenously of dextrose 5% in lactated Ringer’s solution in 8 hours. When you check the intravenous (IV) bag after 2 hours, 700 mL remain in the bag. How many milliliters have already infused?
questions.rationale: The amount infused is equal to the starting amount (1000 mL) minus the amount remaining in the IV bag or other container (700 mL). 1000 – 700 = 300 mL have infused.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 80

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: How is extravasation different from infiltration?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Infiltration occurs in the hand, whereas extravasation occurs in the arm.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Both conditions lead to swelling, but extravasation causes tissue damage.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Infiltration is swelling accompanied by pain, whereas extravasation is not painful.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Extravasation causes phlebitis along with tissue swelling, whereas infiltration causes fluid overload along with swelling.

questions.rationale: Both conditions result from leakage of fluid out of the vein and into surrounding tissues. Infiltration, although uncomfortable, does not result in tissue damage. Extravasation results directly or indirectly in tissue damage. If the damage is severe enough, tissue can become necrotic and slough.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 79

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: An IV infusion order for a patient reads “1000 mL dextrose 5% in normal saline intravenously, immediately.” What additional information does the prescriber need to provide for it to be a valid order?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Drip rate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Drop factor

choices.label: C
choices.text: Duration

choices.label: D
choices.text: Start time

questions.rationale: A valid intravenous (IV) therapy order must include the duration of infusion. The word “immediately” in this prescription refers to when the IV infusion is to start, not its duration.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 77

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: Which problem is a major disadvantage of an intravenous (IV) pump?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The alarms are so sensitive that nurses tend to ignore them when they sound frequently.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patients and families can override the automatic features and reset the infusion rate.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It can “run away” and cause a patient to experience fluid overload.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It can continue to push fluid into the tissue when infiltration occurs.

questions.rationale: IV pumps push fluid into the patient at a rate greater than gravity and can continue to push even when resistance is increased. Infiltration or extravasation may not be detected by the machine until the situation is serious.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 79

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: Which drop factor allows the greatest volume of intravenous fluid to be delivered per hour at a drip rate of 50 gtt per minute?
questions.rationale: A drop factor of 10 means that 10 gtt equal 1 mL. At a drip rate of 50 gtt/min, a drop factor of 10 delivers 5 mL/min or 300 mL/h. At a drip rate of 50 gtt/min, a drop factor of 15 delivers 3.3 mL/min or 188 mL/h. At a drip rate of 50 gtt/min, a drop factor of 20 delivers 2.5 mL/min or 150 mL/h. At a drip rate of 50 gtt/min, a drop factor of 60 delivers 0.8 mL/min or 50 mL/h.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 77 |p. 80

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: What will the drip rate of an intravenous infusion with a drop factor of 20 need to be in order to deliver 1000 mL in 8 hours?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 13 gtt/min

choices.label: B
choices.text: 31 gtt/min

choices.label: C
choices.text: 42 gtt/min

choices.label: D
choices.text: 60 gtt/min

questions.rationale: To deliver 1000 mL over 8 hours, 125 mL must be delivered per hour or 2.08 mL/min. 2.08 mL/min times 20 gtt/mL equals 41.6 gtt/min, round up to 42.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 80

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: Which precaution is most important to teach a patient who is receiving intravenous (IV) drug therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Turn on your call light if the IV machine starts to beep for any reason.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Do not use the arm that has the IV running in it for any reason whatsoever.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Call me immediately if you start to feel any pain or burning in the arm with the IV.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “If you think the IV is running too slowly, just push the up-arrow button on the machine once or twice.”

questions.rationale: Pain and burning at the site are indicators of infiltration and extravasation. These indicators must be investigated as soon as they start to prevent tissue damage. Although it is helpful for the patient to alert someone that the machine is beeping, it is not the most important precaution. The patient should never be told to adjust the flow rate. Depending on the specific location of the needle, the arm with the IV can be used gently.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 79

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: While examining a patient’s peripheral intravenous (IV) site, you observe a red streak along the length of the vein. On palpation, the vein feels hard and cordlike. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check for a blood return and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Discontinue the infusion and remove the IV needle.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Apply ice packs to the vein and continue the infusion.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Change the IV fluid to normal saline and redress the site.

questions.rationale: Phlebitis is an inflammation of the vein. Its presence in a vein being used for IV therapy may be caused by mechanical forces associated with the IV device or by chemical factors related to the composition and osmolarity of the drug solution. The key manifestation is that the symptoms are directly associated with the vein. The vein with phlebitis should not receive any additional fluids and the IV therapy is discontinued. If IV therapy needs to continue, it is restarted in a different vein.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 77

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: A patient is to receive 125 mL of intravenous fluid per hour and the drop factor is 10 gtt/mL. You count the 15-second drip rate to be 8 gtt/min. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nothing, the IV flow rate is correct.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Turn the rate down to 5 gtt/15 s.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Turn the rate up to 11 gtt/15 s.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Turn the rate up to 15 gtt/15 s.

questions.rationale: At 125 mL/h, the patient should receive 2 mL/min (125 mL/60 minutes). With a drop factor of 10 gtt/mL, the total number of drops per minute should be 20. The 15-second drop rate should be 5 (20 gtt/min/4).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: pp. 80-81

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: The intravenous (IV) site of a patient who has been receiving IV therapy for 2 days is red and has a small amount of pus oozing from around the needle. What is your action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Document the finding as an expected response to long-term IV therapy as the only action.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Immediately notify the prescriber to get an order to discontinue the IV therapy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use an iodine solution to clean the site and replace the dressing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Discontinue the IV therapy and notify the prescriber.

questions.rationale: An infected infusion site is an indication to immediately discontinue the IV therapy. An order is not needed under these circumstances, but the prescriber should be notified.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 77

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: A patient is receiving an intravenous (IV) infusion of 1000 mL normal saline solution containing 60 mEq/L of potassium chloride over 6 hours. How many mEq of potassium chloride are delivered per 10 mL of this solution?
questions.rationale: The amount of potassium is evenly distributed throughout the 1000 mL. Thus the potassium amount is proportional. 60 mEq are in 1000 mL, 6 mEq are in 100 mL, 0.6 mEq are in 10 mL, and 0.06 mEq are in 1 mL.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 80

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: A patient’s IV is supposed to have a drip rate of 42 gtt/min. You count 8 gtt in 15 seconds. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nothing, the IV flow rate is correct.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increase the drip rate to 10 gtt/15 seconds.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increase the drip rate to 15 gtt/15 seconds.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decrease the drip rate to 6 gtt/15 seconds.

questions.rationale: The flow rate is incorrect. With 8 gtt in 15 seconds, the drops per minute are only 32. The drip rate must be increased to 10 gtt in 15 seconds to achieve a drip rate of 42 gtt/min. At 15 gtt/15 seconds, the drip rate would be 60 gtt/min. At 6 gtt/15 seconds, the drip rate would be 24 gtt/min.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: pp. 80-81

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: What is the hourly flow rate for 250 mL of normal saline to be administered over 2 hours? mL/h
questions.rationale: The patient should receive 125 mL/h (250 mL/2 hours = 125 mL).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 80

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: A patient is to receive 1000 mL of D5W intravenously over 6 hours. The tubing set has a drop factor of 15 gtt/mL. How many drops per minute are needed to infuse this fluid in the prescribed time? gtt per minute
questions.rationale: 1000 mL in 6 hours = 167 mL/h (1000/6 = 166.6 mL, round up to 167). 2.8 mL/min (167 mL/60 minutes = 2.78 mL, round up to 2.8 mL/min); 42 gtt/min (2.8 mL  drop factor of 15 = 42 gtt/min).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 80

questions.chapter_title: Dosage Calculation of Intravenous Solutions and Drugs
questions.stem: A patient is to receive 100 mL of normal saline over the next 5 hours with microdrip tubing. How many drops per minute are required to infuse this fluid in the prescribed time? gtt per minute
questions.rationale: The flow rate for 100 mL over 5 hours is 20 mL/h (100 mL/5 hours). With microdrip tubing, the drop factor of 60 gtt/mL is the same as the number of minutes in 1 hour (60). The two sets of 60 cancel each other out, and the flow rate for microdrip tubing always equals the drop rate. So, 20 mL/h by microdrip tubing = 20 gtt/min.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 80 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which stage of the inflammatory response involves capillary leak syndrome?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stage I

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stage II

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stage III

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stage IV

questions.rationale: The first stage of the inflammatory response involves white blood cells releasing chemical substances that act on blood vessels, making the pores larger so that fluid leaks out of the blood vessels into the damaged tissue.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 84

questions.stem: Which corticosteroid is applied topically to the skin?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Betamethasone (Celestone)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Triamcinolone (Kenalog)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dexamethosone (Decadron)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Methylprednisolone (Solu-Medrol)

questions.rationale: Betemethasone and dexamethosone are oral drugs. Methylprednisolone is a parenteral drug. Of the drugs on this list, only triamcinolone is applied as a topical drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 86

questions.stem: Which problem is a common side effect of long-term corticosteroid use?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acne

choices.label: B
choices.text: Weight loss

choices.label: C
choices.text: Redness and pain

choices.label: D
choices.text: Low blood pressure

questions.rationale: Corticosteroid use has many side effects including weight gain (not loss), retention of sodium and water leading to high blood pressure (not low blood pressure), and a lot of skin changes, including acne. Corticosteroids are used to reduce inflammation, so they eliminate redness and pain, they do not cause these side effects.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 87

questions.stem: Which side effect of corticosteroid therapy is permanent even after the drug is stopped?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Difficulty sleeping

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stretch marks

choices.label: C
choices.text: Weight gain

choices.label: D
choices.text: Moon face

questions.rationale: After corticosteroid therapy is stopped, many side effects resolve although this can take months to years. The sleep disturbance, weight gain, and moon-shaped face do go away over time. The stretch marks are permanent although they usually change from reddish-purple to silver over time so that they are less noticeable.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 87

questions.stem: Which anti-inflammatory drug is classified as a “COX-1” inhibitor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hydrocortisone (Lanacort)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Celecoxib (Celebrex)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Zyleuton (Zyflo)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Naproxen (Aleve)

questions.rationale: Naproxen is the only COX-1 class anti-inflammatory drug on this list. Hydrocortisone is a corticosteroid. Celecoxib is a COX-2 inhibitor, not COX-1. Zyleuton is a leukotriene inhibitor.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 90

questions.stem: Which NSAID is a COX-2 inhibitor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Celecoxib (Celebrex)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ketorolac (Toradol)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Aspirin (Bufferin)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ibuprofen (Motrin)

questions.rationale: Celecoxib is the only drug from the COX-2 inhibitor class on this list. Aspirin, ibuprofen, and ketorolac are all COX-1 inhibitors.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 90

questions.stem: Which drug should NOT be given to children?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prednisone

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ibuprofen (Motrin)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Aspirin (Bufferin)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Acetaminophen (Tylenol)

questions.rationale: Aspirin is associated with a disorder known as Reye’s syndrome when given to a child who has a viral infection. It is not recommended to be given to anyone under 18 years of age. Prednisone, ibuprofen, and acetaminophen can be given to children if needed.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 93

questions.stem: Which drug should be avoided during the last 3 months of pregnancy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prednisone

choices.label: B
choices.text: Montelukast

choices.label: C
choices.text: Indomethicin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dyphenhydramine

questions.rationale: Although a pregnant woman should avoid taking any unnecessary drugs, some have a low likelihood of increasing the risks for birth defects or fetal damage. Prednisone, montelukast, and dyphenhydramine have this designation. Indomethicin is not given during the last 3 months of pregnancy because it can cause an important opening in fetal circulation to close too early and interfere with the unborn baby’s oxygenation.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 93

questions.stem: What is the most common side effect of antihistamines?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Drowsiness

choices.label: B
choices.text: Weight gain

choices.label: C
choices.text: Difficulty breathing

choices.label: D
choices.text: Excessive urination Memory Aide: Think of bendaryl and how it makes the person feel whom is taking it

questions.rationale: Nearly all antihistamine drugs cause some degree of drowsiness although some are worse for this than are others. Most antihistamines can cause some degree of urinary retention, not excessive urination. These drugs make breathing easier, not harder. None are associated with true weight gain.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 94

questions.stem: Which drug is a leukotriene inhibitor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Piroxicam (Feldene)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Loratadine (Claritin)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Montelukast (Singulair)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Diphenhydramine (Benadryl)

questions.rationale: The only drug on this list that works by inhibiting leukotriene is mentelukast. Loratidine and diphenhydramine are antihistamines. Piroxicam is a COX-1 inhibitor.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 96

questions.stem: Which body-produced substance is targeted and inhibited by most disease modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tumor necrosis factor (TNF)

choices.label: B
choices.text: White blood cells (WBCs)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cyclo-oxygenase (COX)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Interferon (IFN) For intro to pharm… there is no need to know the TNF (according to the PowerPoints)

questions.rationale: Most DMARDs are antibodies to the substance tumor necrosis factor (TNF), which is produced by the body as part of the inflammatory response and can attack and destroy normal body tissues.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 96

questions.stem: Why is inflammation considered a nonspecific body response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inflammation is the basis for almost every type of chronic disease.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Infection by bacteria or viruses is always a part of inflammation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The same tissue responses occur regardless of the type of triggering event.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The discomfort associated with inflammation can be reduced by over-the-counter drugs.

questions.rationale: Inflammation is a syndrome of normal tissue responses that always occur in the same way with any type of injury or invasion, regardless of the location on the body or what caused the response to start.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 83

questions.stem: When is an inflammatory response a health problem rather than a helpful protection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: When a patient’s immune system is suppressed

choices.label: B
choices.text: When infection accompanies inflammation

choices.label: C
choices.text: When inflammation is prolonged

choices.label: D
choices.text: When pain is experienced

questions.rationale: Inflammation starts tissue actions that cause visible and uncomfortable symptoms that are important in ridding the body of harmful organisms and helping repair damaged tissue. However, if the inflammatory response is prolonged or excessive, tissue damage may result.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 85

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed an oral corticosteroid for a chronic inflammatory health problem. Which precaution is most important to teach?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Never stop taking this drug without consulting your prescriber.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Avoid crowds and people who are ill.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Be sure to take this drug with food.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Reduce your salt intake.”

questions.rationale: All of the choices are precautions that the nurse should teach the patient taking an oral corticosteroid for the long term. However, the most critical precaution is to not stop taking the drug because long-term corticosteroid use causes atrophy of the adrenal glands. With adrenal gland atrophy, the individual no longer makes his or her own normal levels of corticosteroids, which are essential for life. Long-term steroid use is never suddenly stopped.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 88

questions.stem: Why are corticosteroid drugs usually prescribed for a short period of time?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The inflammatory process cannot be controlled with less powerful drugs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inhalation of corticosteroid drugs causes irreversible damage to the lungs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: These drugs work less effectively when given over an extended period.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Corticosteroids have many adverse effects and side effects.

questions.rationale: When taken orally or parenterally, corticosteroids are usually given for a short period of time because they have many adverse effects and side effects. These drugs can be given over longer periods with the goal that the patient takes the lowest dose that will control inflammation, so that side effects and adverse effects can be minimized.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 86

questions.stem: A patient who has been taking 30 mg of prednisone daily for the last 10 days reports crying more often and then suddenly getting angry at small issues. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Document the patient’s response as the only action.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hold the next dose and notify the prescriber immediately.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reassure the patient that this is an expected drug response.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Suggest that the patient see a counselor or attend an anger management class.

questions.rationale: Prednisone, a type of corticosteroid, increases a patient’s emotional responses (emotional lability). It is an uncomfortable but expected side effect that will stop after the drug has been discontinued. Usually, this side effect is not a reason to stop drug therapy and counseling or anger management will not stop the responses. It is helpful for the patient to know this and to be able to tell family members that they are not responsible for the changes in the patient’s emotional state.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 87

questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed long-term low-dose prednisone to control inflammation reports concern about the changes in her appearance caused by the drug. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “This often happens when an individual takes this drug. You now have Cushing’s disease.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “When you stop taking this drug, the changes will disappear, but it may take a while.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “You can make use of different styles of clothes to minimize changes in appearance.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Changes in your diet and an exercise program will eliminate these changes while you are on this drug.”

questions.rationale: Changes in appearance are a side effect of corticosteroids. The patient develops a Cushingoid appearance. When a patient stops taking these drugs, the side effects and changes in body appearance disappear, but it may take a year or more for this to occur.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 87

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed topical hydrocortisone 0.1% for skin inflammation. What action is most important to teach the patient for applying this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Use a thick layer of drug just to the area that needs treatment.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Apply a thin layer to the affected area and to the skin near the area.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Apply a thick layer over and 1 inch around the affected area.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Use as thin a layer as possible just to the area that needs treatment.”

questions.rationale: Topical corticosteroids are absorbed through the skin and can have some systemic effects. Thus patients should use only the minimum amount needed to control the skin problem. Teach the patient to apply only a thin layer just to the areas of skin that need treatment.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 89

questions.stem: Which assessment is most important to perform when providing care to an older adult patient who has been prescribed a topical corticosteroid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Skin assessment

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intake and output

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cognition or mental status

choices.label: D
choices.text: Urinary tract infection

questions.rationale: Topical corticosteroid use generally has no systemic effects. The skin exposed to the topical corticosteroid becomes thinner and more fragile, especially in an older adult. This area is at greater risk for injury and loss of integrity.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 89

questions.stem: A nursing home resident has been prescribed to take 20 mg of prednisolone immediately. The facility does not have prednisolone, only prednisone. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Substitute prednisone for prednisolone because they are both corticosteroids.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hold the dose until the pharmacy opens the next day.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Notify the prescriber immediately of this situation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administer the parenteral form of the drug.

questions.rationale: Although prednisone and prednisolone are both corticosteroids, they are not the same strength and only the prescriber can change the drug or its dose. Because it was ordered to be administered as soon as possible, do not wait until the next day but notify the prescriber immediately. It is possible that, if the prednisolone is not available until tomorrow, the prescriber may change the drug, its dose, or its delivery route; however, these decisions must be made only by the prescriber.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 88

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed triamcinolone (Kenalog) ointment for an allergic skin rash. Which precaution is most important to teach this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Always wear gloves when applying this ointment.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Do not use the ointment on an infected skin area.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Keep the drug refrigerated between applications.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Do not wash the ointment off until the area is healed.”

questions.rationale: Topical corticosteroids lower immunity in the area where it is applied. This means that if there is a skin infection and topical corticosteroids are applied in that area, the infection can spread to surrounding areas more easily. For this reason, do not apply a topical corticosteroid if there is any question that the skin is infected instead of just being irritated or having a rash.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 89

questions.stem: A patient who usually takes 40 mg of prednisone daily for a chronic problem has been vomiting for 12 hours. What will you tell the patient about the drug therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Go to the prescriber’s office immediately for an injectable dose of the drug.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “If you stop vomiting by tomorrow, take two doses of the drug.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Take no drugs until you stop vomiting for at least 24 hours.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Take the drug even though you are vomiting.”

questions.rationale: The patient has been taking the drug for a long time, which means his or her adrenal glands have atrophied. Cortisone is essential for life. He or she must receive it daily or acute adrenal insufficiency can occur.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 88

questions.stem: For which health problem will you specifically monitor in the older patient prescribed to take a corticosteroid for the next month?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sudden hair loss

choices.label: B
choices.text: Weight loss

choices.label: C
choices.text: High blood glucose level

choices.label: D
choices.text: Change in sleep patterns

questions.rationale: Corticosteroids reduce the activity of insulin and promote high blood glucose levels. Older adults are more likely to have diabetes and corticosteroids make it more difficult to control.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 89

questions.stem: Which statement about corticosteroid use and pediatric patients is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Adrenal atrophy does not occur during long-term use because this organ is younger and healthier in children.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because these drugs slow cell division, they can interfere with normal growth and are not used in children.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Children are at the same risk for every side effect of long-term corticosteroid use that adults experience.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Drug doses are usually higher than for adults because children metabolize corticosteroids faster than adults.

questions.rationale: Corticosteroids are prescribed for children who have severe or chronic inflammatory problems. Children are at risk for the same corticosteroid side effects as adults, including stomach ulcers.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 89

questions.stem: Which statement made by the patient newly prescribed to take a nonsteroidal anti- inflammatory drug (NSAID) indicates that more teaching is needed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I should avoid taking acetaminophen (Tylenol) any time I am also taking an over-the- counter NSAID.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Because aspirin causes me to have asthma, I should never take another NSAID like ibuprofen (Advil).”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I should try to avoid taking any NSAID, even over-the-counter NSAIDs, for more than a few days or a week.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Because Celebrex (celecoxib) has so few side effects, I don’t have to worry about how often I take it.”

questions.rationale: Because celecoxib mostly suppresses the COX-2 pathway, allowing the normal housekeeping functions of the COX-1 pathway to continue, it has fewer side effects than COX-1 NSAIDs. However, if a patient takes more than the prescribed dose, the side effects are the same as for the COX-1 NSAIDs. This drug does not affect platelet action and blood clotting. So, bruising and gum bleeding are not expected side effects.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 91

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed ibuprofen (Advil) 200 mg four times a day as needed for inflammation and pain related to arthritis. The patient is scheduled for oral surgery. What priority teaching is appropriate for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “The Advil will help relieve your pain after surgery.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Report any bruising to your prescriber immediately.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Ask about having the injectable form of this drug after surgery.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Stop taking this drug at least 36 hours before your scheduled surgery.”

questions.rationale: Ibuprofen is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID). These drugs reversibly inhibit the action of the COX enzyme. Reduced activity of COX-1 decreases platelet clumping and can reduce blood clotting for 1 to 2 days. Any patient taking a COX-1 NSAID is at increased risk for bleeding and should stop taking the drug 1 to 2 days before surgery. There is no injectable form of ibuprofen.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 91

questions.stem: A patient taking a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) tells you that the drug causes an upset stomach. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Always take this drug with food or with milk.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Stop taking the drug and notify your prescriber.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Try chewing the drug instead of swallowing it whole.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Take the drug on an empty stomach to avoid vomiting.”

questions.rationale: Upset stomach is a common side effect for NSAIDs. Most of the time, this side effect can be reduced or avoided by ensuring that the drug is taken on a full stomach, with food or with milk.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 91

questions.stem: For which drug should you ask a patient about an allergy to “sulfa” drugs before administering it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aspirin (Bufferin)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ketorolac (Toradol)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Celecoxib (Celebrex)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Oxaprozin (Daypro)

questions.rationale: Celecoxib (Celebrex) is made from a chemical similar to the “sulfa drug” type of antibiotic. A patient who is allergic to sulfa drugs is likely to also be allergic to celecoxib.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 91

questions.stem: Which new symptom in a patient taking celecoxib (Celebrex) requires immediate medical attention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Headache

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ankle swelling

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chest pain

choices.label: D
choices.text: Itchy nose

questions.rationale: Celecoxib is a COX-2 inhibitor. Other drugs from this class have caused an increased incidence of strokes and heart attacks by promoting inappropriate blood clot formation in small blood vessels. Although the incidence of this problem is lower with celecoxib, any patient with new onset chest pain should be seen immediately.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 92

questions.stem: An older adult is prescribed naproxen (Naprosyn) 250 mg orally twice daily for management of arthritis pain. Which precaution is most important to teach the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Weigh yourself daily every morning, and if you gain more than 3 to 4 lb in a week, report it to your prescriber.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Avoid taking blood pressure medicine at the same time as this drug because Naprosyn inactivates these drugs.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Go to the emergency department for an injection of Naprosyn if you are vomiting and cannot keep the drug down.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Drink a full glass of water every time you take a dose of Naprosyn.”

questions.rationale: Naproxen and other NSAIDs cause salt and water retention that can lead to fluid overload and high blood pressure, increasing the risk for heart attack or heart failure. Teach older adult patients taking NSAIDs to check their weight daily and to report any weight gain of more than 3 to 4 lb to the prescriber. This amount of weight gain is associated with fluid retention rather than increased body fat. NSAIDs reduce the effectiveness of some blood pressure drugs because of the fluid retention, not because they inactivate the drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 93

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed 20 mg of ketorolac (Toradol) intramuscularly. How many milliliters should you draw up from a vial of ketorolac containing 30 mg/mL?
questions.rationale: 1 is to 30 as X is to 20, so 1/30 = X/20; 20 1 = 20; 20/30 = 0.67 mL, rounded up to 0.7 mL.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 90

questions.stem: Why are aspirin and aspirin-containing drugs avoided for use in children?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aspirin is associated with the development of Reye’s syndrome.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aspirin would drop a child’s body temperature below normal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Children are at higher risk for bleeding with aspirin use.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A child’s liver is unable to metabolize aspirin.

questions.rationale: When children have a viral infection and are given aspirin, Reye’s syndrome—a liver disease that can lead to coma, mental retardation, and death—may develop.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 93

questions.stem: A patient has all of the following health problems and seasonal allergies. Which should you warn the patient can be made worse by over-the-counter antihistamines?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Asthma

choices.label: B
choices.text: Breast cancer

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rheumatoid arthritis

choices.label: D
choices.text: High blood pressure

questions.rationale: When antihistamines bind to histamine receptors on blood vessels, they cause blood vessels to constrict. Blood vessel constriction raises blood pressure. When the patient already has high blood pressure, these drugs can make the problem worse.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 95

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man has been taking diphenhydramine (Benadryl) for seasonal allergies. Which new symptom should you report to the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased urination

choices.label: B
choices.text: Drowsiness

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dry mouth

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cough

questions.rationale: Diphenhydramine is an antihistamine with anticholinergic actions that include dry mouth, drowsiness, and urinary retention. An enlarged prostate is common among older men and causes difficulty urinating. This problem can be made much worse when taking a drug that can cause urinary retention.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 95

questions.stem: A patient who has been taking montelukast (Singulair) for 3 months reports having urine the color of coffee. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
