choices.text: “You need to increase your intake of water and decrease your intake of coffee.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “You need to stop taking this drug and notify your prescriber today.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Don’t worry. All patients taking this drug have dark urine.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Do not take this drug any more often than prescribed.”

questions.rationale: Montelukast is a leukotriene inhibitor and can cause liver damage. Dark urine is one sign of liver damage. The patient needs to stop taking the drug and see his or her health care provider to determine whether liver damage is present.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 96

questions.stem: A patient with an inflammatory response is prescribed ranitidine (Zantac) 75 mg once a day. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Give the drug as ordered.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hold the drug and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Teach the patient to take the drug first thing in the morning.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient if he or she is allergic to any drugs or other substances.

questions.rationale: Ranitidine (Zantac) is an H 2 receptor blocker used to decrease production of stomach acids. The prescriber has confused this drug with cetirizine (Zyrtec), an H1 receptor blocker used to slow or stop tissue effects of inflammation.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 94

questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed diphenhydramine (Benadryl) reports a dry mouth and feeling sleepy. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Document the finding and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Remind the patient that naps provide needed rest.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Suggest sucking on hard candy to relieve dry mouth.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reassure the patient that these are expected side effects.

questions.rationale: Diphenhydramine is an H1 receptor blocker commonly used for inflammation triggered by allergic reactions such as hives, watery eyes, and runny nose. Side effects of these drugs include dry mouth and throat as well as drowsiness. Sometimes these drugs are prescribed as a sleep aid.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 94

questions.stem: Which precaution is important to teach a patient who takes antihistamines at home?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Avoid drinking coffee and other beverages containing caffeine.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Avoid drinking alcohol within 4 hours of taking the drug.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Do not stop taking this drug suddenly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Avoid taking aspirin while on this drug.

questions.rationale: Most antihistamines induce some degree of drowsiness or sleepiness. Alcohol potentiates this effect and the patient could fall or have another type of accident.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 94

questions.stem: A patient prescribed zileutin (Zyflo) has yellowing of the sclera. Which laboratory test result is most important to check?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Kidney function tests

choices.label: B
choices.text: Complete blood count

choices.label: C
choices.text: Liver function tests

choices.label: D
choices.text: Serum electrolytes

questions.rationale: Zileutin is a leukotriene inhibitor with rare adverse effects that include liver impairment and allergic reactions.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 96

questions.stem: Which question is most important to ask a patient before giving the first dose of etaneracept (Enbrel)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Do you have diabetes?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Are you pregnant or breastfeeding?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Do you have psoriasis or another skin disorder?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Have you ever had tuberculosis or hepatitis B or C?

questions.rationale: Etaneracept is a disease modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) that lowers immunity. If the patient has ever had certain viral diseases or tuberculosis, the disease can reappear in an active form when the immune response is suppressed. Previous infection with any of these diseases is a contraindication for this drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 97 BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which symptoms of inflammation are caused by leakage of blood plasma into the tissues at the site of injury or invasion? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased production and migration of leukocytes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Phagocytosis and fever

choices.label: C
choices.text: Warmth and redness

choices.label: D
choices.text: Swelling and pain

questions.rationale: With capillary leak, blood plasma leaks into the tissues causing swelling and pain. The swelling creates a cushion of fluid to prevent more tissue injury. Swelling alone can compress pain receptors (nociceptors) and result in pain perception.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 84

questions.stem: Which factors determine the size and severity of the inflammatory response? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intensity of the injury

choices.label: B
choices.text: Type of tissue injured

choices.label: C
choices.text: Severity of the invasion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Gender of the patient

choices.label: E
choices.text: Extent of injury

choices.label: F
choices.text: Duration of the invasion

questions.rationale: The inflammatory response is nonspecific. The same tissue responses occur with any type of invasion or injury. Size and severity of the inflammatory response depend on intensity, severity, duration, and extent of the injury or invasion.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 83

questions.stem: Which main signs and symptoms should you teach a patient to recognize as inflammation? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Warmth

choices.label: B
choices.text: Redness

choices.label: C
choices.text: Headache

choices.label: D
choices.text: Swelling

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pain

choices.label: F
choices.text: Bleeding g Decreased function

questions.rationale: The five main signs and symptoms of inflammation are warmth, redness, swelling, pain, and loss of function.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 84 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.stem: What is another name for an opioid drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nanogram

choices.label: B
choices.text: Narcoleptic

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nociceptor

choices.label: D
choices.text: Narcotic

questions.rationale: Opioid analgesics, also called narcotics, are drugs that contain any  ingredient derived from the poppy plant (or a similar synthetic chemical) that change an individual’s perception of pain and have the potential for psychologic or physical dependence.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 106

questions.stem: How long must pain be present to be considered “chronic pain?”

choices.label: A
choices.text: 6 days

choices.label: B
choices.text: 6 weeks

choices.label: C
choices.text: 6 months

choices.label: D
choices.text: 6 years

questions.rationale: Chronic pain is present daily for 6 months. It persists or increases with time, may not have an identifiable cause, and does not trigger the physiologic responses associated with acute pain. This means that an individual with chronic pain can have severe pain intensity without changes from the normal ranges for heart rate, breathing rate, or blood pressure. Chronic pain may hurt less on some days than others but is always present.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 104

questions.stem: On a 0 to 10 pain rating scale, which of the following numbers reported by a patient represents the worst pain intensity?
questions.rationale: How much pain the patient feels is called pain intensity. There are several ways to work with the patient to determine pain intensity. When a patient can speak and use a pain scale, the lower numbers indicate less pain, while the higher numbers indicate more pain.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 102

questions.stem: What word describes pain that comes and goes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Localized

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intermittent

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chronic

choices.label: D
choices.text: Referred

questions.rationale: Pain is divided into types on the basis of its cause, how long it lasts, and whether it is present continuously or comes and goes (intermittent).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 104

questions.stem: What statement about cancer pain is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cancer pain has more than one cause.

choices.label: B
choices.text: All patients with cancer have severe pain.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cancer pain is considered chronic rather than acute.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Opioids are the only class of pain drugs that reduce cancer pain.

questions.rationale: Cancer pain has many causes and is complex. This means that more than one pain strategy and often more than one type of drug for pain control are needed. The patient with cancer often receives traditional pain-control drugs but at much higher doses than those prescribed for other types of pain.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 104

questions.stem: Which schedule of controlled substance has the lowest potential for addiction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Schedule II

choices.label: B
choices.text: Schedule III

choices.label: C
choices.text: Schedule IV

choices.label: D
choices.text: Schedule V

questions.rationale: In the United States any drug that contains ingredients known to be addictive is classified by the federal government as a controlled substance and is regulated by the Federal Controlled Substances Act of 1970. This act classifies controlled substances into five schedules based on how likely they are to result in addiction. The drugs most likely to lead to addiction are in schedule I. Those with the least potential for addiction are in schedule V.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 105

questions.stem: In which controlled substance class or schedule does heroin belong?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Schedule I

choices.label: B
choices.text: Schedule II

choices.label: C
choices.text: Schedule III

choices.label: D
choices.text: Schedule IV

choices.label: E
choices.text: Schedule V

questions.rationale: Heroin has a very high likelihood of abuse and addiction. In addition, it has no accepted medical use in the United States. Therefore it meets the criteria for a controlled substance schedule I drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 105

questions.stem: Which problem is a psychologic response to chronic opioid use?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tolerance

choices.label: B
choices.text: Addiction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Withdrawal

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dependence

questions.rationale: The definition for addiction is the psychologic need or craving for the “high” feeling that results from using opioids when pain is not present.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 106

questions.stem: What is the most common side effect of an opioid used for 5 days?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Addiction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hallucinations

choices.label: C
choices.text: Constipation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Excessive thirst

questions.rationale: The most common side effect of opioids is constipation because these drugs slow intestinal movement. Most patients who are on opioids for 2 or more days experience constipation. Ask patients who are prescribed opioids about constipation on a daily basis. Be sure to administer any prescribed stool softeners or laxatives.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 108

questions.stem: Which is the best clinical definition of pain?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A state of extreme physical distress or discomfort

choices.label: B
choices.text: A condition of sensation caused by tissue damage

choices.label: C
choices.text: A cognitive awareness of a change in comfort

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whatever the patient says it is

questions.rationale: Pain is a personal experience that includes physical and emotional components. What is painful for one individual may not be painful to another. Because everyone experiences pain in a different way, the most useful clinical definition is that pain is whatever the patient says it is and exists whenever he or she says it does.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 101

questions.stem: Which statement about pain is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Each patient perceives a painful event differently.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patients who cannot describe their pain do not really have pain.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is not necessary to assess for pain in patients who are sleeping.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Infants and young children feel pain less intensely than adults do.

questions.rationale: How we feel and react to pain depends on our emotional makeup along with our previous experiences with pain. Issues like culture, age, gender, and our interactions with society also affect our responses to pain. As a result, no two people perceive pain in exactly the same way.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 103

questions.stem: A patient who is paralyzed from the waist down as a result of an injury that completely severed the spinal cord has an open wound on the right heel. Why is this patient unaware of any pain caused from this wound?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The nociceptors in the heel are no longer stimulated when injury occurs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient has become completely desensitized to this type of chronic pain.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The severed spinal cord prevents the sensation of pain from reaching the brain.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The spinal cord injury results in chronic pain that can mask any acute pain sensation.

questions.rationale: Pain is perceived in the brain, not in the area of tissue injury. When the nociceptors are stimulated by tissue damage, the impulse must be transmitted to the brain before it can be “felt” as pain. With a completely severed spinal cord, the pain impulses are not transmitted to the brain.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 102

questions.stem: A patient with arthritis of the left knee reports pain extending from the knee half-way down the lower leg. What type of pain is this patient perceiving?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Localized

choices.label: B
choices.text: Referred

choices.label: C
choices.text: Radiating

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phantom

questions.rationale: Radiating pain may be felt all around and extending from the damaged area causing the pain.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 104

questions.stem: Pain is considered to be the “fifth vital sign.” How does this principle affect patient care?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It ensures that pain assessment occurs on a regular basis for all patients.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It helps health care workers understand that experiencing pain can change heart rate and respiratory rate.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It helps health care workers to ensure that vital organ function is adequate before administering drugs to reduce pain.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It encourages health care providers to assess two parameters during patient contact to improve time management.

questions.rationale: Pain is common in patients and undertreated pain remains a major yet avoidable health problem. More frequent and more accurate assessment can improve pain management. Making pain assessment the “fifth vital sign” assists health care workers to both recognize the presence of pain and see how the patient responds to drugs and other interventions. These actions increase the likelihood of appropriate pain management.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 104

questions.stem: Which event or condition is most likely to result in chronic pain?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Severe headache associated with a spinal tap

choices.label: B
choices.text: Insertion of a needle for intravenous (IV) therapy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hip replacement surgery

choices.label: D
choices.text: Osteoarthritis

questions.rationale: The definition of chronic pain involves the length of time that pain is experienced and the progressive nature of the problem causing the pain. Osteoarthritis meets these criteria. A headache caused by fluid loss during a spinal tap is severe but has a duration of only 3 to 5 days. Hip replacement surgery, although resulting in postoperative pain, is time limited and often relieves chronic hip pain. IV needle insertion is considered painful by some patients, but is short procedural pain.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 104

questions.stem: How do morphine and other opioid pain medications relieve a patient’s pain?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They reduce tissue damage and alter the physical problems causing the pain.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They bind to opioid receptors in the central nervous system and alter the perception of pain.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They inhibit the generation of impulses along sensory nerve tracts and alter pain transmission.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They redirect substance P release from nociceptors and alter the amount of neurotransmitters reaching the brain.

questions.rationale: Morphine and other opioid agonists do nothing to change the cause or transmission of pain. They bind to naturally occurring opioid receptor sites and alter a patient’s perception of the painful experience.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 107

questions.stem: How do nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) relieve a patient’s pain?

choices.label: A
choices.text: By promoting release of anti-inflammatory chemicals and altering the sensitivity of nerve tracts.

choices.label: B
choices.text: By binding to opioid receptors in the central nervous system and altering the perception of pain.

choices.label: C
choices.text: By reducing the amount of bradykinin at the site of injury and altering the stimulation of nociceptors.

choices.label: D
choices.text: By inhibiting the generation of impulses along sensory nerve tracts and altering pain transmission.

questions.rationale: When pain mediators are released from damaged tissue, especially substance P and bradykinin (which is also an inflammatory mediator), they bind to the nociceptors and activate them. NSAIDs reduce pain by suppressing some part of the inflammatory pathway and reducing the amounts of pain-mediating chemicals, especially bradykinin, present. With less bradykinin present, less stimulation of the nociceptors occurs. As a result, pain is reduced.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 103

questions.stem: A child who is taking gabapentin (Neurontin) for pain control has begun demonstrating all of the following behaviors. Which behavior represents a common side effect of this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fighting at school

choices.label: B
choices.text: Difficulty sleeping

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wetting the bed three to four nights per week

choices.label: D
choices.text: Crying more frequently for no apparent reason

questions.rationale: Gabapentin is an anticonvulsant drug that can reduce certain types of pain. Children taking gabapentin often demonstrate an increase in aggressive behavior.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: pp. 111-112

questions.stem: Which term best describes what occurs when long-term opioid is suddenly stopped?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Addiction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tolerance

choices.label: C
choices.text: Withdrawal

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dependence

questions.rationale: Withdrawal is the occurrence of autonomic nervous system symptoms when long-term opioid therapy is stopped suddenly after physical dependence is present. Symptoms include nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramping, sweating, delirium, and seizures.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 108 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which statement by a patient indicates the need for more teaching about pain and pain control?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “If my pain interferes with my usual activities, I will take medication for it.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “There is no reason for me to take drugs for pain; after all, you can’t cure old age.” Pain is a treatable condition.

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I don’t mind taking pain drugs for my sprained ankle because I know it won’t hurt this way forever.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I will take enough pain medication to make me comfortable without making me too sleepy.”

questions.rationale: One cause of underreporting pain and undertreating it is that many patients and health care providers believe that pain is a normal part of aging. Pain may occur more frequently among older adults, but is never considered “normal.” Patients of any age with pain for any reason deserve to have their pain reduced to a manageable level.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 104

questions.stem: What is the best way for you to determine a patient’s need for pain medication on the second day after an abdominal laparotomy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check when the patient last received medication for pain.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assess the patient’s facial expression and vital signs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Consider the patient’s age and ethnicity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient to rate his or her pain.

questions.rationale: Pain experience and pain tolerance are very personal. True assessment of a patient’s discomfort cannot be determined by and should not be based on the patient’s behaviors or changes in vital signs. Nor can it be based on how recently the patient received a drug for pain. The only way to know is to ask the patient to rate the pain.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 103

questions.stem: After surgery, a patient expresses the fear of becoming addicted to the opioid analgesic that has been prescribed for pain. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Opioid-based drugs are not addictive.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Have you or anyone in your family ever been addicted to drugs?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “When opioid drugs are taken for acute pain, they are rarely addictive.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “If you take the medication no more frequently than every 4 hours, it is not possible for you to become addicted.”

questions.rationale: The fear of addiction to opioids is one cause of poorly treated pain. Remind the patient that addiction will not occur if the drugs are taken to relieve pain. Pain after surgery is acute and temporary pain. The use of opioid drugs, when used for relief of acute pain, even in high doses, rarely results in addiction.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 106

questions.stem: You are assigned to care for a patient with chronic low back pain. What adjustment in pain management do you expect to make?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Encouraging the patient to hold off taking a pain drug as long as possible to reduce the potential for addiction.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Relying on patient report of pain rather than on changes in heart rate, blood pressure, and pulse rate.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Keeping the environment as quiet as possible to avoid distracting or irritating the patient.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Using nondrug measures in place of analgesics to relieve the patient’s pain.

questions.rationale: Adaptation to the presence of chronic pain is physiologic, not psychologic. Thus the usual alterations in physiologic parameters when acute pain is present do not accompany chronic pain.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF: p. 104

questions.stem: You check a patient for pain relief 1 hour after administering 15 mg of morphine intramuscularly. The patient is sleeping and has a respiratory rate of 10 breaths/min. What is your best first action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Attempt to arouse the patient by calling his or her name and lightly shaking the arm.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer oxygen by mask or nasal cannula and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check the patient’s oxygen saturation and raise the head of the bed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Document the finding as the only action.

questions.rationale: Many patients experience some degree of respiratory depression with opioid analgesics. If the patient can be aroused with minimally intrusive techniques and the respiratory rate increases spontaneously, no further intervention is required.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: pp. 108-109

questions.stem: You prepare to give a patient the next scheduled dose of an opioid analgesic. The patient arouses easily but the respiratory rate remains at 10 breaths/min. What is your best first action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hold the dose and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hold the dose and apply oxygen by mask or nasal cannula.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check the patient’s oxygen saturation and ask about his or her pain level.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Call the Rapid Response Team and prepare to administer the prescribed opioid antagonist.

questions.rationale: Many people experience mild respiratory depression with opioid analgesics. If the patient is easily arousable and the oxygen saturation is at normal levels, it is not necessary to apply oxygen, call the Rapid Response Team, or prepare to administer an opioid antagonist. If the patient’s oxygen saturation level is acceptable and he or she is in pain, it is alright to give the next scheduled opioid dose. Checking the patient’s normal respiratory rate is also a good idea. Most people have a usual respiratory rate that is at least 12 breaths/min, but some patients may have a usual rate of only 10 breaths/min. Although this is not a customary response and you should document it in the patient care notes, check other indicators of breathing adequacy before notifying the prescriber.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 109

questions.stem: A patient taking oxycodone with acetaminophen (Percocet) for pain at home 3 days after fracturing an ankle reports constipation and continuing moderate to severe pain that is relieved by the prescribed drug. What is your best advice?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Either increase the time between drug doses or take only half the dose at each scheduled time.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Stop taking the Percocet and switch to acetaminophen alone.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Wrap your foot tightly and walk for at least 30 minutes daily.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Drink at least 3 L of fluid daily and increase fiber intake.”

questions.rationale: This patient still needs the Percocet. Most patients taking opioids for 2 days or longer have constipation. Urge the patient to drink plenty of fluids. Increasing fiber intake, either with food containing fiber or with over-the-counter fiber supplements, can help reduce constipation. Although increasing activity can help reduce constipation, walking for 30 minutes with a fractured ankle is not permitted at this time.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 109

questions.stem: An older adult is taking an oral opioid drug at home for pain control. Which precaution to prevent injury is important for you to teach this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Increase room lighting to reduce the risk for tripping.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Sleep in a sitting position to reduce respiratory problems.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Drink at least 3 L of fluids daily to reduce constipation.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Avoid drinks containing caffeine to prevent inactivating the drug.”

questions.rationale: In addition to the usual side effects and adverse effect of opioids, an older adult is at risk for low vision. The pupil of the older adult does not dilate fully and less light enters the eye, reducing vision. When the older patient takes an opioid drug, the pupil is even smaller than usual, reducing vision even more. This problem increases the older patient’s risk for tripping over objects and falling.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF:  p. 110

questions.stem: What is the most important question to ask before administering the first dose of celecoxib (Celebrex) to a patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Do you floss your teeth daily?”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Are you allergic to sulfa drugs?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Do you have diabetes mellitus?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Have you ever had glaucoma?”

questions.rationale: Celecoxib is similar to the class of antibiotics known as “sulfa drugs.” An allergic reaction to celecoxib is more likely if the patient is also allergic to sulfa drugs.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF:  p. 111

questions.stem: A patient taking warfarin (Coumadin) has mild to moderate pain after exercising. Which over-the-counter pain reliever should you recommend?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aspirin (Bufferin)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ibuprofen (Advil)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Naproxen (Aleve)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Acetaminophen (Tylenol)

questions.rationale: Only acetaminophen does not interfere with blood clotting. The other drugs do interfere with blood clotting, and so does warfarin. Taking warfarin with any other drug that interferes with blood clotting places the patient at extreme risk for excessive bleeding and brain hemorrhage.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 111

questions.stem: A patient is taking acetaminophen (Tylenol) for mild headache pain. Which precaution is most important for you to teach the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Avoid alcoholic beverages while taking this drug.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Avoid coffee and other caffeinated drinks while taking this drug.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “If any decrease in vision occurs, stop the drug and notify your prescriber immediately.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Do not drive or operate dangerous machinery until you know how this drug affects you.”

questions.rationale: Acetaminophen can cause severe liver damage and even liver failure when taken at high doses or too often. This adverse reaction is much more likely to occur in people who drink alcoholic beverages while on acetaminophen therapy.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 111

questions.stem: You note all of the following changes in the last week in a 72-year-old nursing home patient taking nortriptyline (Pamelor) for chronic pain. For which problem do you immediately notify the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Heart rate decreased from 80 to 72 beats/min.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Respiratory rate decreased from 20 to 16 breaths/min.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Weight increased from 128 to 137 lb.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Morning blood glucose increased from 86 to 94 mg/dL.

questions.rationale: Nortriptyline is an antidepressant. These drugs can make heat failure worse and can cause urinary retention. Fluid retention with weight gain is a symptom of worsening heart failure. A weight gain of 9 lb in a week is significant and an indicator of rapidly worsening heart failure.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 111

questions.stem: Eight weeks after surgery, a patient without pain still takes oxycodone and acetaminophen (Percocet) four to six times a day. What phenomenon do you recognize?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Addiction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dependence

choices.label: C
choices.text: Withdrawal

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tolerance

questions.rationale: Dependence is described as physical changes in autonomic nervous system function that can occur when opioids are used long term and are not needed for pain control.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 108

questions.stem: A patient tells you that the usual dose of an opioid drug for cancer pain no longer relieves the pain. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “We have to be careful about increasing opioid drug dosages to prevent drug addiction.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “You may be developing dependence on the drug and that is why it no longer relieves your pain.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Your body may be adjusting to the drug, eliminating it more rapidly, and thus you will need a higher dose to achieve pain relief.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “The danger of increasing your opioid drug dosage is that you may experience respiratory failure.”

questions.rationale: An issue that can occur with longer-term opioid use is drug tolerance. Tolerance is the adjustment of the body to long-term opioid use that increases the rate of drug elimination and reduces the main effect (pain relief) and side effects of the drug. It occurs with anyone who is taking opioids for a long period of time. More drug is needed to achieve the same degree of pain relief.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 106

questions.stem: You are administering the first dose of an opioid drug to a patient. Which specific related actions should you take before and after giving the dose?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask if the patient is allergic to sulfa drugs and monitor for a reaction after giving the drug.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Place the patient on bed rest before giving the drug and keep him or her on bed rest after to avoid accidental falling.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check the patient’s respiratory rate and oxygen saturation before and after giving the drug and monitor for respiratory depression.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administer a drug such as naloxone (Narcan) both before and after giving the opioid drug to prevent respiratory depression.

questions.rationale: When giving the first dose of an opioid to a patient who has never taken an opioid (is opioid naïve), check the patient’s respiratory rate and oxygen saturation. Opioids can cause some degree of respiratory depression. After giving the drug, be sure to monitor the patient’s respiratory rate and oxygen saturation for indications of respiratory depression. This is especially important when the patient is receiving an opioid for the first time or when the drug dosage has been increased.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 108

questions.stem: A patient is receiving morphine (MS Contin) for severe cancer pain. His wife states that he has difficulty swallowing and asks if the tablets can be crushed and given with applesauce or pudding. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “That is an excellent solution and will make it much easier for him to swallow his pain medicine.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “It might be best if we talk with the prescriber about having a feeding tube placed for medication administration.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “You can dissolve the tablets in water then give the drug to your husband mixed with juice.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I will contact the prescriber about this because your husband may need to be prescribed a different form of morphine for his pain.”

questions.rationale: MS Contin is an extended release tablet so crushing or dissolving it can cause a drug overdose from releasing too much drug at a time. The best action is to contact the prescriber with the information about the patient’s difficulty swallowing because a different form or morphine or a different drug may be needed to control the pain. Teach patients and their families to take an extended release (ER) form of an oral opioid drug by swallowing the capsule or tablet whole because chewing it or opening the capsule allows too much of the drug to be absorbed all at once and an overdose can occur.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 109

questions.stem: Which key point should you teach a pregnant woman who is prescribed opioids drugs during pregnancy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Your child may become addicted to opioids and go through withdrawal after birth.

choices.label: B
choices.text: These drugs do not cross the placenta so are safe to use during pregnancy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Some opioids have been found to cause birth defects in animal studies.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Opioids are not present in breast milk so it is safe to breastfeed.

questions.rationale: Opioids may be prescribed to women during pregnancy. These drugs do cross the placenta and enter the fetus. The fetus can become addicted to opioids and go through withdrawal after birth. Opioids also cross into breast milk.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 110

questions.stem: For which opioid pain drug order should you contact and question the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Adult male, hydromorphone (Dilaudid) 4 mg orally every 4 hours

choices.label: B
choices.text: Older adult, meperidine (Demerol) 50 mg IM every 3 hours

choices.label: C
choices.text: Child, codeine 0.5 mg/kg orally every 6 hours

choices.label: D
choices.text: Adult women, hydrocodone with acetaminophen (Vicodin) 5 mg orally every 6 hours

questions.rationale: Opioids, especially meperidine (Demerol), can make the chest muscles of older adults tighter, which makes breathing and coughing more difficult. Thus the risk for pneumonia and hypoxia is greater for them. Check the respiratory rate and depth as well as the oxygen saturation at least every 2 hours. In addition, meperidine causes the buildup of a toxic metabolite in older adults that can result in seizures. Avoid the use of meperidine in older adults.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 110

questions.stem: After receiving the first dose of an opioid drug, a patient is sleeping and has a respiratory rate of less than 8 per minute. When called by name the patient does not respond. What is your next best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Place an ice cube on his or her forehead.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Firmly shake his or her leg or arm.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Apply pressure to his or her nail bed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Squeeze his or her trapezius muscle.

questions.rationale: When the patient is receiving an opioid for the first time or when the drug dosage has been increased, if the respiratory rate is 8 or less and the patient is sleeping, try to wake him or her. First call the patient’s name. If there is no response, gently shake his or her arm or leg. Shake more firmly if needed. If the patient does not respond to these actions, use a slightly stronger trigger (without using enough force to cause harm) such as squeezing the trapezius muscle (located at the angle of the shoulder and neck muscle) or applying pressure to the nail bed.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 109

questions.stem: The patient who received a first dose of an opioid drug is not arousable and has an oxygen saturation of 88% (five percentage points lower than his or her normal saturation) even when fully awake. What is your best next action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Wait 2 minutes and recheck respiratory rate and oxygen saturation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Shake the patient firmly and keep trying to arouse him or her.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Notify respiratory therapy to draw an arterial blood gas.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Call for help and apply supplemental oxygen.

questions.rationale: If the patient cannot be aroused, immediately call for help. If the patient’s oxygen saturation is below 95% or is five percentage points lower than his or her normal saturation, arouse the patient and check the saturation when fully awake. If the saturation does not improve when fully awake, apply supplemental oxygen and notify the charge nurse or prescriber.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 109 BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which side effects are commonly associated with most nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)? (select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bleeding

choices.label: B
choices.text: Constipation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Drowsiness

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dry mouth

choices.label: E
choices.text: Gastrointestinal ulcers

choices.label: F
choices.text: Hypertension g Memory loss (temporary)

questions.rationale: NSAIDs disrupt platelet action and reduce clotting, which increases the risk for bleeding in response to minor trauma. NSAIDs also reduce the thick, gel-like coating of the stomach, allowing normal stomach acids to irritate the stomach lining and form ulcers. Finally, NSAIDs cause the kidneys to retain more sodium and water. These enter the bloodstream and raise blood pressure.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 111 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: A 64 lb child with a broken arm is prescribed to receive morphine 75 mcg/kg by intravenous push immediately. The drug available is morphine 1 mg/mL (1000 mcg/mL). What is the correct dose for this patient, in mL?
questions.rationale: 1 kg = 2.2 lb. The child’s weight in kilogram is 64/2.2 or 29.09 kg (round down to 29). 29  75 mcg = 2175 mcg or 2.175 mL round up to 2.2 mL.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 107

questions.stem: A 2-month-old infant who weighs 11 lb is prescribed to receive acetaminophen 8 mg/kg by oral liquid. The drug on hand is acetaminophen liquid with a concentration of 80 mg/0.8 mL. How many milliliters is the correct dose for this patient?
questions.rationale: 1 kg = 2.2 lb. The infant’s weight in kilogram is 11/2.2 or 5 kg. 5kg  8 mg = 40 mg. The drug concentration is 80 mg/0.8 mL or 10 mg/0.1 mL. 40 mg/10 = 4. 4  0.1 mL = 0.4 mL.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 107 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which type of bacteria cause infection when a patient’s immune system is impaired?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pathogenic

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nonpathogenic

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gram-negative

choices.label: D
choices.text: Opportunistic

questions.rationale: Pathogenic bacteria cause disease or tissue damage while nonpathogenic bacteria do not. Opportunistic bacteria cause disease or tissue damage only when the immune system is not working well.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 115

questions.stem: What does the term “virulence” mean?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bacteria that do not cause infection or systemic disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: How easily a bacterium can be killed by antibiotic therapy

choices.label: C
choices.text: How well bacteria can invade and spread within the body

choices.label: D
choices.text: How many different types of bacteria an antibiotic can kill

questions.rationale: Virulence is a measure of how well or efficiently a microorganism can gain entry into the body, reproduce there, and make the host sick even when the host has a normal immune system. The more virulent a bacterium is, the more dangerous it is and the harder it is to kill or eliminate.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Remembering   REF:  p. 115

questions.stem: What is the main feature of an opportunistic infection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It usually leads rapidly to sepsis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Children are not susceptible to this type of infection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is most often caused by pathogenic microorganisms.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is only found in people who are immunosuppressed.

questions.rationale: We have many microorganisms that are on or in our bodies and do not cause an infection because our immune systems are able to keep their numbers low and prevent them from moving beyond their normal body location. For example, we all have E. coli in our intestinal tracts and this does not cause us harm as long as our immune systems are working well. Even if a few E. coli leave the intestinal tract and enter the blood or the urine, they usually do not cause an infection because our immune systems get rid of these organisms when they are located where they do not belong. However, a person with a suppressed or weak immune system may not be able to remove or destroy these organisms when they are located outside of the intestinal tract and an infection develops. This is called an opportunistic infection because the organism is taking advantage of the host’s weakened immune system and using the opportunity to grow elsewhere in the body.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 115

questions.stem: Which problem is a sign or symptom of an allergic or anaphylactic response to an antibacterial drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Diarrhea

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hair loss

choices.label: C
choices.text: High blood pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Swelling of the face or lips

questions.rationale: Most antibacterial drugs induce diarrhea when taken for a week or longer because they kill off so many of the normal bacteria in the intestinal tract. This is not an allergy and the immune system is taking no part in causing the diarrhea. Hair loss is not a common side effect of antibacterial therapy nor is it an allergic reaction. When a true systemic allergic reaction occurs, it is usually accompanied by low blood pressure because of widespread blood vessel dilation. This blood vessel dilation of a true allergic response often causes swelling of the face, lips, and tongue.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 117

questions.stem: Which drug category includes the penicillins?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Protein synthesis inhibitors

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cell wall synthesis inhibitors

choices.label: C
choices.text: DNA synthesis inhibitors

choices.label: D
choices.text: Metabolism inhibitors

questions.rationale: The action of penicillin is disruption of the ability of a bacterium to make, repair, or maintain its cell walls. So, penicillins belong to the cell wall synthesis inhibitor class of antibacterial drugs. Penicillins do not interfere with the bacteria’s ability to make proteins, make DNA, or metabolize.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 118

questions.stem: Which drug is a first-generation cephalosporin?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cefepime (Maxipime)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cefotaxime (Claforan)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cefoxitin (Mefoxin)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cefazolin (Kefzol)

questions.rationale: Only cefazolin (Kefzol) is a first-generation cephalosporin. The other drugs are all cephalosporins but cefepime is fourth-generation, cefotaxime is third-generation, and cefoxitin is second-generation.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 121

questions.stem: Which two drug types have almost identical chemical structures?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aminoglycosides and “sulfa drugs”

choices.label: B
choices.text: Macrolides and fluoroquinolones

choices.label: C
choices.text: Penicillins and cephalosporins

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tetracyclines and penicillins

questions.rationale: Both the penicillins and the cephalosporins have a beta lactam ring as part of their chemical structures, making them nearly identical in how they work and the side effects they have. All of the other drugs on this list have unique chemical structures.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 121

questions.stem: Which drug is most often used to treat ear infections in children?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Imipenem (Primaxin)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Amoxicillin (Amoxil)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vancomycin (Vancocin)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim (Bactrim)

questions.rationale: Most of the bacteria that invade the middle ear of children (and adults) have cell walls and are sensitive to the less potent cell wall synthesis inhibitors, especially amoxicillin. Both imipenem and vancomycin are powerful antibacterial drugs and are only used for severe infections that do not respond to less potent drugs. Sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim is used mainly for infections of the urinary tract.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 123

questions.stem: Which protein synthesis inhibitors can raise the pressure inside the brain?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aminoglycosides

choices.label: B
choices.text: Macrolides

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lincosamides

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tetracyclines

questions.rationale: Of the drugs on this list, only the tetracyclines have a potential adverse effect of increasing pressure inside the brain.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 131

questions.stem: Which drug can cause tooth discoloration when given to young children?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aminoglycosides

choices.label: B
choices.text: Macrolides

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lincosamides

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tetracyclines

questions.rationale: Tetracyclines disrupt the layering of tooth enamel during tooth development, causing discoloration. For this reason, is it avoided drug pregnancy (when the first teeth are forming) and in children under 14 years of age (when the secondary teeth are forming). These drugs have no effect on teeth that have already formed and have erupted through the gums.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 127

questions.stem: Which antibacterial drugs are most likely to cause severe skin reactions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Erythromycins and aminoglycosides

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sulfonamides and trimethoprim

choices.label: C
choices.text: Penicillins and cephalosporins

choices.label: D
choices.text: Macrolides and tetracyclines

questions.rationale: Although any drug can cause an allergic skin rash, the sulfonamides and trimethoprim are more likely to cause any type of skin problem. These drugs work by interfering with bacterial cell metabolism. They also disrupt skin cell metabolism to some degree, causing skin rashes, dry skin, and peeling skin (as a side effect).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: pp. 130-131

questions.stem: Which side effect of fluoroquinolones is more likely to occur among older adults?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fever

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hair loss

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tendon rupture

choices.label: D
choices.text: Low blood pressure

questions.rationale: Tendon rupture while taking or shortly after stopping the fluroquinolones is a potential adverse reaction with this drug. It is more likely to occur among older adults than any other age-group. The drug does concentrate in muscle, cartilage, and bone. It is not known whether having the drug concentrate in these tissues affects their strength.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 133

questions.stem: Which condition is most likely to lead to antibacterial drug resistance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Having a parent with antibacterial drug resistance

choices.label: B
choices.text: Receiving prolonged antibacterial drug therapy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Taking an antibacterial drug for the very first time

choices.label: D
choices.text: Being allergic to a specific antibacterial drug

questions.rationale: The most common reason why bacteria become resistant to an antibacterial drug is that the organisms were previously exposed to the drug and had a chance to develop specific biologic changes within each bacterium that protected the organism against the action(s) of the drug. Resistance is not inherited by a person and the person is NOT resistant to the drug, the organism is.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 134 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which question would you be sure to ask a patient after giving any antibacterial drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Are you allergic to any foods or medications?”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Have you noticed any changes in your ability to remember things?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Do you know how to check your blood pressure and heart rate?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “How many bowel movements do you have each day and what are they like?”

questions.rationale: Pseudomembranous colitis is a complication of antibacterial therapy that causes severe inflammation in areas of the colon (large intestine). The cause of the problem is the overgrowth of an intestinal organism called Clostridium difficile (also known as “C. difficile”). This organism is not killed by most antibacterial drugs, and it can take over the patient’s intestinal tract when normal flora are killed off. The lining of the colon becomes raw and bleeds. Other symptoms include watery diarrhea, the constant feeling of the need to move the bowels, abdominal cramps, low-grade fever, and bloody stools.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 117

questions.stem: Why is it important to avoid killing off normal flora with antibacterial drugs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Normal flora can help provide protection against the development of pathogenic infections.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Normal flora result in opportunistic infections while other bacteria result in pathogenic infections.

choices.label: C
choices.text: When normal flora are not present, the immune system is suppressed, increasing the risk for infection.

choices.label: D
choices.text: When normal flora are not present, the immune system is overactive, increasing the risk for autoimmune diseases.

questions.rationale: Normal flora are the nonpathogenic bacteria that are always present on skin, mucous membranes, and in the digestive tract. They provide protection by “crowding out” pathogenic organisms and preventing them from entering the body.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding (Comprehension) REF: pp. 114-115

questions.stem: How are bactericidal drugs different from bacteriostatic drugs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bacteriostatic drugs are more likely to cause an allergic response than bactericidal drugs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bacteriostatic drugs work only on bacteria, whereas bactericidal drugs are effective against other types of organisms.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bactericidal drug actions result in killing the bacteria, whereas bacteriostatic drugs only slow bacterial growth.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bactericidal drugs require assistance from the patient’s immune system to be effective, whereas bacteriostatic drugs are effective even when function is poor.

questions.rationale: Bactericidal drugs directly kill bacteria; bacteriostatic drugs only stop bacteria from reproducing while the immune system kills the bacteria.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 115

questions.stem: The patient with an elevated temperature is prescribed antibacterial therapy to be taken at home. The prescribed drug is amoxicillin (Amoxil) 250 mg three times a day. What would you teach the patient about dosing intervals for this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Take the drug three times a day with meals.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Take the drug every 8 hours throughout the day.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Take the drug when you first get up, at lunch time, and with your evening meal.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Take the drug every 6 hours while you are awake.”

questions.rationale: For all antibacterial therapy, it is important to keep the blood level high enough to affect the bacteria causing the infection. Therefore teach the patient to take the drug evenly throughout a 24-hour day. If the drug is to be taken three times daily, teach the patient to take it every 8 hours.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 118

questions.stem: A patient has a bacterial infection, but the causative organism is not known. Which type of antibacterial drug will most likely be prescribed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Narrow-spectrum

choices.label: B
choices.text: Limited-spectrum

choices.label: C
choices.text: Extended-spectrum

choices.label: D
choices.text: Broad-spectrum

questions.rationale: A broad-spectrum antibacterial drug is effective against a wide range of bacteria, both gram- positive and gram-negative. Narrow-spectrum drugs are effective against a few bacteria, while extended-spectrum drugs are effective against several types of bacteria.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 116

questions.stem: Which statement made by a patient prescribed to take antibiotic therapy for a wound infection indicates a correct understanding of the therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “If my temperature is normal for 3 days in a row, the infection is gone and I can stop taking the drug.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “If my temperature goes above 100° F for 2 days, I should double the dose of the drug.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Even if I feel completely well, I should take the drug exactly as prescribed until it is gone.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I should notify my prescriber to change the medication if I develop diarrhea while taking this drug.”

questions.rationale: Antibiotic therapy is most effective when the patient takes the prescribed drug for the entire course and not just when symptoms are present. Most antibiotic therapy results in some degree of diarrhea. Although additional drugs may be needed to control this side effect, it is usually not necessary to stop the drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 116 | p. 118

questions.stem: A patient with a respiratory bacterial infection asks why a sputum sample is being collected before starting antibacterial drug therapy. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Drug therapy for lung infections works better when less sputum is present.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “A sputum sample will help us determine what bacteria is causing your infection.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “The test will determine whether you already have any damage in your lung tissues.

choices.label: D
choices.text: “The dosage of your antibacterial drug is determined by the organism that is causing your infection.”

questions.rationale: Identifying the type of bacteria causing an infection is important for selection of the appropriate drug to treat the infection. The most common way of identifying bacteria is culture and sensitivity. When a drug is effective against the bacteria, the bacteria do not grow. The laboratory test identifying the specific organism does not indicate drug dosage.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 116

questions.stem: A patient receiving antibiotics for 3 days reports a skin rash over the chest, back, and arms. What is your first action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the patient whether he or she has ever developed a rash while taking another drug.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reassure the patient that many people have this expected reaction to antibiotic therapy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the patient whether the rash itches, burns, or causes other types of discomfort.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Document the report as the only action.

questions.rationale: A rash is an indication that the patient is allergic to the drug; however, at this time it is not an emergency. First explore the patient’s response further and hold the dose, then notify the prescriber of this problem.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 118

questions.stem: A patient who has been on antibiotic therapy for 3 weeks has a cottage cheese–like coating on the teeth, gums, and roof of the mouth. What your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Take a specimen and send it to the laboratory for culture.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hold the dose and notify the prescriber immediately.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Document this expected finding as the only response.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assist the patient to perform frequent mouth care

questions.rationale: Prolonged antibacterial therapy can cause an oral yeast infection (thrush) by killing off the normal flora of the mouth. This infection causes a white, cottage cheese–like coating on the gums, roof of the mouth, or insides of the cheeks, which is not an allergic reaction nor is it a reason to stop the drug therapy. Teach patient to use good oral hygiene performing frequent toothbrushing and using mouthwash.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 117

questions.stem: What is the most important action needed when a patient is first started on an intravenous (IV) antibacterial drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check the IV site every 4 hours for redness around the site and the presence of cordlike veins.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check the drip rate every hour to ensure that the proper blood drug level is maintained.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assess the patient’s pulse, blood pressure, and respiratory effect every 15 minutes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assess the patient’s white blood cell count daily to determine drug effectiveness.

questions.rationale: Anaphylaxis is a severe drug allergy that can quickly lead to death. Although drug-induced anaphylaxis can occur at any time, it is most likely to occur early during IV administration of the drug. Signs and symptoms are hives at the IV site, low blood pressure, rapid irregular pulse, swelling of the lips or lower face, and the patient feeling a “lump in the throat.” When the patient is receiving the first dose of an IV antibiotic, checking the patient at least every 15 minutes for symptoms of anaphylaxis is critical.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 117

questions.stem: A patient experiences facial angioedema after receiving a dose of a new antibacterial drug. Which assessment should be performed first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Airway adequacy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Peripheral lung sounds

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rate and depth of respirations

choices.label: D
choices.text: Symmetry of respiratory movement

questions.rationale: Angioedema of the face includes the mouth and throat and can quickly lead to laryngeal edema. Edema in either place can obstruct the airway. Although assessing respirations is important, assess the airway first to determine patency.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 117

questions.stem: A patient with a bacterial infection has been placed on antibacterial therapy. Which assessment finding in the patient indicates that the therapy is effective?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Red blood cell count is 4,500,000 cells/mm3.

choices.label: B
choices.text: White blood cell (WBC) count is 8000 cells/mm3.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wound drainage is thick and yellow.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Temperature is 102.4° F.

questions.rationale: Signs and symptoms of a resolving infection include reduced or absent fever; no chills; wound drainage that is no longer thick, foul-smelling, brown, green, or yellow; wound edges that are not red and raw-looking; and a WBC count that is in the normal range (5000 to 10,000 mm3).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 117

questions.stem: A patient is having an anaphylactic reaction to an intravenous (IV) antibacterial drug. Which action should you perform first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Discontinue the IV therapy immediately and place the patient in shock position.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hold the next dose and notify the prescriber immediately.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Discontinue the IV therapy and restart it at a different site.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Discontinue the drug and maintain the IV access.

questions.rationale: When a patient is having an anaphylactic reaction to an IV drug, the first priority is to prevent any more drug from entering the patient. Stop the drug from infusing but keep the IV access open. If the drug is infusing high into the IV tubing, change the tubing after stopping the drug, and do not let any drug left in the tubing run into the patient. Starting a new IV line may be difficult or impossible during the hypotension that occurs during anaphylaxis.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 117

questions.stem: Which antibacterial drug kills bacteria by binding to cell wall proteins and preventing them from being incorporated into cells walls?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Levofloxacin (Levaquin)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Minocycline (Vectrin)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vancomycin (Vancocin)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Gentamicin

questions.rationale: Of the drugs on this list, only vancomycin is a cell wall synthesis inhibitor. Minocyline and gentamicin are protein synthesis inhibitors. Levofloxacin is a DNA synthesis inhibitor.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 118

questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed cephalexin (Keflex) reports having a severe allergic reaction to penicillin in the past. What is your best first response or action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reassure the patient that Keflex is not penicillin.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Place an allergy alert band on the patient’s wrist.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Notify the prescriber immediately before the first Keflex dose.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Highlight this important information in the patient’s medical record.

questions.rationale: Cephalexin is a cephalosporin and has a chemical structure that is very similar to the structure of penicillin. Often a person who is allergic to penicillin is also allergic to cephalosporins. Even if the prescriber wishes to go ahead with cephalosporin therapy, he or she may first prescribe premedication to reduce the risk for an allergic response.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 123

questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed amoxicillin (Amoxil) 250 mg orally every 8 hours asks if a higher dose of the drug just once a day can be taken instead. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Taking this drug every 8 hours helps keep the blood level of the drug high enough to affect the bacteria.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Giving the drug at regular intervals over a 24-hour period helps prevent side effects.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Let me contact your prescriber and ask whether the drug can be given once a day.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “When given once a day, the dose is higher so allergic reactions are more common.”

questions.rationale: For all antibacterial therapy, it is important to keep the blood level high enough to affect the bacteria causing the infection. For this reason it is best for a patient to take the drug  evenly throughout a 24-hour period.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF:  p. 118

questions.stem: A woman who is breastfeeding is prescribed amoxicillin/clavulanic acid (Augmentin) 250 mg every 8 hours to treat a bacterial infection. What should you teach her?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “This drug does not pass into breast milk so continue to breastfeed while you are taking this drug.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “This drug passes into breast milk but has no side effects so it is safe to continue breastfeeding.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “This drug passes into breast milk and may cause your child to develop an allergy to this drug.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “This drug does not pass into breast milk but you might want to avoid breastfeeding while taking it.”

questions.rationale: All of these drugs pass into breast milk and will affect a nursing infant, possibly causing the infant to develop a drug allergy. These drugs are usually prescribed for only 5 to 14 days, and the breastfeeding mother should be urged to reduce infant exposure to the drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 123

questions.stem: Which side effect should you monitor for when a child is prescribed penicillin V potassium 250 mg twice daily?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gastrointestinal upset

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased urine output

choices.label: C
choices.text: Allergic reaction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Constipation

questions.rationale: Most cell wall synthesis inhibitors have fewer side effects than other types of antibacterial drugs. However, the cell wall synthesis inhibitors, especially penicillin, are more likely to cause allergic reactions. If a rash or hives develop while taking an antibacterial drug, remind the parents to stop giving the drug and call the prescriber immediately. Explain that this is a sign of drug allergy. Tell the parents to call 911 immediately if the child has trouble breathing or has the feeling of a “lump in the throat” because these are signs of a more serious allergic reaction.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 122

questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed oral cephalexin (Keflex) also takes aluminum hydroxide (Mylanta) 1 hour after each meal. When should you give this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Give the two drugs at the same time to prevent GI upset.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Give the cephalexin 1 hour before the aluminum hydroxide.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give the aluminum hydroxide 1 hour before the cephalexin.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Give the aluminum hydroxide 4 hours after the cephalexin.

questions.rationale: Cephalexin is a cephalosporin. These drugs are poorly absorbed with iron supplements or antacids. If a patient is receiving an antacid, the cephalosporin should be given 1 hour before or 4 hours after the antacid or iron supplement.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 123

questions.stem: A patient is to receive penicillin G benzathine (Bicillin LA) 2,400,000 units intramuscularly. The drug on hand is penicillin G benzathine 600,000 units/mL. How many milliliters should your nurse prepare for the correct dose?
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 121

questions.stem: A patient is to receive ticarcillin (Ticar) 3 g by intravenous piggyback. The solution you have is ticarcillin 200 mg/mL. How many milliliters of this solution will you add to the piggyback bag to make a dose of 3 g?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 3 mL

choices.label: B
choices.text: 5 mL

choices.label: C
choices.text: 10 mL

choices.label: D
choices.text: 15 mL

questions.rationale: Want 3 g (3000 mg) in X mL/Have 200 mg in 1 mL. 3000/200 = 15X = 15 mL.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 122

questions.stem: A patient prescribed vancomycin (Vancocin) has developed redness on the face, neck, chest, back, and arms. The family asks the nurse if the drug should be stopped because of this response. What is your best answer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Yes, these problems indicate an allergic reaction.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Yes, these side effects eventually lead to difficulty breathing.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “No, these uncomfortable problems are an expected drug side effect.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “No, the problems are caused by the presence of the infection and are not related to the drug.”

questions.rationale: Vancomycin is a powerful antibacterial drug that has many side effects, including “red man syndrome.” This problem is caused by a histamine release that dilates blood vessels, giving a red appearance to the face, neck, chest, back, and arms. Sometimes this reaction can be reduced by slowing the infusion rate; however, it is not an indication to stop the drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 122

questions.stem: A patient is to receive linezolid (Zyvox) 300 mg orally. You have on hand linezolid tablets of 600 mg/tablet. How many tablets will you give for a 300 mg dose?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 3 tablets

choices.label: B
choices.text: 2 tablets

choices.label: C
choices.text: 1 tablet

choices.label: D
choices.text: 0.5 tablet

questions.rationale: Want 300 mg in X tablets/Have 600 mg in 1 tablet. 300/600 = 0.5 tablet.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 126

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed intravenous ertapenem (Invanz). Which question is most important to ask before giving the first dose of this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Do you have a hearing problem or any trouble with your ears?”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Do you take medications for seizures?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Are you allergic to sulfa drugs?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Have you ever had asthma?”

questions.rationale: Ertapenem belongs to the carbapenem antibacterial class. Drugs in this class can cause seizures in susceptible people. If a patient has a seizure disorder, the drug should be used with caution. The prescriber is notified of this problem because the dosage of anticonvulsive drug or drugs may need to be adjusted before the ertapenem is administered.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 123

questions.stem: An older patient is prescribed azithromycin (Zithromax) 500 mg on the first day and 250 mg for days 2 through 5. For which drug also prescribed for this patient would you notify the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Digoxin (Lanoxin)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atorvastatin (Lipitor)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Atenolol (Tenormin)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Furosemide (Lasix)

questions.rationale: If a patient is prescribed a macrolide, check to see whether he or she is also taking digoxin, warfarin, pimozide, astemizole, terfenadine, or ergotamine. If so, notify the prescriber immediately because macrolides change the metabolism of these drugs, which can cause adverse effects.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 127

questions.stem: A patient is receiving intravenous gentamicin. Which change in condition do you report to the prescriber immediately?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Temperature increases from 38° C to 39° C.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blood pressure decreases from 132/80 to 118/66.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Total 24-hour urine output decreases from 2100 to 1100 mL.

choices.label: D
choices.text: White blood cell (WBC) count decreases from 11,000/mm3 to 8500/mm3.

questions.rationale: Gentamicin is an aminoglycoside antibacterial drug. These drugs are toxic to the kidneys (nephrotoxic). A decrease of urine output by 1000 mL in a 24-hour time period is cause for concern about kidney function. The drug may need to be stopped or the dosage decreased.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 128

questions.stem: A patient taking erythromycin reports experiencing nausea and upset stomach. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hold the drug and notify the prescriber immediately.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensure that the drug is given with or after food.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask if the patient has any drug allergies.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Document the report as the only action.

questions.rationale: Side effects common to erythromycin include nausea, vomiting, and GI  upset. This is not an indication of allergic reaction. Giving the drug with or after food will help prevent these side effects.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 126

questions.stem: After starting to take clindamycin (Cleocin) 150 mg every 8 hours, which laboratory results are important for you to monitor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Potassium

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sodium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Creatinine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Red blood cells

questions.rationale: Check the patient’s current laboratory work, especially BUN and serum creatinine levels, because clindamycin, linezolid, and streptogramin are toxic to the kidneys. If these values are higher than normal before starting the drug, the risk for kidney damage is greater.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 127

questions.stem: An older adult patient is prescribed amikacin (Amikin). Which assessment technique is most important to perform daily?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Asking about numbness in fingers and toes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Measuring calf circumference of both legs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Checking the mouth for open sores

choices.label: D
choices.text: Asking about ringing in the ears

questions.rationale: Amikacin can be toxic to the ears (ototoxic), especially among older adults. One of the first signs of ototoxicity is ringing in the ears (tinnitus) which often occurs before actual hearing loss. The nurse must check the patient daily for tinnitus and changes in hearing.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 123

questions.stem: For which patient with a bacterial infection do you question a prescription for tetracycline (Tetracon)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 35-year-old patient with AIDS

choices.label: B
choices.text: 16-year-old patient with severe acne

choices.label: C
choices.text: 65-year-old patient with hypertension

choices.label: D
choices.text: 25-year-old patient taking oral contraceptives

questions.rationale: The tetracyclines are broad-spectrum drugs that are bacteriostatic against most of the organisms that are sensitive to penicillins. Because they are only bacteriostatic, tetracyclines should be given only to patients with healthy immune systems. The person with AIDS has a severely compromised immune system that cannot fight the infection and needs a drug that is bactericidal rather than bacteriostatic.
