Although these drugs can interfere with tooth development and are not given to children, the 16-year-old patient’s teeth should be fully developed and therefore not susceptible to that effect of the drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 125

questions.stem: An older adult patient has been prescribed oral erythromycin tablets. Which precaution is most important to teach?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Take this drug with food or right after eating to reduce intestinal side effects.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Avoid driving or operating dangerous equipment while taking this drug.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Take your pulse daily and notify your prescriber if it becomes irregular.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Wear a hat and sunscreen when outdoors.”

questions.rationale: All macrolide antibiotics, including erythromycin, have many side effects and interfere with other drugs. They can all cause GI side effects and increase sun sensitivity. The most dangerous side effect among older adults is the development of cardiac rhythm problems. The patient must be instructed to check his or her pulse at least once daily and report any new or worsening irregularities immediately.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 134

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed minocycline (Dynacin) 750 mg orally. What should you do before giving the first dose?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check that the patient is not allergic to penicillin or sulfa drugs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Use a new IV administration set to administer the drug.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hold the dose and contact the prescriber.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Give the drug with milk or food.

questions.rationale: The dosage of minocycline is smaller than most other tetracyclines. The average dose for an adult is 100 mg. It is likely that the prescriber intended to write 75 mg rather than 750 mg, which would be nearly 10 times the usual dosage.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 131

questions.stem: Why should tetracycline drugs be avoided during pregnancy and lactation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tetracycline crosses the placenta and causes brain hemorrhage in the fetus.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The fetal and newborn liver cannot metabolize the drug and anemia results.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The fetus and newborn are more likely to have allergic reactions to tetracycline.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The drug interferes with tooth enamel development causing permanently stained teeth.

questions.rationale: Tetracyclines used during tooth development in the last half of pregnancy and in infancy can cause a permanent yellow-gray discoloration of the teeth and make the tooth enamel thinner. So, these drugs should not be used during pregnancy or when breastfeeding except for anthrax exposure or for another serious infection that is not likely to respond to other antibacterial drugs.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 127

questions.stem: The patient who is receiving intravenous (IV) penicillin is prescribed to also receive intravenous doxycycline (Doxy). Which action is most important for nurse to implement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use IV tubing for doxycycline that has never come into contact with penicillin.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Use a glass container for the tetracycline and a plastic container for the penicillin.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Infuse the doxycycline only into a central line and the penicillin into a peripheral line.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administer doxycycline with sterile saline and administer the penicillin with sterile water.

questions.rationale: Doxycycline is a tetracycline drug. Tetracyclines interfere with the action of penicillin. It is important not to give these two drug types at the same time. They should not be mixed together in the same IV bag or run through the same IV tubing.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 127

questions.stem: An older adult patient is prescribed linezolid (Zyvox) 500 mg oral suspension. The drug on hand is 100 mg/5 mL. How many milliliters should you prepare for the correct dose?
questions.rationale: Want 500 mg in X mL/Have 100 mg in 5 mL. 500/100 = 5X  5 mL = 25 mL.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 126

questions.stem: Which laboratory blood test result for a patient taking clarithromycin (Biaxin) and warfarin (Coumadin) should you report immediately to the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Potassium 3.6 mEq/L

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sodium 134 mEq/L

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) 21 mg/dL

choices.label: D
choices.text: International normalized ratio (INR) 4.6

questions.rationale: Clarithromycin is a macrolide and can interfere with metabolism of many drugs. In particular, they increase the effects of warfarin. This patient’s INR of 4.6 indicates a dangerously long clotting time and a greatly increased risk for bleeding. Although some of the other values are slightly out of the normal range, only the INR requires immediate action.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 127

questions.stem: Which statement made by a patient prescribed to take azithromycin (Zithromax) indicates that additional teaching is needed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “To avoid nausea, I will take my medicine along with food.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I will use another form of birth control along with my oral contraceptives.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Since I only have to take this drug once a day, it will be easy to remember.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “To help get rid of the infection, I will spend 30 minutes each day out in the sun.”

questions.rationale: Although fresh air and sunshine are important, azithromycin is a macrolide and increases skin sensitivity to the sun (photosensitivity), greatly increasing the risk for sunburn, even among patients with dark skin. You must teach patients to avoid direct sunlight and tanning beds while they are taking this drug and for at least 1 week after stopping the drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 128

questions.stem: Why must sulfonamide drugs be avoided during the last trimester of pregnancy and lactation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The drug crosses the placenta and causes brain hemorrhage in the fetus.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The fetal and newborn liver cannot metabolize the drug and jaundice results.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The fetus and newborn are more likely to have allergic reactions to sulfonamides.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The drug interferes with tooth enamel development causing permanently stained teeth.

questions.rationale: Sulfonamides can cause severe jaundice in the infant and should be avoided during the last 2 months of pregnancy to reduce the chances that the baby will be born while the mother is still taking the drug. For the same reason, the breastfeeding mother should use alternate methods of infant feeding during the time she is taking the drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 132

questions.stem: A patient is taking trimethoprim (Primsol) 200 mg orally once a day. For which adverse effect will you monitor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: GI bleeding

choices.label: B
choices.text: Kidney failure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Anaphylaxis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Angioedema

questions.rationale: Trimethoprim is a sulfonamide. The sulfonamides are a type of chemical that can easily turn into crystals. Crystals that form and clump in the kidneys can cause kidney failure or kidney stones.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 130

questions.stem: Which laboratory blood test result for a patient taking trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole (Septra) should you report immediately to the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Red blood cells (RBCs) 2.2 million/mm3

choices.label: B
choices.text: International normalized ratio (INR) 1.6

choices.label: C
choices.text: White blood cells (WBCs) 6000/mm3

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sodium 134 mEq/L

questions.rationale: Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole is a combination drug composed of two metabolism inhibitors. One of the most serious adverse effects of the metabolism inhibitors is suppression of bone marrow cell division, leading to fewer red blood cells and anemia. This patient’s RBC level is only about half of normal.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: pp. 131-132

questions.stem: Which precaution is most important to teach a patient who has been prescribed sulfisoxizole (Gantrisin)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evenly space this drug throughout the 24-hour day.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Take the drug 1 hour before or 2 hours after a meal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Drink at least 3 L of liquids throughout the day.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stop the drug immediately if diarrhea develops.

questions.rationale: Sulfisoxizole is a sulfonamide, which is a type of chemical that can easily turn into crystals. Crystals that form and clump in the kidneys can cause kidney failure or kidney stones. Drinking at least 3 L of fluids daily while taking this drug can prevent crystallization in the kidneys.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: pp. 131-132

questions.stem: A patient is to receive trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole (Septra) intravenously. The drug is mixed in 250 mL of D5W and is to be administered over 90 minutes. With a drop factor of 15 gtt/mL, how many drops per minute is the correct infusion rate?
questions.rationale: At a rate of 250 mL in 90 minutes, the drug must infuse at 2.7 mL/min (250 mL/90 minutes = 2.7 mL/min). With 15 gtt equaling 1 mL, 2.7 mL  15 = 40.5 gtt/min, rounded down to 40 gtt/min.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 117

questions.stem: A patient taking ciprofloxacin (Cipro) reports pain and burning on urination. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Notify the prescriber that the patient’s urinary tract infection is not responding to the drug.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Remind the patient that the pain is related to the body eliminating the infectious bacteria.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Instruct the patient to drink a full glass of water with each drug dose and increase fluids.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient whether blood or pus is also present in the urine.

questions.rationale: Ciprofloxacin is a fluoroquinolone drug that concentrates in the urine, making the urine irritating to surrounding tissues. The patient may experience pain or burning of the urethra and nearby tissues during urination. An incontinent patient may have skin irritation over the entire perineal area. To avoid this, teach the patient to drink a full glass of water with each dose and to drink more fluids throughout the day.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 133

questions.stem: A 75-year-old patient taking levofloxacin (Levaquin) reports all of the following new problems. For which problem do you advise the patient to stop taking the drug immediately and notify the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Having to get up at night to urinate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Swelling and pain in the right wrist

choices.label: C
choices.text: Feeling the heart pound after drinking coffee

choices.label: D
choices.text: Feeling light-headed when changing positions rapidly

questions.rationale: A rare adverse effect of fluoroquinolones is the rupture of a tendon, most often in the shoulder, hand, wrist, or heel (Achilles tendon). This complication is most likely to happen in an older patient and in those who also take corticosteroids.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 133

questions.stem: Why must fluoroquinolone drugs be avoided during pregnancy, lactation, and childhood?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The drug damages muscles, tendons, and bones in the fetus and growing children.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The fetal and newborn liver cannot metabolize the drug, leading to anemia and jaundice.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The fetus and newborn are more likely to have allergic reactions to fluoroquinolones.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The drug interferes with tooth enamel development causing permanently stained teeth.

questions.rationale: The fluoroquinolones have a moderate likelihood of increasing the risk for bone, joint, and tendon defects. These drugs should not be used during pregnancy, lactation, or children younger than 18 years of age unless the infection is life threatening and not sensitive to other drugs.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 133

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed an oral fluoroquinolone drug. Which precaution should you follow when administering this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Give this drug with food.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Give a full glass of water to drink with this drug.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give the drug before meals.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Give the drug with a glass of milk.

questions.rationale: Give patients a full glass of water to drink with oral fluoroquinolone capsules or tablets and urge them to drink more fluids throughout the day. This action prevents forming a concentrated amount of drug in the urine that can irritate the urethra and perineum.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 133 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: A patient is being discharged home on antibacterial drug therapy. What instructions should you teach the patient about allergic reactions? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Notify your prescriber immediately if you vomit.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Stop taking the drug if you develop hives or a rash.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “If you notice diarrhea be sure to stop taking the drug.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Continue taking the drug even if you feel well because it is fighting the infection.”

choices.label: E
choices.text: “Call 911 if you experience difficulty with breathing.”

choices.label: F
choices.text: “Avoid drinking caffeinated beverages with the drug.”

questions.rationale: GI upset, vomiting, and diarrhea are fairly common side effects of antibacterial drugs and are not signs of allergic reactions. When a patient experiences an allergic reaction, he or she should be taught to stop taking the drug if rash or hives develop and to call the prescriber immediately. A patient should also be taught to call 911 immediately for difficulty breathing or a feeling of a lump in the throat because these are signs of a serious allergic reaction.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 118

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed azithromycin (Zithromax) 500 mg on the first day and 250 mg for days 2 through 5. Which side effects will you watch for after the patient starts taking this drug? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased appetite

choices.label: B
choices.text: Abdominal pain

choices.label: C
choices.text: Constipation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nausea

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sun sensitivity

choices.label: F
choices.text: Weight gain

questions.rationale: Azithromycin is a macrolide antibacterial drug. Macrolides have side effects that occur more in the GI tract. They include nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, loss of appetite, and changes in taste sensation. These drugs greatly increase sun sensitivity (photosensitivity), making serious sunburns possible.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 126

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed ciprofloxacin (Cipro) 500 mg every 8 hours. Which are common side effects of this drug? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rash

choices.label: B
choices.text: Flatulence

choices.label: C
choices.text: Muscle pain

choices.label: D
choices.text: Photosensitivity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Joint pain

choices.label: F
choices.text: Headache

questions.rationale: Ciprofloxacin is a fluoroquinolone drug. Common side effects of these drugs include rash, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain, and muscle and joint pain.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 133 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.stem: What does the term “host” mean?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The person infected by a virus.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The type of virus known as a retrovirus.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The type of virus known as a common virus.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The type of infection that occurs only in immunosuppressed people.

questions.rationale: A host is the person infected by a virus whose cells allow viral replication.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 137

questions.stem: Why do most people become ill with a specific viral infection only once?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Once a virus is killed by the body, it is gone from the earth forever.

choices.label: B
choices.text: During a viral infection, the body changes the virus to be less virulent.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antiviral drugs change the viral DNA so it cannot infect the same person again.

choices.label: D
choices.text: During a specific viral infection, the person’s immune system improves protection against that virus.

questions.rationale: Viruses are foreign invaders. When a person is infected with them to the extent that they become sick, the immune system learns to make antibodies against them. Then, on another exposure to the same virus, the antibodies take defensive steps to remove or eliminate the viruses before the person becomes sick again.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 147

questions.stem: Which statement about antiviral drugs is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are all most effective at killing viruses.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are all effective against any type of infection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They all work by slowing the replication of viruses.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are all only effective in people with strong immune systems.

questions.rationale: Drugs are not able to “kill” a virus in the human body. Antiviral drugs are only “virustatic” in that they can only suppress viral replication. Only a person’s immune system can kill, inactivate, or eliminate viruses.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 147

questions.stem: Which antiviral drug is only given by oral inhalation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cyclovir (Zovirax)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Zanamivir (Relenza)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Oseltamivir (Tamiflu)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Amantadine (Symmetrel)

questions.rationale: Acyclovir is given intravenously or as an orally swallowed drug. Oseltamivir and amantadine are swallowed orally.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 140

questions.stem: Which specific virus causes mononucleosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cytomegalovirus

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hantavirus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Epstein-Barr virus

choices.label: D
choices.text: West Nile virus

questions.rationale: Hantavirus causes hantavirus pulmonary syndrome. Epstein-Barr virus causes chronic fatigue syndrome and some types of lymphomas, not mononucleosis. West Nile virus causes an infection with encephalitis and/or meningitis problems, not mononucleosis.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 141

questions.stem: Which antiviral drug is most likely to have more severe central nervous system side effects, including worsening of glaucoma?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acyclovir

choices.label: B
choices.text: Amantadine

choices.label: C
choices.text: Valacyclovir

choices.label: D
choices.text: Zanamavir

questions.rationale: Amantadine crosses the blood-brain barrier and concentrates in the brain tissues. None of the other drugs concentrate in brain tissues.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 143

questions.stem: Which antiviral drug is highly likely to cause birth defects when taken by a pregnant woman?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acyclovir

choices.label: B
choices.text: Zanamivir

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rimantadine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ribavirin

questions.rationale: The likelihood for acyclovir to increase the risk for birth defects or fetal harm is low. Zanamivir is orally inhaled and poorly absorbed, not a risk for birth defects. Rimantadine has a moderate likelihood of increasing the risk for birth defects. Ribavirin is a known teratogen and has a very high likelihood of increasing the risk for birth defects and fetal harm.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 144

questions.stem: What is the major difference between retroviruses and common viruses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Retroviruses cannot be killed by antiviral therapy, but common viruses can.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A retrovirus usually can cause disease over and over again in the same person.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A retrovirus carries the enzyme reverse transcriptase, and common viruses do not.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Retroviruses will respond to antibacterial drugs as well as to all the antiviral drugs.

questions.rationale: Retroviruses carry a series of enzymes with them that allow them to incorporate into human DNA and use the host’s DNA to help replication. This gives them a very high efficiency of infection. Common viruses, although some are very virulent, do not have those enzymes and their efficiency of infection is lower.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 146

questions.stem: Which class of antiretroviral drugs works by preventing new viral particles from leaving the infected cell?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Entry inhibitors

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fusion inhibitors

choices.label: C
choices.text: Protease inhibitors

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reverse-transcriptase inhibitors

questions.rationale: In order for new viral particles to leave the infected cell to go on and infect new cells, they must use “chemical scissors” to cut up their genetic material in the right places. Protease inhibitors suppress the activity of this enzyme so that viral particles are unable to leave the infected cells even though the viruses have reproduced.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 151

questions.stem: Which problem is a sign or symptom of an allergic or anaphylactic response to an antiviral drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nausea

choices.label: B
choices.text: Vivid dreams

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intolerance to fatty foods

choices.label: D
choices.text: Swelling of the face or throat

questions.rationale: Allergic and anaphylactic symptoms include swelling of the face or throat, hives, itching, redness, low blood pressure, feeling a lump in the throat, an irregular heartbeat, a sense that something bad is happening, and light-headedness.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 139

questions.stem: Which drug can only be given by subcutaneous injection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atazanavir (Reytaz)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Enfuvirtide (Fuzeon)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Maraviroc (Selzentry)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Darunavir (Prezista)

questions.rationale: Atazanivir, maraviroc, and darunavir are all administered as oral tablets or capsules. Only enfuvirtide must be administered subcutaneously.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 153

questions.stem: Which drug does not work on all strains of the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) and therefore the patient must be tested before using the drug to make sure it will work?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atazanavir (Reytaz)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Enfuvirtide (Fuzeon)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Maraviroc (Selzentry)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Raltegravis (Issentra)

questions.rationale: Maraviroc prevents cellular infection by blocking the CCR5 receptor on CD4+ T cells. Because this drug is not effective against all HIV subtypes, the patient must first be tested to ensure that his or her HIV infection is likely to respond to this therapy. When the HIV subtype does not use the CCR5 receptor, the drug is not prescribed for that patient.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Remembering   REF:  p. 154

questions.stem: Which drug increases the risk for muscle weakness in older patients who are also taking a “statin” type of lipid-lowering drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atazanavir (Reytaz)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Enfuvirtide (Fuzeon)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Maraviroc (Selzentry)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Raltegravis (Issentra)

questions.rationale: Although atazanavir and maraviroc can cause liver side effects, they do not potentiate the risk for muscle weakness when taken along with a statin. Enfuvirtide does not induce liver problems nor does it increase muscle weakness.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Remembering   REF:  p. 153

questions.stem: What is the purpose of antiviral drug therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To make the patient immune to infection by viruses.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To reduce infection by killing the virus causing disease.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To reduce illness duration by controlling viral reproduction.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To prevent opportunistic infections in immunosuppressed patients.

questions.rationale: Antiviral drugs are only virustatic and reduce the number of viruses by preventing them from reproducing and growing. They are not virucidal and cannot kill the virus. By keeping the number of viruses low, antiviral drugs allow the body’s natural defenses to destroy, eliminate, or inactivate them.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 139

questions.stem: Which statement about human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) and acquired immune deficiency syndrome (AIDS) is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Everyone with AIDS has HIV infection but not everyone with HIV infection has AIDS.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Everyone with HIV infection eventually dies of it because the disease cannot be cured.

choices.label: C
choices.text: With successful antiretroviral therapy, HIV-infected patients become HIV-antibody negative.

choices.label: D
choices.text: When HIV disease becomes AIDS, the infection is in the blood and treatment must be given intravenously.

questions.rationale: People infected with HIV develop antibodies to the virus and become HIV positive. Not everyone who is HIV positive will progress with the disease and develop AIDS, the most severe form of the disease.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 146

questions.stem: Which antiretroviral drug is now prescribed for preexposure prophylaxis to prevent human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acyclovir

choices.label: B
choices.text: Edurant

choices.label: C
choices.text: Crixivan

choices.label: D
choices.text: Truvada

questions.rationale: Truvada, which is a combination of emtricitabine and tenofovir, has been found effective in preventing HIV infection among HIV-negative individuals who have sex with people who are HIV positive.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 155 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: How are viral infections different from bacterial infections?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bacterial infections can be spread from one person to another, whereas viral infections cannot be spread directly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bacterial infections can be cured by treatment with some anti-infective drugs, whereas viral infections are not cured by anti-infective therapy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Viruses only cause disease in a person who is immunocompromised, whereas bacteria can cause disease even among immunocompetent people.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Viruses are the less mature form of a bacterium, so there is essentially no difference between viral infections and bacterial infections.

questions.rationale: Viral infections are not “cured” but are self-limiting, meaning that in a person with a healthy immune system, the illness only lasts for a limited time. If the person’s immune system is working properly, the body fights off the infection by itself. If the person’s immune system is weak or if the body has other health problems, the person may die of the effects of the disease.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 138

questions.stem: What is the most important precaution to teach a patient who is prescribed any antiviral drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Take the drug exactly as prescribed and for as long as prescribed.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Never take any antibacterial drugs while you are on antiviral therapy.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Drink at least 3 L of water daily for the entire time you are taking this drug.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Avoid unnecessary exposure to this drug by stopping it when you have no symptoms for 24 hours.”

questions.rationale: For effective antiviral therapy the patient must take the drug long enough to ensure suppression of viral reproduction. If the patient stops taking the drug as soon as he or she feels better, symptoms of infection may recur and resistant viruses may develop.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 139

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed intravenous (IV) acyclovir (Zovirax). Which question is most important to ask before giving the first dose of this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Do you have a hearing problem or any trouble with your ears?”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Do you take medications for seizures?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Are you allergic to sulfa drugs?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Have you ever had asthma?”

questions.rationale: Acyclovir reduces the effectiveness of phenytoin, a drug that is used to prevent seizures. The prescriber may need to adjust the phenytoin dosage while the patient is on antiviral therapy.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: pp. 140-141

questions.stem: What is the most important precaution to teach a patient who is prescribed oral valacyclovir (Valtrex)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Avoid coffee and other caffeinated beverages.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Wear long sleeves and a hat when going outdoors.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Take this drug 1 hour before or 4 hours after eating a full meal.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Drink at least 3 L of water daily for the entire time you are taking this drug.”

questions.rationale: Valacyclovir consists of a type of chemical that can easily turn into crystals. Crystals that form and clump in the kidneys can cause kidney failure or kidney stones. Drinking at least 3 L of fluids daily while taking this drug can prevent crystallization in the kidneys.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 141

questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed amantadine (Symmetrel) has all of the following health problems. For which problem should you contact the prescriber before giving the first dose?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Asthma

choices.label: B
choices.text: Influenza

choices.label: C
choices.text: Glaucoma

choices.label: D
choices.text: Diabetes mellitus

questions.rationale: Amantadine has central nervous system side effects and can worsen glaucoma. Although the drug may still be given to a person who has glaucoma, more frequent checking of intraocular pressure or adjustment in glaucoma drugs may be needed.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 143

questions.stem: A 2-year-old patient with respiratory syncytial virus is prescribed aerosolized ribavirin (Virazole). Which visitor should you ensure is not in the patient’s room during the aerosol treatments?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 16-year-old brother

choices.label: B
choices.text: 81-year-old grandmother

choices.label: C
choices.text: 32-year-old pregnant mother

choices.label: D
choices.text: 36-year-old father who has diabetes

questions.rationale: A major adverse effect of ribavirin is that it is a teratogen, an agent that has a very high likelihood of increasing the risks for birth defects and fetal damage. It should not be given to pregnant or breastfeeding women, and it should not be handled or inhaled by anyone who is pregnant.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 144

questions.stem: A patient who is taking interferon is also prescribed ribavirin (Virazole). Which laboratory blood test result for this patient do you report immediately to the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Red blood cells (RBCs) 2.2 million/mm3

choices.label: B
choices.text: White blood cells (WBCs) 6000/mm3

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sodium 134 mEq/L

choices.label: D
choices.text: International normalized ratio (INR) 1.6

questions.rationale: One of the many serious adverse effects of ribavirin is suppression of bone marrow cell division, leading to fewer RBCs and anemia. This patient’s RBC level is only about half of normal.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 144

questions.stem: Which precaution about zanamivir (Relenza) therapy do you teach a patient who also uses a bronchodilator for asthma control?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Drink at least 3 L of water daily while using this drug.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Use the bronchodilator 15 minutes before taking the zanamivir.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Avoid taking the zanamivir within 2 hours of using the bronchodilator.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Take your pulse daily while on this drug because when taken with a bronchodilator heart rhythm problems are common.”

questions.rationale: Zanamivir is an inhalation drug. When a patient also takes a bronchodilator for asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, or any other airway problem, the manufacturer recommends using the bronchodilator at least 5 minutes before zanamivir to ensure a wider airway and improved inhalation of the antiviral drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 146

questions.stem: For what reason does adefovir dipivoxil have a black box warning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It induces type 2 diabetes mellitus.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It increases the severity of hepatitis B symptoms.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is highly toxic to both the liver and the kidneys.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It has a high likelihood of increasing the risk for birth defects and fetal damage.

questions.rationale: Adefovir dipivoxil, which is used to treat hepatitis B, is highly toxic to the liver and kidneys. Hepatitis B symptoms are only increased when the drug is stopped suddenly, not while it is being used. It does not increase the risk for birth defects and does not cause diabetes mellitus.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 142

questions.stem: Which common side effects should you watch for when a patient is taking adefovir dipivoxil?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Numbness and tingling in extremities

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dizziness and light-headedness

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased confusion

questions.rationale: The most common side effects of adefovir dipivoxil are nausea and abdominal pain.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 142

questions.stem: What specific precaution would you teach the family of a patient prescribed amantadine?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Be sure that the patient performs mouth care four to six times daily.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Remind the patient that monthly follow-up lab tests will be needed.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Contact the prescriber immediately for any worsening of depression or thoughts of suicide.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Ensure that the patient takes in at least 1000 mL more fluids that he or she puts out.”

questions.rationale: Tell the patient and family to report to the prescriber immediately any worsening of depression or thoughts of suicide.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 143

questions.stem: How are retroviruses different from common viruses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Retroviruses respond to antibacterial drugs but common viruses do not.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Retroviruses have a greater efficiency of infection than common viruses.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Common viral infections can be cured while retroviral infections can only be controlled.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Common viruses can infect anyone whereas retroviruses can only cause infection in an immunosuppressed host.

questions.rationale: A retrovirus is a special virus that always uses RNA as its genetic material, and carries with it the enzymes reverse transcriptase, integrase, and protease, which allow a much higher efficiency of cellular infection than common viruses. This means that disease may result even when low levels of retroviruses enter the body.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 146

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed to begin highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART). What is the most important question to ask this patient before beginning therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Do you have any symptoms now of active infection?”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Is there any possibility that you are pregnant?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Are you currently sexually active?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “What other drugs do you take?”

questions.rationale: Before giving an antiretroviral drug, always obtain a list of all other drugs the patient also takes, because antiretroviral drugs interact with many other drugs. Check with the pharmacist for possible interactions and the need to consult the prescriber about dosage or changing the patient’s other drugs.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 148

questions.stem: A patient taking highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART) for a year has the following blood laboratory test values. Which value do you report to the prescriber immediately?

choices.label: A
choices.text: White blood cell count 3500 cells/mm3

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lactate dehydrogenase 990 IU/L

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sodium 132 mEq/L

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hematocrit 32%

questions.rationale: All of these blood values are abnormal; however, only the lactate dehydrogenase level is very high (four times normal). This value indicates liver impairment. Nearly all antiretroviral drugs can cause liver impairment. This value must be reported immediately so that liver function can be explored and any needed changes in drug therapy made before irreversible liver damage occurs.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 148

questions.stem: A patient has human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) disease and is taking a “cocktail” consisting of protease inhibitors, nucleoside analogue reverse-transcriptase inhibitors, and nonnucleoside analogue reverse-transcriptase inhibitors. Which statement made by the patient indicates that more teaching about the drug therapy is needed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Using three drugs at the same time enhances suppression of viral replication.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “There is no problem using these drugs during the last trimester of my pregnancy.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Because this drug combination kills HIV, I do not need to worry about transmitting the virus.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “If the virus becomes resistant to this cocktail, another combination of drugs may be required to reduce my viral load.”

questions.rationale: Protease inhibitors suppress viral replication and release. They do not kill the virus, and currently no therapy for HIV infection kills the virus. The patient needs to understand that even on antiretroviral therapy, the disease can be spread through exchange of body fluids and precautions still must be taken.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 147

questions.stem: Which test results indicate that a patient’s antiretroviral therapy is effective?

choices.label: A
choices.text: White blood cell count 5000 cells/mm3

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blood urea nitrogen level 15 mg/dL

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blood positive for human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) antibodies

choices.label: D
choices.text: CD4+ to CD8+ ratio increased

questions.rationale: HIV selectively infects and causes the destruction of CD4+ cells (T -helper cells). An increase in ratio of this population of cells compared with CD8+ cells in a patient with HIV disease indicates the drug regimen is effective in suppressing viral replication.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF:  p. 148

questions.stem: A human immunodeficiency virus (HIV)-positive patient taking highly active antiretroviral therapy informs you that she is now pregnant. What advice regarding drug therapy do you give this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Stop taking these drugs for your entire pregnancy.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Stop taking these drugs for the first trimester and start again for the second and third trimesters.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Continue to take these drugs throughout your pregnancy exactly as they have been prescribed.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “It will be necessary to double your dosages of these drugs to ensure your unborn baby is adequately protected.”

questions.rationale: Antiretroviral drugs for HAART are recommended to be taken by pregnant women who are known to be HIV positive because the virus can cross the placenta and infect the fetus. These drugs, when taken as prescribed, can reduce the chances of fetal infection from about 30% to about 8%. Dosages are based on the woman’s viral load, not on weight or pregnancy status.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 149

questions.stem: What is the most important question to ask before giving the first dose of darunavir to a patient newly prescribed this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Do you have a hearing problem or any trouble with your ears?”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Do you take medications for seizures?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Are you allergic to sulfa drugs?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Have you ever had asthma?”

questions.rationale: The formulation of darunavir contains sulfa. A patient who is allergic to sulfa drugs is likely to also be allergic to darunavir and have a serious reaction to the drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 152

questions.stem: A child is prescribed emtricitabine (Emtriva) 120 mg orally. The drug on hand is emtricitabine suspension 10 mg/mL. How many milliliters do you prepare for a correct dose?
questions.rationale: Want 120 mg in X mL/Have 10 mg in 1 mL. 120/10 = 12 mL = 120 mg.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 149

questions.stem: A patient who is taking zidovudine (Retrovir) reports diarrhea. Which dietary change should you suggest for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Avoid fatty and fried foods.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Increase your intake of fiber.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Take an antacid 30 minutes before each meal.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Restrict your intake of fluids to 1 L/day.”

questions.rationale: Fatty foods tend to reduce intestinal absorption and make drug-induced diarrhea worse. Although fiber can prevent some types of diarrhea, it is of no benefit for diarrhea associated with antiviral drug therapy.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 149

questions.stem: A patient who has been taking lamivudine (Epivir) for 6 months reports reduced sensation in the fingers and toes. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Document the report as the only action.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hold the dose and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Remind the patient to continue the drug as usual and take a multiple vitamin daily.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reassure the patient that this is an expected drug side effect and to use injury precautions.

questions.rationale: Peripheral neuropathy with loss of sensation in the extremities is a common and expected side effect of therapy. Drug therapy is not stopped for this effect. The patient needs to implement precautions to prevent injury from not having full sensation for touch, temperature, and pressure.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 149

questions.stem: Which health problem must you be specifically alert for in pregnant women who are prescribed any nucleoside analogue reverse-transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Headache

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lactic acidosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Diabetes mellitus

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nausea/vomiting

questions.rationale: NRTIs increase the risk for lactic acidosis in pregnant women. This problem is not a common occurrence for other patients. Lactic acidosis is the buildup of lactic acid in muscle and other tissues when not enough oxygen is present to allow metabolism to occur normally. Signs and symptoms of lactic acidosis are muscle aches, tiredness and difficulty remaining awake, abdominal pain, hypotension, and a slow, irregular heartbeat.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 150

questions.stem: A patient who has been taking efavirenz (Sustiva) reports a sore throat, fever, and blisters. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hold the dose and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document the report as the only action.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Remind the patient that these are symptoms of opportunistic infection.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reassure the patient that these are common and expected side effects of the drug.

questions.rationale: Efavirenz is a nonnucleotide analogue reverse-transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI). A sore throat, fever, different types of rashes, blisters, or multiple bruises are all signs of serious adverse effects of drugs from this class. The drug should be stopped and the prescriber contacted.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 150

questions.stem: Which food, drink, or herbal supplement do you warn a patient who is prescribed nevirapine (Viramune) to avoid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Caffeinated beverages

choices.label: B
choices.text: Grapefruit juice

choices.label: C
choices.text: St. John’s wort

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dairy products

questions.rationale: Nevirapine is a nonnucleoside analogue reverse-transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI). The effectiveness of drugs from this class is greatly reduced by the herbal supplement St. John’s wort.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 150

questions.stem: How do protease inhibitor (PI) drugs prevent viral replication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are counterfeit bases that prevent reverse transcriptase from synthesizing the DNA needed for viral replication.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They inactivate the enzyme that allows the viral genetic material to be integrated into the human host’s cellular DNA.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They prevent the production of proteins needed for viral particles to leave the cell and infect other cells.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They prevent initial infection by blocking the receptor the virus uses to enter target cells.

questions.rationale: Protease inhibitors prevent viral replication and release of viral particles. Human immunodeficiency virus produces its proteins, including those needed to move viral particles out of the host cell, in one long (human immunodeficiency virus [HIV]) strand. For the proteins to be active, this large protein must be broken down into separate smaller proteins through the action of the viral enzyme HIV protease. Protease inhibitors, when taken into an HIV-infected cell, make the protease enzyme work on the drug rather than on the initial large protein. Thus active proteins are not produced and viral particles cannot leave the cell to infect other cells.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 151

questions.stem: A patient who has been taking indinavir (Crixivan) for a year has just been told that the organism is now resistant to this drug. The patient expresses worry to you that this means the disease will now progress to acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS) and death will soon follow. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Your health care provider can change this drug to the intravenous form, which prevents viral replication even in HIV that has developed drug resistance.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Although the HIV is now resistant to this drug, other antiretroviral drugs are likely to be effective in controlling your disease.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “The HIV easily becomes drug resistant. That is why the drug combinations are switched so often.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “That is a possibility because these drugs do not kill the virus. It is best to be prepared.”

questions.rationale: Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) can develop drug resistance relatively quickly, especially to protease inhibitors. This does not mean that the organism is resistant to all antiretroviral drugs and that the disease will then progress to AIDS. By changing the combination of drugs frequently, even an organism that has developed resistance to one antiretroviral drug can still have its replication suppressed and the disease can be controlled. Simply telling the patient that HIV easily becomes drug resistant may be a true statement, but it does not address the patient’s concerns about the disease’s progression.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 151

questions.stem: A patient on HAART that includes neviripine (Viramune) has all of the following side effects. Which one should you report to the prescriber immediately?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nausea

choices.label: B
choices.text: 2 lb weight loss

choices.label: C
choices.text: Clay-colored stools

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dizziness on standing

questions.rationale: The clay-colored stools are associated with liver dysfunction. Because this drug can cause liver damage, it is important to stop this drug as soon as possible.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 148

questions.stem: A pediatric patient is prescribed nelfinavir (Viracept) 450 mg orally. The available drug is 50 mg/scoop. How many level scoops do you measure into the patient’s pudding to administer the correct dose?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 9 scoops

choices.label: B
choices.text: 6 scoops

choices.label: C
choices.text: 3 scoops

choices.label: D
choices.text: 2 scoops

questions.rationale: Want 450 mg in X scoops/Have 50 mg in 1 scoop. 450/50 = 9 scoops.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 150

questions.stem: Which precaution is most important to teach an older patient who is prescribed atazanavir (Reyataz)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Be sure to take this drug at least 1 hour before or 4 hours after taking an antacid.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Weigh yourself daily and notify your prescriber if you gain more than 2 lb in 1 week.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Check your pulse for a full minute daily and notify your prescriber if it becomes irregular or slow.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Check your calves daily for swelling or redness. If these symptoms appear, go to the emergency department immediately.”

questions.rationale: Atazanavir can impair electrical conduction in the heart and lead to heart block, especially in older adults or people who have an abnormally slow heart rate.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 151

questions.stem: Which precaution is most important to teach a patient who is prescribed enfuvirtide (Fuzeon)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “After the drug is mixed, store it away from light.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Use sterile technique to draw up and inject the drug.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Do not take this drug if there is any possibility that you are pregnant.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Mix the drug with tap water, then rinse the drug’s original container and add that fluid to the drug mixture.”

questions.rationale: Enfuvirtide is given twice daily by subcutaneous injection. The patient self-injects this drug at home and must use sterile technique. Injection site infections are common.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 144

questions.stem: A 40-year-old patient infected with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) asks why the new drug maraviroc (Selzentry) is not being prescribed for her. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “This drug is very expensive and your insurance may not cover it.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “This drug can cause birth defects and should not be prescribed for anyone who is pregnant or might become pregnant.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “This drug is very toxic to the heart and lungs. It is prescribed only for patients who are young and have no other health problems.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “This drug is not effective against all HIV subtypes and the HIV causing your infection does not have the target for this drug.”

questions.rationale: Maraviroc prevents cellular infection by blocking the CCR5 receptor on CD4+ T cells. Because this drug is not effective against all HIV subtypes, the patient must first be tested to ensure that his or her HIV infection is likely to respond to this therapy. When the HIV subtype does not use the CCR5 receptor, the drug is not prescribed for that patient.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 154

questions.stem: Which precaution is most important to teach older adults who are prescribed maraviroc (Selzentry)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “If an injection site infection occurs, do not reuse that site until the skin has healed completely.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Take the drug with food or milk to reduce the likelihood of GI side effects.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Change positions slowly when moving from a sitting to a standing position.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Store the drug at room temperature.”

questions.rationale: Orthostatic hypotension is more likely to develop in older adults taking maraviroc, increasing the risk for falls. Patients need to change positions slowly and use handrails when going up or down steps.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 154

questions.stem: A patient prescribed raltegravir (Isentress) reports all of the following problems or changes since starting this drug. Which problem or change do you report to the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Abdominal cramps and bloating

choices.label: B
choices.text: Muscle aches and weakness

choices.label: C
choices.text: Urinating more at night

choices.label: D
choices.text: Loss of taste for sweets

questions.rationale: This drug can cause the adverse reaction of rhabdomyolysis, which is destruction of skeletal muscle. The symptoms of this problem are muscle aches and weakness. When a patient develops this problem, the drug should be stopped.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 152

questions.stem: What precaution is most important to teach a patient newly prescribed to take Truvada?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Continue to use safer sex practices.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Avoid caffeine and any fatty or fried foods.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Be sure to drink at least 3 L of fluids daily.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “If you develop muscle or joint aches, notify your prescriber immediately.”

questions.rationale: Truvada is a drug used for pre-exposure prophylaxis among HIV negative people who are having sex with HIV positive people. If a person develops HIV disease and becomes positive while taking this drug, the risk for the HIV to become highly drug resistant greatly increases. Patients should be taught to use safer sex practices even though they are taking this drug prophylactically.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 155 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which antiretroviral drugs have greater effectiveness when administered with ritonavir? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Edurant (rilpivirine)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Emtriva (emtricitabine)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Invirase (saquinavir)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lexiva (fosamprenavir)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Prezista (darunavir)

choices.label: F
choices.text: Reyataz (atazanavir) g Tivicay (dolutegravir) h Ziagen (abacavir)

questions.rationale: When administered with these drugs, ritonavir slows their metabolism and elimination. This action allows the drugs to remain in the blood at a higher level, which increases their effectiveness.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 151

questions.stem: Which patient outcomes are major goals of antiretroviral therapy? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prevention of opportunistic infections

choices.label: B
choices.text: Conversion to an HIV-negative antibody status

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reduction of the number of viral particles in the patient’s blood

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increase in CD4+ cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decrease in CD8+ cells

choices.label: F
choices.text: Prevention of disease spread

questions.rationale: The human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) retrovirus selectively infects the helper/inducer T cells, known as CD4+ cells, which coordinate the patient’s immune response. When these cells are destroyed, the person has little protection from opportunistic infection. Successful antiretroviral therapy reduces the number of viral particles in the blood and increases the CD4+ cell numbers in the blood. These actions reduce the risk for opportunistic infection and reduce the likelihood of HIV disease spread by the patient. Antiretroviral therapy does not affect the number of CD8+ cells and does not change the patient’s antibody status.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 148 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: A child who is HIV positive and weighs 40 lb is prescribed to receive atazanavir (Reyataz) at a dose of 7 mg/kg. The drug on hand is a premixed oral suspension of 50 mg/60 mL. How many milliliters will you prepare as the correct dose?
questions.rationale: 1 kg = 2.2 lb. Child’s weight in kilogram is 18.18 (40/2.2 = 18.18). 7 mg  18.18 kg = 127.26 mg round to 127.3 mg. Determine how many milligrams are in 1 mL by dividing 50 mg by 60 mL, which equals 1 mg in 0.83 mg/ mL. Multiply 127.3 mg by 0.83 mL, which equals 105.65 mL, round up to 105.7 mL.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 151

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed to receive 300 mg of ribavirin by oral suspension. The drug on hand is ribavirin oral suspension with a concentration of 50 mg/mL. How many milliliters will you prepare as the correct dose?
questions.rationale: Want 300 mg; Have 50 mg/mL. Because the milliliter concentration of this drug is already known, divide the dose you want (300 mg) by the dose you have (50 mg) to get 6. 6 mL is the correct dose.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 140

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed 45 mg of enfuvirtide by subcutaneous injection. The available drug is a premixed solution of enfuvirtide with a concentration of 90 mg/mL.
questions.rationale: Because the milliliter concentration of the drug is known, divide 45 mg by 90 mg, which is 0.5 mL.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 153 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.stem: What is the major way in which tuberculosis is spread from one person to another?

choices.label: A
choices.text: By blood transfusions

choices.label: B
choices.text: By aerosol transmission

choices.label: C
choices.text: Through direct skin contact

choices.label: D
choices.text: From unprotected sexual intercourse

questions.rationale: The tuberculosis (TB) bacteria are spread when bacteria-filled droplets travel through the air when a person with TB coughs, laughs, sneezes, sings, or whistles. These droplets can be inhaled by another person who then may develop the disease. Bacteria-filled droplets in the air are known as “aerosol transmission.” It is possible to spread TB in other ways but aerosol transmission is by far the most common.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 157

questions.stem: Which statement about tuberculosis (TB) is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The lungs are the only organ ever affected by TB.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A positive TB test means the person has active tuberculosis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Far more people are infected with TB than actually develop the disease.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Any redness in the area where the TB test was performed is considered positive.

questions.rationale: TB is the most common bacterial infection worldwide. However, it requires an overwhelming exposure in a susceptible host to develop the disease. Therefore many more people are exposed to and take in the bacteria than who actually go on to develop the disease.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 157

questions.stem: Which drug is NOT one of the “first-line” antitubercular drugs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Isoniazid (INH)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rifampin (RIF)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pyrazinamide (PAS)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Amphotericin (AMT)

questions.rationale: The four first-line drugs to treat tuberculosis are isoniazid, ethambutal, rifampin, and pyrazinamide. Amphotericin is an antifungal drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 159

questions.stem: Which antitubercular drug causes the urine to turn reddish orange?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Isoniazid (INH)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rifampin (RIF)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pyrazinamide (PAS)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ethambutol (EMB)

questions.rationale: Rifampin is associated with discoloration of the urine, which turns a reddish orange or a reddish brown color. The cause of this discoloration is not known but may be related to the drug’s action of increasing the renal excretion of bile acids.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Remembering   REF:  p. 160

questions.stem: Which antitubercular drug is avoided in the treatment of children who have tuberculosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Isoniazid (INH)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rifampin (RIF)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pyrazinamide (PAS)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ethambutol (EMB)

questions.rationale: Ethambutol can cause serious eye problems that may lead to blindness in people of any age. Its use is avoided in children because they may not recognize vision changes caused by the drug soon enough to avoid permanent vision changes or blindness.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 162

questions.stem: How long is the minimum course of drug therapy for an immunocompetent patient with active tuberculosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 7 to 10 days

choices.label: B
choices.text: 6 weeks

choices.label: C
choices.text: 6 months

choices.label: D
choices.text: 2 years

questions.rationale: A patient who has nondrug-resistant tuberculosis must remain on the prescribed medications for at least 6 months. The organism is slow growing and is harder to control than other types of bacteria.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 159

questions.stem: What type of fungal infections can be treated with topical antifungal agents?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Those that are confined to the skin or mucous membranes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Those that are located under the toenails or fingernails

choices.label: C
choices.text: Those that are infected by only one specific type of fungus

choices.label: D
choices.text: The type that only infect humans and not animals

questions.rationale: Antifungal drugs that are applied topically do not penetrate tissues and only work against fungi that are on the surface.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 163

questions.stem: Which antifungal drug causes “red man syndrome?”

choices.label: A
choices.text: Amphotericin B

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ketoconazole

choices.label: C
choices.text: Caspofungin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fluconazole

questions.rationale: Amphotericin B causes massive vasodilation, especially if given too fast, which results in whole body flushing and a red appearance.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 167

questions.stem: Which class of antifungal agents should never be given with grapefruit juice?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Echinocandins

choices.label: B
choices.text: Alyamines

choices.label: C
choices.text: Polyenes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Azoles

questions.rationale: The activity of azole antifungal drugs can be reduced by grapefruit juice in large quantities. Patients should not take an azole with grapefruit juice and should limit their total grapefruit juice intake to no more than 24 ounces/day.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 167

questions.stem: Which antifungal drug to treat ringworm is given to children as granules that are sprinkled over soft, nonacidic foods?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Amphotericin B

choices.label: B
choices.text: Flucytosine

choices.label: C
choices.text: Terbinafine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Micafungin

questions.rationale: Micafungin and amphotericin B are administered intravenously. Although flucytosine can be administered orally, it is not available as granules and is not prescribed for ringworm.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Remembering   REF:  p. 169

questions.stem: Which antifungal drug causes some degree of renal impairment in everyone who receives it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Amphotericin (Amphocin)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Terbinafine (Lamisil)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Caspofungin (Cancidas)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fluconazole (Diflucan)

questions.rationale: Although some antifungal drugs have the potential to affect renal function if given at higher doses or for prolonged periods, only amphotericin causes renal impairment in everyone who receives it even if they only receive one dose.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Remembering   REF:  p. 166 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Why are most standard antibacterial drugs ineffective against tuberculosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The TB organism is slow growing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The TB organism is not really a type of bacteria.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The TB organism does not have a true cell wall.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The TB organism is usually located in the brain stem where antibacterial drugs cannot reach.

questions.rationale: Because the TB organism is slow growing, many common antibacterial drugs are not effective in controlling or killing it. Combination drug therapy with agents effective against the TB organism is needed to treat TB and prevent its transmission.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Understanding   REF:  p. 158

questions.stem: A patient received a tuberculosis skin test injection of purified protein derivative (PPD) 72 hours ago. Which assessment finding of the test site should be interpreted as a positive reaction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The injected area has a blister-like swelling about 2 mm high and 2 mm in diameter.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The injection site is puffy and soft with pus oozing from the needle hole.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The skin is red and very hard for 12 mm around the injection site.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is a large bruise surrounding the injection site.

questions.rationale: A positive reaction to a TB skin test requires that the skin around the injection site be indurated, not just red. Induration is caused by infiltration of the skin around the test site with many white blood cells, making the area red and swollen, and the tissue much harder than the surrounding normal skin. Bruising is a result of bleeding into the injection site, not infiltration and induration from white blood cells.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 158

questions.stem: A patient with which findings requires drug therapy for active tuberculosis (TB)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Negative TB skin test and chest x-ray but who has productive cough, fever, and shortness of breath

choices.label: B
choices.text: Positive TB skin test, productive cough, and a cavitation on chest x-ray

choices.label: C
choices.text: Positive TB skin test and a TB scar on chest x-ray

choices.label: D
choices.text: Positive TB skin test as the only symptom

questions.rationale: A positive skin test for TB only means that the patient was infected with TB at one time. A TB scar also only indicates old, inactive disease. Cavitation and productive cough along with a positive skin test are indicators of active disease that can be spread to others, and this person must be treated. The person with a negative skin test and chest x-ray but with other symptoms probably has another type of respiratory infection.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 158

questions.stem: Which drug must a patient be taught to avoid during TB drug therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aspirin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ibuprofen

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acetaminophen

choices.label: D
choices.text: Iron supplements

questions.rationale: Teach all patients on TB drug therapy to avoid alcoholic beverages and acetaminophen for the entire therapy period. Both alcohol and acetaminophen are toxic to the liver and so are the antitubercular drugs. Taking all of these drugs and either acetaminophen or drinking alcohol greatly increases the risk for permanent liver damage.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 161

questions.stem: Why is ethambutol always used in combination with other antitubercular drugs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Its presence enhances or potentiates the other first-line antitubercular drugs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Its side effects are more serious when taken alone.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is only bacteriostatic and not bactericidal.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is not a first-line antitubercular drug.

questions.rationale: Ethambutol is only bacteriostatic. When taken alone, it cannot eradicate the tuberculosis bacteria and cure the disease.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 159

questions.stem: A patient with active tuberculosis who has been taking isoniazid (INH, Nydrazid) and rifampin (RIF, Rifadin) reports having urine that is an orange color. What your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Obtain a specimen for culture and test the urine for occult blood.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reassure the patient that this is a normal drug side effect.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hold the dose and contact the prescriber.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Document the report as the only action.

questions.rationale: Rifampin normally turns urine an orange color. No intervention is needed; however, the color change can be very distressing to patients. The patient should be reassured that this color change is normal and be taught how to manage this change so that clothing does not become stained.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF:  p. 160

questions.stem: A patient who is prescribed pyrazinamide and ethambutol for active tuberculosis (TB) also has gout. What adjustments will this patient’s condition require?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gout is not affected by TB drug therapy, and no adjustments are needed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The prescribed therapy will improve gout and reduce the need for drug therapy to treat gout.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The prescribed therapy increases the risk for gout attacks, and the patient should increase his or her intake of water.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The drugs for treatment of gout interact with the drugs for TB, and the patient must not take the gout drugs within 2 hours of taking the TB drugs.

questions.rationale: Both pyrazinamide and ethambutol increase uric acid production and increase the risk for gout attacks. Drinking more water can help prevent the uric acid crystals from precipitating in joints or kidneys.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Understanding   REF:  p. 160

questions.stem: A male patient who has been prescribed isoniazid (INH, Nydrazid) reports that his breasts have enlarged since starting therapy. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “This is common with isoniazid and will disappear after you stop drug therapy.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Is the enlargement the same on both sides or is one breast larger than the other?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “If you are not having difficulty getting an erection, do not worry about this change.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I will report this problem to your prescriber and see if it is possible for you to stop taking this drug.”

questions.rationale: Breast enlargement in men (gynecomastia) is a common side effect of isoniazid. This physical change can be very distressing and embarrassing to men, and they should be reassured that the problem is temporary. Even though it is distressing, breast enlargement is not a reason to stop drug therapy.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF:  p. 160

questions.stem: Which statement made by a patient indicates the need for more teaching about first-line drug therapy for tuberculosis (TB)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “To prevent nausea/vomiting, I have been taking my drugs at night with a small snack.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I have stopped taking all herbal supplements and stopped drinking beer until I finish this drug therapy.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Now that my symptoms have disappeared after a month of drug therapy, I can no longer infect my family.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Now that my symptoms have disappeared after a month of drug therapy, I can stop taking . all of these drugs.”

questions.rationale: Usually TB is no longer contagious and clinical improvement is seen after drugs have been taken for 2 to 3 consecutive weeks. However, the organism growth has only been suppressed and the immune system and drugs have not had sufficient time to eradicate it. Stopping treatment after only a month will allow the disease to again become active and the organisms can become resistant to these drugs. The patient must continue taking the drugs for 6 months or longer, exactly as prescribed.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 161

questions.stem: What is the most important question to ask a patient before administering rifampin (RIF) intravenously?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Have you ever had gout?”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Are you allergic to sulfa drugs?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Are you allergic to sulfite preservatives?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Have you had any alcoholic beverages within the last month?”

questions.rationale: The IV form of rifampin contains a sulfite preservative that can cause hypersensitivity reactions in patients allergic to sulfite preservatives such as sodium metabisulfite or potassium metabisulfite. This problem is not the same as an allergy to “sulfa” drugs.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 160

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed 420 mg of rifampin to be added to 500 mL of dextrose 5% in water (D5W). The vial contains rifampin 60 mg/mL. How many milliliters should you add to the D5W for the correct?

choices.label: B
choices.text: 5mL

choices.label: C
choices.text: 7mL

choices.label: D
choices.text: 8mL

questions.rationale: Want 420 mg/Have 60 mg in 1 mL. 420/60 = 7 mL.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 160

questions.stem: A patient has been prescribed all four first-line drugs for tuberculosis (TB). Which laboratory blood value is most important to report to the prescriber first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Red blood cells (RBCs) 2.2 million/mm3

choices.label: B
choices.text: International normalized ratio 1.6

choices.label: C
choices.text: White blood cells 6000/mm3

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sodium 134 mEq/L

questions.rationale: All of these values are abnormal; however, only the RBC count is seriously out of the normal range. One of the most serious adverse effects of rifampin (a major part of first-line drug therapy for TB) is suppression of RBC production, leading to anemia. This patient’s RBC level is only about half of normal.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 161

questions.stem: Which administration technique should you teach the family of a patient with memory problems for best adherence to first-line drug therapy for tuberculosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Having one family member responsible for giving the drugs and watching the patient swallow them

choices.label: B
choices.text: Setting up the patient’s drugs using a daily pill dispenser that has separate slots for each individual drug

choices.label: C
choices.text: Asking the patient every night whether he or she has remembered to take all the drug doses that day

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administering all the drugs together at the same time every day and ensuring that the patient drinks plenty of water

questions.rationale: Successful drug therapy for TB requires that all first-line drugs be taken correctly without missing doses.
