Patients who have memory problems usually need some form of directly observed therapy (DOT) to ensure absolute adherence to this important therapy. Having one family member responsible for giving the drugs fulfills the requirements of DOT and reduces the risk that the patient will receive either too many drug doses or too few doses to be effective.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 161

questions.stem: Which precaution is most important to teach a patient who is prescribed isoniazid (INH, Nydrazid)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Use another form of birth control in addition to oral contraceptives to prevent an unplanned pregnancy.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Do not drive or operate dangerous equipment until you know how this drug affects you.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Wear glasses rather than contact lenses throughout this drug therapy.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Avoid coffee and any foods or beverages that contain caffeine.”

questions.rationale: Isoniazid raises blood pressure. When this drug is taken in combination with other drugs or agents that also raise blood pressure, the patient can develop dangerous hypertension. Caffeine is a common food ingredient that greatly increases the risk for hypertension when taken in combination with isoniazid.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 162

questions.stem: A patient who is prescribed ethambutol (EMB, Myambutol) reports trouble distinguishing colors. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Document the report as the only action.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hold the dose and notify the prescriber immediately.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Caution the patient to wear sunglasses and a hat when outside.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reassure the patient that this is a common side effect of the drug.

questions.rationale: Ethambutol at high doses can cause optic neuritis vision changes that include reduced color vision, blurred vision, and reduced visual fields. This problem can lead to blindness. When the problem is discovered early, the eye problems are usually reversed when the drug is stopped.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 160

questions.stem: Why are standard antibacterial drugs ineffective against fungal infections?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Funguses are slow growing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Funguses have a thick cell wall.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fungal infections usually occur in the brain stem where antibacterial drugs cannot reach.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fungal infections have so few symptoms that most people are unaware of the problem until the infection is widespread.

questions.rationale: Fungi have many differences from bacteria that make antibacterial drugs less effective or not at all effective in controlling their growth. Fungi have a tough, thick cell wall and a hardy  plasma membrane. Antibacterial drugs are not able to penetrate the thick cell wall or disrupt the plasma membrane.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Understanding   REF:  pp. 162-163

questions.stem: A patient prescribed a systemic antifungal drug reports a change in how food tastes. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Document the report as the only action.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hold the dose and notify the prescriber immediately.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Caution the patient to wear sunglasses and a hat when outside.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reassure the patient that this is a common side effect of the drug.

questions.rationale: Systemic antifungal drugs have many common side effects, including loss of taste or changes in how food tastes. Unless this problem interferes with the patient’s nutritional status, it is of no consequence. The patient should be reassured that the taste changes are an expected side effect and that normal taste sensation will return after the drug has been stopped after several days or a week.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 166

questions.stem: Which teaching point should you stress when a patient is prescribed terbinafine (Lamisil)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Avoid citrus fruits while taking this drug.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Do not drive until you know how the drug affects you.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stay away from crowds or people who are ill.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Get lots of extra rest while you are taking this drug.

questions.rationale: Warn patients taking terbinafine or flucytosine for more than 1 week that they are at an increased risk for infection and should avoid crowds and people who are ill.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 168

questions.stem: A patient taking ketoconazole (Nizoral) for the last 2 weeks has all of the following blood laboratory test values. Which value should you report to the prescriber immediately?

choices.label: A
choices.text: White blood cell count (WBC) 10,500 cells/mm3

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lactate dehydrogenase 880 IU/L

choices.label: C
choices.text: Potassium 3.6 mEq/L

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hematocrit 32%

questions.rationale: The WBC count, lactate dehydrogenase, and hematocrit values are abnormal; however, only the lactate dehydrogenase level is very high (three to four times normal). This value indicates liver impairment, which can be caused by taking ketoconazole. This value must be reported immediately so that liver function can be explored and any needed changes in drug therapy made before irreversible liver damage occurs.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 168

questions.stem: A patient about to start systemic antifungal therapy has a white blood cell (WBC) count of 3200 cells/mm3. Which antifungal drug must be avoided for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fluconazole (Diflucan)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ketoconazole (Nizoral)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Micafungin (Mycamine)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Terbinafine (Lamisil)

questions.rationale: This WBC count is lower than normal. Terbinafine and flucytosine (Ancobon) can further reduce this patient’s WBC count and greatly increase his or her risk for infection.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 168

questions.stem: Which laboratory value in a 76-year-old woman on systemic fluconazole (Diflucan) therapy should you report immediately to the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hematocrit of 38%

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bilirubin of 0.4 mg/dL

choices.label: C
choices.text: Potassium of 2.2 mEq/L

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) of 12.2 mg/dL

questions.rationale: The drug can affect how the kidney reabsorbs or excretes potassium. A serum potassium level of 2.2 is very low and could lead to serious problems. The low potassium level is likely to cause greater problems in an older adult.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 167

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed 50 mg of amphotericin B (Fungizone) by intravenous infusion in 500 mL of dextrose 5% in water (D5W) over 6 hours. How many milliliters (mL) per minute should you set the pump to infuse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 1.4 mL b 2.2 mL

choices.label: C
choices.text: 4.2 mL

choices.label: D
choices.text: 6.2 mL

questions.rationale: 500 mL/6 = 83.3 mL/h. 83.3 mL/60 minutes = 1.38 mL/min. Round up to 1.4 mL/min.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 167

questions.stem: A patient is receiving IV amphotericin B (Fungizone) for a systemic fungal infection. Which assessment parameter should you perform to determine whether the patient is having an adverse reaction to the therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Measure abdominal girth for presence of ascites

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assess mouth and oral cavity for candidiasis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assess infusion site for phlebitis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assess calves for pain

questions.rationale: Among its many adverse effects, amphotericin B is very irritating and causes phlebitis at the site of infusion. This can occur quickly, sometimes after only  one drug dose. When it is present, the IV site needs to be changed to prevent more complications and patient’s discomfort.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 167

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed amphotericin B (Fungizone) 400 mg intravenously. How fast should you infuse this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediately by intravenous (IV) push

choices.label: B
choices.text: Over a period of 60 minutes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Over a period of 2 hours

choices.label: D
choices.text: Over a minimum of 6 hours

questions.rationale: Rapid administration of IV amphotericin B is associated with more severe rigors, hyperkalemia, and a more rapid onset of renal insufficiency. It is recommended that the drug be administered over a 6-hour time period regardless of the dose.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 167

questions.stem: A patient who is very immunosuppressed is prescribed ketoconazole (Nizoral) for prevention of a fungal infection. Which statement made by this patient indicates the need for more teaching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I will stop taking the drug if I should become pregnant.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I will stay in the sun as much as possible to avoid catching an infection.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I will avoid drinking alcoholic beverages during the time I am on this therapy.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “If I miss a dose, I will take it as soon as I remember it and not wait until the next day.”

questions.rationale: Ketoconazole increases sun sensitivity and increases the risk for severe sunburn. Patients taking the drug should avoid direct sunlight, use sunscreen, and wear protective clothing whenever they are outdoors to prevent a severe sunburn. They should also avoid tanning beds and salons.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 168

questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed flucytosine (Ancobon) reports reduced sensation in the fingers and toes. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Document the report as the only action.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hold the dose and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Remind the patient to continue the drug as usual and to take a multiple vitamin daily.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reassure the patient that this is an expected drug side effect and to use injury precautions.

questions.rationale: Peripheral neuropathy with loss of sensation in the extremities is a common and expected side effect of flucytosine therapy. Drug therapy is not stopped for this effect. The patient must implement precautions to prevent injury from not having full sensation for touch, temperature, and pressure.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 166 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which changes are intended responses of first-line antituberculosis drugs? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sputum is mobilized (increased).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cough is decreased.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fatigue is eliminated.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Weight is gained.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sputum culture for TB is negative.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Future TB tests are negative.

questions.rationale: Expected responses of first-line antituberculosis drugs include cough is reduced; sputum production is reduced; fatigue is reduced; weight is gained; and sputum culture is negative for TB organisms.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 160

questions.stem: A pregnant patient is taking all four first-line drugs for tuberculosis (TB). Which additional health care actions are needed for this patient? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ensuring that no weight is gained

choices.label: B
choices.text: Checking liver function tests monthly

choices.label: C
choices.text: Checking vision and intraocular pressure monthly

choices.label: D
choices.text: Testing urine for the presence of the TB organism

choices.label: E
choices.text: Supplementing with B-complex vitamins

choices.label: F
choices.text: Avoiding alcoholic beverages

questions.rationale: The risk for liver toxicity is higher when taking TB drug therapy during pregnancy, and close monitoring of liver function is needed. In addition, the pregnant woman needs higher doses of a B-complex vitamin supplement when taking INH. Avoiding alcohol during TB therapy is very important for all patients and is critical for pregnant women because of the increased risk for liver toxicity as well as fetal alcohol syndrome.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 162

questions.stem: Which precautions are important to teach a woman using a vaginal cream form of an antifungal drug? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Wear gloves to insert the cream.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wash the applicator with soap and water.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Do not tub bathe until treatment is completed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Avoid sexual intercourse during the treatment period.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Remind the patient that the cream can make holes in a condom or diaphragm.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Stop the drug immediately if you think you are pregnant. g Stop the drug when symptoms have disappeared to avoid unnecessary exposure to it.

questions.rationale: Sexual intercourse should be avoided for several reasons: the drug can make holes in a condom or diaphragm and increase the risk for an unplanned pregnancy; in addition, the infection can be spread to a sexual partner. The applicator should be washed regularly with soap and water after each use. Although hands should be washed before and after applying the drug, the vagina is not a sterile body cavity, so it is not necessary to wear gloves during the application. There are no bathing restrictions while using the drug. Vaginal application of the drug does not affect pregnancy (however, a vaginal infection can have adverse effects on the pregnancy). To ensure eradication of the infection and prevent the development of resistant organisms, the drug should be used for as long as prescribed even after symptoms are no longer present.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 164

questions.stem: A patient is receiving IV caspofungin (Cancidas). Which assessments are most important to perform daily? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assessing both calves for swelling, redness, and pain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Examining the skin for rash and blisters

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assessing capillary refill in fingers and toes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assessing the injection site for irritation or phlebitis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reviewing laboratory reports of white blood cell counts

choices.label: F
choices.text: Listening for bowel sounds in all four abdominal quadrants g Reviewing laboratory reports for blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine

questions.rationale: Echinocandins can increase the rate of clot formation, which increases the risk for deep vein thrombosis (DVT). DVT is most likely to occur in the veins of the lower legs and in the pelvis. Symptoms of DVT in an extremity include swelling, warmth, and pain or discomfort. All the systemic antifungal agents can cause renal insufficiency. Anyone prescribed these drugs must have renal function monitored. Any intravenous antifungal drug can irritate veins and cause phlebitis. Skin irritation and rashes can occur with systemic antifungal therapy. Rashes may be severe with many types of lesions (Stevens-Johnson syndrome). If the rashes become widespread with crusting, fever, and tissue necrosis, the condition can be life-threatening. Echinocandins do not affect intestinal motility or capillary refill. They also do not directly affect bone marrow production of white blood cells.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 168

questions.stem: An older adult patient is prescribed systemic antifungal therapy for 1 month. Which precautions are important to teach this patient? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Avoid drinking coffee or any other beverages or food that contain caffeine.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Check your pulse daily and report any new irregularities to your prescriber.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Examine all bowel movements and any vomit for the presence of pus and report it to your prescriber.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Look at the whites of your eyes daily and if a yellow color appears, report it to your prescriber.”

choices.label: E
choices.text: “If your daily urine output drops below a liter less than your daily fluid intake, notify your prescriber immediately.”

choices.label: F
choices.text: “If you should develop abdominal bloating and diarrhea, go to the nearest emergency department as soon as possible.”

questions.rationale: Most of the antifungal drugs can affect cardiac rhythm. Patients should check their pulse daily for a full minute, and if new irregularities are present or the rate is slow, the prescriber should be notified. The risk for drug-induced liver toxicity is higher among older adults; often the first symptom of reduced liver function is yellowing of the sclera and skin. Older adults are also more likely to have some degree of renal insufficiency and can develop kidney problems more quickly while taking an antifungal drug; normal daily urine output should be very close to the volume of fluid taken in each day. There is no recommendation to avoid caffeine with any antifungal drug. GI infection is not a specific risk associated with antifungal therapy. Abdominal bloating and diarrhea are not especially associated with antifungal therapy and are not indications of the need to seek immediate medical attention.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 161 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: A patient who weighs 220 lb is prescribed 20 mg/kg of ethambutol (EMB). The ethambutol tablets available 400 mg/scored tablet. How many tablets will you give?
questions.rationale: 1 kg = 2.2 lb. Patient’s weight in kilogram = 100 kg (220/2.2 = 100). 100 kg  2 kg/mg = 2000 mg (or 2 g). Want 2000 mg, Have 400 mg/tablet. 2000/400 = 5 tablets.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 160

questions.stem: A patient is to receive 200 mg of fluconazole (Diflucan) by intravenous (IV) piggyback over the next hour. The piggyback bag containing the fluconazole has a total of 120 mL and the drop factor of the administration set is 15 gtt/mL. How many drops per minute are needed to administer the 200 mg of fluconazole in 1 hour?
questions.rationale: 120 mL/h is 2 mL/min (120 mL/60 minutes in 1 hour). 2 mL/min  15 gtt/mL = 30 gtt/min.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 165 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.stem: What is the main role of insulin in glucose (carbohydrate) metabolism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Conversion of complex carbohydrates into glucose

choices.label: B
choices.text: Movement of glucose from blood into the cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prevention of kidney excretion of glucose

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prevention of the formation of fat cells

questions.rationale: Many body cells have membranes that do not allow glucose to move into the cells to participate in metabolism. When insulin binds to membrane insulin receptors, the membranes become more open to glucose, allowing it to enter.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Remembering   REF:  p. 220

questions.stem: By which action does insulin lower blood glucose levels?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enhancing the enzymes that break down glucose

choices.label: B
choices.text: Helping glucose to move from the blood into cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Converting glucose into proteins in the liver and brain

choices.label: D
choices.text: Converting glycogen into glucose in the liver and brain

questions.rationale: When insulin binds to insulin receptors on cells, the cells’ membranes become more open (permeable) to glucose and glucose transport proteins in the cell membranes become more active. The overall result is movement of glucose into the cells, which lowers blood glucose levels.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 220

questions.stem: Which term means an elevated blood sugar (glucose) level?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Glycogen

choices.label: B
choices.text: Euglycemia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypoglycemia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hyperglycemia

questions.rationale: Glycogen is a stored form of human carbohydrate. Euglycemia means a blood sugar level within the normal range. Hypoglycemia is a lower than normal blood sugar level.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 210

questions.stem: Which hormone has an action opposite to the action of insulin?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Glycogen

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glucagon

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hemoglobin A1c

choices.label: D
choices.text: Adenosine triphosphate

questions.rationale: Glucagon, which is known as the hormone of starvation, is secreted from the alpha cells of the pancreas when blood glucose levels are lower than normal. Glucagon goes to the liver and triggers the breakdown of glycogen and release of glucose into the blood, raising the blood glucose level. These actions are exactly the opposite of insulin’s actions.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 212

questions.stem: What is the main reason that insulin is needed as drug therapy for people who have type 1 diabetes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The beta cells of the pancreas no longer make insulin.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The alpha cells of the pancreas no longer make insulin.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The beta cells of the pancreas make too much glucagon.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The alpha cells of the pancreas make too much glucagon.

questions.rationale: With type 1 diabetes, the beta cells of the pancreas are destroyed and no longer produce any insulin. Insulin is necessary for life, so drug therapy for type 1 diabetes requires insulin. People who have type 2 diabetes often continue to secrete insulin from their beta cells, thus extra insulin may not be needed. Drugs for this condition often work by forcing the beta cells to release the patient’s own insulin.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 212

questions.stem: Which condition is the most dangerous adverse effect of insulin?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypoglycemia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hyperglycemia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Severe hypotension

choices.label: D
choices.text: Development of insulin allergy

questions.rationale: The main adverse effect of insulin is the lowering of blood glucose levels below normal (hypoglycemia). This response, also called insulin shock, is dangerous because brain cells are very sensitive to low blood glucose levels and the patient can become nonresponsive very quickly. If the problem is not corrected immediately, the patient can die or have irreversible brain damage.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 215

questions.stem: Which problem associated with insulin therapy and diabetes is intensified for a patient who is also prescribed warfarin (Coumadin) or a beta adrenergic blocker?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypoglycemia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nausea/vomiting

choices.label: C
choices.text: Coronary artery disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: Risk for excessive bleeding

questions.rationale: Some drugs intensify the effects of insulin and greatly increase the risk for hypoglycemia. In addition, drugs such as beta adrenergic blockers also reduce the patient’s perception of hypoglycemia, further increasing hypoglycemic risk. Insulin does not affect the action of warfarin, so it does not increase the risk for bleeding. Although diabetes increases the risk for coronary artery disease, insulin therapy helps reduce that risk. Insulin is not associated with nausea/vomiting as side effects.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 219

questions.stem: Which drug therapy class is considered the treatment of choice for people who have gestational diabetes mellitus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Insulin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Insulin sensitizers

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thiazolidinediones

choices.label: D
choices.text: Alpha-glucosidase inhibitors

questions.rationale: Insulin is the only class of antidiabetic drugs approved for use in patients who have gestational diabetes mellitus. This type of diabetes is more difficult to control and with other agents tends to have more periods of hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 219

questions.stem: What makes insulin a “high-alert” drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Many people are very allergic to insulin.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Serious harm can occur if the wrong dose is given.

choices.label: C
choices.text: If too much insulin is given, the respiratory system is depressed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: If too much insulin is given, the blood glucose level could climb to dangerously high levels.

questions.rationale: Insulin as a drug works very quickly. Great harm can come to a patient who receives it and does not have diabetes. Also, if too much of it is given to a patient who does have diabetes, the patient can become severely hypoglycemic and die. If too little insulin is given to a patient who has diabetes, blood glucose levels remain too high and cause organ damage.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 214

questions.stem: Which type of insulin has the shortest duration of action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rapid-acting insulin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Short-acting insulin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intermediate-acting insulin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Long-acting insulin

questions.rationale: Not only does rapid-acting insulin have a quick onset of action, it also has a very short duration of action and is inactivated quickly.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 214

questions.stem: Which problem is a common side effect of insulin therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased blood clotting

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased blood clotting

choices.label: C
choices.text: Injection site infection

choices.label: D
choices.text: Foot ulcer formation

questions.rationale: Insulin therapy does not affect blood clotting. Foot ulcer formation occurs as a result of diabetes and not because of insulin therapy. Insulin is delivered as an injection and people with diabetes have a greater risk for infection. So, the only side effect for insulin on this list is injection site infection.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 215

questions.stem: Which problem is a major symptom of low blood sugar levels?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypertension

choices.label: B
choices.text: Flushed skin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Weight gain

choices.label: D
choices.text: Confusion

questions.rationale: The brain depends on a constant supply of glucose in the blood for proper function. When blood glucose levels become too low, the brain does not function properly.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 215

questions.stem: Which body area should never be used to inject insulin?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The actual umbilicus

choices.label: B
choices.text: The outer thighs

choices.label: C
choices.text: The upper arms

choices.label: D
choices.text: The lower back

questions.rationale: The most preferred injection site is within a 2-inch circumference around (but not in) the umbilicus. The skin of the umbilicus is thick and contains fewer blood vessels. Insulin absorption would be slow here and the area is at greater risk for infection.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 217

questions.stem: What is the action of the biguanide class of antidiabetic drugs that lowers blood glucose levels?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Directly forcing beta cells to release more insulin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inhibiting the enzyme that breaks down insulin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Preventing the breakdown of glycogen into glucose

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increasing the person’s need for glucose

questions.rationale: The biguanides (metformin) work in two ways to lower blood glucose levels. They decrease conversion of glycogen into glucose within the liver and they increase the sensitivity of the insulin receptor to binding insulin. The sulfonylureas and the meglitinide analogues force beta cells to release more insulin. None of the drugs inactivate the enzyme that breaks down insulin, nor do they increase the person’s need for glucose.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 222

questions.stem: Which oral antidiabetic drug belongs to the alpha-glucosidase inhibitor class?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acarbose (Precose)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glyburide (Diabeta)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nateglinide (Starlix)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pioglitazone (Actos)

questions.rationale: Acarbose (Precose) belongs to the alpha-glucosidase inhibitor class. Glyburide (Diabeta) belongs to the Insulin Secretagogues class. Nateglinide (Starlix) belongs to the Meglitinides class and Pioglitazone (Actos) belongs to the Thiazolidinediones class.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 221

questions.stem: Which class of oral antidiabetic drug can cause severe hypoglycemia when used alone?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alpha-glucosidase inhibitors

choices.label: B
choices.text: Thiazolidinediones

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sulfonylureas

choices.label: D
choices.text: Biguanides

questions.rationale: The sulfonylurea drugs force the beta cells of the pancreas to secrete preformed insulin. If the patient has not eaten at a time close to when he or she took the drug, blood glucose levels could drop sharply and endanger the patient. Other drugs on this list do not increase insulin production and are not likely to cause hypoglycemia when used as a single agent.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 222

questions.stem: Which drug used to treat diabetes by increasing incretins and amylin is taken orally?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Exenatide (Byetta)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Liraglutide (Victoza)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pramlintide (Symlin)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sitagliptin (Januvia)

questions.rationale: Exenatide, liraglutide, and pramlintide are all administered by subcutaneous injection. Only sitagliptin is an oral agent.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 222

questions.stem: Which antidiabetic drug can increase the risk for pancreatitis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alogliptin (Nesina)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glyburide (Diabeta)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Metformin (Glucophage)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dapagliflozin (Farxiga)

questions.rationale: Dapagliflozin is a drug from the sodium-glucose cotransport inhibitor class. Drugs from this class increase the risk for pancreatitis.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 222 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: How does the glucose we eat influence metabolism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It combines with the glucose we make to form the proteins needed to replace those lost daily in stool.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is composed of extra or “empty” calories that are not necessary for life and only contribute to obesity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is used inside cells to form the high-energy substance adenosine triphosphate (ATP) needed to perform most cell functions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is converted to fats (lipids) which are used to protect the body from injury, insulate against heat loss, and provide the greatest number of needed calories.

questions.rationale: Although both proteins and fats can make fuel to run the body’s actions, the main fuel provider is carbohydrate. We eat carbohydrates as sugars and starches. Once inside the digestive tract, the body converts most of the carbohydrates we eat into glucose (also called dextrose). Glucose is the most common simple carbohydrate and the main fuel for the human body. Inside cells, glucose is used to form ATP, which is the main chemical energy substance that drives all the body’s cellular reactions.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 210

questions.stem: What is the basic underlying pathology of diabetes mellitus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Failure of insulin production or of insulin binding to its receptors

choices.label: B
choices.text: Loss of the ability for insulin to convert body fat into carbohydrates or glucose

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inability of pancreatic insulin to break down glycogen during periods of fasting

choices.label: D
choices.text: Loss of the ability of pancreatic insulin to process carbohydrates for intestinal elimination, resulting in excessively high blood glucose levels

questions.rationale: The lack of insulin in diabetes, either from a lack of insulin secretion or from insulin receptor pathology, prevents insulin-sensitive cells from using glucose as a fuel source needed to generate the high-energy substance adenosine triphosphate (ATP).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 212

questions.stem: Why is it important to maintain normal blood glucose levels in the body?

choices.label: A
choices.text: High levels increase the risk for heart disease, strokes, blindness, and kidney failure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: High levels increase the risk for seizure disorders, arthritis, osteoporosis, and bone fractures.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Low levels increase the risk for peripheral neuropathy, Alzheimer’s disease, and premature aging.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Low levels increase the risk for obesity, pancreatitis, dehydration, and certain types of . cancer.

questions.rationale: A person with high blood glucose levels develops major changes in blood vessels that lead to organ damage, serious health problems, and early death. The long-term complications of diabetes include heart attacks, strokes, and kidney failure. In addition, diabetes is the main cause of foot and leg amputations and of new cases of blindness.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 213

questions.stem: How do type 1 and type 2 diabetes differ from each other?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Type 1 diabetes develops in people under 40 years old, whereas type 2 diabetes develops only in older people.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Type 2 diabetes develops in people under 40 years old, whereas type 1 diabetes develops only in older people.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patients with type 1 diabetes are at higher risk for obesity and heart disease, whereas patients with type 2 diabetes are at higher risk for strokes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patients with type 1 diabetes produce no insulin, whereas patients with type 2 diabetes produce insulin but their insulin receptors are not very sensitive to it.

questions.rationale: The main problem of type 1 diabetes is that the person can no longer make insulin. Without insulin, the patient’s blood glucose level becomes very high, but glucose cannot enter many cells. Patients who have type 1 diabetes must use insulin daily for the rest of their lives or receive a pancreas transplant. With type 2 diabetes, the person still has beta cells that make some insulin. In fact, some people who have type 2 diabetes have normal levels of insulin; however, the insulin receptors are not very sensitive to insulin. As a result, insulin does not bind as tightly to its receptors as it should and less glucose moves from the blood into the cells.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: pp. 212-213

questions.stem: Why is insulin only given by injection and not as an oral drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Injected insulin works faster than oral drugs to lower blood glucose levels.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Insulin is a small protein that is destroyed by stomach acids and intestinal enzymes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Insulin is a “high-alert drug” and could more easily be abused if it were available as an oral agent.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Oral insulin has a high “first pass loss” rate in the liver and would require very high dosages to be effective.

questions.rationale: Because insulin is a small protein that is easily destroyed by stomach acids and intestinal enzymes, it cannot be used as an oral drug. Most commonly it is injected subcutaneously.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 213

questions.stem: A patient who has been newly diagnosed with diabetes and prescribed insulin therapy asks why more than one injection of insulin each day will be required. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “You need to start with multiple injections for practice until you become more proficient at self-injection.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “A single daily dose of insulin would not match your blood insulin levels to your food intake patterns closely enough.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Using just one dose of insulin daily would require you to eat no more than one meal each day and you could not exercise.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “If all your insulin were in just one injection, the dose would be too large to be absorbed predictably and you would be in danger of unexpected insulin shock.”

questions.rationale: Even when a single injection of insulin contains a combined dose of different acting insulins, the timing of the actions and the timing of food intake may not match well enough to prevent wide variation in blood glucose levels. The best control of glucose levels occurs with multiple injections throughout the day.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 210

questions.stem: Which statement about insulin glargine is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It has no time to peak action.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It normally has a cloudy appearance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It can be mixed with any other insulin type.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It should be avoided as therapy for anyone who has a sulfa allergy.

questions.rationale: Insulin glargine has a formulation that makes it long-acting and slow-releasing. As a result, the blood levels are low and the action is prolonged. It has no particular time to peak action. It normally has a clear, not a cloudy appearance and should not be mixed with most other insulin types. It does not contain sulfa and does not trigger an allergic reaction in anyone who has a sulfa allergy.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 214

questions.stem: Which precaution is most important to teach patients who have any type of diabetes about alcohol use?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Be sure to take your antidiabetic drug or drugs 15 minutes before drinking any alcohol.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Only drink alcohol right before bedtime because it will make you drowsy.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Increase your water intake whenever you drink alcohol.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Only drink alcohol with a meal or shortly after a meal.”

questions.rationale: Drinking alcoholic beverages leads to hypoglycemia. To prevent hypoglycemia, alcohol consumption should be limited to no more than one serving per day and should be taken either with a meal or very shortly after a meal.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 223

questions.stem: What is the most important action to perform before administering a premeal short-acting insulin to a patient with type 1 diabetes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Looking up the time the long-acting insulin was administered

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assessing the body area in which the last insulin dose was injected

choices.label: C
choices.text: Checking whether the patient’s meal or snack is already on the unit

choices.label: D
choices.text: Holding the dose if the patient’s blood glucose level is under 110 mg/dL

questions.rationale: The most critical adverse reaction of any insulin, especially short-acting insulin, is hypoglycemia. The patient should eat a meal or snack within 15 minutes of the insulin injection. To prevent hypoglycemia, it is best not to give insulin until food is actually on the unit and available to the patient. A delay in eating could result in severe injury. The purpose of a premeal injection of short-acting insulin is to make sure the next blood glucose measurement is normal. Therefore, even if the patient’s blood glucose level is under 110 mg/dL, the premeal dose is still given.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 216

questions.stem: When teaching a patient about injection site selection and rotation, and how to self-inject insulin, which statement made by the patient indicates that clarification is needed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “The abdominal site is best because it is closest to the pancreas.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I can reach my thigh the best, so I will use different areas of the same thigh.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “By rotating the sites within one area, my chance of having tissue damage is less.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “If I change injection sites from the thigh to the arm, the rate the insulin is absorbed may be different.”

questions.rationale: The abdominal site has the fastest rate of absorption because of the blood vessels in the area and not because of its proximity to the pancreas. It is a good site to use because most patients have sufficient subcutaneous tissue in the area and the patient can easily see and reach it.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 217

questions.stem: A patient is receiving an injection of Humulin 70/30 at 7:00 a.m. At what time should you expect the peak action for this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Between 7:30 a.m. and 8:00 a.m.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Between 9:00 a.m. and 11:00 a.m.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Between 1:00 p.m. and 5:00 p.m.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Between 4:00 p.m. and 11:00 p.m.

questions.rationale: Humulin 70/30 contains 70% human insulin isophane and 30% human regular insulin. It is classified as “intermediate-acting” and has a peak action time between 6 and 10 hours.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 214

questions.stem: Which patient taking insulin is at greatest risk for developing hypoglycemia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 38-year-old who is pregnant with twins

choices.label: B
choices.text: 50-year-old who drinks 6 to 8 cups of coffee daily

choices.label: C
choices.text: 55-year-old who takes hormone replacement therapy for menopausal symptoms

choices.label: D
choices.text: 70-year-old who also takes warfarin (Coumadin) daily for atrial fibrillation

questions.rationale: Older adults are at greater risk for hypoglycemia while on insulin therapy. In addition, warfarin increases the hypoglycemic response.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 219

questions.stem: With which patient should you be prepared to administer glucagon as a drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 60-year-old with type 2 diabetes who has renal impairment

choices.label: B
choices.text: 42-year-old who has a blood glucose level of 25 mg/dL

choices.label: C
choices.text: 28-year-old who has type 2 diabetes and is in diabetic ketoacidosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: 10-year-old with a fasting blood glucose of 87 mg/dL

questions.rationale: As a hormone or a drug glucagon has actions that are opposite insulin. It is given when hypoglycemia is severe to convert liver glycogen into glucose and release into the blood to raise the blood glucose level.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF:  p. 211

questions.stem: After gently rotating a vial of isophane insulin NPH (Humulin N), you note that the drug has a cloudy appearance. What step should you take before administering it to a patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Shaking the vial for 30 seconds before using it.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Placing the vial in warm water to clarify the drug.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Discarding the current vial and opening a new one.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rechecking the dosage and drawing it into the syringe.

questions.rationale: Isophane insulin NPH is normally cloudy and does not clarify. If no particles are present, it is ready to use after gently rotating the vial to mix the drug. Insulin vials should never be shaken because this creates bubbles that interfere with obtaining the correct dosage.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 216

questions.stem: Why should you avoid aspirating the syringe before injecting an insulin dose?

choices.label: A
choices.text: There are no blood vessels in subcutaneous tissue.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The needle is too thin to penetrate the wall of a vein.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tissue can be damaged by pulling it into the needle.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aspirating blood into the syringe affects insulin activity.

questions.rationale: Blood vessels in subcutaneous tissue are small and it is not necessary to check for a blood return. Pulling tissue back into the needle can cause bruising and other tissue damage.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Understanding   REF:  p. 217

questions.stem: When administering insulin by subcutaneous injection to a very thin patient, which adjustment in injection technique should you use to reduce the risk for complications?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Selecting a 30-gauge needle rather than a 28-gauge needle

choices.label: B
choices.text: Switching the injection site to the thigh rather than the abdomen

choices.label: C
choices.text: Applying pressure for at least 5 minutes after removing the needle

choices.label: D
choices.text: Placing the needle at a 45-degree angle rather than at a 90-degree angle

questions.rationale: A very thin patient has a thinner layer of subcutaneous tissue anywhere in the body compared with a patient of normal weight. Injecting at a 90-degree angle could penetrate through the thin patient’s subcutaneous tissue and allow the drug to enter a muscle, which could then be absorbed too rapidly.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 217

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed to take insulin glargine once per day and regular insulin four times per day. The first dose of regular insulin occurs at the same time of the day as the insulin Glargine dose. Which technique do you use to administer these two drugs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Drawing up and injecting the insulin glargine first, then drawing up and injecting the dose of regular insulin.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Drawing up and injecting the insulin glargine first, waiting 1 hour, then drawing up and injecting the dose of regular insulin.

choices.label: C
choices.text: First drawing up the dose of regular insulin, then drawing up the dose of insulin glargine in the same syringe, followed by injecting the two insulins together.

choices.label: D
choices.text: First drawing up the dose of insulin glargine, then drawing up the dose of regular insulin in the same syringe, followed by injecting the two insulins together.

questions.rationale: Insulin glargine must not be diluted or mixed with any other insulin or solution. Mixing results in an unpredictable alteration in the onset of action and time to peak action. Thus even though these two drugs are to be administered at the same time, separate injections are needed.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 217

questions.stem: A patient who received 22 units of regular insulin an hour ago is now pale, sweaty, and has trembling hands, but is alert and able to talk. What is your best first action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prepare to administer intravenous (IV) glucose immediately.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check the patient’s blood glucose level immediately.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Notify the prescriber or rapid response team immediately.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Give the patient a snack containing protein and a complex carbohydrate immediately.

questions.rationale: The symptoms that the patient is experiencing are associated with hypoglycemia and that is the most likely problem, although other health issues can also cause these symptoms. Because the patient is alert and able to follow commands, IV administration of glucose is not necessary and calling the prescriber or rapid response team is not needed. It may be necessary to administer oral glucose but there is time to immediately check the patient’s blood glucose level to determine how much to give. An oral snack of protein and complex carbohydrate would not be useful for an actual hypoglycemic situation although it could prevent a later hypoglycemic episode.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 218

questions.stem: Which blood laboratory test result indicates that the patient is maintaining overall good control of his or her diabetes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hemoglobin A1c is 5.2%.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ketone levels are negative.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fasting blood glucose level is 88 mg/dL.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Glucose level immediately following a meal is 150%.

questions.rationale: Hemoglobin A1c levels reflect the amount of fluctuation of blood glucose levels for at least the last 60 days. The closer this number is to normal, the fewer episodes of hyperglycemia the patient will have experienced. Although a fasting blood glucose level of 88% and a negative blood ketone level are good, they only reflect the control a patient has experienced for the past 24 hours. Glucose levels immediately after a meal are the least accurate way to measure glucose control.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 210

questions.stem: What is the most important precaution to teach a patient who is on insulin therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Avoid sharing needles with family members.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Do not skip meals when taking insulin.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Always refrigerate your insulin.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Avoid eating carbohydrates.

questions.rationale: Although sharing needles with family members greatly increases the risk for infection and for transmission of bloodborne diseases, skipping meals while taking insulin can cause severe hypoglycemia that results in brain damage or death. Most insulins do not have to be refrigerated after opening as long as they are not exposed to high temperatures or direct sunlight. The amount of carbohydrate that a patient with diabetes eats varies with activity and control of the disease. Although they may be limited, carbohydrates should not be avoided.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 219

questions.stem: An older patient with diabetes is visually impaired and asks if syringes can be prefilled and stored for use later. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Yes, prefilled syringes can be stored for up to 3 weeks in the refrigerator in a vertical position with the needle pointing up.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Yes, prefilled syringes can be stored for up to 3 weeks in the refrigerator, placed in a horizontal position.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Insulin reacts with plastic, so prefilled syringes must be made of glass and placed in a horizontal position.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “No, insulin cannot be stored for any length of time outside of the container.”

questions.rationale: Insulin is relatively stable when stored in a cool, dry place away from light. When refrigerated, prefilled syringes are stable up to 3 weeks and should be stored in the vertical position with the needle pointing up to prevent suspended insulin particles from clogging the needle.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 224

questions.stem: A patient who has been self-injecting insulin for 10 years now has warmth, redness, and pain at the injection site. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hold the next dose of insulin and notify the prescriber as soon as possible.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Teach the patient to apply ice to the area and use a different site for injection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask how long the problem has been present and assess the patient for other symptoms of infection.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Document the response and reassure the patient that this is a common response that requires no action.

questions.rationale: These symptoms are indicators of injection site infection. Patients with diabetes are at a greater risk for any type of infection, and infections can become severe very quickly. The nurse must determine if the area is infected or just irritated. Other indicators of infection include purulent drainage, increased firmness to the touch, and possibly fever. In either case, the area is not used for insulin injection until the infection or irritation clears. If infection is suspected, the prescriber must be notified and the patient started on antibiotic therapy as soon as possible.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF:  p. 224

questions.stem: Why are oral antidiabetic drugs not effective in the management of type 1 diabetes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: These drugs are too powerful to give to children.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patients with type 1 diabetes do not produce insulin.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The oral drugs are less predictable in lowering blood glucose levels.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patients with type 1 diabetes are more likely to have allergies to these drugs.

questions.rationale: Oral antidiabetic drugs are not insulin. Most just help the patient use his or her naturally produced insulin more efficiently, although some drugs also prevent glucose from entering the blood too quickly.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 219

questions.stem: How does acarbose (Precose) prevent blood glucose levels from rising too high?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It limits the intestinal enzyme that converts complex carbohydrates into glucose.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It blocks the absorption of carbohydrates and glucose in the intestines.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It enhances the binding of insulin to its membrane receptors.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It releases more insulin from pancreatic beta cells.

questions.rationale: Acarbose is an alpha-glucosidase inhibitor. Drugs from this class work by slowing the digestion of dietary starches and other carbohydrates by inhibiting an enzyme that breaks them down into glucose. The result of this action is that blood glucose does not rise as far or as fast after a meal.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Understanding   REF:  p. 220

questions.stem: When teaching a patient newly diagnosed with type 2 diabetes who is prescribed to take miglitol (Glyset) as antidiabetic therapy, which instruction is most important?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Rotate the injection site on a weekly basis.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Take this drug with the first bite of your meal.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Do not mix this drug in the same syringe as insulin.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Be sure to take this drug at the usual time even if your meal is delayed.”

questions.rationale: Miglitol is an oral antidiabetic drug from the alpha-glucosidase inhibitor class. Its action is to slow the digestion of starches and other carbohydrates in the intestinal tract. In order to be effective, it must be taken at the beginning of a meal.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 221

questions.stem: A patient with type 2 diabetes and heart failure who is prescribed metformin extended release (Glucophage XR) once daily now has muscle aches, drowsiness, low blood pressure, and a slow irregular heartbeat. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assess the patient’s blood glucose level and prepare to administer intravenous (IV) glucose.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assess the patient’s blood glucose level and prepare to administer insulin.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hold the dose and notify the prescriber immediately.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administer the drug at bedtime to prevent falls.

questions.rationale: The muscle aches, drowsiness, low blood pressure, and a slow irregular heartbeat are symptoms of lactic acidosis, an adverse reaction to metformin. The drug should be stopped and the prescriber notified so that steps can be taken to reduce the acidosis.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 222

questions.stem: By which action do the sodium-glucose cotransport inhibitors lower blood glucose levels?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inhibiting the conversion of glucose to glycogen in the liver

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inhibiting the conversion of glycogen to glucose in the liver

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increasing the sensitivity of insulin receptors to insulin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increasing the renal excretion of filtered glucose

questions.rationale: The sodium-glucose cotransport inhibitors inhibit the reabsorption of filtered glucose in the kidney so that more is removed from the body in the urine. It has no action on liver glucose or glycogen, and does not increase insulin receptor sensitivity.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 220

questions.stem: A patient newly diagnosed with type 2 diabetes who is prescribed rosiglitazone (Avandia) has all the following health problems. For which problem do you check with the prescriber to make certain rosiglitazone is an appropriate drug choice?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Severe asthma for the last 3 years

choices.label: B
choices.text: Myocardial infarction 1 year ago

choices.label: C
choices.text: Uncontrolled hypertension

choices.label: D
choices.text: Glaucoma

questions.rationale: Rosiglitazone carries a black box warning because it can cause heart failure in some patients. Patients most at risk are those who have had a previous myocardial infarction. Although heart failure can worsen asthma, it is not worsened by rosiglitazone, nor does the drug cause hypertension or glaucoma.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 222

questions.stem: Which blood laboratory test result is most important to check before administering the first prescribed dose of miglitol (Glyset) to a patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: International normalized ratio (INR)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) level

choices.label: C
choices.text: White blood cell (WBC) count

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)

questions.rationale: Miglitol is an alpha-glucosidase inhibitor. Drugs from this class can cause liver impairment. They should not be prescribed for anyone who already had a change in liver function. A common test of liver function is blood levels of lactate dehydrogenase. Normal lactate dehydrogenase levels range between 115 and 225 IU/L.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 224

questions.stem: What precaution do you teach a patient who is prescribed to take an oral sulfonylurea agent to maintain control of blood glucose levels?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Change positions slowly.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Stop taking this drug at the first sign of an infection.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Avoid taking nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Do not skip a dose of the drug even if you are unable to eat.”

questions.rationale: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) potentiate the hypoglycemic effects of sulfonylurea agents. Patients should avoid these drugs altogether or use them with great caution.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 224

questions.stem: A patient who had been taking the oral antidiabetic agents glyburide and metformin is now prescribed only Glucovance. The patient asks why only one drug is needed. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Glucovance is a new oral insulin.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Your diabetes is better and only one drug is needed.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “This new drug is more effective than either glyburide or metformin.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Glucovance contains a combination of both glyburide and metformin.”

questions.rationale: Glucovance is composed of glyburide and metformin in commonly used dosages to increase the convenience of antidiabetic therapy with both drugs.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 225

questions.stem: A patient with type 2 diabetes is prescribed nateglinide (Starlix). Which statement indicates that the patient has a correct understanding of this therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I will try not to chew or break this tablet.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “For best results, I need to take this drug right before I eat.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “If this drug causes nausea, I will take it just before I go to bed.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “If I miss a meal, I will take this drug anyway at its regularly scheduled time.”

questions.rationale: Nateglinide causes beta cells of the pancreas to undergo depolarization and release a small amount of preformed insulin. The peak action occurs in about 20 minutes after ingestion. To have the best action and prevent hypoglycemia, patients are instructed to take the drug within 15 minutes before eating.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 220

questions.stem: A patient who usually takes metformin (Glucophage) was switched to insulin immediately following an angiogram. Which patient finding indicates it is safe for the patient to switch back to metformin?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 24-hour urine output greater than 2 L

choices.label: B
choices.text: International normalized ratio is now less than 1.2

choices.label: C
choices.text: Weight is unchanged from that of 1 month ago

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fasting blood glucose level was less than 90 mg/dL for 2 days in a row

questions.rationale: Metformin can cause renal impairment when the patient is also exposed to radio-opaque dyes and some anesthetic agents. This can happen up to 48 hours after the exposure. The drug can be restarted after 48 hours if the kidney function, as measured by an adequate urine output, is normal.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 222

questions.stem: A patient who is prescribed glyburide (DiaBeta) asks if having a glass of wine once a week is okay. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Insulin activity is dramatically reduced under the influence of alcohol, and drinking even one glass of wine will increase your insulin requirements.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Diabetes reduces your kidney function, so you should avoid ingesting alcohol in all forms at all times.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “You should not drink any alcohol as it is likely to increase your sense of hunger and make you overeat.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “One glass of wine can be ingested with a meal on occasion and cause no problems.”

questions.rationale: Under normal circumstances, blood glucose levels will not be affected by moderate use of alcohol when diabetes is well controlled. When using insulin, two alcoholic beverages for men and one for women can be ingested in addition to the normal meal plan. Because alcohol can induce hypoglycemia, it should be ingested with or shortly after a meal. Although alcohol can lead to or worsen liver problems and should not be taken with drugs that affect the liver, glyburide does not appear to cause liver problems.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 223

questions.stem: A patient who has been taking metformin (Glucophage) for a year is seen in the clinic and has all of the following laboratory results. Which result do you report to the prescriber immediately?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) 38 mg/dL

choices.label: B
choices.text: Red blood cell (RBC) count 4.1 million/mm3

choices.label: C
choices.text: Random blood glucose level 135 mg/dL

choices.label: D
choices.text: Alkaline phosphatase 40 IU/L

questions.rationale: BUN levels are one measure of kidney health. The normal range is 10 to 20 mg/dL. An elevated BUN may indicate kidney problems or dehydration. Metformin can cause kidney problems, and the risk for lactic acidosis is increased in any patient taking metformin who has impairment of kidney function. The prescriber must be aware of the situation and explore the possibility of kidney impairment further. The drug may need to be changed to a different class of antidiabetic drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 222

questions.stem: Which fasting blood glucose level indicates that the antidiabetic drug prescribed for a patient with type 2 diabetes is effective at maintaining target blood glucose levels?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 40 mg/dL

choices.label: B
choices.text: 80 mg/dL

choices.label: C
choices.text: 120 mg/dL

choices.label: D
choices.text: 160 mg/dL

questions.rationale: The most common target range of fasting blood glucose levels for a patient with type 2 diabetes is 70 to 110 mg/dL. The 40 mg level represents hypoglycemia, which is not the intention of therapy and can cause neurologic problems. Levels of 120 and 160 are considered hyperglycemia.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 210

questions.stem: Which assessment precaution is most important to teach a patient who is prescribed pioglitazone (Actos) as his or her only antidiabetic drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Measure your blood glucose level about 1 hour after you take this drug.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Note whether you experience vivid dreams or nightmares.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check your urine daily for a pinkish color.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Weigh yourself daily and keep a record.

questions.rationale: Pioglitazone is a drug from the thiazolidinedione class. Drugs from this class are associated with heart failure. An early symptom of heart failure is fluid retention leading to weight gain. A weight gain of more than 3 lb in 1 week is an indication of fluid retention.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 222

questions.stem: Why are sulfonylurea oral antidiabetic drugs not recommended for women who are breastfeeding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They increase the risk of the infant developing type 2 diabetes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They can lower the infant’s blood glucose to dangerous levels.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They can cause the infant to develop an allergy to sulfa drugs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are not eliminated by the infant’s immature intestinal system.

questions.rationale: None of the oral antidiabetic drugs should be used by breastfeeding women because they enter the breast milk and induce side effects in the infant. Those drugs that cause hypoglycemia, such as the sulfonylureas, are especially dangerous to the infant and can lead to permanent brain damage.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 224

questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed sitagliptin (Januvia) calls the clinic and reports swelling of the face, lips, and tongue. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Do not worry, this is a common side effect of the drug and does not require any changes.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Take only half the drug dose and see the prescriber within the next week.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Apply cold compresses to the affected areas and take an aspirin.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Stop taking the drug and call 911.”

questions.rationale: The patient has symptoms of angioedema, an allergic reaction to the drug. The swelling can continue and eventually block the patient’s airway. This is an emergency situation and the patient needs immediate attention. Once this reaction occurs with a drug, the patient should not take the drug or any other drug from the same class again.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 224

questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed exenatide (Byetta) reports a weight loss of 11 lb in the last month. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hold the next dose and contact the prescriber immediately.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the patient to keep a daily diary of all foods eaten for the next month.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reassure the patient that this is an expected effect of the drug and no action is needed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Instruct the patient to skip one dose of the drug per day and increase his or her daily calories.

questions.rationale: The actions of exenatide include reducing the sensation of hunger and slowing the rate that food leaves the stomach. Both of these actions help the patient eat less and lose weight, a desired outcome for patients with type 2 diabetes. An 11 lb weight loss in 1 month is not excessive and no action needs to be taken at this time.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: pp. 222-223 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed repaglinide (Prandin) 3 mg. The drug available is repaglinide 0.5 mg/tablet. How many tablets do you administer to the patient for a correct dose? tablet(s)
questions.rationale: Want 3 mg, Have 0.5 mg/tablet. 3/0.5 = 6  1 tablet = 6 tablets.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 220 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: A patient is to receive 15 units of NPH insulin and 20 units of regular insulin in the same syringe. In what order should you perform the steps to prepare this injection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Make sure the syringe is free from air bubbles.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check the syringe for a volume of insulin that is 35 units.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Clean the rubber stoppers of each bottle with separate alcohol swabs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Check to make sure the concentration and types of insulin are correct.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Draw up 20 units of air and inject it into the regular insulin (short-acting insulin) bottle with the bottle in its normal, upright position.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Draw up 15 units of air and inject it into the NPH bottle with the bottle in its normal, upright position. g Place the same needle with the syringe attached into the NPH bottle, invert the bottle, and withdraw 15 units of NPH insulin into the same syringe with the regular insulin. h Without removing the needle, turn the bottle upside down and withdraw 20 units of regular insulin, then withdraw the needle from the bottle.

questions.stem: D
C
F
E
H
A
G
B
correct_answers: NPH INSULIN AND REGULAR INSULIN ARE COMPATIBLE AND CAN BE GIVEN IN THE SAME SYRINGE. NPH
questions.rationale: insulin already contains some regular insulin but regular insulin contains no NPH insulin. Insulin is a high-alert drug and the insulin types and concentrations must be carefully checked to prevent a medication error. The tops of both vials are cleaned separately to reduce contamination with microorganisms. The amount of air injected into each vial is equal to the amount of drug to be withdrawn from the vial. Always inject the air into the intermediate-acting insulin bottle first, then inject the correct air volume into the regular insulin vial. To prevent contaminating the regular insulin, it is drawn up first after air has been injected into both vials. Drawing up the regular insulin right after injecting air into the regular insulin vial reduces the number of times the vial is penetrated. Checking for air bubbles and eliminating them before drawing up the next insulin helps ensure an accurate dose. Drawing up the NPH insulin second prevents the accidental contamination of regular insulin with NPH insulin. Checking that the volume of insulin matches the volume of each insulin added together helps ensure an accurate dose.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 217, Box 13-4 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: Which mineral is critical for the thyroid to use in making thyroid hormones?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lithium

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sodium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chloride

choices.label: D
choices.text: Iodine

questions.rationale: Thyroid hormones (TH) are made from the amino acid tyrosine and the mineral iodine. For the thyroid gland to make correct amounts of TH, the diet must contain enough protein and iodine.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 228

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: Which symptom of hyperthyroidism occurs only with the Graves’ disease form of the problem?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Goiter

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypertension

choices.label: C
choices.text: Exophthalmos

choices.label: D
choices.text: Thinning scalp hair

questions.rationale: Exophthalmos is a bulging or protrusion of the eyes, causing blurred vision. This problem is unique to hyperthyroidism because the disorder causes tissue behind the eye to swell, pushing the eyeball forward.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 232

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: Which term means the energy use of each cell and the work performed by the body?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Goiter

choices.label: B
choices.text: Menarche

choices.label: C
choices.text: Metabolism

choices.label: D
choices.text: Thyrotoxicosis

questions.rationale: The term metabolism means the energy use of each cell and the work performed by the body. The thyroid gland has great influence over all of metabolism. Menarche is the time when a young girl begins menstruating. A goiter is a distinct swelling of the thyroid gland in the neck. Thyrotoxicosis is another name for hyperthyroidism.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 232

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: What does a goiter indicate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypothyroidism

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hyperthyroidism

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thyroid problem

choices.label: D
choices.text: Premature menopause

questions.rationale: Although a goiter is a distinct swelling of the thyroid gland and the neck, it only indicates a thyroid problem. It is associated with some forms of hypothyroidism and some forms of hyperthyroidism.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 229

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: Which problem is a side effect of excessive thyroid hormone replacement drugs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Weight gain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypertension

choices.label: C
choices.text: Peripheral neuropathy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased daytime sleepiness

questions.rationale: Hyperthyroidism increases overall metabolism and speeds up the entire cardiovascular system. The increased action of the cardiac system includes an increased heart rate, hypertension (which can become severe), and an irregular heart rate. People with hyperthyroidism lose weight rather than gain weight and often have difficulty sleeping any time of the day. It does not cause peripheral neuropathy.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 232

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: Which is the best dietary source of iodine?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Salt substitutes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Salted nuts

choices.label: C
choices.text: Saltwater fish

choices.label: D
choices.text: Salted chips

questions.rationale: The most common dietary sources of iodine are saltwater fish and table salt to which iodide is added.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 228

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: The action of which drug is known to be increased by thyroid hormone replacement drugs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Warfarin (Coumadin)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acetaminophen (Tylenol)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Diphenhydramine (Benadryl)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fiber supplements (FiberCon)

questions.rationale: Thyroid agonists used for thyroid hormone replacement therapy enhance the action of drugs that reduce blood clotting (anticoagulants), especially warfarin. Acetaminophen is a pain reliever. Diphenhydramine is an antihistamine. And fiber supplements are dietary supplements, not drugs, and are unaffected by thyroid hormone replacement therapy although their use can inhibit intestinal uptake of thyroid agonists.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 230

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: Which drug suppresses thyroid activity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Methimazole (Tapazole)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Metolazone (Mykrox)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Levothyroxine (Synthroid)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Liothyronine (Cytomel)

questions.rationale: Levothyroxine and liothyronine are thyroid hormone agonists and increase metabolism. Metolazone is a diuretic, not a thyroid suppression agent.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 232

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
