questions.stem: Which statement about adrenal cortex hormones is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: When underproduced, these hormones can lead to high blood pressure and stroke.

choices.label: B
choices.text: When overproduced, these hormones can cause cancer.

choices.label: C
choices.text: These hormones differ between men and women.

choices.label: D
choices.text: These hormones are essential for life.

questions.rationale: Hormones from the adrenal cortex, especially the glucocorticoids, are essential for life. They regulate the stress response; carbohydrate, protein, and fat metabolism; immune function; sodium and water balance; and excitability of heart muscle cells. If these hormones are not present in sufficient amounts or are not provided as drugs, death will occur.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 234

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: Which drug belongs to the mineralocorticoid class?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aminoglutethimide (Cytadren)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fludrocortisone (Florinef)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cortisol (Prednisone)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mitotane (Lysodren)

questions.rationale: Fludrocortisone is a drug used for mineralocorticoid replacement and raises blood glucose levels as well as increasing blood sodium levels and decreasing blood potassium levels. Cortisol is a glucocorticoid. Aminoglutethimide and mitotane are adrenal gland suppressants.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 235

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: Which side effects are most common when taking any drug to manage adrenal gland hyperfunction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased urine output and increased sweating

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blurred vision and dry mouth

choices.label: C
choices.text: Headache and mouth sores

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nausea/vomiting

questions.rationale: The most common side effects for any drug used to manage adrenal gland function are nausea/vomiting. They also cause many other gastrointestinal upsets. They are not associated with blurred vision, dry mouth, headache, mouth sores, or increased sweating. Urine output is usually increased, not decreased.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 236 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: Laboratory blood tests show that a patient has a T3 level of 190 ng/dL. How should you interpret this finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypothyroidism

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hyperthyroidism

choices.label: C
choices.text: Normal thyroid function

choices.label: D
choices.text: Thyroid function totally absent

questions.rationale: The normal level for T3 (triiodothyronine) ranges between 70 and 205 ng/dL.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: pp. 228-229

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: A patient with hypothyroidism as a result of Hashimoto’s thyroiditis asks how long thyroid replacement drugs will need to be taken. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “You will need to take the thyroid replacement drug until the goiter is completely gone.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “You will need thyroid replacement drugs for the rest of your life because thyroid function will not return.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “The thyroiditis will be cured with antibiotics and then you will no longer need the thyroid replacement drugs.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “When your thyroid function studies indicate a normal blood level of thyroid hormones you will be able to discontinue the drugs.”

questions.rationale: Hashimoto’s thyroiditis results in a permanent loss of thyroid function. Without continuous thyroid replacement drugs, the patient’s metabolism would eventually stop and death would occur.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 229

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: Which assessment is most important to perform before giving a patient who is prescribed levothyroxine (Synthroid) the first dose of the drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Measuring heart rate and rhythm

choices.label: B
choices.text: Determining level of consciousness

choices.label: C
choices.text: Asking about an allergy to “sulfa” drugs

choices.label: D
choices.text: Checking intake and output for the last 24 hours

questions.rationale: The side effects and adverse effects of thyroid hormone replacement drugs increase metabolic rate and cardiac activity. Checking heart rate and rhythm before giving the drug provides a baseline to determine whether or not the drug is working correctly or is causing an overdose effect.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 230

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: A patient is prescribed levothyroxine 25 mcg by IV push. The drug on hand is levothyroxine sodium 100 mcg/mL. How many milliliters should you administer?
questions.rationale: Want 25 mcg/X mL, Have 100 mcg/1 mL; 25/100 = 0.25  1 mL = 0.25 mL.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 230

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: A patient is prescribed levothyroxine sodium (Synthroid) 25 mcg orally once a day. The pharmacy sends liothyronine sodium (Cytomel) 25 mcg. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Give the drug as sent from the pharmacy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Contact the nurse manager for advice.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Call the pharmacy to send the correct drug.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Explain to the patient that both drugs are thyroid hormone agonists.

questions.rationale: Never substitute one type or brand of thyroid hormone replacement drug with another. Drug strengths vary (e.g., liothyronine is four times as potent as levothyroxine), and patient responses vary.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 230

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: A patient who is prescribed liothyronine (Cytomel) also takes warfarin (Coumadin). Which laboratory blood test result indicates a possible drug interaction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hematocrit 42%

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hemoglobin 14.2 g/dL

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) 12 mg/dL

choices.label: D
choices.text: International normalized ratio (INR) 4.5

questions.rationale: Liothyronine increases the effectiveness of warfarin. Warfarin’s effectiveness is measured by the INR. This value should be between 1.5 and 3.0 in a patient taking warfarin. With an INR level of 4.5 the patient is at extreme risk for excessive bleeding.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: pp. 233-234

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: A patient who has been taking levothyroxine (Synthroid) for 2 months has all of the following conditions. Which condition indicates that the drug therapy is effective?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased thirst

choices.label: B
choices.text: Daily bowel movement for 1 week

choices.label: C
choices.text: White blood cell count of 6000/mm3

choices.label: D
choices.text: Weight unchanged for the last 3 weeks

questions.rationale: Hypothyroidism decreases GI motility significantly. One of the parameters that can be used to determine if changes in the dose of a thyroid replacement drug should be adjusted is the frequency of bowel movements. A bowel movement everyday is a strong indication that the dose of thyroid replacement hormone is adequate.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 230

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: Which precaution is most important to teach a patient who is newly prescribed a thyroid hormone replacement drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Avoid taking fiber supplements while on this drug.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Be sure to take this drug at the same time everyday.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Weigh yourself daily and keep a record of these weights.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Do not increase the dose without your prescriber’s knowledge.”

questions.rationale: Although all instructions are appropriate when a patient begins treatment with thyroid replacement drugs, the most important instruction is D. The starting drug dosage is very low, with no attempt to bring the blood thyroid hormone level back to normal quickly. These drugs can change metabolism quickly and lead to adverse effects such as heart attack or seizure. Dosages start low and are raised very slowly (about every 3 weeks). The patient should never increase the dose unless instructed to do so by the prescriber.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 231

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: Why should pregnant patients who have hypothyroidism continue to take thyroid replacement drugs throughout pregnancy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: These drugs have a low likelihood of increasing the risks for birth defects.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Without continuing thyroid replacement drugs, the pregnancy may not continue.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pregnancy is a high metabolism condition and less drug is needed but should still be taken.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The fetus produces thyroid hormones that help supplement the pregnant woman’s hormone level, but minimal replacement is needed during pregnancy.

questions.rationale: The thyroid gland secretes hormones that are necessary for life. Not taking the drug can lead to problems with the pregnancy and with the fetus. The pregnant woman often needs a higher dose of the drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 231

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: How do thyroid replacement drugs work to treat hypothyroidism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Forcing the thyroid gland to secrete more thyroid hormones

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inhibiting the enzymes that break down thyroid hormones

choices.label: C
choices.text: Preventing damage to thyroid endocrine cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: Providing thyroid hormones

questions.rationale: Hypothyroidism is low thyroid functioning, which is demonstrated by very low production of thyroid hormones. Thyroid replacement drugs are thyroid hormone agonists. Taking these drugs provides an exogenous source of thyroid hormones.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 229

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: How do thyroid replacement drugs affect diabetes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thyroid replacement drugs reduce the effectiveness of all antidiabetic drugs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: When thyroid replacement drugs return metabolism to normal, diabetes is gone.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thyroid replacement drugs can allow a patient to switch from insulin to an oral antidiabetic drug.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Thyroid replacement drugs require that a patient taking an oral antidiabetic drug be placed on insulin.

questions.rationale: Thyroid hormone replacement drugs change the effectiveness of insulin and other drugs for diabetes, and often these drugs need to be increased to prevent high blood glucose levels (hyperglycemia). Patients with diabetes and hypothyroidism should be taught to check blood glucose levels more frequently.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 231

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: A patient is prescribed levothyroxine (Synthroid) and a fiber supplement. What key point would you be sure to teach the patient about these drugs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Take the drugs together as there are no interactions between them.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Be sure to take the fiber supplement 2 hours before a meal and the levothyroxine 3 hours after a meal.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Take the fiber supplement in the morning with breakfast and the levothyroxine in the evening before you go to bed.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Do not take the drugs together because a fiber supplement will decrease the absorption of your levothyroxine.”

questions.rationale: Taking a thyroid hormone agonist drug with food or with a fiber supplement reduces the absorption of the drug. Teach patients to take it 2 to 3 hours before a meal or taking a fiber supplement or at least 3 hours after a meal or taking the supplement.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 231

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: Which patient condition indicates that drug therapy for hypothyroidism is effective?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient is thirsty.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient’s weight has been the same for 3 weeks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient’s total white blood cell count is 6000 cells/mm3.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient has had a daily bowel movement for the past 8 days.

questions.rationale: With hypothyroidism all body functions are slow. Constipation is always present and may be severe. Having a daily bowel movement indicates a positive response to therapy for hypothyroidism.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 233

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: Which statement made by a patient who is taking a thyroid replacement drug indicates that a higher dose may be needed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “My goiter seems to be smaller.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I seem to feel the heat more than other people.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Food just doesn’t taste good without a lot of salt.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I am always tired, even when I get 10 or 12 hours of sleep.”

questions.rationale: When blood thyroid hormone levels are insufficient to maintain a normal metabolic rate, symptoms of hypothyroidism return. Patients usually feel tired or weak and often report an increase in time spent sleeping, sometimes up to 14 to 16 h/day.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 230

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: A patient with hyperthyroidism is in the hospital waiting for surgery to remove the thyroid gland. This patient has all of the following conditions. For which condition should you notify the prescriber immediately?

choices.label: A
choices.text: High level of anxiety about having surgery on the neck

choices.label: B
choices.text: Temperature increase of 2  F

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pulse pressure decrease of 10 mm Hg

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased bowel sounds and flatulence

questions.rationale: A complication of severe hyperthyroidism is thyroid crisis (also known as thyroid storm). Symptoms include fever, dangerously high blood pressure, and a rapid, irregular heartbeat. The symptoms can develop quickly, and if not treated, this problem can lead to seizures, heart failure, and death. An elevation in temperature may be an indication of impending thyroid crisis and requires immediate attention.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 232

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: Why does a patient who is taking drugs for thyroid suppression continue to have symptoms of hyperthyroidism for 3 weeks (or longer) after drug therapy is started?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thyroid hormones are stored and thyroid suppressing drugs only stop the synthesis of new hormones.

choices.label: B
choices.text: At first, the patient’s metabolism is so high that the thyroid cells continue to synthesize thyroid hormones.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The activity of the intestinal tract is increased and the drug is eliminated before complete suppression can occur.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The side effects of thyroid suppression drugs are so uncomfortable that most patients do not take the full dose of the drug that is needed to prevent further hormone secretion.

questions.rationale: Thyroid-suppressing drugs enter the thyroid gland and combine with the enzyme responsible for connecting iodine (iodide) with tyrosine to make active thyroid hormones, preventing further thyroid hormone synthesis. These drugs do not affect the hormones already formed and stored in the thyroid gland, so it may take as long as 3 or 4 weeks for the person to use up all the thyroid hormones made before the drug was started. Until that time, the person continues to have symptoms of hyperthyroidism.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 232

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: A patient who has been taking methimazole (Tapazole) for 3 weeks has all of the following laboratory blood test results. Which result do you report immediately to the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: White blood cell count 3500 cells/mm3

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lactate dehydrogenase 990 IU/L

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sodium 132 mEq/L

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hematocrit 32%

questions.rationale: All of these blood values are abnormal; however, only the lactate dehydrogenase level is very high (four times normal). This value indicates liver impairment. Methimazole can cause liver impairment. This value must be reported immediately so that liver function can be explored and any needed changes in drug therapy made before irreversible liver damage occurs.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 233

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: With which patient should you question the order for methimazole (Tapazole)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 10-year-old girl who has new onset hyperthyroidism

choices.label: B
choices.text: 30-year-old woman who also takes oral contraceptives

choices.label: C
choices.text: 50-year-old man who has active hepatitis B

choices.label: D
choices.text: 60-year-old man who has an enlarged prostate gland

questions.rationale: Methimazole is toxic to the liver and should not be used in a person who has any type of liver disorder or impairment. Hepatitis B, especially during an active phase, significantly reduces liver function and makes the liver at great risk for other damage.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 233

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: A patient who is prescribed propylthiouracil (Propacil) also takes warfarin (Coumadin). Which laboratory blood test result indicates a possible drug interaction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hematocrit 42%

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hemoglobin 14.2 g/dL

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) 12 mg/dL

choices.label: D
choices.text: International normalized ratio (INR) 5.0

questions.rationale: Propylthiouracil increases the effectiveness of warfarin. Warfarin’s effectiveness is measured by the INR. This value should be between 1.5 and 3.0 in a patient taking warfarin. With an INR level of 4.5 the patient is at extreme risk for excessive bleeding.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: pp. 233-234

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: Which precaution is important to teach an older patient who is prescribed methimazole (Tapazole)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “If you miss a dose, go to the nearest emergency department and get an injection of methimazole.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Be sure to wear sunscreen and a hat whenever you go outside into the sunshine.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Drink at least 3 L of fluid daily while taking this drug.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Avoid contact with crowds or anyone who is ill.”

questions.rationale: The older adult’s resistance to infection is already lower than a younger adult’s because of age- related changes that occur in the immune system. The decreased bone marrow activity caused by methimazole makes this problem worse, greatly increasing the older adult’s risk for infection.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 234

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: Which new assessment finding in a patient taking fludrocortisone (Florinef) indicates the dose may be too high?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Serum potassium change from 5.2 to 4.4 mEq/L

choices.label: B
choices.text: Heart rate decrease from 88 to 76 beats/min

choices.label: C
choices.text: Crackles in the bases of the lungs

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased taste sensation

questions.rationale: Fludrocortisone causes an increase in fluid and sodium reabsorption and potassium excretion. Congestive heart failure is a possible outcome of this therapy. Crackles in the bases of the lungs can occur along with heart failure when too much water and sodium are reabsorbed.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 235

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: With which patient is an order for mifepristone (Korlym) most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 62-year-old patient who has adrenal gland hypofunction and breast cancer

choices.label: B
choices.text: 52-year-old patient who has adrenal gland hyperfunction and type 2 diabetes

choices.label: C
choices.text: 42-year-old patient who has adrenal gland hypofunction and type 1 diabetes

choices.label: D
choices.text: 32-year-old who has adrenal gland hyperfunction and is 4 months pregnant

questions.rationale: Mifepristone works to control adrenal gland hyperfunction by blocking corticosteroid receptors, which results in inhibiting the action of cortisol. It is approved for use only in people who have type 2 diabetes along with adrenal gland hyperfunction. Because it can cause pregnancy loss, it should not be given to a pregnant woman except to induce an abortion.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 236

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: Which precaution is most important to teach a patient taking fludrocortisone (Florinef)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Weigh yourself daily and keep a record.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Avoid aspirin and aspirin-containing drugs.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Increase your intake of carbohydrates while taking this drug.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Do not take prednisone or prednisolone while taking this drug.”

questions.rationale: Fludrocortisone is a mineralocorticoid that increases sodium and water reabsorption. It can lead to congestive heart failure. Monitoring daily weights is a great way to assess whether congestive heart failure may be developing.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF:  p. 235 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: Which are intended responses of thyroid hormone agonist drugs? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Body temperature is normal.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Activity level is decreased.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vital signs are normal.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Body weight is maintained.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Bowel movements occur at least twice a week.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Patient is mentally alert.

questions.rationale: Intended responses of thyroid hormone agonist drugs include: body temperature is normal; level of activity is normal; heart rate, blood pressure, and respiratory rate are normal for the patient’s age and size; body weight is maintained when the patient takes in the amount of calories needed for his or her age, size, and activity level; the patient is mentally alert (he or she is able to remember people, places, and events from the recent and distant past); and bowel movement pattern is normal.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 230 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: A patient with hyperthyroidism is prescribed an immediate dose of propylthiouracil (Propacil) 150 mg orally. The available drug is propylthiouracil 50 mg/tablet. How many tablets should you administer? tablets
questions.rationale: Want 150 mg, Have 50 mg/tablet; 150/50 = 3; 3  1 tablet = 3 tablets.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 233

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: A 6-year-old child is being started on levothyroxine (Synthroid) intravenously and is prescribed 5 mcg/kg. The child weighs 32lb. The drug vial contains 100 mcg/mL. How many milliliters should you draw up for a correct dose?
questions.rationale: 1 kg = 2.2 lb The child’s weight in kilogram is 14.54 (32 lb/2.2 = 14.54 kg). The correct dose is 72.7 mcg (5 mcg  14.54 kg). The drug has a concentration of 100 mcg/mL. Divide the dose you want (72.7 mcg) by the dose you have (100 mcg/mL), which equals 0.727 mL. Round down to 0.7 mL.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 230

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Thyroid and Adrenal Gland Problems
questions.stem: An adult who has adrenal gland hyperfunction is prescribed to receive 2 g of mitotane (Lysodren) orally immediately. The drug on hand is mitotane 500 mg/scored tablet. How many tablets should you prepare to give?
questions.rationale: Want 2 g (2000 mg), Have 500 mg/tablet. 2000/500 = 4.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 235 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.stem: What is the main intended action of any type of diuretic drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Weight loss

choices.label: B
choices.text: Potassium loss

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased urine output

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased sense of thirst

questions.rationale: Diuretics are drugs that help rid the body of excess water and sodium by increasing a person’s urine output. They may work on the kidneys directly or may increase blood flow to the kidney. Either way, these drugs cause a person to urinate more and lose water from the body. Although potassium is often lost with a diuretic that is not a diuretic’s main intended action.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Remembering   REF:  p. 239

questions.stem: For which complication should you remain alert when a patient is taking any type of diuretic?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Loss of appetite

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bladder spasms

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypertension

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dehydration

questions.rationale: Any type of diuretic increases water loss through urination. This water loss can cause dehydration if a patient’s fluid intake does not keep pace with his or her urine output.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: pp. 240-241

questions.stem: What is the most common health problem treated by diuretic drugs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: High blood pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tongue swelling

choices.label: C
choices.text: Constipation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obesity

questions.rationale: Diuretics are used most often to treat problems when the body is retaining too much water, too much sodium, or too much potassium. They are often prescribed for people who have the following health problems: high blood pressure (hypertension), heart failure, kidney disease, and liver disease (cirrhosis).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 241

questions.stem: Which type of diuretic can cause higher than normal blood calcium levels?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Loop diuretics

choices.label: B
choices.text: Osmotic diuretics

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thiazide diuretics

choices.label: D
choices.text: Potassium-sparing diuretics

questions.rationale: Potential side effects of thiazide diuretics include fluid and electrolyte imbalances such as decreased blood volume, decreased potassium (hypokalemia), decreased sodium (hyponatremia), decreased chloride (hypochloremia), and decreased magnesium (hypomagnesemia), along with increased calcium (hypercalcemia) and increased urea (hyperuremia).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 243

questions.stem: Which class of diuretics is the most powerful?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Loop diuretics

choices.label: B
choices.text: Osmotic diuretics

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thiazide diuretics

choices.label: D
choices.text: Potassium-sparing diuretics

questions.rationale: Loop diuretics (also called “high-ceiling” diuretics) slow down or turn off the sodium pumps in the nephron tubes in a place different from thiazide diuretic action. They cause more sodium, potassium, and water to stay in the urine and leave the body through urination. Loop diuretics are the most powerful diuretics.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 244

questions.stem: Which symptom indicates too much sodium has been lost when a loop diuretic has been used?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blood pressure gets higher as the day passes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blood levels of potassium are increased every morning.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient’s urine is a light yellow color and has no perceptible odor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient becomes light-headed and dizzy when moving from a sitting to a standing position.

questions.rationale: Among the more common side effects of loop diuretics is dizziness or light-headedness when the patient moves from a sitting or lying position to a standing position. This occurs because blood pressure drops in response to the loss of fluid from the blood vessels (postural hypotension). Other signs and symptoms of low sodium include confusion, convulsions, decreased mental activity, irritability, muscle cramps, and unusual fatigue or weakness.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 244

questions.stem: Which diuretic is potassium-sparing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chlorothiazide (Oretic)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Furosemide (Lasix)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ethacrynic acid (Edecrin)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Spironolactone (Aldactone)

questions.rationale: Spironolactone is a potassium-sparing diuretic. Chlorothiazide is a thiazide diuretic; furosemide and ethacrynic acid are loop diuretics.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 246

questions.stem: Which diuretic can cause breast enlargement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Amiloride

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bumetanide

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chlorothiazide

choices.label: D
choices.text: Torsemide

questions.rationale: Amiloride (Midamor) is a potassium-sparing diuretic. A side effect in both men and women prescribed these drugs is the possible development of breast enlargement (gynecomastia in men).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 247

questions.stem: What is a common side effect of antispasmodic drugs for overactive bladder (OAB)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dry mouth

choices.label: B
choices.text: Diarrhea

choices.label: C
choices.text: Insomnia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Incontinence

questions.rationale: Frequent side effects of drugs for OAB include dry mouth, dry eyes, headache, dizziness, and constipation.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 248

questions.stem: Which side effect is associated only with loop diuretics?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dizziness

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hearing loss

choices.label: C
choices.text: Urinary frequency

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased sun sensitivity

questions.rationale: All loop diuretics are ototoxic (can cause hearing loss). No other class of diuretics is ototoxic.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 245

questions.stem: Which diuretic may cause an adverse effect of a higher than normal serum potassium level?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Spironolactone (Aldactone)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bumetanide (Bumex)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chlorothiazide (Diuril)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Furosemide (Lasix)

questions.rationale: Spironolactone (Aldactone) is a potassium-sparing diuretic that reduces the amount of potassium excreted by the kidneys. As a result, serum potassium levels can become higher than normal.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 247

questions.stem: Which laboratory value is always checked before giving a dose of furosemide (Lasix)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Calcium

choices.label: B
choices.text: Magnesium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Creatinine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Potassium

questions.rationale: Blood levels of potassium often decrease when furosemide (a loop diuretic) is administered, causing dry mouth, increased thirst, irregular heartbeat, mental and mood changes, muscle cramps or muscle pain, nausea/vomiting, tiredness, weakness, and weak pulses.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 245 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: How do antispasmodic drugs for overactive bladder work?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They reduce the urine-making action of the kidney.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They relax the muscles of the bladder.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They shrink the prostate gland.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They reduce the thirst reflex.

questions.rationale: Antispasmodic drugs relax the muscles of the bladder and decrease the spasms of the detrusor muscle.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 248

questions.stem: A patient who has been taking a diuretic for the past 2 weeks now experiences all of the following changes. Which change indicates that the diuretic is effective?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Weight loss of 7 lb

choices.label: B
choices.text: Heart rate increased from 72 to 80 beats/min

choices.label: C
choices.text: Respiratory rate decreased from 20 to 16 breaths/min

choices.label: D
choices.text: Morning blood glucose decreased from 142 to 110 mg/dL

questions.rationale: Diuretic drugs cause water loss and are often prescribed for edema. One liter of water weighs 2.2 lb. In helping the patient rid the body of excess water, the patient is expected to lose weight.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 241

questions.stem: A patient prescribed a once-daily diuretic calls the prescriber’s office to report that yesterday’s drug dose was missed. What is your best advice?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Take today’s dose now and restrict today’s fluid intake to 1 L.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Take yesterday’s dose now and take today’s dose after another 6 hours.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Take today’s dose now and maintain your normal intake of food and fluids.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Skip today’s doses of all your medications and then begin everything fresh tomorrow.”

questions.rationale: Too much time has passed to take both yesterday’s dose and today’s dose. Additional dosing would amount to doubling the dose, which could lead to more side effects and possible complications.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 242

questions.stem: You are teaching a patient about diuretic therapy. Which statement made by the patient indicates that more teaching is needed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I am so thankful that my high blood pressure has been cured by this drug.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I always try to drink just about the same amount of fluid that I urinate each day.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I will call my health care provider if my heart rate is less than 60 beats/min.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I have been taking this drug early in the day so that I don’t have to get up during the night.”

questions.rationale: Diuretics do not cure high blood pressure (hypertension), they only control the problem. If the patient stops taking the diuretic, blood pressure will increase.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 241

questions.stem: A patient taking a thiazide diuretic has the following blood laboratory values for kidney function. Which value will you report to the prescriber immediately?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sodium 136 mEq/L

choices.label: B
choices.text: Potassium 2.6 mEq/L

choices.label: C
choices.text: Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blood urea nitrogen 6 mg/dL

questions.rationale: Normal blood levels of potassium range between 3.5 and 5.0 mEq/L. The value listed here, 2.6 mEq/L, is low (hypokalemia) and can weaken the skeletal muscles of respiration. Most likely, the diuretic therapy caused the kidneys to excrete too much potassium. Although the blood urea nitrogen level is also lower than normal, it does not pose an immediate health threat. As the potassium level decreases, the patient is more at risk for cardiac dysrhythmias.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 243

questions.stem: Why should you teach a patient who is prescribed a thiazide diuretic to change positions slowly?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Moving rapidly from a standing position to a sitting position can raise blood pressure and increase the patient’s risk for a stroke.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Moving rapidly from a standing position to a sitting position can cause excess body fluids to collect in the feet and ankles increasing the patient’s risk for edema.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Moving rapidly from a sitting position to a standing position can put pressure on the bladder and increase the patient’s risk for incontinence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Moving rapidly from a sitting position to a standing position can cause blood pressure to drop and increase the patient’s risk for falling.

questions.rationale: Diuretics reduce the amount of blood in the circulatory system at any one time, lowering blood pressure. When the patient moves from a sitting position to a standing position too rapidly, blood pressure falls very quickly (orthostatic hypotension), causing too little blood to reach the brain and making the patient dizzy. This can cause the patient to faint or fall.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 243

questions.stem: You are to administer 20 mg of furosemide (Lasix) to a patient by the intravenous (IV) route. Which action is most important for you to take?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Give the drug slowly over at least 2 minutes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check the patient carefully for symptoms of low blood glucose levels.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mix the drug with potassium chloride to prevent a rapid drop in serum potassium levels.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Monitor the IV site after giving the drug because furosemide causes severe tissue damage if infiltration occurs.

questions.rationale: Furosemide is ototoxic (can reduce hearing). This effect is more likely to occur when the drug is administered intravenously at a rapid rate (faster than 10 mg/min).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 244

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed spironolactone (Aldactone). Why should you advise the patient to avoid the use of salt substitutes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They increase a patient’s risk for a high potassium level.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They can increase the patient’s risk for hypertension.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They may lead to hypokalemia.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They can cause water retention.

questions.rationale: Most salt substitutes are made by replacing sodium with potassium. Use of salt substitutes at the same time as potassium-sparing diuretics such as spironolactone increases the patient’s risk of a high potassium level (hyperkalemia).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 247

questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed amiloride (Midamor) for the past 3 months reports that she must shave her legs more frequently. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hold the next dose and notify the prescriber immediately.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Instruct the patient to stop taking oral contraceptives while she is taking this drug.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Document the response and reassure the patient that this is an expected side effect.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient whether she has noticed any changes in the thickness of her scalp hair.

questions.rationale: A common and nonharmful side effect of amiloride and other potassium-sparing diuretics is an increase in body hair (hirsutism) in women. It is not necessary to stop taking this drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 247

questions.stem: You prepare to give a second dose of furosemide (Lasix) to a patient by intravenous (IV) push. Before the injection is started, the patient reports having chest pain since the last dose of the drug. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assist the patient to lay flat and encourage him or her to take slow, deep breaths.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document the report as the only action for this expected side effect.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Slow the IV drip rate and examine the infusion site for infiltration.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hold the dose and notify the prescriber immediately.

questions.rationale: Chest pain is a serious side effect or adverse reaction to furosemide and can indicate that the patient is having a heart attack. Another dose could cause a more severe response. The prescriber should be notified about this response immediately for preventive action.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: pp. 245-246

questions.stem: A patient with overactive bladder has been prescribed tolterodine (Detrol). While assessing the patient, you discover the presence of the following health problems. For which problem should you contact the prescriber and question the drug order?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Asthma

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glaucoma

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypotension

choices.label: D
choices.text: Diabetes mellitus

questions.rationale: Tolterodine (Detrol) is an anticholinergic drug that can close the angle of the iris of the eye and decrease the outflow of aqueous fluid in the eye. For people who have closed angle glaucoma, the intraocular pressure can become even higher and the risk for blindness increases.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: pp. 248-249

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed an extended-release drug for overactive bladder. Which precaution is most important for you to teach the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Avoid taking this drug at bedtime.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Drink at least 3 L of fluid daily.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Swallow the tablet or capsule whole.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Perform a home pregnancy test monthly.”

questions.rationale: Extended-release tablets or capsules are meant to release a drug at a relatively even dose throughout the day. Chewing or crushing the drug ruins the timed-release feature and allows most of the drug dose to be absorbed at once. This can cause more side effects and limits how long the drug will be effective.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF:  p. 249

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed a urinary antispasmodic drug for overactive bladder. Which instruction should you provide to prevent a serious complication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Be sure to let your prescriber know if your symptoms do not improve.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “When using the patch, press it firmly to make sure it stays in place.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Use alcohol in moderation while taking this drug.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Avoid becoming overheated or dehydrated.”

questions.rationale: Urinary antispasmodic drugs decrease the sweating response, increasing the risk for heat stroke. Patients can reduce this risk by ensuring that they keep themselves well hydrated during exercise or when in hot environments.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 249

questions.stem: What must you teach any patient who is taking a diuretic drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Avoid foods that are rich in potassium such as bananas and broccoli.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sit on the side of the bed for 1 to 2 minutes before getting out of bed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Notify the prescriber if the heart rate is less than 70 beats/min.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Keep a record of dietary intake for a few weeks.

questions.rationale: A common side effect of diuretics is hypotension and patients should be advised to change positions slowly. Patients should also be taught the signs of hypotension such as dizziness and light-headedness.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 241

questions.stem: A hospitalized patient has received furosemide (Lasix), 40 mg orally, 30 minutes ago. To prevent injury to the patient, what should you do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assist the patient to the bathroom

choices.label: B
choices.text: Keep the patient on bed rest

choices.label: C
choices.text: Place all four side rails in the elevated position

choices.label: D
choices.text: Provide the patient with a bedpan or urinal

questions.rationale: An expected action of diuretic drugs is loss of excess fluid. This can lead to hypovolemia with signs of decreased volume including dizziness and light-headedness. To provide a safe environment for the patient, you should ensure that the patient has assistance when getting out of bed.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 241

questions.stem: A patient who is prescribed hydrochlorothiazide (HCTZ) informs you that she plans to become pregnant. What should you teach the patient about this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: This drug is safe for use during pregnancy and breastfeeding.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The prescriber will most likely decrease your dose while you are pregnant.

choices.label: C
choices.text: You may take this drug during pregnancy, but should not use it if you plan to breastfeed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: This drug should be avoided both during pregnancy and the time when you are breastfeeding.

questions.rationale: Thiazide diuretics should be avoided during pregnancy because they may cause side effects in the newborn, including jaundice and low potassium levels. Thiazide diuretics should also be avoided during breastfeeding because they pass into breast milk. The action of these drugs may decrease the flow of breast milk.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 243

questions.stem: A patient taking tolterodine (Detrol) reports decreased urination, ankle swelling, and a weight gain of 5 lb over the past 2 days. What should you do next?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hold the dose and notifies the prescriber.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check the patient’s blood pressure and heart rate.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give the next dose as prescribed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Document the finding as the only action.

questions.rationale: Adverse effects of drugs for overactive bladder include chest pain, fast or irregular heart rate, shortness of breath, swelling (edema) and rapid weight gain, confusion, and hallucinations. Additionally, these drugs may cause decreased urination or no urine output, and painful or difficult urination. The dose should be held and the prescriber notified.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 249

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed a diuretic drug twice a day. When should you advise him or her to take the last dose each day?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Around noon with lunch

choices.label: B
choices.text: Before 6:00 p.m.

choices.label: C
choices.text: At 9:00 p.m. or bedtime

choices.label: D
choices.text: Twelve hours after the first dose

questions.rationale: If a patient is taking more than one dose of a diuretic per day, the last dose should be taken no later than 6:00 p.m. to avoid frequent nighttime urination and disruption of sleep and rest.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 242

questions.stem: For which diuretic should you teach a patient to wear long sleeves, a hat, and sunscreen when going outdoors?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Furosemide (Lasix)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ethacrynic acid (Edecrin)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Torsemide (Demadex)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Spironolactone (Aldactone)

questions.rationale: A side effect of furosemide is increased sensitivity of skin to sunlight (photosensitivity), possibly with skin rash, itching, redness, or severe sunburn. Teach patients prescribed furosemide to stay out of direct sunlight, wear protective clothing, and use sun block products with a skin protection factor (SPF) of at least 15. Remind them not to use sunlamps or tanning beds.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 245

questions.stem: A patient prescribed a drug for overactive bladder (OAB) tells you that he or she regularly exercises throughout the year. What would you be sure to teach him or her about exercising during summer heat?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “When the weather is hot, you are at increased risk for heat stroke.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “During summer always exercise early in the day or after the sun goes down.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Exercise will help you lose weight which will help decrease your OAB symptoms.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Be sure to wear disposable pads to absorb any urine that leaks from your bladder while exercising.”

questions.rationale: Remind patients to avoid becoming overheated or dehydrated during exercise or hot weather. Patients may be at increased risk for heatstroke during exercise or hot weather because these drugs decrease perspiration (sweating).
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 248

questions.stem: A patient who has diabetes is now prescribed the thiazide diuretic metolazone (Zaroxolyn). In addition to the usual precautions, what else should you teach this patient about the new drug therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Always carry a simple sugar with you.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check your blood glucose level more frequently.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Be sure to restrict your fluid intake after 6:00 p.m.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tell your dentist you are taking this drug before any dental work is performed.

questions.rationale: Metolazone can cause impaired glucose tolerance, glucosuria, and hyperglycemia in patients who have diabetes. This makes the disease harder to control.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 243

questions.stem: During medication reconciliation, the patient tells you that he takes Luvox daily as a “water pill.” You note that this drug is not on his current list of ordered drugs. How should you proceed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask him what strength or dose of Luvox he takes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Request that the prescriber add Luvox to the patient’s orders.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the patient to have all his drugs brought from home to the hospital.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Check with the pharmacist to determine whether Luvox interacts with his ordered drugs.

questions.rationale: Luvox and Lasix are sound alike drugs and can easily be confused. Although this patient may indeed be prescribed to take Luvox (an antidepressant), the fact that he said he takes it as a “water pill” is a red flag. You must investigate this discrepancy further and not just make assumptions.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 244

questions.stem: Which class of drugs can increase the blood potassium level when taken with triamterene (Dyrenium)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: High-ceiling “loop” diuretics

choices.label: B
choices.text: Metabolism inhibiting antibacterials

choices.label: C
choices.text: Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nonnucleoside analogue reverse transcriptase inhibitors

questions.rationale: Drugs from the angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor class promote potassium retention. When taken along with a potassium-sparing diuretic like triamterene, blood potassium levels can become too high. Drugs from the other classes on this list do not have the potassium retention action or effect.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 247

questions.stem: Why would a child be prescribed oxybutynin (Ditropan)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To reduce bed-wetting

choices.label: B
choices.text: To prevent urinary retention

choices.label: C
choices.text: To increase elimination of urea

choices.label: D
choices.text: To decrease the risk for urinary tract infection

questions.rationale: Bed-wetting is a form of urinary incontinence that occurs at night. The major indication for the use of oxybutynin is to control incontinence.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 249 BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which are common signs and symptoms when a patient is dehydrated? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased pulse rate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Low blood pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thirst

choices.label: D
choices.text: Swollen eyeballs

choices.label: E
choices.text: Dry mouth

choices.label: F
choices.text: Skin tenting on back of hand

questions.rationale: Dehydration is a condition caused by the loss of too much water from the body. Signs and symptoms of dehydration to watch for include: increased pulse rate with a “thready” pulse that may be hard to feel, low blood pressure (hypotension), thirst, sunken appearance to the eyeballs, dry mouth with thick sticky coating on tongue, and skin “tenting” on the forehead or chest.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 241 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which are intended responses of antispasmodic drugs used to treat overactive bladder? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Urine leakage is contained in adult diapers.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Frequency of urination is decreased.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Perspiration (sweating) is increased.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Incontinence of urine is decreased.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Detrusor muscle spasm is strengthened.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Sense if urinary urgency is decreased.

questions.rationale: Intended responses of urinary antispasmodic drugs include: urinary frequency is decreased, urinary urgency is decreased, and urinary incontinence is decreased.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 248 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: A 10-year-old child is prescribed a one-time dose of oral furosemide (Lasix) 2 mg/kg. The child’s weight is 75 lb. How many milligrams will you give the child?
questions.rationale: 1 kg = 2.2 lb. The child’s weight is pounds divided by 2.2 to find the child’s weight in kilograms. 75/2.2 = 34.09 kg rounded down to 34 kg. To determine the dose multiply the child’s weight in kilograms times 2 (34  2 = 68 mg).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 244

questions.stem: The adult patient is prescribed 100 mg of hydrochlorothiazide (HCTZ [Oretic]) in 2 divided doses per day. HCTZ is available on 25 mg tablets. How many tablets per dose will you give the patient?
questions.rationale: Total dose per day is 100 mg/2 = 50 mg/dose. Tablets come in 25 mg/tablet. 50/25 = 2 tablets.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 242 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which term means high blood pressure from no known cause?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Orthostatic hypertension

choices.label: B
choices.text: Orthostatic hypotension

choices.label: C
choices.text: Primary hypertension

choices.label: D
choices.text: Secondary hypertension

questions.rationale: Primary hypertension is defined as hypertension for which there is no known cause but is associated with risk factors; 85% to 90% of cases of hypertension are of this type.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 254

questions.stem: What condition is a major cause of long-term high blood pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atherosclerosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bleeding disorders

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chronic liver disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: Diabetes insipidus

questions.rationale: High blood pressure causes arterial walls to thicken and harden. With atherosclerosis plaques are formed inside the walls of arteries. As arterial walls harden and thicken, the arteries become narrow resulting in increased blood pressure over time.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 254

questions.stem: What types of over-the-counter drugs can worsen high blood pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Drugs for asthma

choices.label: B
choices.text: Laxatives

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cortisone creams

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aspirin

questions.rationale: Patients with hypertension should not take over-the-counter drugs (such as drugs for appetite control, asthma, colds, and hay fever) without asking their prescriber. These drugs can cause worsening of high blood pressure because they all constrict blood vessels.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Remembering   REF:  p. 251

questions.stem: How do most diuretics lower blood pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Preventing clots from forming

choices.label: B
choices.text: Promoting water and sodium loss

choices.label: C
choices.text: Constricting blood vessel muscles

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reversing atherosclerosis

questions.rationale: Diuretics are drugs that decrease blood pressure by eliminating excess water and salt from the body.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 256

questions.stem: How do angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors lower blood pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Preventing clots from forming

choices.label: B
choices.text: Promoting water and potassium retention

choices.label: C
choices.text: Relaxing blood vessel muscles

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reversing atherosclerosis

questions.rationale: ACE inhibitors block production of substances that constrict (narrow) blood vessels. They also help decrease the buildup of water and salt in the blood and body tissues. The exact way that these drugs work is not known but they block an enzyme in the body that is necessary for production of angiotensin II (a substance that causes blood vessels to tighten or constrict). The result is that blood vessels relax and blood pressure is decreased.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 257

questions.stem: Which side effect of ACE inhibitors is very common?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypertension

choices.label: B
choices.text: Difficulty sleeping

choices.label: C
choices.text: Constipation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dry, hacking cough

questions.rationale: The more common side effects of ACE inhibitors include hypotension; protein in the urine; taste disturbances; increased blood potassium level (hyperkalemia); headache; and persistent, dry cough. If one ACE inhibitor causes a cough, it is likely that others will also, and the patient will need to be prescribed another type of antihypertensive drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 257

questions.stem: What is a possible life-threatening adverse effect that can occur when a person is taking an ACE inhibitor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Angioedema

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rebound hypertension

choices.label: C
choices.text: Loss of taste sensation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rapid development of glaucoma

questions.rationale: Angioedema is a diffuse swelling of the eyes, lips, and tongue. It may occur with allergic reactions to ACE inhibitors and may be life threatening. Swelling of the trachea (windpipe/airway) can interfere with breathing, a life-threatening event. Angioedema can occur months or even years after ACE inhibitor therapy is started.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 258

questions.stem: What substance should patients taking ACE inhibitors avoid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alcoholic beverages

choices.label: B
choices.text: Salt substitutes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Citrus fruits

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aspirin

questions.rationale: Teach patients to avoid salt substitutes. Salt substitutes contain potassium, and a side effect of ACE inhibitors is increased blood potassium level (hyperkalemia). Salt substitutes and ACE inhibitors together can significantly increase potassium levels.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 258

questions.stem: Which drug is an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lisinopril

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bumetanide

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ramipril

choices.label: D
choices.text: Losartan

questions.rationale: ARBs block the effects of angiotensin II (vasoconstriction, sodium, and water retention) by directly blocking the binding of angiotensin II to angiotensin II type 1 receptors. The generic names for ARBs end in “-sartan” (e.g., valsartan, losartan, candesartan).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 259

questions.stem: Which condition can be a severe adverse reaction to angiotensin II receptor blockers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Asthma

choices.label: B
choices.text: Heart failure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Kidney failure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Seizure activity

questions.rationale: Adverse effects of ARBs are rare but include kidney failure and life-threatening angioedema.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 259

questions.stem: How do calcium channel blockers help to lower blood pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Relaxing arteries and slowing heart contractions

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increasing constriction of muscles in veins

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inducing weight loss and reducing obesity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increasing water and sodium loss

questions.rationale: Calcium channel blockers block calcium from entering the muscle cells of the heart and arteries. Blocking calcium causes a decrease in the contraction of the heart and also dilates (widens) the arteries. Widening the arteries causes a decrease in blood pressure and reduces the workload of the heart.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 260

questions.stem: What side effect is common to calcium channel blockers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Diarrhea

choices.label: B
choices.text: Leg and ankle swelling

choices.label: C
choices.text: Worsening of glaucoma

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dry, hacking cough

questions.rationale: The most common side effects of calcium channel blockers are constipation, nausea, headache, flushing, rash, edema (legs, ankles), hypotension, drowsiness, and dizziness.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 261

questions.stem: Which drug for high blood pressure is a beta blocker?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Verapamil

choices.label: B
choices.text: Telmisartan

choices.label: C
choices.text: Captopril

choices.label: D
choices.text: Propranolol

questions.rationale: Beta blockers block the effects of epinephrine (adrenaline) on the heart. They decrease the heart rate and force of heart contractions, which leads to decreased blood pressure. As a result the heart does not work as hard and requires less oxygen. The generic names of beta blockers end with “- olol” (e.g., metoprolol, atenolol, propranolol).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 263

questions.stem: Which condition is a possible severe adverse effect of beta blockers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Difficulty breathing

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blood clots

choices.label: C
choices.text: Kidney failure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Worsening glaucoma

questions.rationale: Signs of beta blocker overdose include very slow heart rate, chest pain, severe dizziness or fainting, fast or irregular heart rate, difficulty breathing, bluish-colored fingernails and palms, and seizures. Report these signs and symptoms to the prescriber at once.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 263

questions.stem: How do alpha blockers work to lower blood pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Preventing clots from forming

choices.label: B
choices.text: Promoting water and potassium loss

choices.label: C
choices.text: Relaxing blood vessel muscles

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reversing atherosclerosis

questions.rationale: Alpha blockers block receptors in arteries and smooth muscle. This relaxes the blood vessels and leads to an increase in blood flow and a lower blood pressure.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 264

questions.stem: What are the most common side effects of alpha blockers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Shakiness and tremors

choices.label: B
choices.text: Leg and ankle swelling

choices.label: C
choices.text: Constipation and nausea

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dizziness and drowsiness

questions.rationale: The most common side effects of alpha blockers are dizziness, drowsiness, fatigue, headache, nervousness, irritability, stuffy or runny nose, nausea, pain in the arms and legs, hypotension, and weakness.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 265

questions.stem: Which drug acts directly on the peripheral arteries, causing them to dilate and leading to decreased blood pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Minoxidil (Loniten)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Propranolol (Inderal)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Captopril (Capoten)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Diltiazem (Cardizem)

questions.rationale: Minoxidil is a direct vasodilator that acts on peripheral arteries, causing them to dilate (widen) and leading to decreased blood pressure. Propranolol is a beta blocker, captopril is an angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor, and diltiazem is a calcium channel blocker.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 268

questions.stem: Which class of antihypertensive drugs has a higher incidence of side effects?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors

choices.label: B
choices.text: Calcium channel blockers

choices.label: C
choices.text: Alpha-beta blockers

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct vasodilators

questions.rationale: Direct vasodilators and centrally acting drugs have a higher incidence of side effects.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 269 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: A patient’s blood pressure is consistently around 138/88 mm Hg. What does this reading represent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Normal blood pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prehypertension

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stage 1 hypertension

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stage 2 hypertension

questions.rationale: There are five classifications of hypertension (see Table 16-1 in the text). This patient’s blood pressure is consistently within the range for prehypertension.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 253

questions.stem: What is the difference between primary hypertension and secondary hypertension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Secondary hypertension is not as severe as primary hypertension.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Primary hypertension usually occurs at an earlier age than secondary hypertension.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Secondary hypertension is caused by another health problem, whereas primary hypertension does not have a known cause.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Primary hypertension can be treated with antihypertensive drugs, whereas secondary hypertension does not respond to drug therapy.

questions.rationale: Although there are many factors that contribute to the development of primary hypertension (e.g., obesity, smoking, stress, unhealthy diet), it has no single cause. (Many people with hypertension risk factors do not always develop hypertension.) On the other hand, secondary hypertension results from either another health problem or from the drug treatment of another health problem.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 254

questions.stem: You are teaching a patient about antihypertensive therapy. Which statement by the patient indicates the need for more teaching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Now that my blood pressure is normal, I won’t need to take my medication anymore.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “When I take my blood pressure at home, I always try to take it at just about the same time every day.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I check the labels on cans and other food packages to be sure they do not have too much sodium.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I hope that by continuing to lose weight, I might not have to take medications to manage my high blood pressure.”

questions.rationale: Antihypertensive drugs do not cure high blood pressure, they only control it. The patient must continue to take the drug to keep blood pressure at target levels unless the factors that are increasing the blood pressure are changed or eliminated. For example, sometimes blood pressure becomes normal again when the person is no longer overweight. In addition, if another drug is causing the hypertension, like corticosteroids, and the patient no longer needs to take that drug, blood pressure can become normal again.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 255

questions.stem: Which drug action is most likely to lower blood pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increasing the effectiveness of heart pumping

choices.label: B
choices.text: Relaxing (dilating) blood vessel smooth muscle

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increasing the concentration of urine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Retaining the body’s concentration of potassium

questions.rationale: The most common physiologic change that increases blood pressure is narrowing or constricting of the small arteries. Drugs that cause blood vessels to relax or dilate are most effective at lowering blood pressure.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 256

questions.stem: Which action or precaution is most important for before giving any type of drug for hypertension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check the patient’s blood pressure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensure that the patient is in a sitting position.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check the patient’s pulse for regularity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Have the patient drink a full glass of water.

questions.rationale: Drugs that lower blood pressure can make a normal pressure too low and can make a low blood pressure worse. Checking the patient’s blood pressure before giving a drug ensures that the patient is not hypotensive at the time he or she receives the drug. Some drugs can change the body’s electrolyte concentration so much that an irregular heart rate can occur; however, all antihypertensive drugs can lower blood pressure.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 255

questions.stem: You are checking a patient’s blood pressure before administering an antihypertensive. The reading of 88/52 mm Hg. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Give the patient a cup of coffee and retake the blood pressure in 30 minutes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document the finding as the only action and administer the drug as usual.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Raise the side rails and apply oxygen by mask or nasal cannula.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hold the dose and notify the prescriber.

questions.rationale: This patient’s blood pressure is quite low. If the patient is receiving this drug because he or she has hypertension, another dose of the drug right now could make the patient’s blood pressure dangerously low. Sometimes a patient may be prescribed an antihypertensive drug for another reason. You must check with the prescriber before administering this antihypertensive drug dose.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 255

questions.stem: You give the first dose of an antihypertensive drug to a hospitalized patient. Which instruction should you give to the patient next?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Stay in bed and call for help if you need to get up for any reason.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Urinate in a container so that we can keep track of your urine output.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “If you develop a headache, we can give you some acetaminophen (Tylenol).”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “You may get up but only if you need to use the bathroom.”

questions.rationale: After taking the first dose of an antihypertensive drug, a patient may develop dizziness, light- headedness, or orthostatic hypotension. The patient should be given the call light and instructed to call for help getting up out of bed. The patient should also be instructed to change positions slowly.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 265

questions.stem: A patient prescribed a thiazide diuretic for hypertension reports having cramps and feeling like the heart is skipping beats. What side effect of this drug do you suspect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Muscle breakdown

choices.label: B
choices.text: Orthostatic hypotension

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypokalemia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dysrhythmia

questions.rationale: A side effect of both thiazide and loop diuretics is loss of potassium resulting in hypokalemia (low blood potassium). Signs of hypokalemia include cramps, sudden decrease in urination, and irregular heartbeats.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF: p. 257

questions.stem: A patient taking a thiazide diuretic has the following blood laboratory values for kidney function. Which value will you report to the prescriber immediately?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sodium 124 mEq/L

choices.label: B
choices.text: Potassium 3.6 mEq/L

choices.label: C
choices.text: Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blood urea nitrogen 16 mg/dL

questions.rationale: Normal blood levels of sodium range between 135 and 145 mEq/L. The value listed here, 124 mEq/L, is low (hyponatremia) and can weaken the skeletal muscles, increasing the risk for falls. Most likely, the diuretic therapy caused the kidneys to excrete too much sodium.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 257

questions.stem: A patient asks you how atenolol (Tenormin) will help reduce hypertension. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “It will help your body rid itself of excess fluids and decrease the work your heart must do to pump blood.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “It will help decrease built-up water and salt in your blood and tissues.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “It will block vasoconstriction as well as salt and water retention.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “It will decrease your heart rate and how hard your heart pumps.”

questions.rationale: Atenolol is a beta blocker that blocks the action of epinephrine on the heart. This results in a decreased heart rate and force of contraction, which leads to decreased blood pressure. As a result the heart does not work as hard and requires less oxygen.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 262

questions.stem: A patient taking metoprolol (Lopressor) for hypertension reports all of the following side effects. For which side effect will you notify the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased urination during the daytime

choices.label: B
choices.text: Heart rate of 68 per minute

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chest pain during exercise

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased sexual ability

questions.rationale: Chest pain is never considered normal. Chest pain should be reported to the prescriber any time a patient experiences it because this is one symptom of many serious cardiac problems such as angina or heart attack.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF: p. 264

questions.stem: Which precaution is most important for you to teach a patient who has been prescribed a beta blocker drug for hypertension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Avoid alcoholic beverages while taking this drug.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Weigh yourself daily at the same time every morning.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Wear gloves and other warm clothing during cold weather.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Do not suddenly stop taking this drug without notifying your prescriber.”

questions.rationale: Beta blocker therapy increases the number of beta-adrenergic receptors in the heart and blood vessels. If these drugs are suddenly stopped, there are more beta receptors present and the patient’s own adrenaline (epinephrine and norepinephrine) can bind to these receptors and cause dangerously high blood pressure.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF: p. 264

questions.stem: A 38-year-old patient has been prescribed nadolol (Corgard) 80 mg daily for hypertension. The patient tells you that he has stopped taking the drug. What is your best first action at this time?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Instruct the patient to begin taking the drug immediately.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the patient if he is experiencing side effects of the drug.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Document this finding in detail.

choices.label: D
