choices.text: Immediately notify the prescriber.

questions.rationale: It is important to address the reason that the patient has stopped taking the drug at this time. Fairly common side effects of beta blockers include decreased sexual ability, dizziness, drowsiness, trouble sleeping, tiredness, and weakness. All of the other actions are important and appropriate, but do not focus on the patient’s concerns with regard to why he stopped taking the drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF: p. 264

questions.stem: A patient has a dose of labetalol (Normodyne) ordered twice a day and is due to have an exercise stress test this morning. What is your best action at this time?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Give the dose as ordered.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hold the dose and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give half the dose now and half after the procedure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient about chest pain, dizziness, and light-headedness.

questions.rationale: Labetalol is a beta blocker which decreases heart rate, force of heart contraction, and blood pressure. Its actions can affect the results of the test, causing inaccurate results.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 266

questions.stem: For which patient is labetalol (Normodyne) contraindicated as an antihypertensive drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 48-year-old woman who is going through menopause

choices.label: B
choices.text: 72-year-old man who has an enlarged prostate gland

choices.label: C
choices.text: 80-year-old woman with severe glaucoma

choices.label: D
choices.text: 60-year-old man with severe asthma

questions.rationale: Labetalol is a beta blocking agent. Although it relaxes blood vessel smooth muscle to lower blood pressure, it causes constriction of smooth muscles in the airways and can worsen asthma. It should be given to anyone with respiratory problems, especially severe asthma.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 266

questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed enalapril (Vasotec) has developed a persistent nonproductive cough. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the prescriber to order something for the patient’s cough.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Suggest that the family bring the patient some cough drops.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Offer the patient sips of water and ice chips.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hold the dose and notify the prescriber.

questions.rationale: Development of a dry, persistent cough is a side effect of angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor drugs such as enalapril. The cough indicates that the patient is having a reaction to the drug. The drug needs to be discontinued and the patient should be started on another drug to control hypertension.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 257

questions.stem: A patient who has been taking lisinopril (Zestril) develops swollen lips and reports that the tongue feels thick. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Document the report and reassure the patient that this is a common drug side effect.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check the patient’s pulse for rhythm and take the patient’s blood pressure.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the patient whether this has ever happened before.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hold the lisinopril dose and notify the prescriber.

questions.rationale: About 15% of patients taking an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor develop allergies to the drug with the first symptom angioedema (swelling) of the face, lips, tongue, and neck. The swelling can become severe enough to block the patient’s airway. Although this reaction is not usually as immediately life threatening as anaphylaxis, another dose of the drug would worsen the problem.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 257

questions.stem: An older patient has been prescribed an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor. Which precaution is most important for you to teach the patient about this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Avoid caffeinated drinks that may increase your blood pressure.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Take this drug at night so that you won’t be aware of nausea.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Change positions slowly when rising up from a sitting position.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Weigh yourself daily upon first arising in the morning.”

questions.rationale: Older adults are at greater risk for postural (orthostatic) hypotension when taking ACE inhibitors because of the cardiovascular changes associated with aging. Quickly changing from lying or sitting position to standing can lower blood pressure so rapidly that the patient can get dizzy or faint, leading to a fall.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 259

questions.stem: You are to give the first dose of enalapril (Vasotec) to a patient whose medication reconciliation indicates he also takes furosemide (Lasix) for hypertension. What action should you take?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer both drugs as prescribed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Be sure to separate administering the two drugs by at least 6 hours.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Notify the prescriber that this patient already takes a drug for hypertension.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Suggest to the patient that he take the enalapril in the morning and the furosemide at bedtime.

questions.rationale: Sometimes hypertension is not well controlled using only one antihypertensive agent. Often two drugs from different classes are prescribed for better control. Enalapril is an angiotensin- converting enzyme inhibitor and furosemide is a “loop” diuretic. They have different mechanisms of action to lower blood pressure and can be taken together.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 259

questions.stem: A patient has been prescribed losartan (Cozaar). Which substance, drug, or activity do you teach the patient to avoid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Oral contraceptives

choices.label: B
choices.text: Salt substitutes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Caffeine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Running

questions.rationale: Salt substitutes are mostly composed of potassium. Losartan is an angiotensin receptor blocker, and this class of drugs causes the kidneys to retain potassium. Taking in more potassium along with this drug could lead to high blood potassium levels (hyperkalemia), which has severe effects on heart contractility.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 258

questions.stem: How does the drug diltiazem (Cardizem) lower blood pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prevents angiotensin from binding to its receptors.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blocks nerve stimulation of blood vessels.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increases water and sodium loss.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Widens small arteries.

questions.rationale: Constricted blood vessels increase peripheral resistance and raise blood pressure. Calcium channel blockers work in several ways to lower blood pressure. The main way is by preventing calcium from entering smooth muscle cells in blood vessels, especially small arteries. With less calcium entering these smooth muscle cells, they are more relaxed, dilating (widening) the blood vessels.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 261

questions.stem: An older adult man who takes verapamil (Calan) reports that his breasts have enlarged. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the patient whether he has had any difficulty getting or maintaining an erection.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document the response and reassure the patient that this is a common side effect.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Instruct the patient to use an elastic wrap to bind his breasts during the day.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hold the drug dose and notify the prescriber.

questions.rationale: Breast enlargement (gynecomastia) is a common side effect of verapamil in men. The cause of the enlargement is not completely known, but it does not represent cancer or any health problem. Unless the patient is very distressed by the presence of the breast enlargement, it is not necessary to stop the drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 261

questions.stem: A patient who is taking nicardipine (Cardene) develops skin lesions, itching, fever, and achy joints. What is your best first action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hold the drug and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document the finding as expected with this drug.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer a dose of oral diphenhydramine (Benadryl).

choices.label: D
choices.text: Apply a thin coat of hydrocortisone ointment to the lesions.

questions.rationale: An adverse effect of calcium channel blocker drugs is Stevens-Johnson syndrome (erythema multiforme), a skin disorder from an allergic reaction. It causes damage to blood vessels of the skin. Symptoms include many different types of skin lesions, itching, fever, joint aching, and generally feeling ill.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF: p. 261

questions.stem: You are teaching a patient who has just been prescribed prazosin (Minipress) for hypertension. You immediately notify the prescriber and question the order upon discovering that the patient is also taking which drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aspirin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atenolol (Tenormin)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chlorothiazide (Diuril)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sildenafil (Viagra)

questions.rationale: Sildenafil is a drug for erectile dysfunction and works by dilating blood vessels. When taken with an alpha blocker, sildenafil can cause a very rapid drop in blood pressure to the extent that the patient may fall or pass out.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 265

questions.stem: A patient with hypertension is prescribed terazosin (Hytrin). When should you administer the first dose of this drug to the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: In the morning to avoid waking the patient at night

choices.label: B
choices.text: With meals to avoid GI problems

choices.label: C
choices.text: With a full glass of water

choices.label: D
choices.text: At bedtime

questions.rationale: Orthostatic hypotension is a common side effect of the first dose of an alpha blocker drug. You should give the first dose at bedtime and caution the patient not to get up without assistance.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF: p. 265

questions.stem: A patient with hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus is taking carvedilol (Coreg). What special precaution should you teach the patient to take when taking this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check blood pressure in both arms.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Keep a strict record of intake and output.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check blood sugar levels regularly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Avoid sugar-containing drinks.

questions.rationale: Carvedilol is an alpha-beta blocker drug. These drugs can cause elevated blood glucose levels, so you should teach a patient with diabetes to monitor blood glucose levels regularly.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 266

questions.stem: You are teaching a patient how to administer clonidine (Catapres) using the transdermal patch system. How often do you teach the patient to change the patch?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Daily

choices.label: B
choices.text: Every 3 days

choices.label: C
choices.text: Every 7 days

choices.label: D
choices.text: Every 14 days

questions.rationale: The nature of the clonidine patch delivers a low dose of the drug continually from the patch for 7 days. After 7 days, minimal drug is left in the patch and the patient will no longer receive a therapeutic dose. To ensure a continual therapeutic dose, the patch is removed after 7 days and a fresh one applied.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 267

questions.stem: Which antihypertensive drug would you teach a patient is considered safe for use during pregnancy or breastfeeding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Clonidine (Catapres)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Methyldopa (Aldomet)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Labetalol (Normodyne)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Carvedilol (Coreg)

questions.rationale: Many drugs are not considered safe for use during pregnancy or breastfeeding. Methyldopa, a centrally acting adrenergic drug, has been used safely during both pregnancy and breastfeeding to treat hypertension. Hydralazine (Apresoline), a direct vasodilator, has also been used safely during both pregnancy and breastfeeding. However, small amounts of hydralazine pass into breast milk, putting infants at minimal risk for side effects.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 268

questions.stem: Which statement by a patient prescribed enalapril (Vasotec) indicates the need for additional teaching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I will use a salt substitute instead of table salt when I eat.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I will avoid alcohol while I am taking this medication.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I will wear long sleeves, a hat, and sunscreen when I go outdoors.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I will call my prescriber immediately if I notice any swelling in my face.”

questions.rationale: Teach patients to avoid salt substitutes. Salt substitutes contain potassium, and a side effect of ACE inhibitors is increased blood potassium level (hyperkalemia).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 258

questions.stem: A 33-year-old female patient is currently taking captopril (Capoten) and metoprolol (Lopressor) to control her blood pressure. She tells you that she plans to become pregnant. What should you teach the patient about the use of this drug during pregnancy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ACE inhibitors affect women differently than men.

choices.label: B
choices.text: ACE inhibitors have fewer side effects than other hypertension drugs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: ACE inhibitors should not be prescribed for women who are pregnant.

choices.label: D
choices.text: ACE inhibitors are only safe for use during the third trimester of pregnancy.

questions.rationale: ACE inhibitors should not be prescribed for women who are pregnant. They can cause low blood pressure, severe kidney failure, increased potassium, and even death in a newborn when used after the first trimester of pregnancy.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 258

questions.stem: A hospitalized patient prescribed nifedipine XL (Procardia) 60 mg once a day informs you that at home he was taking nicardipine (Cardene) 20 mg three times a day and thinks this is the wrong drug. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Give the dose as ordered after informing the patient that this is a new medication the prescriber wants him or her to take.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hold the drug and contact the prescriber to clarify the order because of the patient’s concern before administering the dose.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Contact the pharmacy to ask what the prescriber ordered and if there was a mistake when the prescription was filled.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administer the dose because nifedipine and nicardipine are from the same class of drugs and may be substituted for each other.

questions.rationale: Because of the patient’s question, it is essential to contact the prescriber to clarify the order. Both nifedipine and nicardipine are calcium channel blockers but may not be substituted for each other. Nifedipine XL is an extended release form of the drug that only needs to be taken once a day.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 261 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which statements are true about blood pressure? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blood pressure decreases when a person is resting.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blood pressure increases when a person is active.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Low blood pressure is called hypotension.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Systolic blood pressure is the pressure against artery walls when the heart relaxes.

choices.label: E
choices.text: As people get older they are more likely to develop high blood pressure.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Primary hypertension is caused by another health problem or prescribed drug.

questions.rationale: Systolic blood pressure is the pressure of blood against the artery walls when the heart contracts. Diastolic blood pressure represents pressure against artery walls when the heart relaxes. The cause of primary hypertension is unknown while secondary hypertension is caused by another health problem or drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 253

questions.stem: Which conditions or drugs can cause secondary hypertension? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Adrenal gland tumors

choices.label: B
choices.text: Estrogen in birth control pills

choices.label: C
choices.text: Leukemia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cortisol

choices.label: E
choices.text: Kidney infection

questions.rationale: Secondary hypertension is the result of other health problems or drugs. Table 16-2 in the text lists the health problems and drugs that can cause secondary hypertension.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 254 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: A patient who weighs 220 lb is to receive labetalol 0.25 mg/kg intravenously. The solution of labetalol on hand is 100 mg/20 mL. How many milliliters of labetalol will you prepare for a correct dose?
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 262

questions.stem: An adult patient is prescribed metoprolol (Lopressor) 50 mg orally twice a day. Metoprolol is available in 100 mg tablets. How many tablets will you give for each dose?
questions.rationale: 100 mg/1 tablet : 50 mg/X tablet = tablet (0.5 tablet).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 262

questions.stem: A child who weighs 68 lb and has very high blood pressure as a result of an adrenal gland tumor is prescribed a STAT dose of oral prazosin (Minipress) at 200 mcg/kg. You have on hand prazosin 2 mg/capsule. How many capsules will you administer to this child?
questions.rationale: 1 kg=2.2 lb. 66/2.2 = 30 kg. 30 kg  200 mcg = 6000 mcg (1000 mcg = 1 mg); 6000/1000 = 6 mg. 1 capsule = 2 mg. 6 mg/2 = 3 capsules.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 264 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.stem: What does the term “afterload” mean?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The amount of blood entering the left ventricle that must be pumped out of the heart

choices.label: B
choices.text: The peripheral resistance of the arterial system, including mean arterial pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: The ability of the living muscle fibers of the heart to contract and pump blood

choices.label: D
choices.text: The percentage of blood pumped out of the heart with each heartbeat

questions.rationale: Afterload is the pressure in the aorta that the left ventricle must overcome before blood can move from it into the aorta. Mean arterial pressure (MAP) is also the pressure that the left ventricle must overcome to move blood from the left ventricle into the aorta during contraction (afterload). Afterload increases because of increased peripheral resistance usually as a result of high blood pressure.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 274

questions.stem: What is the most common cause of heart failure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: High blood pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Low blood pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Defective heart valves

choices.label: D
choices.text: Defective coronary arteries

questions.rationale: Most heart failure is caused by hypertension. Other causes of heart failure include myocardial infarction, coronary artery disease, cardiomyopathy, substance abuse (alcohol and illicit or prescribed drugs), heart valve disease, congenital defects, cardiac infections and inflammations, and conditions that increase cardiac output and energy demands such as sepsis.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 278

questions.stem: What is the main physiological problem in most types of heart failure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Overstretching of the right ventricle so that too much blood leaves the heart

choices.label: B
choices.text: Overstretching of the right ventricle so that too little blood leaves the heart

choices.label: C
choices.text: Overstretching of the left ventricle so that too much blood leaves the heart

choices.label: D
choices.text: Overstretching of the left ventricle so that too little blood leaves the heart

questions.rationale: The muscle of the left ventricle becomes overstretched. When any muscle is overstretched, its contraction is weaker. When the muscles of the left ventricle are overstretched or flabby and the contraction is weak, too much blood remains in the left ventricle, and more blood arriving from the left atrium is added to it. This overstretches the muscle more and continues to weaken contractions, leading to heart failure.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 274

questions.stem: Which symptom occurs with only pure right-sided heart failure and does not occur with pure left-sided heart failure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Confusion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Shortness of breath

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased afterload

choices.label: D
choices.text: Swelling in the ankles and legs

questions.rationale: When the right ventricle contracts poorly, signs and symptoms of peripheral congestion occur (Box 17-2 in text) such as weight gain, swelling in the legs, jugular vein distention (Figure 17-4 in text), and increased blood pressure.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 276, Box 17-2

questions.stem: Which statement about drug therapy for heart failure is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The drugs work only directly on the heart muscle.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Drug therapy improves heart function but does not cure heart failure.

choices.label: C
choices.text: When heart function returns to normal, the drug therapy can be stopped.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Drug therapy for heart failure does not work when the patient continues to smoke.

questions.rationale: Drug therapy only improves heart function; drugs do not cure heart failure. Because the damage to the heart muscle is not reversible, the only real cure for heart failure is a heart transplant.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 277

questions.stem: What is a common side effect of hydralazine (Apresoline) therapy for heart failure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tachycardia

choices.label: B
choices.text: High blood pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Constipation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Difficulty sleeping

questions.rationale: Common side effects of hydralazine include tachycardia and salt retention.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 280

questions.stem: How should sublingual nitroglycerin tablets for heart failure be taken?

choices.label: A
choices.text: On a full stomach

choices.label: B
choices.text: On an empty stomach

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dissolved under the tongue

choices.label: D
choices.text: Placed under a patch on the chest

questions.rationale: Sublingual means to place the drug under the tongue. Remind patients that sublingual nitroglycerin should be kept in place until dissolved.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 281

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action for cardiac glycosides?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Slowing the heart rate and increasing the force of the heart muscle contraction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increasing the heart rate and decreasing the force of the heart muscle contraction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Directly relaxing blood vessel muscles and decreasing afterload

choices.label: D
choices.text: Directly constricting blood vessel muscles and increasing afterload

questions.rationale: Cardiac glycosides are a class of drugs that improve heart failure by slowing down a heart rate that is too fast, allowing more time for the left ventricle to fill. They also work on the muscle fibers in the heart and increase the force of each heartbeat (contractility).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: pp. 281-282

questions.stem: What is the most common side effect of cardiac glycoside drugs for heart failure therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Abdominal cramping and constipation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Heart rhythm disturbances

choices.label: C
choices.text: High blood pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Skin rashes

questions.rationale: The most common side effects of digoxin (Lanoxin), a cardiac glycoside, are heart rhythm disturbances that are related to digoxin toxicity. Other common side effects to watch for include fatigue, bradycardia (slow heart rate less than 60 beats/min), anorexia (loss of appetite), nausea, and vomiting.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 282

questions.stem: Which drug for heart failure is a common positive inotrope?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Amiloride (Midamor)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nesiritide (Natrecor)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Captopril (Capoten)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dobutamine (Dobutrex)

questions.rationale: Dobutamine (Dobutrex) is a positive inotrope. This drug is a heart pump drug that makes the heart muscle contract more forcefully. It also relaxes blood vessels so blood can flow better.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 285

questions.stem: When are vasodilator drugs usually prescribed for heart failure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: When angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors cannot be taken

choices.label: B
choices.text: When venous dilation is also needed

choices.label: C
choices.text: When beta blockers are also being taken

choices.label: D
choices.text: When heart failure is improving

questions.rationale: Vasodilators act directly on the peripheral arteries, causing them to dilate, which leads to decreased blood pressure and workload on the heart. When patients cannot take ACE inhibitors or angiotensin II receptor blockers, vasodilators are often prescribed.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 279 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Why must the muscles of the left ventricle be the strongest ones in the heart?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The mitral (bicuspid) valve is larger than the aortic valve.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The left ventricle receives blood under high pressure from the pulmonary system.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The pressure in the aorta is higher than the pressures elsewhere in the circulatory system.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blood in the left ventricle is oxygenated, making it thicker and harder to move than deoxygenated blood.

questions.rationale: Movement of blood through the other chambers of the heart is against low pressure, and the distance is short. Blood leaving the left ventricle must move with enough force to first overcome pressure in the aorta, which is the highest in the entire circulatory system, and then move long distances throughout the entire body. Contraction of the muscles in the walls of the left ventricle is what provides the pressure to move blood forward into general circulation.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 273

questions.stem: Which two factors are most likely to result in improved cardiac output?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Greater preload; greater afterload

choices.label: B
choices.text: Greater preload; reduced afterload

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reduced preload; greater afterload

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduced preload; reduced afterload

questions.rationale: Cardiac output is the movement of blood out of the heart and into general circulation. Preload is the amount of blood in the left ventricle before contraction. This volume stretches the muscle of the left ventricle to result in a better force of contraction. So, to a point, greater preload helps improve cardiac output. Afterload is the pressure in the aorta that must be overcome for blood to leave the left ventricle. When this pressure is lower, the heart does not have to work as hard to move blood out of the left ventricle into the aorta. So, greater preload and reduced afterload tend to improve cardiac output.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 275

questions.stem: Which symptom is commonly assessed when a patient has left ventricular heart failure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Weight gain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Swelling in the legs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Jugular vein distention

choices.label: D
choices.text: Crackles in the lungs

questions.rationale: When the left ventricle fails, less blood is pumped out to the body and blood backs up into the pulmonary system causing signs of pulmonary congestion such as crackles and wheezes. Weight gain, peripheral swelling, and jugular vein distention are all signs of right ventricular failure.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 275

questions.stem: How does hydralazine (Apresoline) help improve heart failure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: By directly stimulating the right heart muscle to increase the strength of heart muscle contraction

choices.label: B
choices.text: By directly stimulating the left heart muscle to increase the strength of heart muscle contraction

choices.label: C
choices.text: By directly relaxing blood vessel muscles and decreasing afterload

choices.label: D
choices.text: By directly constricting blood vessel muscles and increasing afterload

questions.rationale: Hydralazine is a vasodilator. Vasodilators are a class of drugs that act directly on the peripheral arteries to cause them to dilate (widen). This leads to lowering of blood pressure and decreases the workload of the heart.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Remembering   REF:  p. 280

questions.stem: How do natriuretic peptides work to help improve heart failure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increasing preload

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreasing preload

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreasing water loss and raising blood pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increasing water loss and lowering blood pressure

questions.rationale: Nesiritide (Natrecor) is human B-type natriuretic peptide, which is a hormone that is produced by the heart ventricles and a synthetic drug. The actions of this drug include increased water elimination and blood vessel dilation. Both are helpful when treating a patient with heart failure. This drug is given by the IV route and helps the body get rid of extra salt and water, thus lowering blood pressure.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 284

questions.stem: Which condition alerts you to assess a patient for worsening heart failure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blood pressure of 106/40 mm Hg

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pounding headache

choices.label: C
choices.text: Foul urine odor

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ankle swelling

questions.rationale: Ankle swelling is associated with heart failure, although other conditions also can cause it. If this is a new symptom or is occurring even when the patient is not spending a lot of time sitting or standing, the nurse should assess the patient for other symptoms of heart failure. Although the blood pressure is not high, the pulse pressure is wide which does not indicate failure. A pounding headache is most associated with elevated blood pressure. Foul-smelling urine is associated with urinary tract infection.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 281

questions.stem: You prepare to administer a drug for heart failure to a patient. Which assessment finding do you report to the prescriber before administering the drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Systolic blood pressure increase from 128 to 136

choices.label: B
choices.text: Urine output of 2100 mL in 24 hours

choices.label: C
choices.text: Weight gain of 1 lb in 3 days

choices.label: D
choices.text: Heart rate of 54 beats/min

questions.rationale: Some drugs for heart failure also slow the heart rate. If the heart rate is slow before taking the drug, the drug can slow the heart rate so much that the patient cannot adequately perfuse and oxygenate his or her vital organs. Usually, if the heart rate is less than 60 beats/min, the prescriber is notified. The next dose may be held until the pulse rate returns to normal.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 283

questions.stem: Which statement made by a patient with heart failure indicates that more teaching is needed about the prescribed drug therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I always try to take my heart failure drugs at the same time each day.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Now I am using a weekly pill dispenser to keep my drugs straight.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Now that my heart failure is cured I can cut back the drugs I take.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “If I gain more than 3 lb in a week I will call my doctor.”

questions.rationale: Heart failure can be improved with drug therapy but the underlying condition remains. When heart failure is a result of damage, it is not cured. Drug dosage needs may change to control heart failure, but usually the dosages only increase as time goes on.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 277

questions.stem: A patient asks you how a prescribed diuretic can help heart failure. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Urinating more prevents heart failure from damaging your kidneys.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Diuretics reduce blood pressure so your heart won’t have to work as hard.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Taking a diuretic reduces salt levels so you don’t have to limit your intake of salty foods.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “The diuretic counteracts the side effects of the other drugs prescribed for your heart failure.”

questions.rationale: The actions of diuretic drugs help heart failure in several ways. They can relax blood vessels and lower blood pressure so that less effort is needed for the heart contraction to move blood into the general circulation. Removing extra fluid from the circulation also can reduce blood pressure. In addition, some diuretics work on the heart muscle itself.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 278

questions.stem: A patient with heart failure asks you why the antihypertensive drug enalapril (Vasotec) has been prescribed. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Hypertension is the most common cause of heart failure.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Lowering your blood pressure will allow your heart to pump more easily.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “This drug will decrease the amount of blood your heart has to pump, leading to less work for your heart.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Enalapril will cause your heart to reestablish its normal electrical functions so that it works more effectively as a pump.”

questions.rationale: Decreasing blood pressure helps improve the heart’s action as a pump and decreases the heart’s workload. Diuretics are used to decrease blood volume. Enalopril is an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor that interferes with the transition of angiotensin I to angiotensin II. Angiotensin II is a potent vasoconstrictor that causes increased blood pressure and increased workload for the heart.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 279

questions.stem: A patient taking lisinopril (Zestril) for heart failure reports a nagging, nonproductive cough. What is your best first action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the patient whether he or she has received the annual influenza vaccination.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the patient how much the cough is interfering with sleep or other activities.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Document the report as the only action.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Notify the prescriber immediately.

questions.rationale: Many conditions can cause a cough, but a dry, nonproductive cough is the most common side effect of lisinopril and all other ACE inhibitors. Coughs associated with pulmonary infections or worsening of heart failure are usually productive. After assessing the patient’s issues with the cough, if the patient is uncomfortable or if the cough interferes with sleeping and other activities, report these problems to the prescriber.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 278

questions.stem: After giving the first dose of any antihypertensive drug for heart failure, what action must be taken for a hospitalized patient’s safety?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recheck the drug order for accuracy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensure that the call light is within reach.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Place a wheelchair in the patient’s room.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Raise all four side rails on the patient’s bed.

questions.rationale: Antihypertensive drugs decrease blood pressure and increase a patient’s risk for dizziness, light- headedness, and hypotension. After giving the first dose of any antihypertensive drug, you should be sure that the call light is within easy reach and instruct the patient to call for assistance when getting out of bed. A wheelchair is not necessary and standards of practice state that all four side rails should not be raised because there is a risk for falls if the patient tries to get out of bed alone.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 278

questions.stem: Which safety precaution should be taught to a patient going home on any antihypertensive drug for heart failure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Always change positions slowly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Never get up without assistance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Take the drug in the evening before bedtime.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Avoid all over-the-counter drugs while taking this drug.

questions.rationale: Antihypertensive drugs lower blood pressure, placing the patient at increased risk for dizziness and hypotension. Changing positions slowly gives the body time to adjust, preventing dizziness and falls. The patient can get up at home without assistance if he or she changes positions slowly. Some, but not all, over-the-counter drugs may not be safe with these drugs. You should teach the patient to always consult with the prescriber before taking an over-the-counter drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 278

questions.stem: A patient with heart failure reports a 5 lb weight gain during the past week and episodes of feeling more short of breath. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “These changes are to be expected because you have heart failure.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “You will probably need a decreased dose of your diuretic drug.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I will ask the dietitian to discuss a weight loss diet with you.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Your prescriber must be notified because your heart failure is getting worse.”

questions.rationale: Patient weight gain and increased shortness of breath are signs of worsening heart failure. The prescriber should be notified because the plan of care may need to be modified. If the patient is taking a diuretic, it is likely that the dose may be increased.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 277

questions.stem: A patient with heart failure is prescribed oral captopril (Capoten) and carvedilol (Coreg). The heart rate after giving these drugs is decreased from 84 per minute to 68 per minute. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hold the next dose.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Immediately notify the prescriber.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Document the finding as the only action.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Schedule the captopril and carvedilol to be given at different times.

questions.rationale: Beta blockers and ACE inhibitors are often used together to treat heart failure. Beta blockers block the effects of epinephrine on the heart resulting in deceased heart rate and force of contraction, thus decreased blood pressure. You should notify the prescriber if the heart rate is less than 60 per minute.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 279

questions.stem: A patient with heart failure is prescribed metoprolol (Toprol XL) 25 mg daily. The pharmacy sends metoprolol (Lopressor) 25 mg. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Give the dose as provided because the drugs are the same.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the pharmacy to send the drug as ordered.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Contact the prescriber for clarification.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Check the patient’s heart rate before giving the dose.

questions.rationale: When prescribed to treat heart failure, only the sustained-release form of metoprolol is used. Metoprolol (Toprol XL) is the sustained-release form of the drug, whereas metoprolol (Lopressor) is not.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 279

questions.stem: Which precaution should you stress when teaching a patient about a prescribed nitroglycerin transdermal patch?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “If a patch comes loose, tape it tightly to the skin with several layers of tape.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Do not remove old patches, just let them fall off over time.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Take care to apply the patches directly over your heart.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Remove the old patch before applying the new patch.”

questions.rationale: The previous patch must be removed (and the area wiped clean of drug) before applying a new patch (to a different site) to prevent leftover drug from contributing to a drug overdose. Applying the patch directly over the heart does not increase the speed of drug action on the heart.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 280

questions.stem: A patient with severe heart failure is discharged with a prescription for nitroglycerin ointment. What technique do you teach the patient’s spouse to use to avoid experiencing side effects when applying this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Squeeze the ointment onto the special paper.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cleanse the skin before applying the drug.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rotate the drug application skin sites.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Wear a pair of disposable gloves.

questions.rationale: Common side effects of nitroglycerin ointment include hypotension and headaches. When this drug is administered, if a person’s skin comes into contact with the drug as it is squeezed onto the special lined paper, these side effects may develop. Wearing gloves prevents skin contact with the drug and decreases the risk for side effects.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 280

questions.stem: Which statement by a patient receiving nitroglycerin ointment indicates the need for additional teaching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I will remove the previous dose before I put on the new dose.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I will reapply the dose every 4 hours around the clock.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I must avoid rubbing my skin when I put on a new dose.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I will put tape over the paper so that the dose stays in place.”

questions.rationale: Nitroglycerin ointment or patches lose their effectiveness when used continuously. The drug works much better when there is a “drug free” time during a 24-hour period. Usually the drug is removed at night when the patient has his or her longest sleeping period because the heart is less stressed during that time.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 280

questions.stem: For which drug order would you contact the prescriber to verify it is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Furosemide (Lasix) 40 mg orally each day

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fosinopril (Monopril) 20 mg orally each day

choices.label: C
choices.text: Digoxin (Lanoxin) 2.5 mg orally each day

choices.label: D
choices.text: Amiloride (Midamor) 5 mg orally each day

questions.rationale: The usual dosage of digoxin is 0.125 to 0.25 mg/day. The order is for 10 to 20 times the normal dose. If this dose is given the results would be adverse effects of even death. The other drug dosages are within the correct ranges.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 282

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed isosorbide (Isordil). Which condition will you teach the patient is an expected side effect of this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Frequent headaches

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cold hands and feet

choices.label: C
choices.text: Change in urine color

choices.label: D
choices.text: Distaste for sweet foods or drinks

questions.rationale: Isosorbide dilates blood vessels to allow better blood flow to the heart muscle. However, blood vessels are dilated in many body areas. When blood vessels in the brain or head are dilated, headaches can occur.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF: p. 280

questions.stem: Which vasodilator drug can be safely prescribed for a patient with heart failure during pregnancy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hydralazine (Apresoline)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Isosorbide (Isordil)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Minoxidil (Lonitin)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nitroglycerin (Nitrostat)

questions.rationale: Hydralazine can be safely used for blood pressure control during pregnancy, but isosorbide and nitroglycerin may affect fetal circulation and should be used with caution during pregnancy. Minoxidil is a vasodilator that is not generally used in the treatment of heart failure.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 281

questions.stem: A patient asks you how prescribed digoxin (Lanoxin) will help improve heart failure. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “It dilates your arteries and decreases your blood pressure.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “It directly perfuses your kidneys which decreases your fluid volume and blood pressure.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “It increases your heart rate which will result in improved cardiac output.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “It increases the force of heart contractions and slows down the heart rate to improve cardiac output.”

questions.rationale: Digoxin is a cardiac glycoside drug that works on the muscle fiber of the heart to crease the force of each contraction. It also slows down the heart rate. These actions help to improve cardiac output.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF: p. 281

questions.stem: A patient with heart failure is prescribed digoxin (Lanoxin). The patient reports nausea, blurry vision, and feeling like the heart is skipping beats. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer the scheduled dose because it will correct these heart failure symptoms.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assess the patient’s heart rate and then administer the scheduled dose.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hold the dose and notify the prescriber immediately.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Place the patient on complete bed rest.

questions.rationale: Nausea, blurry vision, and heart rate or rhythm changes are signs of digoxin overdose. The dose should be held and the prescriber notified. Most likely you will also be asked to obtain a blood sample to measure the digoxin level. Digoxin has a very narrow therapeutic range (0.8 to 2 ng/mL) and this level should be checked whenever a patient shows any signs of toxicity. Symptoms usually resolve when the drug is held and the body eliminates it.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF: p. 283

questions.stem: A patient taking digoxin (Lanoxin) has all of the following laboratory blood values. Which value do you report to the prescriber before administering the next dose of digoxin?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sodium 133 mEq/L

choices.label: B
choices.text: Potassium 2.8 mEq/L

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blood urea nitrogen 9 mg/dL

choices.label: D
choices.text: White blood cell count 11,000 cells/mm3

questions.rationale: Serum potassium level affects the activity of digoxin. A value of 2.8 is low (hypokalemia). Any abnormal potassium value (high or low) requires the prescriber to change the digoxin dosage. In addition, action is needed to bring this critical electrolyte value back to its normal range. Although the other laboratory values are slightly abnormal, none are critically abnormal or likely to have an effect on digoxin activity.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF: p. 283

questions.stem: What is the most important action for you to teach a patient to perform before taking his or her daily dose of digoxin (Lanoxin)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check the pulse for a full 60 seconds.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Look for edema in your ankles.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Notify the prescriber for any heart palpitations.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Take the drug at exactly the same time every day.

questions.rationale: Although all the actions are important, the most important is to ensure that the pulse rate is between 60 and 100 beats/min and is regular before administering digoxin or any other cardiac glycoside. For an irregular heart rate or one that is outside of this range, the dose must be held and the prescriber notified immediately.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF: p. 283

questions.stem: A patient with heart failure asks why two diuretic drugs, furosemide (Lasix) and spironolactone (Aldactone), have been prescribed. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Your prescriber wants you to lose fluids and that’s why you are taking two diuretics.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Each of these diuretics works in a different way to decrease workload on your heart.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Using two diuretics will double the amount of fluid you lose to decrease the work your heart must do.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Let me contact your prescriber because patients usually do not take two diuretics at the same time.”

questions.rationale: Spironolactone, when used in low doses, blocks the action of aldosterone, which causes the body to lose salt and water. Furosemide works in the loop of Henle to decrease reabsorption of salt and water. Spironolactone is usually prescribed with another diuretic to decrease the volume of fluid in the blood vessels, which reduces the workload of the heart. Additionally when used together, these drugs help the body maintain a more normal blood potassium level.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF: pp. 283-284

questions.stem: A patient with heart failure is prescribed a one-time dose of furosemide (Lasix) 400 mg by intravenous (IV) push immediately. What is the best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Contact the prescriber and request that the dose be changed from IV push to IV piggyback.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Give the dose by IV push over a 10-minute period of time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hold the dose and notify the prescriber immediately.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Start an IV and administer the drug as prescribed.

questions.rationale: The usual IV dose of furosemide ranges between 10 and 80 mg. A dose of 400 mg is very large. You must verify the correct drug dose before administering it.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF: p. 272

questions.stem: A patient receiving dobutamine (Dobutrex) at 180 mcg/min is having all of the following physiologic responses. Which response indicates that the drip rate may be too high?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Systolic blood pressure increase from 106 to 122 mm Hg

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hourly urine output of 100 mL

choices.label: C
choices.text: Facial flushing

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chest pain

questions.rationale: Dobutamine is a positive inotropic drug. Side effects include improved urine output and increased blood pressure. Facial flushing is common but not serious. Chest pain indicates the heart is working too hard, which may be related to the dosage level. You should slow the drip rate and immediately notify the prescriber.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF: p. 286

questions.stem: A patient with heart failure is receiving dobutamine (Dobutrex) intravenously at home. Which sign or symptom should the patient and family  be instructed to report to the prescriber immediately?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Burning or pain at the intravenous (IV) site

choices.label: B
choices.text: Heart rate between 60 and 70 beats/min

choices.label: C
choices.text: Urine output of 1600 mL over 24 hours

choices.label: D
choices.text: Brief episode of shortness of breath

questions.rationale: Patients receiving dobutamine at home should be taught the signs and symptoms of IV lines that are no longer patent or have developed infection (e.g., burning, pain, redness, swelling, warmth at the site). Patients should be instructed to report these signs immediately to the prescriber or home health nurse.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF: p. 286

questions.stem: What should you teach a patient who has been prescribed potassium (K-Dur) tablets?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “If you miss a dose, double your next dose to keep your blood level of potassium normal.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Take your potassium with food or a full glass or water to avoid nausea/vomiting.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Be sure to use salt substitutes instead of salt so that your body will not retain water.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Eat lots of foods that are high in potassium such as bananas, spinach, broccoli, and sweet potatoes.”

questions.rationale: Common side effects of potassium include nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, gas, and abdominal discomfort. Taking the drug with food or right after meals with a full glass of water or fruit juice will decrease or prevent these side effects. A patient should never take a double dose of a prescribed drug. Most salt substitutes are made by replacing sodium with potassium. Use of salt substitutes or eating excessive amounts of foods that are high in potassium while taking a potassium supplement increases the risk of hyperkalemia (high blood potassium).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF: p. 288

questions.stem: A patient is receiving magnesium by intravenous infusion. Which finding indicates that the patient’s blood magnesium level may be too high?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Respirations are 10 per minute and shallow.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Heart rate is 66 beats/min and regular.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient reports difficulty staying asleep.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Episodes of diarrhea.

questions.rationale: High blood levels of magnesium interfere with muscle contraction, causing muscle weakness. Breathing is dependent on good contraction of respiratory muscles. Shallow and slow respirations are a serious manifestation of excess blood magnesium levels.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF: p. 288

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed inamrinone (Inocor) by continuous intravenous infusion. When you make rounds and assess the patient, the intravenous bag infusing is labeled (Primacor). What should you do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stop the infusion and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assess the infusion site for a blood return.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Document the infusion and site conditions as the only action.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Slow the infusion rate because Primacor is more potent than Inocor.

questions.rationale: The two drugs, Inocor and Primacor, are not the same, although both are positive inotropes. Their dosages and infusion rates are very different. The pharmacy has sent up the wrong drug or has labeled the infusion bag incorrectly. Either way, you must stop the existing infusion and clarify all of this with the prescriber first and then the pharmacy.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 285

questions.stem: What is the most important reason intravenous potassium is considered a high alert drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: When intravenous potassium is administered at too low a dose its mechanism of action is greatly reduced.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intravenous potassium is a highly irritating solution and can cause significant pain or burning at the infusion site.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administering too much intravenous potassium or administering it too quickly may cause life-threatening problems.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Excessive blood potassium levels can lead to nausea, vomiting, severe diarrhea, abdominal cramping, and other intestinal problems.

questions.rationale: Blood potassium levels have a very narrow range of normal. When intravenous potassium is administered at too high a dose or too rapidly, life-threatening cardiac rhythm problems develop and death is possible. Always check an intravenous potassium dose with another health care professional and never administer it either by intravenous push or as a bolus.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 287 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which are sympathetic nervous system compensatory mechanisms of the body for heart failure? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased heart rate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased respiratory rate

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased contractility

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased body temperature

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased cardiac output

choices.label: F
choices.text: Decreased cardiac output

questions.rationale: The sympathetic nervous system releases the catecholamine hormones epinephrine and norepinephrine, which act on the heart in two ways. First they increase the heart rate, and second they increase the power of the heart muscle fibers to contract so that the heart pumps more forcefully. These actions increase cardiac output.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 277

questions.stem: Which lifestyle changes should you suggest to a patient with heart failure? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fluid restriction of 1000 mL/day

choices.label: B
choices.text: Weight loss program

choices.label: C
choices.text: Smoking cessation program

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aerobic exercise program

choices.label: E
choices.text: Low-salt, low-fat diet

questions.rationale: Lifestyle changes that are important in treating heart failure include weight loss, smoking cessation, and a low-salt and low-fat diet. A fluid restriction of 1000 mL can result in decreased perfusion of the kidneys. Patients with heart failure may not be able to tolerate an aerobic exercise program.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 277

questions.stem: A patient has been prescribed sublingual nitroglycerin. Which key points will you teach the patient about this drug? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Keep the tablet in place until it is dissolved.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Swallow the tablet with a full glass of water.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A tingling sensation means that the drug is potent.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Do not eat anything until after the tablet is dissolved.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Call your prescriber if chest pain persists after one tablet.

questions.rationale: Sublingual or buccal nitroglycerin should not be swallowed because the liver destroys most of the drug and makes it ineffective. The drug should be kept in place until it dissolved. Patients should not drink or eat until the tablet is dissolved. A tingling sensation as the tablet dissolves indicates that the drug is potent and any time a patient’s chest pain persists after using one tablet, the prescriber should be notified.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 281

questions.stem: A patient is to receive nesiritide (Natrecor). Which patient assessments must be checked before giving this drug? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Heart rate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Swallowing reflex

choices.label: C
choices.text: IV line for patency

choices.label: D
choices.text: Oral intake

choices.label: E
choices.text: Respiratory rate factors

questions.rationale: Nesiritide is given by the IV route, so you should always ensure that the IV line is patent. Heart and respiratory rate should always be assessed before giving this drug. The heart rate should be between 60 and 100 beats/min and the respiratory rate should be between 12 and 20 breaths/min.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 284 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed digoxin (Lanoxin) 0.03 mg as a liquid dose. The drug on hand is digoxin 0.05 mg/mL. How many milliliters do you give?
questions.rationale: Want 0.03 mg, Have 0.05 mg/1 mL. Cancel mg; 0.05X = 0.03; X = 0.6. 0.6  1 mL = 0.6 mL.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 282

questions.stem: The patient is prescribed potassium (K-Dur) tablets 30 mg orally each morning. You have potassium (K-Dur) in 10 mg tablets. How many tablets will you give for each dose?
questions.rationale: Want 30 mg, Have 10 mg/tablet. 30 mg/X tablets : 10 mg/1 tablet. Cancel mg; 30/10 = 3 tablets.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 287

questions.stem: A patient who weighs 259 lb is prescribed to receive nesiritide (Natrecor) 2 mcg/kg as an intravenous bolus. The drug available is 1.5 mg/5 mL. How many milliliters of the drug will you prepare to make the correct dose?
questions.rationale: 1 kg = 2.2 lb. The patient’s weight in kilogram is 117.72 kg (259/2.2). The correct dose is 2 mcg  117.72 kg, 235.4 mcg. 1 mL of the drug = 0.3 mg (divide 1.5 mg by 5 to get the concentration per mL) or 300 mcg/mL (1 mg = 1000 mcg). Divide 235.4 mcg by 300 mcg for the correct volume to prepare, which is 0.78 mL, round up to 0.8 mL.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 284 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.stem: What does the term “asystole” mean?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Absence of electrical activity in the heart

choices.label: B
choices.text: The ability of the heart to fire an impulse on its own

choices.label: C
choices.text: Full heart contraction that occurs earlier than it should

choices.label: D
choices.text: The normal rhythm of the heart as measured by an electrocardiogram

questions.rationale: Asystole is the absence of electrical or contraction activity within the heart. It appears on the electrocardiogram (ECG) monitor as a straight line or “flatline.”
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 297

questions.stem: Which part of the heart normally controls the heart rate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atrioventricular (AV) node

choices.label: B
choices.text: His-Purkinje system

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sinoatrial (SA) node

choices.label: D
choices.text: Left ventricle

questions.rationale: The normal pacemaker of the heart is the SA node, which initiates 60 to 100 electrical impulses to the heart per minute.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 292

questions.stem: Which person is likely to have the lowest resting heart rate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Newborn infant

choices.label: B
choices.text: Exercising child

choices.label: C
choices.text: Young adult athlete

choices.label: D
choices.text: Older adult female

questions.rationale: Heart rate is related to a person’s state of health and whether he or she is exercising or resting. For example, a young athlete may have a normal resting heart rate of 50 beats/min with an exercising heart rate of 100, whereas an older adult may have a resting heart rate of 80 or 90 beats/min and an exercising heart rate of 120 to 140. Newborns and children generally have faster resting heart rates.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 292

questions.stem: Why must most cardiac dysrhythmias be treated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They increase preload.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They decrease preload.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They increase cardiac output.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They decrease cardiac output.

questions.rationale: Most dysrhythmias have a negative effect on how well the heart works as a pump by decreasing cardiac output (the amount of blood the heart pumps in a minute). Dysrhythmias decrease cardiac output, which leads to symptoms such as dizziness, light-headedness, fainting (syncope), and decreased peripheral pulses.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 293

questions.stem: What cardiac dysrhythmia can be helped or corrected by the drug atropine?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tachycardia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bradycardia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Premature atrial contractions

choices.label: D
choices.text: Premature ventricular contractions

questions.rationale: Atropine is a competitive muscarinic acetylcholine receptor antagonist used to treat abnormally slow heart rhythms known as bradydysrhythmias (bradycardia). The vagus nerve slows heart rate, and atropine blocks the actions of the vagus nerve on the heart. This drug is used for patients who have symptomatic bradycardia.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 296

questions.stem: Which condition is a common side effect of atropine?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Urinary retention

choices.label: B
choices.text: Loss of appetite

choices.label: C
choices.text: Weight gain

choices.label: D
choices.text: Difficulty sleeping

questions.rationale: Common side effects of atropine include tachycardia, drowsiness, blurred vision, dry mouth, and urinary hesitancy or retention.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 297

questions.stem: What step must be taken before giving digoxin (Lanoxin)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Measure the size of both ankles.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Take the apical pulse for a full minute.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Make sure the patient has not eaten for at least an hour.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Take the blood pressure in both the right arm and the left arm.

questions.rationale: Digoxin (Lanoxin) is a cardiac glycoside that may be used in small doses to increase contractility and slow conduction through the AV node, causing slowing of the heart rate. Always check the patient’s pulse for a full minute before giving the dose. If the heart rate is less than 60 per minute hold the drug and notify the prescriber. Teach patients going home on this drug how to perform a pulse check and when to notify the prescriber.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 296

questions.stem: In which class of antidysrhythmic drugs do beta blockers belong?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Class I

choices.label: B
choices.text: Class II

choices.label: C
choices.text: Class III

choices.label: D
choices.text: Class IV

questions.rationale: Beta blockers are class II antidysrhythmic drugs. Class I drugs include sodium channel blockers. Class III drugs include potassium channel blockers. Class IV drugs include the calcium channel blockers. Although beta blockers can be considered class III drugs, their main class is class II.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Remembering   REF:  p. 304

questions.stem: Which patient problem can be made worse by beta blocker drugs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Depression

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glaucoma

choices.label: C
choices.text: Indigestion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Heat intolerance

questions.rationale: Depression is a side effect that has been associated with taking  beta blockers. A patient with a history of depression may notice that it becomes worse while taking these drugs. Beta blockers may also cause depression for the first time.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Remembering   REF:  p. 304

questions.stem: By what route is the potassium channel blocker ibutilide (Corvert) usually given?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Oral tablet

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sublingual tablet

choices.label: C
choices.text: Subcutaneously

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenously

questions.rationale: Ibutilide is administered by the IV route and the dosage is weight based (if a patient weighs more than 60 kg: 1 mg IV over 10 minutes; less than 60 kg: 0.01 mg/kg IV over 10 minutes; dose can be repeated once after 10 minutes if necessary).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 305

questions.stem: What is the most common side effect of adenosine (Adenocard)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nightmares

choices.label: B
choices.text: Constipation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Facial flushing

choices.label: D
choices.text: Leg and ankle swelling

questions.rationale: Common side effects of adenosine include facial flushing, shortness of breath, and transient dysrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation and atrial flutter.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 308

questions.stem: What type of dysrhythmia or cardiac problem is usually treated with magnesium sulfate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Asystole

choices.label: B
choices.text: Heart block

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chest pain

choices.label: D
choices.text: Torsades de pointes

questions.rationale: Magnesium sulfate is used intravenously to prevent the ventricular dysrhythmia torsades de pointes from returning after a patient has been defibrillated (given an electric shock) to return into a normal rhythm. An IV magnesium sulfate bolus can sometimes eliminate torsades de pointes in a patient who is not symptomatic.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 309 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: A patient’s heart rate is regular at 68 beats/min. The electrocardiogram (ECG) tracing shows P waves before every QRS complex. What is the likely pacemaker of the heart?

choices.label: A
choices.text: SA node

choices.label: B
choices.text: AV node

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bundle of His

choices.label: D
choices.text: Purkinje fibers

questions.rationale: The SA node initiates electrical impulses at a rate of 60 to 100 per minute. When the ECG shows P waves before every QRS complex with a rate between 60 and 100 per minute, the rhythm is a normal sinus rhythm which is initiated by the SA node.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 292

questions.stem: A patient whose heart rate is 52 beats/min reports feeling dizzy and light-headed. What is your best first action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Start an IV line.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask if the patient has experienced this before.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Notify the prescriber immediately.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Check the patient’s blood pressure.

questions.rationale: A slow heart rate (less than 50 beats/min) results in decreased in cardiac output, blood pressure, and perfusion to a patient’s vital organs. This leads to symptoms such as dizziness, light- headedness, syncope, and decreased peripheral pulses. While you will want to notify the prescriber, the patient’s blood pressure should be checked first.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: pp. 294-295

questions.stem: A patient on a telemetry monitor reports feeling like the heart is skipping beats, and asks you what could be causing this. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Have you been exercising recently?”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Do you notice any other symptoms when your heart skips beats?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “You will have to tell your prescriber about this and ask him or her what is the cause.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Let me first listen to your heart, measure your blood pressure, and check your heart monitor.”

questions.rationale: To answer the patient’s question, you need to gather more information including  heart rhythm, blood pressure, and ECG tracing. You may also want to notify the prescriber about the patient’s sensation of skipped heartbeats.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 296

questions.stem: A patient with an upper respiratory infection reports feeling like the heart is pounding. After finding out that the patient does not having chest pain or difficulty breathing, what is the most important question you must ask the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “How long have you had upper respiratory infection symptoms?”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Are you using any over-the-counter cold or cough drugs?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Is this the first time you have felt like this?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “What exercise have you done today?”

questions.rationale: Over-the-counter cold and cough drugs contain substances that can cause a rapid heart rate (tachycardia).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 296

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed digoxin. The patient’s apical heart rate is 58 beats/min. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Give the drug as ordered.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document the finding because this is an expected effect of the drug.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recheck the heart rate and blood pressure after 30 minutes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hold the drug and notify the prescriber.

questions.rationale: Digoxin can cause a decrease in heart rate. You must check the apical heart rate for a full minute before giving this drug. If the heart rate is less than 60 beats/min, hold the drug and notify the prescriber.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 301

questions.stem: A patient given atropine (atropine sulfate) intravenously as a one-time dose for bradycardia now reports a very dry mouth. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Notify the prescriber immediately.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document the report as the only action.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reassure the patient that this is a normal drug response.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Offer the patient the opportunity to brush his or her teeth and rinse the mouth.

questions.rationale: A dry mouth is an expected response to atropine, which inhibits oral secretions. You should also offer the patient the opportunity to brush his or her teeth and rinse the mouth; however, the first action is to relieve the patient’s concerns about this side effect.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 297

questions.stem: A 70-year-old patient who has been taking digoxin (Lanoxin) for 4 years has all of the following laboratory blood tests. For which test value should you notify the prescriber immediately?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sodium (Na) 132 mEq/L

choices.label: B
choices.text: Potassium (K) 2.1 mEq/L

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) 9 mg/dL

choices.label: D
choices.text: International normalized ratio (INR) 1.5

questions.rationale: Although all of these values are abnormal, only the potassium level is dangerously out of the normal range (it is low, indicating hypokalemia; normal is 3.5 to 5 mEq/L). Abnormal potassium levels change the effectiveness of digoxin. In the case of hypokalemia, the sensitivity of the cardiac muscle membrane is increased to the effects of digoxin. This means that the risk for toxicity is greatly increased. The prescriber will probably order a digoxin level and lower the dosage of the drug. The patient is also at risk for cardiac rhythm changes.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF:  p. 298

questions.stem: Nausea, vomiting, and an irregular heart rate develop in a patient who takes oral digoxin every morning. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Give prescribed promethazine (Phenergan) as needed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check the patient’s cardiac monitor strip.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assess the apical pulse for a full minute.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Check laboratory results for a digoxin level.

questions.rationale: Signs of digoxin overdose (toxicity) include nausea, vomiting, loss of appetite, diarrhea, and vision problems. Other signs include heart rate or rhythm changes, palpitations, and fainting. When these signs and symptoms occur, hold the dose, notify the prescriber, and check the patient’s serum digoxin level. This drug has a very narrow therapeutic range (0.8 to 2 ng/mL).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 301

questions.stem: A patient who is prescribed oral quinidine (Quinaglute) for a tachydysrhythmia is on a cardiac monitor, which shows lengthening PR intervals. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Perform a 12-lead electrocardiogram (ECG).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Send a serum potassium level to the laboratory.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Document this as an expected action of the drug.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Notify the prescriber immediately.

questions.rationale: Quinidine is a class Ia antidysrhythmic drug that can slow the conduction of electrical impulses through the heart and lengthen the interval between the QRS complex and the T wave. This increases the patient’s risk for development of an abnormal life-threatening ventricular rhythm called torsades de pointes, which can rapidly lead to ventricular fibrillation.
