questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 300

questions.stem: Which statement by a patient who has been prescribed quinidine (Quinaglute) indicates the need for additional teaching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I use over-the-counter St. John’s wort to help with my memory.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I am careful not to eat too much food that is high in alkaline ash.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I check my heart rate and blood pressure at least once a day.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I take a multivitamin and calcium supplement daily.”

questions.rationale: Taking St. John’s wort at the same time as quinidine can cause decreased serum levels of the drug and decrease the intended effects of the drug. Teach patients to avoid this combination.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 301

questions.stem: An older adult patient with frequent premature ventricular contractions (PVCs) is receiving intravenous (IV) lidocaine (Xylocaine) by continuous infusion. The patient becomes confused and sees insects on the walls. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reorient the patient to person, place, and time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the patient’s family about alcohol use or abuse.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check the patient’s chart for a history of dementia.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Slow the infusion and notify the prescriber immediately.

questions.rationale: With older adults, signs of confusion may indicate lidocaine toxicity. Older adults are more sensitive to the effects and side effects of this drug. Notify the prescriber immediately; the drug must be stopped and the patient started on another antidysrhythmic drug to prevent life- threatening dysrhythmias such as ventricular tachycardia. Once the drug is discontinued and metabolized, these symptoms will resolve.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 303

questions.stem: A patient who is prescribed propafenone (Rythmol) asks how the drug works. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “It slows the conduction of electrical impulses in your heart.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “It increases your heart rate and the blood flow to your tissues.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “It prevents your heart muscle from sensing electrical impulses.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “It keeps the conduction of electrical impulses completely normal.”

questions.rationale: Propafenone (Rythmol) is a class Ic oral antidysrhythmic drug that works by slowing the conduction of electrical impulses in the heart. The results of these drugs include decreased episodes of ventricular and supraventricular dysrhythmias.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 303

questions.stem: A patient is to receive propafenone (Rythmol). What important question should you ask the patient before giving the first dose?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Do you have any hearing problems?”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Are you having difficulty reading?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Have you ever had a problem with asthma?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “What other problems are being treated by your prescriber?”

questions.rationale: When a patient is prescribed propafenone, you should always check whether the patient has a history of asthma. This drug blocks beta-adrenergic activity and can cause bronchospasm which worsens asthma.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 304

questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed acebutolol (Sectral) develops a heart rate of 48 beats/min, dizziness, difficulty breathing, and bluish fingernails. What is your best first action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Instruct the patient to take several slow deep breaths.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Place the patient in high Fowler’s position.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hold the drug and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Apply oxygen by mask or nasal cannula.

questions.rationale: Acebutolol is a beta blocker. Signs of drug overdose for these drugs include a very slow heart rate, severe dizziness or fainting, difficult breathing, bluish-colored fingernails or palms, and seizures. Although you should hold the drug and immediately notify the prescriber, the best first action for this patient who is showing signs of acute hypoxia is to apply oxygen, even when there might not be an order for it. This is an emergency situation.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 304

questions.stem: A patient who has a history of depression is prescribed propranolol (Inderal). Which precaution is most important for you to teach the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “You should expect a decrease in depression symptoms while you are taking propranolol.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “While taking propranolol you may notice that your depression gets worse.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Stop taking propranolol whenever you experience depression symptoms.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Propranolol may cause difficulty with your ability to perform sexually.”

questions.rationale: Depression is a side effect associated with taking beta blockers such as propranolol. The depression may be new onset, or a patient with a history of depression may find that it gets worse while taking these drugs. The patient should be instructed to report increased depression symptoms to the prescriber. The patient should not suddenly stop taking the drug. Although beta blockers often do affect sexual ability, this is not related to depression.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 304

questions.stem: What is the most important action when caring for a patient with type 2 diabetes mellitus who is prescribed a beta blocker?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assess the patient’s feet daily.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Monitor for infection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer oral diabetic drugs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Regularly check the patient’s blood glucose.

questions.rationale: Beta blockers can increase or decrease blood glucose levels. They can also mask the signs of hypoglycemia such as rapid heart rate, making it difficult to recognize and treat. Whereas all of these actions are important, the most important action related to a diabetic patient receiving a beta blocker is to regularly check the patient’s blood glucose.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 304

questions.stem: A patient who is prescribed sotalol (Betapace) develops the following problems. For which finding should you immediately notify the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Heart rate of 60 beats/min

choices.label: B
choices.text: Difficulty sleeping

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cold hands and feet

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chest discomfort

questions.rationale: All of these assessment findings can be side effects of sotalol, a beta blocker. However, if a patient develops chest pain it should be reported to the prescriber immediately because this symptom may indicate heart disease and require additional diagnostic testing. In addition, safe activity levels should be discussed with the patient.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 304

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed an oral loading dose of amiodarone (Cordarone) 1600 mg. Amiodarone is available in 400 mg tablets. How many tablets should you give?
questions.rationale: Want 1600 mg/X tablets, Have 400 mg/1 tablet; 1600/400 = 4 tablets.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 305

questions.stem: A patient prescribed amiodarone (Cordarone) tells you that his scrotum is swollen and painful. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Instruct the patient that this side effect is reversible and will go away over several months.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document this expected side effect as the only action.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Support the patient’s scrotum on a pillow.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hold the drug and notify the prescriber.

questions.rationale: A male patient may experience pain or swelling in the scrotum while taking amiodarone. This should be reported to the prescriber immediately so that the drug dosage can be decreased.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 307

questions.stem: An older patient with a history of liver impairment is prescribed diltiazem (Cardizem) SR 120 mg twice a day for supraventricular tachycardia. What is your best action before giving the first dose?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hold the drug and contact the prescriber.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer the drug as ordered.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give 60 mg four times a day.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Give an XR capsule of 240 mg once a day.

questions.rationale: When a calcium channel blocker such as diltiazem is prescribed for an older patient, or a patient with hepatic (liver) or renal (kidney) impairment, lower than normal initial doses are prescribed. You should hold the drug and contact the prescriber about the dosage that has been prescribed for this patient.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 307

questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed verapamil (Calan) develops skin lesions, itching, achy joints, and a temperature of 101o F. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer an as-needed dose of diphenhydramine (Benadryl).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Contact the prescriber and request an order for acetaminophen 650 mg.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Draw a set of blood cultures to detect any infection.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hold the drug and notify the prescriber.

questions.rationale: Skin lesions, itching, achy joints, and fever are signs of Stevens-Johnson syndrome, an adverse effect of class IV antidysrhythmic drugs (calcium channel blockers). These symptoms should be reported immediately to the prescriber and the drug should be held because this syndrome has the potential to be fatal.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 307

questions.stem: Why is adenosine (Adenocard) given to a patient rapidly by intravenous (IV) push?

choices.label: A
choices.text: If given slowly, the heart rate will dramatically increase.

choices.label: B
choices.text: When given slowly, the drug is eliminated before it can act on the heart.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Slow administration can lead to tissue irritation and IV infiltration.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The drug is given rapidly so that within a minute, second or third doses may be given if needed.

questions.rationale: Adenosine is an IV drug used to treat supraventricular tachycardia. It should be given as a rapid IV bolus injection (1 to 2 seconds). When given slowly, adeno sine is eliminated from the body before it can get to the heart and act to slow down the rhythm.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Understanding   REF:  pp. 307-308

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed intravenous (IV) adenosine (Adenocard) for treatment of supraventricular tachycardia. Which safety precaution is most important to perform before this drug is administered?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bring the crash cart and defibrillator to the patient’s bedside.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Make sure that all four bed side rails are in the upright position.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Place the patient’s bed in the lowest position.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Continuously monitor blood pressure.

questions.rationale: Adenosine slows electrical impulse conduction through the AV node. It is always given rapidly by IV push (1 to 2 seconds). After the drug is given, there is usually a very brief period of asystole (when the heart stops beating) before the heart resumes a normal rhythm. Because of this, emergency equipment must be available at the patient’s bedside before the drug is given.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 308

questions.stem: A patient who received an intravenous (IV) dose of magnesium sulfate has developed diarrhea. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reassure the patient that diarrhea is a common side effect of magnesium sulfate.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Order a bedside commode so that the patient does not have to walk to the bathroom.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hold the drug and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Document the finding as the only action.

questions.rationale: The only common side effect of IV magnesium sulfate is diarrhea. Additional actions would include assisting the patient to the bathroom and providing skin care to prevent breakdown.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 309

questions.stem: What should you teach any patient prescribed an antidysrhythmic drug to do every day?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Keep accurate intake and output records.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Drink half of a glass of water with each drug.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check heart rate and blood pressure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Participate in an aerobic exercise program.

questions.rationale: Teach patients who have been prescribed antidysrhythmic drugs to check and record their heart rate and blood pressure daily.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 296

questions.stem: What dose of atropine is prescribed to be administered via endotracheal tube?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The same dose as normal

choices.label: B
choices.text: Twice the normal dose

choices.label: C
choices.text: Three times the normal dose

choices.label: D
choices.text: Four times the normal dose

questions.rationale: Give twice the normal dose when atropine is given by ET tube. Mix the dose with 5 to 10 mL normal saline. Use the acronym NAVEL to remember drugs that may be given by ET tube: narcan, atropine, Valium, epinephrine, and lidocaine.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 297

questions.stem: The prescriber orders atropine 0.3 mg IV push for an adult patient with symptomatic bradycardia. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Contact the prescriber and question the order.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Give the drug exactly as prescribed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer a prepared amp of 1 mg atropine in 5 mL NS.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Give half an amp of prepared atropine 1 mg in 5 mL NS.

questions.rationale: Contact the prescriber and question the order because administering a dose of atropine less than 0.5 mg may make the bradycardia worse (paradoxical bradycardia). The normal dose for an adult is 0.5 to 1 mg IV every 5 minutes up to 3 mg or 0.04 mg/kg.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 297

questions.stem: What advice would you give a patient prescribed tocainide (Tonocard) to avoid an adverse effect of this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Be sure to avoid foods that are high in alkaline ash such as citrus fruits.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Wear protective clothing and a strong sunscreen when you go outdoors.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Avoid large crowds and people with flu or cold symptoms.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Report any pulmonary symptoms (e.g., cough) to your prescriber right away.”

questions.rationale: Decreased white blood cell count with increased risk for infection (neutropenia) is an adverse effect of both tocainide and phenytoin.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 302

questions.stem: For signs and symptom of which endocrine problem must you monitor when a patient is prescribed amiodarone (Cordarone)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypoaldosteronism

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hyperaldosteronism

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypothyroidism

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hyperthyroidism

questions.rationale: Monitor for signs of thyroid problems such as changes in heart rate, which are more likely to occur during the first few weeks of treatment with amiodarone (Cordarone).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 306

questions.stem: How should you prepare to administer adenosine (Adenocard) to a patient with persistent supraventricular tachycardia (SVT)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sublingual spray

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rapid IV bolus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Continuous slow infusion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Continuous transdermal patch

questions.rationale: Adenosine is rapidly metabolized and rendered ineffective. Its onset of action is immediate when given by IV bolus.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 307

questions.stem: A patient who received an oral dose of procainamide immediate release an hour ago at 9:00 a.m. is now prescribed to receive an oral dose of disopyramide. When is the earliest you should give the disopyramide?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediately

choices.label: B
choices.text: 12:00 noon

questions.rationale: Disopyramide should not be given close to the time procainamide was taken to avoid serious complications. The earliest it can be given after the immediate release formulation of procainamide is 6 hours.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 300 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which antidysrhythmic drugs may be given by the endotracheal tube route? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atropine (Atropine Sulfate)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Digoxin (Lanoxin)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Epinephrine (Adrenalin)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lidocaine (Xylocaine)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Procainamide (Pronestyl)

questions.rationale: Use the acronym NAVEL to remember which drugs may be given by endotracheal tube: narcan, atropine, Valium, epinephrine, and lidocaine.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 297

questions.stem: Which foods should you teach patients are good sources of magnesium? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Baked potato

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bran cereal

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yogurt

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whole-grain rice

choices.label: E
choices.text: Bananas

questions.rationale: Baked potato, yogurt, and bananas are good sources for potassium. Box 17-4 lists additional food sources for magnesium.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 309 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed sotalol (Betapace) 320 mg orally once a day. Sotalol comes in 80 mg tablets. How many tablets would you give for each daily dose?
questions.rationale: Want 320 mg/X tablets, Have 80 mg/1 tablet; 320/80 = 4 tablets.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 305

questions.stem: A pediatric patient is prescribed 0.008 mg of digoxin (Lanoxin). The drug on hand is digoxin 0.05 mg/mL. One milliliter equals 15 gtt. How many drops (gtt) will you give?
questions.rationale: Want 0.008 mg/X mL, Have 0.050 mg/1 mL; 0.008/0.050 = 0.16  1 mL = 0.16 mL, at 15 gtt/mL = 0.16  15 = 2.4 gtt, rounded down to 2 gtt.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 298

questions.stem: A patient who weighs 126 lb is prescribed to receive an intramuscular dose of procainamide (Pronestyl) of 12.5 mg/kg immediately. The available parenteral form of procainamide is a solution with a concentration of 500 mg/mL. How many milliliters will you prepare for the correct dose?
questions.rationale: 1 kg = 2.2 lb. The patient’s weight in kilogram is 57.28 (126/2.2), round up to 57.3 kg. The correct dosage is 57.3  12.5 mg, which is 716.25 mg. Because the concentration for 1 mL is already known, divide the dose you want (716.25 mg) by the dose you have (500 mg), which is 1.43 mL. Round down to 1.4 mL.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 299 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: What does the term “rhabdomyolysis” mean?

choices.label: A
choices.text: High blood lipid levels

choices.label: B
choices.text: Skeletal muscle breakdown

choices.label: C
choices.text: Coronary artery disease from atherosclerosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Skin yellowing (jaundice) from liver complications

questions.rationale: Patients taking the “statin” type of cholesterol-lowering drugs may develop rhabdomyolysis (muscle breakdown). Signs and symptoms of rhabdomyolysis include general muscle soreness, muscle pain and weakness, vomiting, stomach pain, and brown urine. The urine turns brown because small reddish-brown pieces of broken-down muscle are removed from the body through the urine.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 315

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: What problem is usually caused by high blood lipid levels?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atherosclerosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blindness from glaucoma

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chronic hypotension

choices.label: D
choices.text: Deadly dysrhythmias

questions.rationale: Cholesterol is produced by the liver, and it is in the foods that you eat. However, your body needs only a small amount of cholesterol; too much cholesterol in the bloodstream can deposit in the walls of blood vessels forming plaque that contributes to the development of narrowed arteries such as atherosclerosis and coronary artery disease.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 312

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: What enzyme is inhibited by the “statin” type of lipid-lowering drugs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Integrase

choices.label: B
choices.text: Alteplase

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reverse transcriptase

choices.label: D
choices.text: HMG CoA reductase

questions.rationale: Statins inhibit HMG CoA reductase, an enzyme that controls cholesterol production in the body. They lower blood lipid levels by slowing the production of cholesterol and increasing the ability of the liver to remove LDL cholesterol from the blood. Statins are the most effective group of these drugs for lowering LDL cholesterol.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 314

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: For which patient should statin drugs be avoided?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Children over age 8 years

choices.label: B
choices.text: Women who are pregnant

choices.label: C
choices.text: Men who are over age 65 years

choices.label: D
choices.text: Women who are over age 65 years

questions.rationale: Statins should not be given to women who are pregnant, plan to become pregnant, or are breastfeeding. Statins decrease the amount of fat in the body. Fat is essential to brain development in the fetus and infant. When there is not enough fat in the body during pregnancy and infancy, the fetus can suffer poor brain development and mental retardation.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 316

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: How do bile acid sequestrants work to lower blood lipids?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increasing the intestinal excretion of dietary fat

choices.label: B
choices.text: Preventing the liver from making excess lipids

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreasing the movement of blood lipids into blood vessel cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: Moving extra lipids out of atheromas and into the intestinal tract

questions.rationale: Bile acid sequestrants help the body lose cholesterol. The drugs are taken by mouth and work directly on dietary fats in the intestine. They bind with cholesterol in the intestine, preventing the fats from being absorbed into the blood. This action then eliminates the cholesterol from the body through the stool.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 316

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: What is the most common side effect of bile acid sequestrants?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intestinal gas

choices.label: B
choices.text: Facial flushing

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blurred vision

choices.label: D
choices.text: Muscle weakness

questions.rationale: Side effects of bile acid sequestrants are rarely serious. The most common side effects are gastrointestinal symptoms, including constipation, bloating, nausea, vomiting, and gas.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 316

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: What is one of the main intended responses of fibrates?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased triglycerides

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased blood clotting

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased LDL cholesterol

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased HDL cholesterol

questions.rationale: Intended responses of fibrates include: triglycerides are decreased and HDL cholesterol is mildly increased.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 319

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: What is the most common side effect of nicotinic acid agents?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Itching

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blurred vision

choices.label: C
choices.text: Muscle weakness

choices.label: D
choices.text: High blood pressure

questions.rationale: Nicotinic acid agents may cause many side effects. The most common are itching and nasal inflammation because the drug makes blood vessels dilate. Other common side effects include gastrointestinal symptoms such as nausea, indigestion, gas, vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal pain. The patient may also experience flushing (redness) and hot flashes, chills, dizziness, fainting, headaches, rapid heart rate (tachycardia), shortness of breath (dyspnea), sweating (diaphoresis), and swelling caused by fluid retention.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 320

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: Which lipid-lowering drug type can cause gout?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Statins

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fibrates

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nicotinic acid agents

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bile acid sequestrants

questions.rationale: Nicotinic acid agents can cause the adverse effect of gout. Gout (painful swelling and redness of the toes, feet, or ankles) can occur because of a buildup of excess uric acid and calcium.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 320

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: Which lipid-lowering drug type can cause gallstones?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Statins

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fibrates

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nicotinic acid agents

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bile acid sequestrants

questions.rationale: Fibrates increase cholesterol loss in bile, which may lead to the development of cholesterol- based gallstones.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 319

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: Which statement about cholesterol is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The only source of cholesterol is from the foods a person eats.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cholesterol always has harmful effects on a person’s body.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Too much cholesterol leads to a deficit of bile acids that digest fat.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is a fatty, waxy material present in cell membranes of the body.

questions.rationale: Cholesterol is a fatty, waxy material that the body needs to function and is present in cell membranes everywhere in the body. There are two sources of cholesterol: food and the liver. The body needs small amounts of cholesterol for important functions including production of hormones, vitamin D, and bile acids that help digest fat. Inadequate amounts of cholesterol would lead to a deficit of bile acids.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 312 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: A patient has been prescribed an antihyperlipidemic drug. Which laboratory value should you report to the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Total cholesterol 198 mg/dL

choices.label: B
choices.text: Triglycerides 135 mg/dL

choices.label: C
choices.text: Low-density lipoprotein (LDL) 195 mg/dL

choices.label: D
choices.text: High-density lipoprotein (HDL) 60 mg/dL

questions.rationale: The total cholesterol, triglyceride, and HDL levels are all within the normal range. The LDL level is high and should be reported to the prescriber because a high level increases the patient’s risk for atherosclerosis.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 312, Table 19-1

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: A patient with high blood lipids asks why the lipid profile did not improve after 3 months following a low-fat diet. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “You may need to follow a no-fat diet to improve your lipid profile.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “You must follow a low-fat diet for at least 6 months to see improvement.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “You will definitely need to be prescribed a drug to see improvement.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “You may have a genetic factor that is causing your high blood lipid levels.”

questions.rationale: People who do not show lipid profile improvement after following a low-fat diet for 3 months often have a genetic factor that leads to familial hyperlipidemia. This tends to run in families and requires antilipidemic drugs to lower blood lipid levels. Telling the patient that a drug is definitely required may be correct in this case, but it does not address the patient’s question.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 312

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: Which statement by a patient who has been prescribed an antilipidemic drug indicates the need for additional teaching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Once my lipid profile levels are normal, I will no longer need to take the drug.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Taking this drug will decrease my risk for having a heart attack.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “My goal is to increase my HDL cholesterol and decrease my LDL cholesterol.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I will continue walking and watching the fat in my diet while I’m taking this drug.”

questions.rationale: All lipid-lowering drugs reduce high blood lipid levels, but they do not cure the problem. Treatment is long term, and these drugs need to be taken even after blood fat levels are normal.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 314

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: How do antihyperlipidemic drugs from the statin class lower “bad” cholesterol levels?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They act as a filter in the blood to trap bad cholesterol and allow white blood cells to destroy it.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They block the absorption of dietary fats through the walls of the intestinal tract.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They bind to cholesterol in the intestinal tract and promote its excretion in stool.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They inhibit HMG CoA reductase and decrease normal liver production of cholesterol.

questions.rationale: Statins inhibit HMG CoA reductase, an enzyme that controls cholesterol production in the body. They lower blood lipid levels by slowing the production of cholesterol and increasing the ability of the liver to remove LDL cholesterol from the blood.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 311

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: Which laboratory finding should be reported to the prescriber before giving any antihyperlipidemic drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aspartate aminotransferase 41 IU/L

choices.label: B
choices.text: Alanine aminotransferase 24 IU/L

choices.label: C
choices.text: Alkaline phosphatase 130 IU/L

choices.label: D
choices.text: Gamma-glutamyltransferase 50 IU/L

questions.rationale: The normal range for aspartate aminotransferase is 10 to 34 IU/L. This laboratory value is high and indicates that the patient has liver problems. One of the side effects of these drugs is liver damage. All of the other liver functions tests are within normal limits. See Table 1-3.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 314

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: A patient prescribed atorvastatin (Lipitor) reports all of the following problems or changes since starting this drug. Which problem or change do you report to the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Abdominal cramps and bloating

choices.label: B
choices.text: Muscle aches and weakness

choices.label: C
choices.text: Urinating more at night

choices.label: D
choices.text: Loss of taste for sweets

questions.rationale: Atorvastatin can cause the adverse reaction of rhabdomyolysis, which is the breakdown of skeletal muscle. The symptoms of this problem are muscle aches and weakness. When a patient develops this problem, the drug should be stopped and a different antihyperlipidemic drug started.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF: p. 315

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: A patient prescribed atorvastatin (Lipitor) reports experiencing some muscle pain and weakness. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer ordered acetaminophen (Tylenol) as needed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document the finding as the only action.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reassure the patient that this is an expected side effect.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hold the drug and notify the prescriber.

questions.rationale: Atorvastatin is an HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor (statin) drug. Patients may develop rhabdomyolysis (muscle breakdown) as an adverse effect of these drugs. Signs and symptoms include general muscle soreness, muscle pain, and weakness. The drug should be held and the prescriber notified. The patient will need to be prescribed another antihyperlipidemic drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 315

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed lovastatin (Mevacor) asks why all urine must be saved for intake and output measurements. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “All patients on this unit have orders for strict intake and output measurements.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Intake and output measurements are important indicators of how well your kidneys are functioning.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “A side effect of this drug can be blockage of urine flow through the kidneys and decreased urine output.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Sometimes this drug can cause the kidneys to make extra urine resulting in increased urine output and dehydration.”

questions.rationale: Renal failure can occur if rhabdomyolysis (muscle breakdown) happens as an adverse effect of statin drugs. The protein released from broken-down muscle can block urine flow through the kidneys leading to decreased urine output. Telling the patient that intake and output measurement is an important assessment of kidney function is essentially correct, but it does not fully address the patient’s question.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 315

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: A patient who is prescribed simvastatin (Zocor) asks why liver function tests must be drawn every 6 months. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “The tests are important because early liver problems do not cause symptoms.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “The tests help your prescriber decide what your dose of simvastatin should be.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “The tests tell your prescriber how well your body is responding to the therapy.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “The tests indicate how much cholesterol is being produced by your liver.”

questions.rationale: Simvastatin is a statin drug. These drugs may cause decreased liver function. Because of this, the prescriber orders liver function tests every 3 to 6 months. The tests are essential and important because early or mild liver problems may not cause symptoms.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 315

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: Which statement made by a patient who has been prescribed simvastatin (Zocor) indicates the need for more teaching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I have been taking this drug every night at bedtime as directed by my pharmacist.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I have stopped using butter when I cook and have been using olive oil instead.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “My LDL cholesterol level today is 101 mg/dL so the drug has cured my cholesterol problem.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I hope that by reducing my fatty food intake, I might not have to take medications to manage my cholesterol problem.”

questions.rationale: Statin drugs can help control LDL cholesterol levels but do not cure hyperlipidemia. For some people, lifestyle changes and reduced intake of cholesterol and saturated fats can lower cholesterol levels; however, no one is considered cured of hyperlipidemia based on a single test of blood cholesterol level. The fact that this patient’s LDL cholesterol is in the normal range is likely a response to the drug. Continuing to have a normal LDL level is most likely to require continued therapy with simvastatin.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 314

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: A hospitalized patient who is prescribed lovastatin (Mevacor) 80 mg orally looks at the four tablets you provides and says, “At home, I only take two pills for 80 mg. I think this is too many pills.” What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hold the dose and contact the prescriber or pharmacist to confirm that the order is correct.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Remind the patient that the prescriber knows what is best for his or her particular health problem.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Explain that each tablet contains 20 mg of the drug instead of 40 mg, and that four 20 mg tablets are the same dosage as two 40 mg tablets.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Explain to the patient that while at home a lower dosage of the drug may have been prescribed than what is required while being hospitalized.

questions.rationale: There are many different dosage strengths for this drug, including both 20 and 40 mg tablets. The patient is right to question a dose that seems different from what is taken at home. You know that the drug available on the hospital unit is available as lovastatin 20 mg tablets and should reassure the patient by explaining the differences in tablet content. If further evidence is needed to completely reassure the patient, you can provide the drug container.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 314

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: What should you teach a woman of childbearing age who is prescribed a statin drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “These drugs should not be taken during pregnancy, but are safe to use when breastfeeding.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Statins drugs can interfere with fetal brain development and should not be taken during . pregnancy or breastfeeding.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Because these drugs control fats produced by the liver, they will help control weight gain during pregnancy.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “You can take these drugs during pregnancy as long as you do not have a history of muscle or liver problems.”

questions.rationale: Statin drugs have a high likelihood of increasing the risk for birth defects or fetal damage. They should not be taken by women who plan to become pregnant, are pregnant, or are breastfeeding. These drugs decrease the amount of fat in the body. Fat is essential to brain development in the fetus and infant. When there is not enough fat in the body during pregnancy and infancy, the results can be poor brain development and mental retardation.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 316

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: Why do the most common side effects of any bile acid sequestrant include bloating, abdominal discomfort, and constipation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Many patients are lactose intolerant and these drugs contain lactose.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bile acid sequestrants exert their effects directly in the intestinal tract.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The action of bile acid sequestrants on the liver releases bile into the intestinal tract.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The drugs inhibit the absorption of dietary fiber, increasing its concentration and effects in the intestinal tract.

questions.rationale: Bile acid sequestrants are taken by mouth and work directly on dietary fats in the intestinal tract. They bind with cholesterol in the intestine, preventing fats from being absorbed into the blood. This action then eliminates the cholesterol from the body through the stool and is also likely to change the general activity of the intestinal tract, leading to intestinal side effects.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 316

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: A patient who is prescribed cholestyramine (Questran) tablets reports experiencing bloating, nausea, and gas. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hold the drug and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document the finding as the only action.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer the drug before meals to avoid nausea.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Teach the patient to drink 12 to 16 ounces of water with the drug.

questions.rationale: GI symptoms including constipation, bloating, nausea, vomiting, and gas are common side effects of bile acid sequestrants such as cholestyramine. The drug should not be held. You should teach the patient to take the tablet form of this drug with at least 12 to 16 ounces of water to prevent stomach and intestinal problems such as bowel obstruction. Administering the drug with meals can help to decrease GI symptoms and aid their action, which is to bind with cholesterol in the intestines.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 317

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: Which laboratory blood value is most important for you to monitor when a patient is prescribed both colestipol (Colestid) and warfarin (Coumadin)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hematocrit

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hemoglobin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Red blood cell (RBC) count

choices.label: D
choices.text: International normalized ratio (INR)

questions.rationale: Colestipol can change the action of the anticoagulant warfarin in two ways. It can decrease the absorption of vitamin K, which would intensify the effects of warfarin and increase the risk for bleeding (as evidenced by a high INR). Bile acid sequestrants can also directly bind warfarin in the intestinal tract and cause its rapid elimination. This action would inactivate warfarin activity and increase the risk for clot formation (as evidenced by an INR level that is lower than the therapeutic range). Although all of the above laboratory blood values can help diagnose a bleeding problem, the most sensitive test is the INR.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 320

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: A patient prescribed ezetimibe (Zetia) has developed swelling around the eyes, nose, and lips. What is your priority action at this time?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Elevate the head of the bed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Notify the prescriber.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assess the patient’s airway.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Check the patient’s blood pressure.

questions.rationale: Swelling under the skin, usually around the eyes, nose, and lips are symptoms of angioedema, a rare adverse effect of ezetimibe. It is caused by blood vessel dilation and may be life threatening when it affects the airways.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 318

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: A patient who has been taking niacin (Niacor) daily for 1 week reports intense itching. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “This is a common side effect and many people can control it by taking aspirin.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Stop taking the drug and talk with your prescriber as soon as possible.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Unless your skin forms blisters or peels, continue to take the drug.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Do you have any other drug, food, pet, or pollen allergies?”

questions.rationale: Niacin causes a mild systemic inflammatory response with vasodilation that can result in the sensation of itching. This is not an allergy but can be very distressing to the patient. Often, taking aspirin reduces the inflammatory response and eliminates the itchiness. If the patient has no other health problems that would be worsened by aspirin, this should be tried first.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 320

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: A patient who is prescribed niacin (Niacor) reports experiencing flushing and hot flashes. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hold the drug and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Give the niacin at least 1 hour before meals.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reassure the patient that this is an expected side effect.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administer the ordered NSAID 30 minutes before the niacin.

questions.rationale: Flushing (redness) and hot flashes can be reduced by the use of aspirin or an NSAID 30 minutes before the niacin, or by giving the niacin during or after meals. While reassuring the patient that flushing and hot flashes are expected side effects is correct, it does not address the patient’s discomfort.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 321

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: A patient prescribed niacin extended action (Niaspan) reports all of the following new onset problems. For which problem should you notify the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Moderate itchiness, especially at night

choices.label: B
choices.text: Six to seven loose stools daily

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased nighttime urination

choices.label: D
choices.text: Swollen, painful great toe

questions.rationale: One adverse reaction to niacin is an increase in uric acid crystals and calcium that can precipitate in joints and cause gout. The great toe is one of the most common sites of gout. The condition is painful and reduces mobility. Usually, the patient can continue the niacin therapy along with another drug that reduces uric acid production. Teach the patient that drinking plenty of water can help reduce the precipitation of uric acid.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 320

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: A patient is prescribed niacin (Niaspan) 500 mg orally. The pharmacy sends niacin (Niacor) 500 mg. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hold the drug and contact the prescriber.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer the Niacor in place of the Niaspan.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the pharmacy to send the patient’s ordered Niaspan.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Check the patient’s chart to find out if he or she takes Niacor at home.

questions.rationale: Niaspan is an extended-release form of niacin that is taken once a day. Niacor is an immediate- release form of niacin. Immediate-release niacin should not be substituted for extended-release niacin. Additionally, extended-release niacin should be swallowed whole and never crushed or chewed because this causes immediate release of the entire drug dose and could lead to overdose.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 320

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: What action must you take for a diabetic patient who is prescribed niacin (Niacor)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Give insulin only in the presence of food.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check the blood glucose level more often.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decrease the morning insulin dosage.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Encourage increased caloric intake.

questions.rationale: Niacin may increase blood glucose levels. You would check the patient’s blood glucose level more often. Doses of drugs used to control blood glucose may need to be increased.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 321

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: Which food, drink, or herbal supplement should you warn a patient prescribed gemfibrozil (Lopid) to avoid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Caffeinated beverages

choices.label: B
choices.text: Grapefruit juice

choices.label: C
choices.text: St. John’s wort

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dairy products

questions.rationale: The metabolism of gemfibrozil is affected by grapefruit and grapefruit juice to the extent that the drug’s activity is reduced. If possible, patients should avoid grapefruit juice completely, or at least, patients should not take gemfibrozil with grapefruit juice.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 319

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: A patient is prescribed colesevelam (WelChol) 1875 mg twice a day. Available tablets are 625 mg each. How many tablets should you administer to the patient for each dose?
questions.rationale: Need 1875 mg/X tablets, Have 625 mg/1 tablets. 1875/625 = 3 tablets.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 316

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: A patient is prescribed Cozaar (losartan) 25 mg orally for hyperlipidemia. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer the drug as ordered.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check that the dosage is correct.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Always give the drug early in the morning.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hold the drug and contact the prescriber.

questions.rationale: An order for Zocor (simvastatin) may be confused with Cozaar (losartan). Zocor is a lipid- lowering statin, whereas Cozaar is a blood pressure–lowering angiotensin II receptor antagonist.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 315

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: A patient is prescribed cholestyramine (Questran) is also taking warfarin (Coumadin). For which specific action should you be prepared?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To administer IV vitamin K

choices.label: B
choices.text: To ask the patient about diarrhea

choices.label: C
choices.text: To keep accurate intake and output records

choices.label: D
choices.text: To teach the patient about the need for a fat-free diet

questions.rationale: Bile acid sequestrants inhibit absorption of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, and K), so patients may need to take a daily vitamin supplement. Bile acid sequestrants may change the action of the anticoagulant warfarin (Coumadin) in two ways. They can decrease the absorption of vitamin K which would intensify (increase) the effects of warfarin and increase the risk of bleeding. If administering a bile acid sequestrant to a patient who is also taking warfarin, be prepared to administer vitamin K, which is the antidote for warfarin. Bile acid sequestrants can also directly bind warfarin in the intestinal tract and cause its rapid elimination.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 316

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: Why should the use of cholestyramine (Questran) and colestipol (Colestid) be avoided in children?

choices.label: A
choices.text: These drugs can cause muscle breakdown in children.

choices.label: B
choices.text: These drugs can cause intestinal obstruction in children.

choices.label: C
choices.text: These drugs can cause mental deficits in children.

choices.label: D
choices.text: These drugs can cause bleeding and easy bruising in children.

questions.rationale: Cholestyramine (Questran) and colestipol (Colestid) should be avoided in children because they can cause intestinal obstructions.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 317

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: Why should you ask a patient prescribed a “statin” drug for hyperlipidemia about his or her daily alcohol consumption?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alcohol increases the action of most statin drugs and can lead to side effects.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Statin drugs increase the action of alcoholic beverages causing a person to feel drunk only after two drinks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Taking a statin and consuming more than two alcoholic drinks per day is stressful on the liver.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Alcohol interferes with the action of statin drugs so the prescriber must order a much higher dose.

questions.rationale: Ask patients about their alcohol consumption. Statin drugs can damage the liver. Statins should not be given to patients who consume more than two alcoholic drinks per day because drinking alcohol puts even more stress on the liver.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Understanding   REF:  p. 315

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: For which patient is drug therapy with nicotinic acid contraindicated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 28-year-old who has familial hyperlipidemia

choices.label: B
choices.text: 38-year-old who had hyperthyroidism and lactose intolerance

choices.label: C
choices.text: 58-year-old who has an actively bleeding duodenal ulcer

choices.label: D
choices.text: 78-year-old who takes an oral drug for type 2 diabetes

questions.rationale: Nicotinic acid-based drugs dilate blood vessels and potentiate bleeding. Patients with any condition that includes active bleeding, such as a duodenal ulcer, should not be prescribed nicotinic acid.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Understanding   REF:  p. 320 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: Which health problems should you teach a patient can be caused by chronic hyperlipidemia? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypertension

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pancreatitis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Peptic ulcer disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: Xanthoma

choices.label: E
choices.text: Diabetes mellitus

questions.rationale: Chronic hyperlipidemia contributes to development of narrowed arteries. It can cause health problems including atherosclerosis, coronary artery disease (angina, heart attack), hypertension, pancreatitis, peripheral vascular disease, stroke, and xanthomas (skin atheromas or abnormal fat deposits).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 312

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: Which teaching points should you include for a patient prescribed any antihyperlipidemic drug? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continue to follow a low-fat diet.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Be sure to exercise regularly.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Always take these drugs with food.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fast for at least 8 hours before your lipid levels are drawn.

choices.label: E
choices.text: You will need follow-up laboratory tests once a year.

questions.rationale: Patients taking antihyperlipidemic drugs should be taught to continue lifestyle changes such as low-fat diets, exercise, and weight loss while taking the drugs. Some, but not all, are best taken with food. Follow-up laboratory specimens must be drawn every 3 to 6 months and the patient should fast for at least 8 hours before the blood is taken because test results can be changed by some substances in food or fluids.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 314

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: What are the symptoms of late liver disease? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yellowing of the skin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Light-colored diluted urine

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pale gray-colored stools

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pain on the right side just below the ribs

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reddened mucous membranes in the mouth

choices.label: F
choices.text: Increased risk for toenail fungal infections

questions.rationale: Late symptoms of liver disease include yellowing (jaundice) of the skin, the whites of the eyes, and the roof of the mouth; pain over the liver on the right side just below the ribs; darkened urine; and pale gray-colored stools. The bile and bilirubin made by the liver usually leave the body in the stool, giving it a medium to dark brown color. When the liver is not working well, these products do not reach the stool, so it is a light gray or green color instead of brown. Bilirubin enters the urine and turns it dark, and gets into the skin and mucous membranes, making them yellow.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Understanding    REF:  p. 315

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: Which actions are important when a patient is prescribed a bile acid sequestrant drug and is also prescribed warfarin (Coumadin)? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer a daily multivitamin.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Give other medications at least an hour before this drug.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Schedule the drug for 1 hour before meals.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient about symptoms of constipation.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mix powdered forms of the drug with 2 ounces of juice or water.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Check for signs and symptoms of bleeding.

questions.rationale: Bile acid sequestrants decrease the body’s ability to absorb oral drugs. Give other drugs at least an hour before these drugs. They also inhibit fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K), so patients may need to take a daily vitamin supplement. Giving the drugs with meals facilitates the drug’s action and decreases common GI side effects including constipation. Powdered forms should be mixed with 4 to 6 ounces of juice or water. If the patient is taking warfarin, monitor for signs of bleeding such as easy bruising, clammy skin, pale skin, dizziness, increased heart rate, decreased blood pressure, shortness of breath, or confusion. Monitor INRs for changes that are higher or lower than the patient’s prescribed therapeutic range.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 316

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: What are the expected and intended responses for nicotinic acid agents? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Total cholesterol is decreased.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Total triglyceride level is increased.

choices.label: C
choices.text: LDL cholesterol level is decreased.

choices.label: D
choices.text: VLDL cholesterol is increased.

choices.label: E
choices.text: HDL cholesterol level is increased.

questions.rationale: Intended responses for nicotinic acid agents include: total cholesterol level is decreased; total triglyceride level is decreased; LDL cholesterol level is decreased; and HDL cholesterol level is increased.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 320

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: What are the common side effects of gemfibrozil (Lopid)? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Diarrhea

choices.label: B
choices.text: Constipation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Heartburn

choices.label: D
choices.text: Polyps

choices.label: E
choices.text: Rash

choices.label: F
choices.text: Headache

questions.rationale: The side effects of fibrates are usually mild. The most common side effects are stomach upset and diarrhea. Other common side effects include gastrointestinal discomfort such as indigestion or heartburn (dyspepsia) and nausea. Patients may also experience muscle weakness, headache, pruritus, and rash.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 318 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for High Blood Lipids
questions.stem: A patient is prescribed to receive simvastatin (Zocor) 60 mg orally. The drug on hand is simvastatin 40 mg/scored tablet. How many tablets should you give for the correct dose?
questions.rationale: Want 60 mg, Have 40 mg/tablet. Divide 60 by 40, which equals 1.5.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 314 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which blood component forms the initial plug that helps stop bleeding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fibrin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Platelets

choices.label: C
choices.text: Neutrophils

choices.label: D
choices.text: Eosinophils

questions.rationale: While fibrin creates threads that make the plasma sticky and able to clot, it is platelets that clump together to create the initial plug that helps to stop bleeding. Neutrophils and eosinophils are types of white blood cells that help the body fight off infection.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 325

questions.stem: Which patient factor increases the risk for bleeding during anticoagulant therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pregnancy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recent major surgery

choices.label: C
choices.text: History of severe arthritis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Uncontrolled low blood pressure

questions.rationale: Recent major surgery results in damage to blood vessels. When blood vessels lose their integrity, bleeding results. It takes some time for blood vessels to heal after surgery, during which time the patient remains at risk for bleeding. When the patient is also given a drug that reduces blood clotting before all blood vessels have completely healed, the risk for bleeding is greatly increased.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 327

questions.stem: What is the name of a blood clot that forms and stays within a blood vessel or the heart?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Antibus

choices.label: B
choices.text: Embolus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thrombus

choices.label: D
choices.text: Coagulus

questions.rationale: Antibus and coagulus are not medical terms. An embolus is a clot that travels through the body and can lodge in a smaller blood vessel. A thrombus is a clot that usually forms within a blood vessel and stays there, often preventing blood from moving past that point.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Remembering   REF:  p. 325

questions.stem: Which category of drugs actually dissolves existing clots?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Heparins

choices.label: B
choices.text: Coagulants

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antiplatelets

choices.label: D
choices.text: Thrombolytics

questions.rationale: Heparin and antiplatelet drugs prevent clots from forming and also prevent existing clots from becoming larger. They do not dissolve existing clots. Coagulants would enhance blood clotting, not dissolve clots.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 325

questions.stem: Which thrombin inhibitor can be given by subcutaneous injection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Heparin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Argatroban

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lepirudin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bivalirudin

questions.rationale: Argatroban, lepirudin, and bivalirudin are administered only intravenously. Heparin can be administered as an intravenous bolus, as a continuous intravenous infusion, or as a subcutaneous injection.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 328

questions.stem: What is the most common purpose of the international normalized ratio (INR)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Measuring the total amount of blood a person has

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reporting the results of warfarin anticoagulation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assessing the number of clotting factors present

choices.label: D
choices.text: Determining the number of platelets

questions.rationale: The most common purpose of the INR blood test is to determine how quickly a person’s blood is able to begin clotting. It is used to determine the effectiveness of warfarin therapy and whether any adjustments in dosages are needed.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 330

questions.stem: Which category of anticoagulant drugs includes warfarin (Coumadin)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Platelet inhibitors

choices.label: B
choices.text: Heparin inhibitors

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thrombin inhibitors

choices.label: D
choices.text: Clotting factor synthesis inhibitors

questions.rationale: Warfarin decreases the synthesis of vitamin K in the intestinal tract, which then reduces the production of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X, along with the anticoagulant proteins C and S. When the amounts of these critical clotting factors are reduced, anticoagulation results.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 330

questions.stem: What is the intended response of clotting factor synthesis inhibitor drugs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased clotting times

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased clotting times

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased platelet counts

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased platelet counts

questions.rationale: Clotting factor synthesis inhibitor drugs reduce the amount of certain clotting factors available to form a true blood clot. When this effect occurs, it takes much longer for blood to clot. These drugs do not affect the number of platelets a person has.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 330

questions.stem: What health problem is a possible adverse effect of any drug that reduces blood clotting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pulmonary embolism

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hemorrhage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thrombosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Diabetes

questions.rationale: All drugs that reduce the ability of the blood to form clots when needed greatly increase the risk for hemorrhage any time an injury occurs. A pulmonary embolism and a thrombosis are both clots. Reduced blood clotting reduces the incidence of clot formation. These drugs do nothing to stimulate the development of diabetes.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 331

questions.stem: Which common over-the-counter agent is an antiplatelet drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aspirin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tylenol

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vitamin C

choices.label: D
choices.text: Magnesium

questions.rationale: Aspirin is the most common antiplatelet drug and is available over-the-counter. It disrupts an enzyme system within platelets that prevents their activation. Tylenol has no antiplatelet activity and neither does vitamin C. Magnesium is a mineral that helps blood to clot in different areas of the blood clotting cascade.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 332

questions.stem: What type of clotting problem is most often treated with a thrombin inhibitor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hemophilia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hemorrhage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Heart attack

choices.label: D
choices.text: Gastric ulcers

questions.rationale: A thrombin inhibitor is used to prevent a heart attack by reducing the blood’s ability to clot. Hemophilia, hemorrhage, and gastric ulcers are all conditions that are or can lead to excessive bleeding, not clotting.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 328

questions.stem: What is the intended response to an erythropoiesis-stimulating agent (ESA)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased red blood cell counts

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased red blood cell counts

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased platelet counts

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased platelet counts

questions.rationale: Erythropoiesis is the production of red blood cells in the bone marrow. ESAs are drugs that increase this process resulting in greater or increased red blood cell counts. ESAs may also increase the platelet count but that is not their intended response.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 335

questions.stem: What health problem is a possible side effect of erythropoiesis-stimulating agent (ESA) therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased risk for infection

choices.label: B
choices.text: Low blood pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypertension

choices.label: D
choices.text: Anemia

questions.rationale: Although ESAs have the intended response of increasing the production of red blood cells, other cell types also have a somewhat increased production. This causes hyperviscous, thicker blood that increases the friction in blood vessels. As a result, blood pressure can increase, sometimes to dangerous levels.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 336

questions.stem: For what specific health problem is urokinase (Abbokinase) approved?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute myocardial infarction (heart attack) in which the symptoms began 24 hours ago

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stroke caused by an embolus lodged in a small artery in the brain

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stroke caused by a ruptured blood vessel in the brain

choices.label: D
choices.text: Massive acute pulmonary embolism

questions.rationale: Although urokinase was used for other thrombotic events at one time, its only current approved use is for treatment of a massive, acute, pulmonary embolism.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 334 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: How do antiplatelet drugs work?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increasing the elimination of formed platelets

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreasing the formation of new platelets

choices.label: C
choices.text: Preventing platelets from aggregating

choices.label: D
choices.text: Trapping platelets in the spleen

questions.rationale: Platelets normally circulate in the blood freely without clumping (aggregating). To begin coagulation, platelets must become active and begin clumping. Antiplatelet drugs do not decrease the number of platelets the body makes or maintains in circulation, they prevent platelets from becoming active and aggregating.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 328

questions.stem: How are anticoagulant drugs and thrombolytic drugs different?

choices.label: A
choices.text: There is no difference; they both have the same actions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anticoagulants prevent clots from forming whereas thrombolytics can dissolve clots that have already formed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thrombolytics must be administered intravenously while all anticoagulants are administered as oral agents.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Anticoagulants prevent clots by actually thinning the blood whereas thrombolytics reduce platelet aggregation and do not affect blood thickness.

questions.rationale: Both thrombolytics and anticoagulants disrupt steps in clot formation. However, anticoagulants have no effect on clots that have already formed. Only thrombolytics can dissolve an existing clot. Neither drug causes a “thinning” of the blood. Blood viscosity (thickness or specific gravity) remains the same when a person receives either class of drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: pp. 332-333

questions.stem: Which condition occurs as a result of a thrombus developing in a coronary artery?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cerebrovascular accident

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deep vein thrombosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pulmonary embolus

choices.label: D
choices.text: Myocardial infarction

questions.rationale: When a thrombus develops in a coronary artery and blocks the blood supply to a part of the heart muscle, a heart attack (myocardial infarction) occurs. Cerebrovascular accident (stroke) occurs when a thrombus blocks an artery in the brain. Deep vein thrombosis occurs when a clot blocks a deep vein, such as a leg vein. Pulmonary embolus occurs when an embolus blocks blood flow in an artery in the lungs.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 325

questions.stem: A patient asks why an anticoagulant has been prescribed. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “It will dissolve any clots in your blood vessels.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “It will prevent any new clots from forming.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “It will prevent a clot from migrating.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “It will thin your blood.”

questions.rationale: Anticoagulant drugs are sometimes called “blood thinners” but they do not thin the blood, prevent clots from migrating, or dissolve existing clots. These drugs prevent new clots from forming and prevent existing clots from becoming larger.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 325

questions.stem: A patient prescribed an anticoagulant long term engages in all of the following activities. Which activity do you teach the patient to avoid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Golfing

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bowling

choices.label: C
choices.text: Jumping rope

choices.label: D
choices.text: Walking 2 miles daily

questions.rationale: Any person taking anticoagulants long term must avoid activities that can traumatize tissue and lead to bleeding, such as contact sports (e.g., football, hockey), and activities that put extra weight and stress on the leg joints and spine (e.g., jumping rope, jogging, high impact exercise). Jumping rope can cause bleeding into the joints, especially the knee, which can lead to permanent damage.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 328

questions.stem: A patient in the emergency department developed stroke symptoms 1 hour ago. Which type of drug should you expect to prepare to resolve this problem?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thrombolytic

choices.label: B
choices.text: Thrombin inhibitor

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antiplatelet drug

choices.label: D
choices.text: Clotting factor synthesis inhibitor

questions.rationale: When an ischemic stroke occurs there is an existing clot in an artery in the brain. To resolve this problem early after symptoms develop, a thrombolytic drug (clot buster) is prescribed. These drugs must be administered within 3 hours of the onset of symptoms for stroke. Thrombin inhibitors and clotting factor synthesis inhibitors prevent new clots from forming and existing clots from getting larger. Antiplatelet drugs prevent platelets from clumping together (aggregating).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 334

questions.stem: Which action should you avoid performing on a patient after he or she has received a thrombolytic drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administering IV drugs

choices.label: B
choices.text: Using electric razors

choices.label: C
choices.text: Providing a soft toothbrush

choices.label: D
choices.text: Giving an intramuscular injection

questions.rationale: Thrombolytic drugs dissolve existing clots, so there is a high risk for bleeding with any invasive procedures such as intramuscular injections. Administration of IV drugs through an already existing line is acceptable, but you would not want to place a new line or discontinue an existing line. Use of electric razors and soft toothbrushes are safe with patients who have received a thrombolytic drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 335

questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed continuous intravenous (IV) heparin asks why being weighed is necessary. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “All patients are weighed upon admission to the hospital.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “IV heparin has been known to cause weight gain.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “The initial IV bolus of heparin is prescribed based on your weight.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “The rate of the heparin infusion is based on your weight.”

questions.rationale: Always get an accurate weight on a patient to be placed on continuous IV heparin because the initial bolus is based on weight. The rate of the infusion is based on aPTT results. IV heparin is not the cause of weight gain. While all patients are weighed on admission to the hospital, this response does not answer the patient’s question.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 329

questions.stem: The aPTT of a patient who is prescribed continuous intravenous (IV) heparin is two times the control value. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increase the IV rate as ordered.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decrease the IV rate as ordered.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Leave the rate unchanged.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stop the infusion and notify the prescriber.

questions.rationale: The goal of continuous heparin therapy is to keep the aPTT within a therapeutic range of 1.5 to 2.5 times greater than the laboratory-established control value. Two times the control value is within this range. The prescriber should be notified, but the infusion rate, which is therapeutic, will not change at this time.
