questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF:  p. 325, Table 20-1

questions.stem: A patient is being discharged on enoxaparin (Lovenox). Which statement should you include in the discharge teaching plan?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “An advantage of this drug is that labs do not need to be drawn to guide therapy.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Follow-up lab work must be drawn every 2 to 6 months.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Enoxaparin is given intramuscularly twice a day.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “The purpose of this drug is to dissolve clots.”

questions.rationale: A major advantage of using low-molecular-weight heparins such as enoxaparin is that patients are not required to have laboratory work done to guide their therapy. The drug is given two times daily subcutaneously. Its purpose is to prevent venous thromboembolic events and pulmonary embolism.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 328

questions.stem: A patient who has been receiving heparin subcutaneously for 3 weeks has all of the following laboratory blood test values. Which value should you report immediately to the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Activated partial thromboplastin time 1.5

choices.label: B
choices.text: International normalized ratio (INR) 1.7

choices.label: C
choices.text: Red blood cells 4.2 million/mm3

choices.label: D
choices.text: Platelets 20,000/mm3

questions.rationale: The normal range for platelets is 200,000 to 400,000/mm3. Platelets are needed for blood clotting. This patient’s platelet count is extremely low and he or she is at grave risk for severe bleeding. The low platelet count is an indication of an adverse reaction to heparin known as heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT). The heparin needs to be discontinued and the patient needs to receive platelet therapy before life-threatening hemorrhage occurs.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 329

questions.stem: A patient is taking warfarin (Coumadin) daily to prevent blood clots from forming in deep veins. Which statement made by the patient indicates that more teaching is needed about this therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I have been eating more salads and other green, leafy vegetables to prevent constipation.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I have been using acetaminophen (Tylenol) instead of aspirin for pain.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Instead of a safety razor, I have been using an electric shaver.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “On hot days, I make sure to drink at least two quarts of water.”

questions.rationale: Vitamin K is present in green, leafy vegetables and increases blood clotting by increasing the synthesis of specific clotting factors in the liver. Warfarin is a vitamin K antagonist. Ingestion of large amounts of vitamin K can counteract the therapeutic effects of warfarin.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 331

questions.stem: A patient has been discontinued from warfarin (Coumadin) therapy for 3 weeks. Which laboratory test indicates that all effects of the warfarin have been eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: International normalized ratio (INR) of 0.9

choices.label: B
choices.text: Red blood cell count of 5 million/mm3

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hemoglobin of 16 g/dL

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hematocrit of 42%

questions.rationale: Warfarin therapy increases the INR, which normally ranges between 0.7 and 1.8. Therapeutic warfarin levels should maintain the INR between 2.0 and 3.0. When the effects of warfarin are no longer present, the INR returns to normal levels. Warfarin therapy does not affect white blood cell count, serum ferritin levels, or the reticulocyte count.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 325, Table 20-1

questions.stem: A patient with a vein thromboembolism is ordered to be started on oral warfarin (Coumadin) while still receiving intravenous heparin. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer the two drugs as prescribed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Remind the prescriber that two anticoagulants should not be administered concurrently.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hold the dose of warfarin until the patient’s activated partial thromboplastin time is the same as the control value.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Monitor the patient for clinical manifestations of internal or external bleeding at least every 2 hours.

questions.rationale: Although both heparin and warfarin are anticoagulants, they have different mechanisms of action and onsets of action. Because warfarin has such a slow onset, it must be started while the patient is still receiving heparin.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 330

questions.stem: Why is warfarin (Coumadin) avoided during pregnancy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Warfarin can induce premature closure of the foramen ovale in the fetus.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The fetal and newborn liver cannot metabolize warfarin and anemia results.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The fetus and newborn are more likely to have allergic reactions to warfarin.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Warfarin crosses the placenta and causes birth defects or hemorrhage in the fetus.

questions.rationale: Warfarin has a high likelihood of increasing the risk for birth defects or fetal harm. It is a teratogen with a chemical composition the same as rat poison. It can cause birth defects and can reduce clotting in the fetus to such an extent that severe and fatal hemorrhage in the fetus is possible (especially brain hemorrhage).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 332

questions.stem: A patient prescribed subcutaneous heparin tells you that her menstrual bleeding is heavier than usual. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I will hold the drug and notify your prescriber.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “This is a fairly common and expected side effect of the drug.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Heavy bleeding is a concern because you might become anemic.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I will have to give you a shot of protamine sulfate to reverse the drug’s action.”

questions.rationale: Common side effects of anticoagulant therapy include increased risk for bleeding. Bleeding from the gums, oozing from cuts or wounds, nosebleeds, and heavier than usual menstrual bleeding can occur.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 329

questions.stem: A patient who has been on warfarin (Coumadin) therapy for a year calls to tell you he forgot to take the drug yesterday. What is your best advice?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Take today’s dose as usual.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Take the forgotten dose right now and take today’s dose at the usual time.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Come to the clinic to receive an injection of warfarin as soon as possible.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Take today’s dose as usual and also take an 81 mg tablet of aspirin at the same time.”

questions.rationale: Warfarin is a long-acting drug. Although it is best taken daily, the effects of the drug are present for days. The patient should take today’s drug dose at the usual time and not try to make up for the missed dose (which, if taken today, could greatly increase the risk for hemorrhage). There is no injectable form of warfarin. It is only an oral preparation. Patients should not take aspirin with warfarin because the risk for a cerebral hemorrhage is greatly increased.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 327

questions.stem: Which blood test should be performed on a regular basis for a patient who takes an oral direct thrombin inhibitor daily?

choices.label: A
choices.text: International normalized ratio (INR)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Complete blood cell count

choices.label: C
choices.text: Potassium

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sodium

questions.rationale: All of the direct thrombin inhibitors can increase the blood potassium levels and cause hyperkalemia. This blood level should be monitored on a regular basis. The INR is not accurate in determining the effectiveness of direct thrombin inhibitors.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Understanding   REF:  p. 329

questions.stem: A patient who delivered a baby 6 weeks ago is now prescribed low-molecular-weight heparin for a venous thrombosis in her leg. She asks whether she can continue to breastfeed her baby while taking this drug. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Yes, the baby’s digestive system will inactivate the drug.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Yes, the drug does not pass into breast milk.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “No, the drug will increase the baby’s risk for bleeding.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “No, the drug can delay your baby’s growth and development.”

questions.rationale: Heparin exerts effects directly in the blood and does not enter breast milk.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 330

questions.stem: Which statement by a patient who is prescribed heparin indicates the need for additional teaching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I’ll be sure to have my follow-up aPTT drawn when the prescriber tells me to.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I will not take over-the-counter drugs that contain aspirin.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I will get plenty of exercise playing soccer.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I’ll tell my prescriber if I have headaches.”

questions.rationale: While taking heparin, patients should avoid contact sports and other activities that may cause injury. Falls, blows to the body or head, headaches that won’t go away, or other injuries should be reported to the prescriber right away.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 328

questions.stem: Which dietary teaching do you include for a patient who is prescribed warfarin (Coumadin) on discharge?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Weight loss diets such as the Atkins diet are OK while taking this drug.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Be sure to eat lots of green leafy vegetables for extra vitamin K.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Dietary changes do not affect the action of this drug.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Drinking alcoholic beverages can change the action of warfarin.”

questions.rationale: Patients should be taught to maintain their current diet and not to adopt strict diets such as an all- vegetarian diet or the Atkins diet. Abrupt dietary changes can alter the INR test results. Foods rich in vitamin K may interfere with the action of warfarin (vitamin K is the antidote for warfarin). Alcohol can also interfere with the action of warfarin and patients should be instructed to discuss this with their prescriber before drinking.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 332

questions.stem: Which antiplatelet drug prevents platelet aggregation by blocking a receptor on the surface of platelets?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aspirin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acetaminophen (Tylenol)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Clopidogrel (Plavix)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dipyridamole

questions.rationale: Aspirin inhibits the enzyme that results in the formation of the proclotting substance, thromboxane A2. Acetaminophen does not prevent platelet aggregation. Dipyridamole inhibits another enzyme important in platelet aggregation, not the receptors.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 332

questions.stem: When should antiplatelet drugs be administered?

choices.label: A
choices.text: With or just after a meal

choices.label: B
choices.text: At bedtime to prevent light-headedness

choices.label: C
choices.text: At the same time as an oral antacid

choices.label: D
choices.text: One hour before or 2 hours after a meal

questions.rationale: Antiplatelet drugs can cause nausea, stomach upsets, and gastric irritation. They should be taken with or shortly after a meal. They should not be taken with an antacid because antacids inhibit their absorption.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 333

questions.stem: A patient who received the thrombolytic drug alteplase (Activase) asks why continuous heparin is now infusing intravenously. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “There may be additional clots in your body and we want to be sure they are all dissolved.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Once the clot is dissolved, heparin is given continuously to prevent any new clots from forming.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “The heparin finishes the job of dissolving the clot that the alteplase started.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Heparin is infused continuously in case we need to give additional dosages of alteplase.”

questions.rationale: After thrombolytic therapy to dissolve an existing clot, anticoagulation with heparin prevents the formation of new clots.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 336

questions.stem: Why are erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs) prescribed for patients with chronic kidney disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To protect the kidney from further hypoxic damage during anemia episodes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To improve blood cell counts and reduce the need for blood transfusions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To prevent vein thromboembolism during periods of dehydration.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To reduce the risk of uric acid precipitation and renal failure.

questions.rationale: ESAs stimulate the bone marrow to produce more red blood cells. The person with chronic kidney disease has chronic anemia because the damaged kidney no longer produces natural erythropoietin. The use of ESAs in patients with chronic kidney disease improves red blood cell counts and reduces anemia to such an extent that blood transfusions often are no longer needed.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 336

questions.stem: A patient coming to the clinic for a weekly infusion of darbepoetin alpha (Aranesp) has all of the following changes. For which change should you hold the dose of the drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Weight loss of 5 lb

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hemoglobin increase from 8 to 10 g/dL

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blood potassium increase from 3.8 to 4.8 mEq/L

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blood pressure increase from 126/80 to 148/92 mm Hg

questions.rationale: Because these drugs increase blood cell production, the blood becomes more viscous (thicker). This effect raises blood pressure, increases clot formation, and slows blood movement through small vessels. This patient is hypertensive, so the next dose of darbepoetin can increase the pressure to dangerous levels and greatly increase the risk for stroke and heart attack. The drug should not be administered until the patient’s blood pressure is in the normal range.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 336

questions.stem: A patient who has been self-injecting epoetin alfa (Procrit) three times weekly for anemia now reports all of these problems. For which one do you tell him or her to call 911?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fever and chills

choices.label: B
choices.text: Drooping on the right side of the face

choices.label: C
choices.text: 3 lb weight gain over the past 2 weeks

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intermittent pain and redness at the injection site

questions.rationale: Procrit can increase the blood viscosity and cause clots to form. Drooping on one side of the face is a sign of a stroke. Immediate attention is needed to prevent permanent loss of neurological function from a stroke.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 337

questions.stem: A patient is receiving oprelvekin (Neumega) subcutaneously daily. Which laboratory value indicates that the therapy is effective?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hemoglobin 12 g/dL

choices.label: B
choices.text: Platelet level 55,000/mm3

choices.label: C
choices.text: Red blood cells 4.5 million/mm3

choices.label: D
choices.text: International normalized ratio (INR) 1.0

questions.rationale: Oprelvekin is a thrombopoietic colony-stimulating factor that increases production of platelets. It is only used until the patient’s platelet level reaches 50,000/mm3.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 336 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which techniques should you perform to prevent excessive bleeding when administering an intramuscular injection to a patient who is on anticoagulation therapy? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Using the smallest possible injection needle

choices.label: B
choices.text: Injecting the drug slowly, over at least a 60-second period

choices.label: C
choices.text: Applying pressure to the site for at least 5 minutes after the injection

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ensuring that the needle is placed within a previous injection puncture site

choices.label: E
choices.text: Cleansing the skin at the injection site for at least 30 seconds before the injection

choices.label: F
choices.text: Applying warm compresses to the site for at least 15 minutes after the injection

questions.rationale: Using the smallest needle possible results in less tissue trauma and less risk for bleeding. Applying pressure over the injection site for at least 5 minutes after administration compresses surrounding blood vessels and reduces the risk for bleeding. Injecting the drug slowly can increase the risk for bleeding by traumatizing tissues longer. Placing the needle in the same puncture site as a previous injection can increase the risk for bleeding by enlarging the hole and not allowing the previously damaged tissue to heal. Cleansing the skin can reduce infection risk but does nothing to reduce bleeding risk. Applying warm compresses is more likely to increase bleeding risk by dilating blood vessels in the area. Cold compresses or ice applied to the area would reduce bleeding risk.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 327

questions.stem: What actions are performed when giving a subcutaneous injection of low-molecular- weight heparin? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Position the patient lying down.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Expel the air bubble before injection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Insert the needle into a skinfold held between the thumb and forefinger.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Release the skinfold then inject the drug.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Rub the injection site to avoid bruising.

questions.rationale: Low-molecular-weight heparin is given by deep subcutaneous injection with the patient lying down. The needle should be inserted into a skinfold held between the thumb and the forefinger. To avoid losing any of the drug, the nurse should not expel the air bubble before injection. The skinfold should be held until the injection is completed. Do not rub the injection site.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: pp. 329-330

questions.stem: Which factors are absolute contraindications for thrombolytic therapy? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Active peptic ulcer disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: Active internal bleeding

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recent head trauma

choices.label: D
choices.text: Endocarditis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Suspected aortic aneurysm

choices.label: F
choices.text: Surgery within the last 10 days

questions.rationale: An accurate patient history is essential before giving a thrombolytic drug. Absolute contraindications include active internal bleeding, known allergy to streptokinase products, recent head trauma, known bleeding disorders, suspected aortic dissection, increased blood pressure of 200/120 mm Hg, pregnancy or recent delivery, cerebrovascular processes (e.g., recent stroke, recent spinal or cerebral surgery, cranial neoplasm, and prolonged cardiopulmonary resuscitation). Active peptic ulcer disease, endocarditis, and surgery within the past 10 days are relative contraindications for this therapy. The prescriber weighs the pros and cons of the treatment before making a decision to use the drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 335, Box 20-1 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed 15,000 units/mL of heparin subcutaneously. The drug on hand is heparin 20,000 units/mL. How many milliliters should you administer? mL
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 329

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed 300 mg of oral clopidogrel (Plavix) as a first dose. The drug on hand is clopidogrel 75 mg. How many tablets should you give?
questions.rationale: Want 300 mg/X tablets, Have 75 mg/tablet. 300/75 = 4 tablets.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 332

questions.stem: A child who weighs 48 lb has been ordered warfarin (Coumadin) 0.1 mg/kg. The available drug is 1 mg/scored tablet. How many tablets will you give this child?
questions.rationale: 1 kg = 2.2 lb. Child’s weight in kilogram is 48/2.2, which is 21.8 kg. 0.1 mg  21.8 kg = 2.18 mg, rounded up to 2.2 mg. Want 2.2 mg/X tablets, Have 1 mg/scored tablet. 2.2/1 = 2.2 tablets. You cannot cut a scored tablet into less than 0.5 tablet. Therefore, the dose must be rounded down to 2 tablets.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 331 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: Which type of problem narrows the airways by tightening bronchial smooth muscle?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alveoli

choices.label: B
choices.text: Emphysema

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chronic bronchitis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bronchoconstriction

questions.rationale: The alveoli are not airways, they are air sacs. Emphysema is a disease of flabby airways, not of bronchoconstriction. With chronic bronchitis, the airways are narrowed with swollen mucous membranes not by constriction of the muscles in the airways.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 341

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: Which part of the airway is affected by excessive mucus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lumen

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cartilage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Smooth muscle

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mucous membrane

questions.rationale: The center of the airway, known as the lumen, is partially or completely plugged with excessive mucus (produced by cells in the mucous membranes). Cartilage and smooth muscle do not come into contact with mucus and are not affected by it.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Remembering   REF:  p. 341

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: Which part of the respiratory system is most affected by asthma?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alveoli

choices.label: B
choices.text: Airway

choices.label: C
choices.text: Trachea

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cartilage

questions.rationale: Asthma is a chronic disease characterized by intermittent changes in the lower and smaller airways, not the alveoli or the cartilage. The trachea, although it is an airway is too large to be affected by asthma.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 340

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: Which type of asthma problem is helped most by bronchodilator drugs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thick mucus

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mucosal swelling

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bronchoconstriction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Large, flabby alveoli

questions.rationale: Bronchodilators relax bronchiolar smooth muscle and widen the airways. They do nothing to help flabby alveoli and do nothing to reduce inflammation. Inflammation is responsible for mucus production and mucosal swelling.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 342

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: What is a common side effect of inhaled anti-inflammatory drugs for asthma?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anemia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bronchoconstriction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cutaneous itching

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dry mouth

questions.rationale: Anti-inflammatory drugs reduce inflammation and dry all mucous membranes including those in the mouth and throat. They would reduce any cutaneous (skin) itching. They do not work on bronchial smooth muscles or the bone marrow (which produces red blood cells).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 345

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: Which type of asthma problem is helped most directly by anti-inflammatory drugs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thick mucus

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mucosal swelling

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bronchoconstriction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Large, flabby alveoli

questions.rationale: Anti-inflammatory drugs reduce inflammation and do not help bronchoconstriction. They directly inhibit mucosal swelling and, thus indirectly, reduce mucosal production of mucus. They do not affect mucous that has already formed. Flabby alveoli are not caused by inflammation.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 343

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: Which of these bronchodilators is a cholinergic antagonist?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Albuterol

choices.label: B
choices.text: Terbutaline

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ipratropium

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aminophylline

questions.rationale: Albuterol and terbutaline are beta2 agonists. Aminophylline is a xanthine.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 344

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: Which problem is a common side effect of beta-adrenergic agonist types of bronchodilators?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypertension

choices.label: B
choices.text: Urinary retention

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased bleeding

choices.label: D
choices.text: Shortness of breath

questions.rationale: Although beta-adrenergic agonists relax bronchial smooth muscle, they cause constriction of vascular (blood vessel) smooth muscle. This action increases peripheral resistance and raises blood pressure.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 345

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: What is the most common route used to deliver short-acting beta-adrenergic agonists?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Oral

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intravenous

choices.label: C
choices.text: Transdermal

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inhalation

questions.rationale: Short-acting beta-adrenergic agonists work directly on the airways. They are delivered by oral inhalation so that their effects are exerted almost exclusively on the airway tissues.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 344

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: Which drug is more commonly used as therapy for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) than as therapy for asthma?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Epoprostenol (Flolan)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Warfarin (Coumadin)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Guaifenesin (Organidin)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Salmeterol (Serevent)

questions.rationale: The presence of thick mucus in the respiratory tract is continuous in COPD and intermittent in asthma. It is often necessary to use systemic mucolytic agents or drugs that decrease mucous production in COPD, but is only rarely indicated for asthma.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 349

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: Drugs from which classes are considered the mainstays of therapy for pulmonary fibrosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Amiodarone and ambrisentan

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatories and opiates

choices.label: C
choices.text: Corticosteroids and immunosuppressants

choices.label: D
choices.text: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs and aspirin

questions.rationale: Pulmonary fibrosis is characterized by increased cell division and formation of fibrous tissue. The goal of therapy is to slow cell division and the progression of the fibrosis. Only the corticosteroids and immunosuppressants work in this way. In fact, amiodarone and ambrisentan can lead to the development of pulmonary fibrosis.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 353

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: The use of which drug or drugs is/are associated with later development of pulmonary artery hypertension (PAH)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Amiodarone and ambrisentan

choices.label: B
choices.text: Macitentan and cyclophosphamide

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fenfluramine/phentermine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs

questions.rationale: People who took the weight loss drug fenfluramine/phentermine (known as “fen-phen”) had a greatly increased incidence of the relatively rare health problem of pulmonary artery hypertension.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 350

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: Which problem is the main adverse effect of epoprostenol (Flolan)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Impotence

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prolonged bleeding

choices.label: C
choices.text: Severe hypertension

choices.label: D
choices.text: Congestive heart failure

questions.rationale: The action of epoprostenol increases the cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) in blood vessel smooth muscle, which would cause hypotension not hypertension. It does increase the risk for prolonged bleeding. It is not associated with impotence. Congestive heart failure is a complication of disease and not of this drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 351

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: What is the main way iloprost (Ilomedin) is administered?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Oral inhalation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Subcutaneous injection

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sublingual tablet

choices.label: D
choices.text: Continuous intravenous infusion

questions.rationale: Iloprost is an inhaled drug used to treat pulmonary artery hypertension.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 350 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: Which statement about the pathology of asthma is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Breath sounds are normal between attacks.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mucous plugs are the major cause of airway obstruction.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Eventually the alveoli are destroyed and gas exchange is limited.

choices.label: D
choices.text: During an attack, arterial oxygen levels and carbon dioxide levels are decreased.

questions.rationale: The two causes of reversible and intermittent airway obstruction with asthma are bronchoconstriction and inflammation. Between asthma attacks the airways are open and normal breath sounds are heard. Mucous plugs can make the airway obstruction worse, but are not major causes of airway obstruction. Asthma affects only the airways, not the alveoli.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 342

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: Why are long-acting beta-adrenergic agonists not useful during an actual acute asthma attack?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are likely to cause allergies.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They need time to build up an effect.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They take too long to be absorbed by the intestinal tract.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They reduce inflammation rather than relax bronchial smooth muscle.

questions.rationale: Long-acting beta2-adrenergic agonists (LABAs) work in the same way as SABAs but need time to build up an effect. Therefore LABAs are used to prevent an asthma attack because their effects last longer but have no value during an acute attack.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 343

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: A patient has been medicated during an asthma attack. Which assessment finding indicates that the therapy is ineffective?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Trachea is at the midline.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Oxygen saturation is 96%.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Respiratory rate is 22 breaths/min.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Peak expiratory flow is 40% below expected value.

questions.rationale: A peak expiratory flow rate of 40% below the patient’s personal best indicates that the patient is still having difficulty moving air into the respiratory passages because of airway narrowing. The asthma is not responding sufficiently to the drug. Although the respiratory rate is slightly high, many conditions can cause this and it alone is not an indication of ineffective drug response.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 346

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: A patient with asthma is prescribed albuterol (Proventil) as needed and salmeterol (Serevent) every 12 hours. When the patient asks why two inhaler drugs are needed, what is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Albuterol opens your airways and salmeterol decreases the inflammation.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I will check with the prescriber to determine whether you can just use one drug.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Albuterol is a rescue drug to stop asthma attacks and salmeterol prevents attacks.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Salmeterol helps you breathe better and albuterol opens alveoli for gas exchange.”

questions.rationale: Even though both drugs are beta2 agonists, they are both normally prescribed for the patient with asthma. Short-acting (rapid-acting) beta2 agonists (albuterol) are used to reduce the severity or stop an asthma attack. Long-acting beta2 agonists (salmeterol) can prevent attacks when taken daily; however, their onset of action is so slow that these drugs are not helpful to stop an attack that has already started.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 349

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: A patient newly diagnosed with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is prescribed inhaled pirbuterol (Maxair) every 6 hours. You should instruct the patient to take additional drug doses during which specific times or conditions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: At bedtime

choices.label: B
choices.text: When feeling especially nervous

choices.label: C
choices.text: When feeling especially breathless

choices.label: D
choices.text: If the peak expiratory flow rate is 80% or greater than his or her personal best

questions.rationale: Unlike for asthma, short-acting beta2-adrenergic agonists are often prescribed on a schedule and as an additional rescue drug for the patient with COPD. Extra doses may be needed to open the airways whenever the patient feels especially breathless.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 347

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: A patient reaches for the salmeterol (Serevent) inhaler with the onset of an asthma attack. What is your best instruction to the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use the albuterol (Proventil) inhaler instead.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wait at least one full minute between puffs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Attach the spacer to the inhaler before using it.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Take a deep breath, hold it for 15 seconds, and then exhale before using the inhaler.

questions.rationale: Salmeterol is a long-acting beta2 agonist. This type of drug needs time to build up an effect and is useful in preventing asthma attacks. The effects of this drug are longer lasting, but are not of value during an acute asthma attack.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 344

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: A patient with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) who is prescribed a short- acting inhaled beta2 agonist reports hating the inhaler and asks why the drug can’t be taken as a pill. What is the nurse’s best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Inhaled drugs work more slowly.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Inhaled drugs have no side effects.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Oral drugs are usually more expensive.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Oral drugs have more systemic side effects.”

questions.rationale: When used as prescribed, inhaled drugs go more to the site where the intended responses are needed (the airways) and less drug is absorbed systemically. Thus inhaled drugs have fewer (but still have some) side effects. Oral drugs always have systemic side effects.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 349

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: A patient with asthma asks why he must take regularly scheduled systemic drugs when he can stop several asthma attacks each day within a few minutes of their onset by using a short- acting beta agonist inhaler. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Frequent asthma attacks, even if they are halted relatively quickly, damage the bronchial tissues over time.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “If asthma attacks are uncontrolled they lead to the eventual development of lung cancer and emphysema.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Using only short-acting beta agonists will lead to drug resistance and then the drug won’t work when you need it.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Inhaled beta agonist drugs only treat the constriction aspects of asthma and do not help the inflammatory aspects of the disease.”

questions.rationale: Because damage can occur with any asthma attack, the main focus of therapy should be on attack prevention rather than just on symptom management.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 349

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: Why are xanthines seldom used to treat asthma now?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They increase bronchial secretions at the same time that they induce bronchial dilation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They have no role in preventing bronchoconstriction or inflammation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are all administered intravenously.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They have severe systemic side effects.

questions.rationale: The xanthines and methylxanthines are powerful systemic drugs that have a narrow therapeutic range. They have many dangerous and systemic side effects, especially for the cardiovascular and neurological systems.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 344

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: A patient is prescribed a dry-powder inhaler (DPI) for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). Which statement made by the patient indicates that more instruction is needed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I won’t exhale into the inhaler.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “The inhaler will be kept in the drawer of my bedroom dresser.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I will wash the inhaler mouthpiece daily with soap and water.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I’ll inhale twice as hard through this inhaler as I do with my aerosol inhaler.”

questions.rationale: Washing the DPI may cause the medication in the inhaler to clump together. This action reduces the precision of the delivery of the drug to the patient.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 346, Box 21-3

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: Which assessment is most important to perform before administering a bronchodilator by inhalation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Listening with a stethoscope to the patient’s breath sounds.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Measuring blood pressure in the sitting and standing positions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Asking questions to assess the patient’s cognition and mental status.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Checking the oral mucous membranes for white or cream-colored patches.

questions.rationale: The intended action of bronchodilators is to widen the airways to improve breathing. Listening to the lungs with a stethoscope determines drug effectiveness by comparing the patient’s breath sounds before and after therapy.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 345

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: A patient is prescribed to receive 90 mcg of levalbuterol (Xopenex) every 4 hours by oral inhalation. The available drug is levalbuterol 45 mcg/puff. How many total puffs will you tell this patient to take daily?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 4 puffs

choices.label: B
choices.text: 8 puffs

choices.label: C
choices.text: 12 puffs

choices.label: D
choices.text: 16 puffs

questions.rationale: At 45 mcg/puff, two puffs are needed per dose for 90 mcg. A drug taken every 4 hours should be taken a total of six times in 24 hours. 6  2 = 12 puffs.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 344

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: For which asthma patient is a beta2-adrenergic agonist best delivered by nebulizer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 3-year-old child who has a broken leg

choices.label: B
choices.text: 16-year-old boy who has a broken nose

choices.label: C
choices.text: 25-year-old woman who is 8 months pregnant

choices.label: D
choices.text: 60-year-old man who has an enlarged prostate gland

questions.rationale: Children under the age of 4 years are rarely able to use an aerosol inhaler effectively. Administering the drug by nebulizer with a face mask is the best delivery method.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 347

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: A patient who is prescribed oral terbutaline (Brethine) reports all of the following signs and symptoms. Which should you report to the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Difficulty sleeping

choices.label: B
choices.text: Taste changes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chest pain

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dry mouth

questions.rationale: Terbutaline is a beta2 agonist that dilates airways and constricts blood vessels. Expected side effects of this drug include dry mouth, feeling of nervousness, difficulty sleeping, bad taste in the mouth, rapid heart rate, and increased blood pressure. When a patient is extra sensitive to the drug or has other cardiac problems, constriction of the blood vessels may include the coronary arteries and lead to angina (chest pain) or a heart attack. Notify the prescriber immediately if a patient develops chest pain.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 347

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: A 75-year-old patient with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is prescribed tiotropium (Spiriva), an inhaled drug that must be loaded into a HandiHaler with each dose. The patient has all of the following health problems. For which problem do you suggest to the prescriber that this drug may not be appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reduced hearing

choices.label: B
choices.text: Visual impairment

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chronic high blood pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Arthritis of hands and knees

questions.rationale: The HandiHaler is a dry-powder inhaler that needs to be loaded by the patient and requires considerable manipulation. This action can be difficult or even impossible for patients with arthritis of the hands. This drug delivery choice is not appropriate for this patient and you should ask the prescriber to consider an inhaler type that does not require loading of the drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 347

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: A patient is prescribed an inhaled bronchodilator and an inhaled corticosteroid. What precaution for using these drugs should you stress to the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Use the corticosteroid right before meals to reduce mucus secretion.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Use the bronchodilator inhaler only in the morning to prevent sleep difficulties.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Use the bronchodilator first, wait at least 5 minutes, then use the corticosteroid.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Use the corticosteroid first, wait at least 5 minutes, then use the bronchodilator.”

questions.rationale: When a patient is prescribed both an inhaled bronchodilator and anti-inflammatory, give the bronchodilator first, and then wait at least 5 minutes before giving the inhaled anti-inflammatory. Giving the bronchodilator first allows the greatest widening of the airways so that the anti- inflammatory can be inhaled more deeply into the respiratory tract and be more effective.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 348

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: How do corticosteroids improve breathing in patients with asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Relaxing bronchial smooth muscle, leading to wider airways

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reducing mucosal swelling in the lumens, leading to wider airways

choices.label: C
choices.text: Triggering the sympathetic nervous system, leading to deeper respirations

choices.label: D
choices.text: Controlling anxiety, leading to an increased ability to focus on respiratory effort

questions.rationale: Corticosteroids decrease the production of the body chemicals that trigger inflammation. Inhaled corticosteroids can prevent or reduce the inflammation in the airways that leads to mucosal swelling in the airway lumen. This swelling narrows the lumen, reducing airflow.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: pp. 347-348

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: A home care patient with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) has oral candidiasis. What additional information should you obtain from this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “How often are you using your steroid inhaler?”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Do you share a toothbrush with any members of your family?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “When was the last time your inhaler drug prescriptions were filled?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Have you taken any over-the-counter drugs for a cold or flu lately?”

questions.rationale: Excessive use of steroid inhalers reduces local immune function and increases the patient’s risk for oral-pharyngeal infections, including candidiasis.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 348

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed an inhaled corticosteroid develops a thick white coating on the tongue and patches on the inside cheeks. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Instruct the patient to stop using the inhaler until the coating is gone.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Obtain a culture of the coating and take the patient’s temperature.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hold the dose and notify the prescriber immediately.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Document the finding and notify the prescriber.

questions.rationale: When a patient is prescribed an anti-inflammatory drug, there is an increased risk of opportunistic infections. A thick white coating on the tongue and mucous membrane suggests that the patient has developed a candidiasis (yeast) infection that will need to be treated with an antifungal drug. The nurse should document the finding and notify the prescriber so that appropriate treatment actions can be taken.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 348

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: Which instruction is most important to teach a patient using an aerosol inhaler with a spacer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Breathe in rapidly until the spacer makes a continuous whistling sound.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Seal your lips tightly around the spacer’s mouthpiece.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Release both doses into the spacer before breathing in.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Avoid breathing back into the spacer.”

questions.rationale: As outlined in Box 21-1, it is important for the patient using an aerosol inhaler with a spacer to seal his or her lips tightly around the spacer’s mouthpiece. Dose should be placed into the spacer and then inhaled one at a time. The drug works best when it is inhaled slowly and deeply. Breathing back into the spacer does no harm.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 346, Box 21-1

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: A patient taking montelukast (Singulair) for the last 2 months has the following blood laboratory test values. Which value do you report to the prescriber immediately?

choices.label: A
choices.text: White blood cell count of 10,500 cells/mm3

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lactate dehydrogenase 950 IU/L

choices.label: C
choices.text: Potassium 3.6 mEq/L

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hematocrit 32%

questions.rationale: The white blood cell count, lactate dehydrogenase, and hematocrit values are abnormal; however, only the lactate dehydrogenase level is very high (four to five times normal). This value indicates liver impairment. Leukotriene inhibitors can cause liver impairment. This value must be reported immediately so that liver function can be explored and any needed changes in drug therapy made before irreversible liver damage occurs.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 347

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: How are asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) different?

choices.label: A
choices.text: COPD is a genetic disease whereas asthma is largely caused by allergies.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Breathing problems with asthma are reversible but those with COPD are permanent.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Asthma is a disease of children and younger adults whereas COPD is found only in older adults.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Breathing problems with COPD can lead to death, whereas those with asthma are not severe enough to be fatal.

questions.rationale: Asthma is a problem of airway obstruction caused by constriction of the bronchial smooth muscles that surround the airways and by inflammation in the airways. It occurs in episodes or attacks. Between attacks, the airways are open. Thus the problem is intermittent and reversible. COPD, however, has permanent airway changes and is not cured by therapy. Both disorders can cause such severe airway obstruction that death occurs.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 342

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: A patient is starting on the drug cromolyn sodium. Which statement made by the patient indicates a need for clarification about the purpose, administration schedule, or side effects of this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “If I get a skin rash, I will call my doctor right away.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I will keep using the drug even if I don’t notice a difference in my asthma after the first week.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I will keep the inhaler with me at all times so that I can use it quickly when an asthma attack occurs.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I have been trying to inhale deeply when I trigger the inhaler so that more of the drug reaches my lungs.”

questions.rationale: Cromolyn sodium is a prophylactic (preventive) drug that stabilizes mast cell membranes, inhibiting inflammatory responses in the tissues it contacts. It is not useful during acute attacks.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: pp. 347-348

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: Which action should be taught to the individual prescribed to use a dry-powder inhaler for asthma control?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Shake the inhaler well before using.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Breathe in deeply to pull the drug into your lungs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Keep the inhaler in the refrigerator between uses.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Clean the mouthpiece daily by rinsing it in warm running water.

questions.rationale: There is no propellant in the inhaler. Only a deep breath pulls the drug into the lungs.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 346, Box 21-3

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: How do mucolytics help asthma and chronic obstructive lung disease (COPD)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reducing inflammation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inducing bronchodilation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reducing mucus thickness

choices.label: D
choices.text: Enhancing respiratory rate

questions.rationale: People who have COPD often have thick, sticky mucus that partially blocks airways and is difficult to cough up. With asthma, there is less mucus but some is still present. Mucolytic drugs reduce the thickness of mucus, allowing it to be more easily moved out of the airways.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 349

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: What is the most important precaution to teach a patient about anti-inflammatory drugs used for respiratory problems?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To keep the drug with them at all times to use for sudden narrowing of the airways.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To take the drug daily, even when symptoms of airway obstruction are not present.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To avoid crowds and people who are ill because of the greatly increased risk for infection.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To brush their teeth at least four times daily to remove the drug from the mouth and . improve taste.

questions.rationale: Anti-inflammatory drugs are not rescue drugs; they are controller or prevention drugs. They only work when taken daily on a scheduled basis. They are not helpful when there is a sudden narrowing of the airways.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 349

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: What is the most important precaution to teach a patient who has a continuous infusion of treprostinil to manage pulmonary artery hypertension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Avoid crowds and people who are ill.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Use at least two reliable forms of birth control.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Go to the nearest emergency room if the line clogs.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Report a headache lasting 24 hours to your prescriber immediately.”

questions.rationale: It is critical that there is no interruption in therapy while on continuous infusions of a prostacyclin agent. Even an interruption of minutes increases the risk for death.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 352

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: A patient receiving a prostacyclin agent and an endothelin-receptor antagonist for treatment of pulmonary artery hypertension (PAH) has an international normalized ratio (INR) of 1.9. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Document the INR level as the only action.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hold the next dose of the drugs and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the patient whether he or she has lost more than 3 lb this week.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inspect the infusion site for any other indications of inflammation or infection.

questions.rationale: Patients with PAH are using anticoagulants. In addition, prostacyclin agents can increase the risk for serious bleeding. The parameter for this therapy is to keep the INR under 3.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 352 BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: Which responses are possible side effects of inhaled beta2-adrenergic agonists? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bad taste in the mouth

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased salivation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Difficulty sleeping

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rapid heart rate

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hypotension

choices.label: F
choices.text: Diarrhea

questions.rationale: Beta2-adrenergic agonists have actions similar to the sympathetic division of the autonomic nervous system. When these drugs are used heavily, they can have systemic effects, which include rapid heart rate, increased blood pressure, a feeling of nervousness, and difficulty sleeping. The inhaled drugs can dry the mouth and throat and also may leave a bad taste in the mouth. These drugs, even when absorbed systemically, do not cause diarrhea.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 345

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: Which drugs are useful in the management of pulmonary artery hypertension (PAH)? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anoro Ellipta

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bosentan (Tracleer)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cyclophosphamide (Cytoxan)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Guaifenesin (Naldecon)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Macitentan (Opsumit)

choices.label: F
choices.text: Prednisone g Riociguat (Adempas)

questions.rationale: PAH is managed using anticoagulants, guanylate cyclase stimulators, prostacyclin agents, and endothelin-receptor antagonists. Bosentan and macitentan are endothelin-receptor antagonists and riociguat is a guanylate cyclase stimulator.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Remembering   REF:  pp. 350-351 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: Which side effects should you monitor for after a patient receives ipratropium (Atrovent)? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Headache

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased urine output

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blurred vision

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nausea

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreased heart rate

choices.label: F
choices.text: Eye pain g Muscle aches

questions.rationale: Ipratropium (Atrovent) is a cholinergic antagonist drug. Cholinergic antagonists can cause some specific side effects if they reach the bloodstream. These effects include urinary retention, blurred vision, eye pain, nausea, and headache.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 345 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Asthma and Other Respiratory Problems
questions.stem: A patient who weighs 125 lb is prescribed to receive treprostinil (Remodulin) 1.25 ng/kg/min for the next 4 hours. What is the total dose this patient should receive in 4 hours?
questions.rationale: 1 kg = 2.2 lb Patient’s weight in kilograms is 56.8 kg. Dose is 1.25 ng multiplied by 56.8 kg, 71 ng. There are 240 minutes in 4 hours. 71 ng multiplied by 240 minutes = 17,040 ng.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 350 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.stem: What types of drugs prevent or control nausea/vomiting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Antiemetic drugs

choices.label: B
choices.text: Antimotility drugs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antidiarrheal drugs

choices.label: D
choices.text: Antipropulsion drugs

questions.rationale: Antiemetic drugs control nausea and prevent vomiting. Nausea/vomiting often occur together, and the same drugs are used for both problems.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 357

questions.stem: How is retching different from vomiting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nausea is not present in retching but is in vomiting.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nausea is not present in vomiting but is in retching.

choices.label: C
choices.text: With retching, no vomiting occurs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: With vomiting, no retching occurs.

questions.rationale: The three phases in the process of vomiting are nausea, retching, and vomiting. Retching (dry heaves) involves labored respiratory movements against a closed throat, with contractions of the abdominal muscles, chest wall muscles, and diaphragm without vomiting. Vomiting does not always follow retching, but retching usually causes enough pressure buildup to lead to vomiting.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 356

questions.stem: Nausea/vomiting from which cause is helped best by antihistamines?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Allergies

choices.label: B
choices.text: Opiate drugs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cancer chemotherapy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Exposure to bad odors

questions.rationale: Antihistamine drugs work best against nausea/vomiting caused by opiate drugs or motion.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 357

questions.stem: Which common drug for nausea/vomiting is a 5HT3-receptor antagonist?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Granisetron

choices.label: B
choices.text: Promethazine

choices.label: C
choices.text: Scopolamine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Metoclopramide

questions.rationale: Granisetron (Kytril) is a 5HT3-receptor antagonist that works well for nausea/vomiting caused by chemotherapy treatments.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 358

questions.stem: Which drug for nausea/vomiting can cause decreased sweating and increased risk for overheating?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Meclizine (Antivert)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ondansetron (Zofran)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Trimethobenzamide (Tigan)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prochlorperazine (Compazine)

questions.rationale: Prochlorperazine (Compazine) can cause decrease in sweating, increasing the risk for overheating of the patient’s body. Check body temperature every 4 to 8 hours while a patient is taking this drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 359

questions.stem: Which drug for nausea/vomiting can cause muscle spasms of the jaw, neck, and back when given to children?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Meclizine (Antivert)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Metoclopramide (Reglan)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Trimethobenzamide (Tigan)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prochlorperazine (Compazine)

questions.rationale: Children may have muscle spasms of the jaw, neck, and back, along with jerky movements of the head and face while taking metoclopramide.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 361

questions.stem: Which drug used to treat constipation is an osmotic laxative?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Methylcellulose

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lubiprostone

choices.label: C
choices.text: Castor oil

choices.label: D
choices.text: Docusate

questions.rationale: Osmotic laxatives cause retention of fluid in the bowel, increasing the water content in stool. Lubiprostone (Amitiza) is an osmotic laxative.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 364

questions.stem: Which drug used to treat constipation is a lubricant?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bisacodyl

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lubiprostone

choices.label: C
choices.text: Castor oil

choices.label: D
choices.text: Docusate

questions.rationale: Lubricants coat the surface of stool and help it hold water so the body can more easily expel it. Lubricants include castor oil and glycerin suppositories.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 364

questions.stem: Which problems are the most common side effects of drugs used to treat constipation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Abdominal cramps and diarrhea

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypertension and swollen feet

choices.label: C
choices.text: Skin rash and blurred vision

choices.label: D
choices.text: Insomnia and infection

questions.rationale: The most common side effects of drugs for constipation are diarrhea, abdominal cramps, abdominal distention, and nausea. For a list of common side effect for individual constipation drugs, see Table 22-3 in the text.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 365

questions.stem: Which drug used to treat diarrhea is an antimotility drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bismuth subsalicylate (Pepto-Bismol)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Calcium polycarbophil (FiberCon)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Metoclopramide (Reglan)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Loperamide (Imodium)

questions.rationale: Antimotility drugs slow down peristalsis (movement) in the gastrointestinal (GI) track, and are used to treat diarrhea. Loperamide (Imodium) is an antimotility drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 368

questions.stem: Which laboratory value must be monitored after a patient takes bisacodyl (Dulcolax) for constipation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sodium

choices.label: B
choices.text: Potassium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Creatinine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blood urea nitrogen

questions.rationale: A common side effect of bisacodyl is hypokalemia (low blood potassium). You must monitor this value when a patient is prescribed this drug and report decreased values to the prescriber.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 365 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which statement about normal bowel function is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bowel movements should occur once a day every day.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bowel movement frequency is more important than their consistency.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bowel movements are very simple processes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bowel movements should be soft and easily pass out of the bowel.

questions.rationale: Normal bowel function varies from individual to individual, from several times a day to several times a week. Consistency is more important than frequency. Stool should be soft enough to pass easily from the bowel, but not liquid. Bowel movements are a complex process involving several muscles and nerves located on the pelvic floor.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 356

questions.stem: Which statement about the vomiting reflex is accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The vomiting process consists of four phases.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Retching precedes vomiting in the process.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nausea usually occurs prior to vomiting.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vomiting may be a reflex or a voluntary action.

questions.rationale: The vomiting process involves three phases: nausea, vomiting, and retching. Nausea usually occurs before vomiting, and retching occurs after vomiting. Vomiting is a reflex, not a voluntary action.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 356

questions.stem: You are instructing a patient on how to prevent constipation. Which point should you stress?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Be sure to eat a diet that is low in fiber.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Use a laxative on a daily basis to prevent constipation.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Physical inactivity will not affect your bowel function.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “You should drink plenty of fluids every day to prevent constipation.”

questions.rationale: To prevent constipation, a patient should be taught to consume a diet that is high in fiber, drink plenty of fluids, and be physically active. Misuse of laxatives can cause constipation because the body becomes dependent on these drugs, needing higher and higher doses until the bowel no longer works.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 361

questions.stem: You are instructing a patient about how to help prevent the spread of diarrhea. Which important point should you stress?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “When travelling internationally it is best to drink bottled water with ice cubes.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Be sure to wash your hands after using the bathroom or changing diapers.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Always wear clean gloves when handling raw meat or poultry.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Cut down on your fluid intake to decrease the number of diarrhea episodes.”

questions.rationale: While drinking bottled water is good, ice cubes may be made with tap water, which in certain countries may contain bacteria that can cause diarrhea. Washing hands before and after handling raw meat or poultry is important, but it is not necessary to wear gloves. The patient should continue drinking adequate fluids while having diarrhea to prevent dehydration. Washing hands after using the bathroom or changing diapers can prevent the spread of diarrhea.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: pp. 370-371

questions.stem: A patient receiving chemotherapy and prescribed ondansetron (Zofran) asks why the drug is given before meals. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Ondansetron is given 30 minutes before your meals to prevent nausea.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “The purpose of ondansetron is to move food rapidly through your GI tract.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Ondansetron works by preventing nausea caused by morphine given for your pain.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “If ondansetron is given after your meals, food would interfere with its absorption.”

questions.rationale: Ondansetron is an antinausea drug that is given 30 minutes before meals and at bedtime to prevent the nausea that is associated with chemotherapy. Metoclopramide (Reglan) increases peristalsis to help move food through the GI tract. Antihistamines prevent the nausea/vomiting caused by opiate drugs such as morphine. If the drug were given after meals, its purpose would not be met.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 361

questions.stem: A patient prescribed metoclopramide (Reglan) reports having difficulty sleeping, difficulty concentrating, tiredness, and feeling hopeless. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Instruct the patient that these are expected side effects of the drug.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document these findings as the only action.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check the patient’s chart history of depression.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hold the drug and notify the prescriber.

questions.rationale: Difficulty sleeping and concentrating, tiredness, and feeling hopeless are signs of depression. Metoclopramide can cause mild to severe depression. You should hold the drug and notify the prescriber. This drug should not be prescribed for patients with a history of depression.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 361

questions.stem: A patient prescribed metoclopramide (Reglan) tells you that his abdomen is making gurgling sounds. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tell the patient that this is an expected effect of the drug.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document this finding as the only action.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hold the drug and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Listen for bowel sounds in all four quadrants of the abdomen.

questions.rationale: Metoclopramide increases stomach and small intestine contractions (peristalsis) which helps move food through the GI system. Increased peristalsis causes increased and sometimes loud bowel sounds. The patient should be instructed that this is an expected action of the drug.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 363

questions.stem: A 10-year-old child is prescribed cyclizine (Marezine) 25 mg orally for motion sickness. The drug is available as a 50 mg tablet. How many tablets should you instruct the parents to give the child?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One fourth

choices.label: B
choices.text: One half

choices.label: C
choices.text: Two

choices.label: D
choices.text: Four

questions.rationale: Want 25 mg, Have 50 mg/tablet. 25 mg/50 mg =  tablet.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 358

questions.stem: The spouse of a patient who is prescribed promethazine (Phenergan) as part of her antiemetic therapy with chemotherapy reports that after the last dose the patient did not remember the drive home. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thank the spouse for reporting the problem, and document the adverse drug reaction.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hold the dose of promethazine for this round of chemotherapy until the patient is seen by the prescriber.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reassure the patient and spouse that this is a normal response to the drug and stress that the patient should not drive.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perform a mini-mental status examination and assess the patient’s pupillary reflexes before administering the promethazine.

questions.rationale: Promethazine, a phenothiazine-based drug, induces sedation and confusion in addition to having antiemetic effects. Some patients have reduced memory about events occurring within a few hours after receiving the drug. This is an expected, temporary side effect and does not denote any permanent reduced cognition. Both the patient and the spouse should be aware of this effect so that the patient is not at risk for injury. Driving, cooking, or operating dangerous equipment should not be performed until the drug’s effects have worn off.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 361

questions.stem: What is the most important assessment question to ask a patient before administering intravenous metoclopramide (Reglan)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Are you being treated for depression?”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Is your type 1 diabetes well controlled?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Do you have a gastric or duodenal ulcer?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Are you taking aspirin or any aspirin-containing products?”

questions.rationale: Metoclopramide can make depression worse and intensify thoughts of suicide. Patients with a history of depression should not be prescribed this drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 361

questions.stem: You are instructing a patient who is experiencing chronic nausea/vomiting, about antiemetic therapy. What is the most important precaution you must teach the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Rinse your mouth frequently to reduce the unpleasant sensation of dry mouth.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Report any constipation lasting 2 days or longer to your prescriber.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Wear sunscreen and protective clothing when going outdoors.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Avoid drinking alcoholic beverages while taking this drug.”

questions.rationale: All of the antiemetic drugs cause some degree of central nervous system depression, although some drugs have a more profound depressive action than others. Alcohol increases CNS depression, increasing the risks for injury, cognitive changes, and respiratory depression. In addition, alcohol may intensify the experience of nausea. Although it is also important to remind patients to avoid sun exposure because of increased sun sensitivity, and to rinse the mouth to reduce dry mouth sensation, alcohol avoidance is the most important precaution because it can lead to more serious complications.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 361

questions.stem: An older male patient with nausea is prescribed scopolamine (L-hyoscine). Which assessment determines whether he is experiencing a serious side effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Checking capillary refill

choices.label: B
choices.text: Measuring abdominal girth

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evaluating handgrip strength

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evaluating daily intake and output

questions.rationale: Scopolamine is an anticholinergic drug that can cause urinary retention. Older men often have some degree of prostate enlargement that may be undiagnosed and result in some urinary retention. Scopolamine makes this problem worse and can cause kidney damage. Comparing urine output to fluid intake is important for this patient to determine whether severe urinary retention is occurring.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 361

questions.stem: A patient who is prescribed prochlorperazine (Compazine) for postoperative nausea/vomiting has all of the following changes. For which change should you immediately contact the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Systolic blood pressure decrease of 12 mm Hg

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased sleepiness but arouses with light shaking

choices.label: C
choices.text: Oral temperature increase of 2° F

choices.label: D
choices.text: Urine color change from yellow to reddish-brown
