questions.rationale: Prochlorperazine can trigger neuroleptic malignant syndrome, a rare and life-threatening side effect in which dangerously high body temperatures can occur. Without prompt and expert treatment, this condition can be fatal in as many as 20% of those who develop it. One of the first warning signs is increasing body temperature. At this point, steps must be taken quickly to prevent this adverse reaction from becoming worse.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 360

questions.stem: The mother of a 9-month-old child reports that the child has a bowel movement only every 2 or 3 days and that the stool is small and hard, “like marbles.” What should you recommend as a beginning treatment for this problem?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use castor oil every 3 days.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increase the child’s fluid intake.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ensure that the child gets adequate exercise.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mix a bulk-forming laxative in with the child’s cereal.

questions.rationale: The most common cause of constipation in children of this age-group is insufficient fluid intake. A 9-month-old does not ask for fluids and is dependent on others to provide them. Increasing fluids is the action least likely to cause problems in this situation, as long as the intake does not exceed 2 L/day.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 361

questions.stem: How do osmotic laxatives reduce constipation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They cause retention of fluid in the bowel, increasing the water content in stool.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They add bulk to the stool, which increases stool mass that stimulates peristalsis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They soften stool, allowing the stool to mix with fatty substances, making it easier to eliminate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They cause chemical irritation of nerve receptors in the intestinal lining, which then triggers peristalsis.

questions.rationale: Osmotic laxatives pull water from other body areas into the intestinal tract and prevent it from being reabsorbed through intestinal walls. The extra fluid enters stool, keeping it soft and easier to expel.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 364

questions.stem: Which action is most important after giving a patient bisacodyl (Dulcolax)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Comparing daily urine output with fluid intake.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assessing pulse rate and regularity every 4 hours.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ensuring that the patient complies with a low-sodium diet.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Testing the patient’s blood glucose level at least every shift.

questions.rationale: Bisacodyl may cause hypokalemia, which can lead to life-threatening dysrhythmias. Serum electrolytes may not have been ordered. Checking pulse regularity can help recognize new or worsening dysrhythmias.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 368

questions.stem: The mother of a 6-year-old child receiving diphenoxylate (Lomotil) for the last 4 days for diarrhea reports by telephone that the diarrhea has stopped and that the child’s pants cannot be zipped or buttoned today. What is your best advice?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Give the child only half the dose of the drug today.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Do not give the child fluids for the next 24 hours.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Stop the drug and go immediately to the prescriber’s office.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “This is expected because when diarrhea is cured the stool enlarges the abdomen.”

questions.rationale: Increasing abdominal size while taking a drug for diarrhea is an indication of toxic megacolon, a serious complication of this therapy. Children taking diphenoxylate are more sensitive to the side effects and can develop a toxic megacolon quickly. Other signs and symptoms of this condition include fever, abdominal pain, rapid heart rate, and dehydration. A patient with toxic megacolon may go into shock. When this condition is not recognized and treated early, there is a risk for death.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 370

questions.stem: A patient who is prescribed promethazine (Phenergan) currently has a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min when it is time to give the next dose. What is your most important action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Apply oxygen at 2 L per nasal cannula.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check the patient’s oxygen saturation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hold the drug and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Raise the head of the bed 90 degrees.

questions.rationale: Respiratory depression (decreased drive for breathing) is a life-threatening adverse effect that can occur with several antiemetic drugs, including promethazine. You must be sure to monitor the patient’s respiratory rate after administering this drug and report a decrease in respiratory rate to the prescriber. The drug should be held because a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min is very low. Raising the head of the bed, checking oxygen saturation, and applying oxygen are all good treatment strategies for the low respiratory rate, but the most important action is to notify the prescriber and hold the drug to prevent further respiratory depression.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 360

questions.stem: You are administering a patient’s first dose of cyclizine (Marezine). What safety action should you take to ensure safety for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Instruct the patient to call for help when getting out of bed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Raise all four side rails to the upright position.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give the patient a full glass of water with the medication.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tell the patient to avoid eating for at least 2 hours.

questions.rationale: Cyclizine is an antiemetic drug. Most of these drugs cause drowsiness as a side effect. You must instruct the patient to call for help when getting out of bed and make sure that the call light is within easy reach. This is especially important because this is the patient’s first dose of the drug and its effects for this patient are unknown.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 360

questions.stem: The wife of an inpatient who is prescribed dexamethasone (Decadron) tells you over the phone that she has developed a cold with a low-grade fever. What is your best advice at this time?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “If you come in to visit you must wear an isolation gown.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Hospital rules do not allow visitors with colds or fevers for any patients.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “It would be best to speak with your husband by phone and stay home today.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “You must take care of yourself to be able to care for your husband after discharge.”

questions.rationale: Dexamethasone is a drug that also has an immunosuppression action. Patients taking this drug are at increased risk for infections and should avoid being exposed to any infection. You should monitor patients receiving this drug for any signs of infection.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 361

questions.stem: A patient reports taking an over-the-counter laxative for constipation daily for the past 3 weeks. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Remind the patient about the importance of adequate fluid intake and exercise to prevent constipation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Instruct the patient that these drugs should not be used for more than a week without consulting the prescriber.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the patient about usual fluid intake, urinary and bowel habits, and have the patient describe the nature of his or her stools.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Contact the prescriber about an order for a stronger laxative because the one the patient is taking is not working.

questions.rationale: Laxative drugs are not meant for long-term use and should not be used for longer than 1 week unless following a prescriber’s advice. The use of laxatives long-term can cause other health problems.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 363

questions.stem: A patient reports taking psyllium (Metamucil) every morning to prevent constipation. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Teach that over-the-counter laxatives should not be taken for more than 1 week.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Instruct the patient that long-term use of psyllium can cause health problems.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hold the drug and notify the prescriber immediately.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Document this information as the only action.

questions.rationale: Psyllium is a bulk-forming laxative and can be used once a day to help prevent constipation. Bulk-forming laxatives are the one exception to the rule that laxatives should not be used long- term. These drugs are not absorbed from the intestines into the body and are safe for long-term use.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 363

questions.stem: What problem is most important to assess for in a patient who is prescribed loperamide (Imodium)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tachycardia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Abdominal distention

choices.label: C
choices.text: Peripheral edema

choices.label: D
choices.text: Respiratory crackles

questions.rationale: A potential life-threatening adverse effect of antimotility drugs such as loperamide is toxic megacolon which is characterized by a very inflated colon and abdominal distention. After giving an antimotility drug, you must be sure to check the patient’s abdomen for distention and report this finding to the prescriber.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 370

questions.stem: A patient reports taking attapulgite (Kaopectate) for diarrhea over the past 4 days. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Send a stool specimen to the lab for analysis including culture, sensitivity, and parasites.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Instruct the patient to notify nursing staff for episodes of diarrhea and save the stool for occult blood check.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check the patient’s blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, and oxygen saturation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Teach the patient that antidiarrheal drugs should not be taken for more than 2 days unless instructed to by their prescriber.

questions.rationale: Antidiarrheal drugs should not be taken for more than 2 days unless instructed by a prescriber. When a patient’s diarrhea is not relieved after 2 days, he or she should contact the prescriber.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 370

questions.stem: When should metoclopramide (Reglan) be taken?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 1 hour before meals and at bedtime

choices.label: B
choices.text: 30 minutes before meals and at bedtime

choices.label: C
choices.text: 30 minutes after meals and at bedtime

choices.label: D
choices.text: 1 hour after meals and at bedtime

questions.rationale: Instruct patients to take this drug 30 minutes before meals and at bedtime to help prevent reflux of stomach contents into the esophagus.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 359

questions.stem: A patient prescribed metoclopramide (Reglan) for 15 months develops these findings: uncontrolled rhythmic movement of the face, lip smacking, uncontrolled chewing, and rapid or wormlike movements of the tongue. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Transfer the patient to a critical care bed immediately.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document the findings and contact the prescriber.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask if the patient has any drug, pet or food allergies.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Place the patient on bed rest and keep him or her NPO.

questions.rationale: Metoclopramide (Reglan) can cause tardive dyskinesia, a chronic disorder of the nervous system. Signs and symptoms include uncontrolled rhythmic movement of the mouth, face, or extremities; lip smacking or puckering; puffing of cheeks; uncontrolled chewing; and rapid or wormlike movements of the tongue. This adverse effect usually occurs after a year or more of continued use of these drugs and is often irreversible. If diagnosed early, tardive dyskinesia may be reversed by stopping the drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 360

questions.stem: A patient prescribed prochlorperazine (Compazine) has reddish-brown urine. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Collect a urine sample and send it for culture and sensitivity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check the patient’s vital signs with special emphasis on body temperature.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assess for signs of bleeding around the urethra.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Teach the patient that this is an expected side effect of the drug.

questions.rationale: Prochlorperazine (Compazine) may cause urine to change color to pink or reddish-brown. This is temporary and disappears within days after the drug is discontinued.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 361

questions.stem: A patient with constipation is prescribed bisacodyl (Dulcolax). The patient also states he has severe abdominal pain. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer the drug as prescribed to relieve the constipation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensure that the patient drinks a full glass of water with the drug.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Listen for bowel sounds in all four quadrants before giving the drug.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hold the drug and contact the prescriber.

questions.rationale: Ask the patient about abdominal pain. Drugs for constipation should not be given to a patient experiencing undiagnosed abdominal pain or acute abdomen, because these drugs increase peristalsis and the risk of bowel perforation.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 366

questions.stem: Which statement by a patient prescribed an antidiarrheal drug indicates the need for additional teaching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I will never take a double dose of my medication even if I miss a dose.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I will get up slowly when I get out of bed or rise from a chair.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I will contact my prescriber if the diarrhea is not relieved within 4 days.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I will avoid alcohol while taking my antidiarrheal medication.”

questions.rationale: Antidiarrheal drugs should relieve diarrheal symptoms within 2 days. Remind patients to notify their prescriber if the diarrhea is not relieved in 2 days while taking antidiarrheal drugs. Antidiarrheal drugs should not be taken for more than 2 days unless instructed to do so by the prescriber.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 370

questions.stem: Why should bismuth subsalicylate (Pepto-Bismol) be avoided in children?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It contains an aspirin-like drug that may cause Reye’s syndrome.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Safe use of this drug in children has not been researched.

choices.label: C
choices.text: This drug can inhibit the action of growth hormone, stunting a child’s growth.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It can increase the risk for dehydration and diarrhea in children.

questions.rationale: Children should not be given bismuth subsalicylate because it contains an aspirin-like drug and may cause Reye’s syndrome. This is a life-threatening condition that affects the liver and CNS and causes vomiting and confusion. It occurs soon after the onset of a viral illness in which a child was treated with aspirin.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 371

questions.stem: For which patient should question the order for trimethobenzamide (Tigan)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 70-year-old who has prostate cancer

choices.label: B
choices.text: 50-year-old who has hypertension

choices.label: C
choices.text: 40-year-old with a seizure disorder

choices.label: D
choices.text: 30-year-old with asthma

questions.rationale: An adverse effect of trimethobenzamide is increased risk for seizures and coma. This problem occurs more frequently among people who have a seizure disorder.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 360 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which are potential complications of nausea/vomiting? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bleeding

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aortic aneurysm

choices.label: C
choices.text: Aspiration pneumonia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bowel perforation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reopening of surgical wounds

choices.label: F
choices.text: Respiratory acidosis

questions.rationale: Nausea/vomiting create physiological complications which include bleeding, aspiration pneumonia, dehydration, and reopening of surgical wounds. These complications can be very costly and prolong hospital stays.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 357

questions.stem: Which are symptoms of constipation? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Having less than one bowel movement a day.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stools that is harder than normal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sudden increase in frequency of bowel movements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bowels that feel full after a bowel movement.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sensation of feeling bloated.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Coffee-ground appearance of stools.

questions.rationale: Signs and symptoms of constipation include fewer than three bowel movements a week, a sudden decrease in the frequency of bowel movements, stools that are harder than normal, bowels that still feel full after a bowel movement, and feeling bloated.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Understanding   REF:  p. 362

questions.stem: What are the symptoms of diarrhea? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Weight loss

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bowels still feeling full after a bowel movement

choices.label: C
choices.text: Abdominal pain and cramping

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fever and chills

choices.label: E
choices.text: Feeling bloated

choices.label: F
choices.text: Feeling ill

questions.rationale: Signs and symptoms of diarrhea include a frequent need to have a bowel movement, abdominal pain and cramping, fever, chills, generally feeling ill, and weight loss. Bowels feeling full after a bowel movement and feeling bloated are symptoms of constipation.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Understanding   REF:  p. 367

questions.stem: Which teaching points would you include for a patient who is taking laxatives for constipation? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Drink between 1500 and 2000 mL of fluids every day.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Avoid bulk foods such as whole-grain bread and vegetables.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use the bathroom right away when you feel the urge to have a bowel movement.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Try to get some regular exercise each day to prevent constipation.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Take your laxative with at least 4 ounces of water so it can be effective.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Laxatives are usually prescribed for one 2 to 3 weeks.

questions.rationale: Adequate fluid intake (1500 to 2000 mL), bulk foods in the diet, and regular daily exercise are important strategies to prevent constipation. Patients should be taught to use the bathroom right away when the urge to have a bowel movement occurs. At least 9 ounces of fluid should be given with oral laxatives so that they can be safe and effective. Laxatives should not be taken for more than a week without consulting with a prescriber.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Understanding    REF:  p. 366

questions.stem: Which are expected and intended responses to antiemetic drugs? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vomiting reflex is inhibited.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Vomiting reflex pathway is uninterrupted.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient is sedated.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nausea is relieved.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Retching occurs as expected.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Vomiting is prevented.

questions.rationale: Intended responses of antiemetic drugs include: vomiting reflex is inhibited, vomiting reflex pathways are interrupted or disrupted, patient is sedated, nausea is relieved, and vomiting is prevented.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Understanding   REF:  p. 359 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: A child is prescribed metoclopramide (Reglan) 8 mg by oral solution. The available drug solution is 5 mg/5 mL of solution. How many milliliters does the nurse administer?
questions.rationale: Want 8 mg/X mL, Have 5 mg/5 mL = 1.6  5 mL = 8 mL.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 359

questions.stem: A patient for whom other drugs have failed to control his chemotherapy induced nausea/vomiting is prescribed to receive metoclopramide (Reglan) 1 mg/kg intravenously right before he receives chemotherapy. He weighs 186 lb. The available drug is metoclopramide 10 mg/mL. How many milliliters should you prepare for the correct dose?
questions.rationale: 1 kg = 2.2 lb, Divide the patient’s weight in pounds (186) by 2.2 to get 84.5 kg. Multiply 84.5 kg by 1 mg to get 84.5 mg. Divide 84.5 mg by 10 mg to get 8.45 mL, round up to 8.5 mL.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 359 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Which bacteria is often the cause of stomach ulcers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Escherichia coli

choices.label: B
choices.text: Helicobacter pylori

choices.label: C
choices.text: Staphylococcus aureus

choices.label: D
choices.text: Streptococcus pneumoniae

questions.rationale: Research has shown that 80% to 90% of gastric ulcers are caused by infection with the Helicobacter pylori (H. pylori) bacteria. H. pylori infection is present in 20% to 30% of people in the United States. Some people experience no signs or symptoms, whereas others develop ulcers.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 374

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Which problem indicates a gastric ulcer is getting worse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gaining weight

choices.label: B
choices.text: Abdominal gas

choices.label: C
choices.text: Frequent diarrhea

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blood in the stools

questions.rationale: A major sign that indicates an ulcer is getting worse is blood in stools. Other signs are listed in Box 23-2 in the text.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 376, Box 23-2

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: What is the most common symptom of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Heartburn

choices.label: B
choices.text: Drowsiness

choices.label: C
choices.text: Constipation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bloody sputum

questions.rationale: The most common symptom of GERD is dyspepsia (heartburn). Other common symptoms include sour or bitter taste; bitter stomach fluid going into the mouth, especially during sleep; hoarseness; water brash (regurgitation of watery acid from the stomach); a repeated need to clear the throat; difficulty swallowing food or liquid; wheezing or coughing at night; and worsening of symptoms after eating or when bending over or lying down.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 376

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Which drug used to treat peptic ulcer disease (PUD) and gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) is a histamine H2 blocker?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Metoclopramide (Reglan)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Esomeprazole (Nexium)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Famotidine (Pepcid)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cetirizine (Zyrtec)

questions.rationale: Histamine H2 blockers decrease the secretion of gastric acid. Famotidine is an example of this class of drugs.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 379

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Which activity interferes with the action of histamine H2 blockers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Going to bed late

choices.label: B
choices.text: Smoking cigarettes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Eating dairy products

choices.label: D
choices.text: Taking oral contraceptives

questions.rationale: Smoking interferes with the action of histamine H2 blockers so encourage patients to quit smoking.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 380

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: How do proton pump inhibitors work to treat peptic ulcer disease (PUD) and gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Forming a thick stomach coating

choices.label: B
choices.text: Neutralizing acids in the stomach

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blocking the production of stomach acid

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreasing the stimulation of H2 receptors

questions.rationale: Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) work by completely blocking the production of stomach acid. These drugs block the action of “pumps” located in acid-secreting cells, which totally blocks stomach acid secretion.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 381

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: What is the common side effect of antacids containing calcium or aluminum?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Allergies

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bleeding

choices.label: C
choices.text: Constipation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased urine output

questions.rationale: Side effects of antacids are very rare when they are taken as directed. The most common side effect of antacids containing calcium or aluminum salts is constipation. The most common side effect of antacids containing magnesium salts is diarrhea.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 383

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Which drug forms a protective coating over an ulcer but does not coat normal stomach mucosa?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sucralfate (Carafate)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lansoprazole (Prevacid)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Metoclopramide (Reglan)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bismuth subsalicylate (Pepto-Bismol)

questions.rationale: Sucralfate (Carafate) forms a protective coating over an ulcer but does not coat normal stomach mucosa.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 385

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Which antibiotic should be prescribed for H. pylori infection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Penicillin (Pen V)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ciprofloxacin (Cipro)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Erythromycin (Erythrocin)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Clarithromycin (Biaxin)

questions.rationale: Antibiotics are essential to the treatment plan for PUD and are used to treat H. pylori infections of the GI tract. Clarithromycin is an example of a drug used to treat H. pylori infection.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Remembering    REF:  p. 375

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Which protective mechanism by the stomach protects it from stomach acids and the formation of gastric ulcers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Buffers such as bicarbonate neutralize stomach acids.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The stomach secretes a thick gel-like mucus to coat itself.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acid secretion is decreased when food is not present in the stomach.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stomach secretions such as lipase digest foods and protect the stomach.

questions.rationale: The stomach secretes a thick gel-like mucus that coats and protects it from contact with stomach acids. Acid production is usually balanced by mucous secretion. When acid production exceeds mucous production, the risk for tissue damage and ulcer formation increases.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Remembering    REF:  p. 374

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Which symptoms are associated with gastric ulcers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Constant bloating and diarrhea

choices.label: B
choices.text: Shortness of breath in any position

choices.label: C
choices.text: Burning pain that is relieved by eating

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lower abdominal pain that is relieved by moving the bowels

questions.rationale: The most common symptom of a peptic ulcer is burning, gnawing pain caused by stomach acid coming into contact with the open wound (ulcer). It often occurs when the stomach is empty and can be relieved by eating foods that buffer stomach acids or by taking a drug that reduces stomach acid such as an antacid.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Remembering    REF:  p. 375 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Why does the backward flow of stomach contents into the esophagus cause tissue damage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No digestive processes occur in the esophagus.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The esophagus does not have the thick gel-like mucus to protect it from acid.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The esophagus secretes only bicarbonate, which is not strong enough to neutralize stomach acids.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The esophagus cannot expand with extra volume and the excessive stretching damages the muscle layer.

questions.rationale: The stomach is the site where protein digestion begins. Acid is secreted in the stomach to enhance this process. Because the acids can damage the stomach lining, the stomach secretes a thick gel-like mucus to protect it. Neither the esophagus nor the duodenum has this protection. Stomach contents that reflux up into the esophagus are highly acidic and cause inflammatory damage with ulcer formation to the lining.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 376

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: A patient with a history of GI ulcers reports feeling new onset burning, gnawing stomach pain. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assess the patient’s abdomen for the presence of active bowel sounds.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer the already ordered as-needed dose of liquid antacid.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Offer the patient food to buffer excess stomach acid.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Notify the prescriber immediately.

questions.rationale: The most common symptom of peptic ulcer is burning, gnawing pain caused by stomach acid coming into contact with an open wound (ulcer). The pain usually occurs somewhere between the navel and breastbone and may last from a few minutes to many hours. Even though foods and antacids may relieve the pain, the fact that this is new onset pain indicates that you should notify the prescriber immediately.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 375

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: A patient who is prescribed sucralfate asks how this drug will help treat his gastric ulcer. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Sucralfate decreases secretion of gastric acids to help your ulcer heal.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Sucralfate completely blocks the secretion of gastric acids so your ulcer can heal.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Sucralfate treats the infection with H. pylori that is the major cause of gastric ulcers.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Sucralfate forms a thick coating to cover the ulcer, protect it from further damage, and allow healing.”

questions.rationale: Sucralfate is a cryoprotective drug. These drugs form a thick coating that covers an ulcer to protect the open sore from further damage and allow healing to occur. Histamine blockers decrease secretion of gastric acids. Proton pump inhibitors block secretion of gastric acids. Antibiotics are used to treat H. pylori infections.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 385

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: How do histamine (H2) blockers help heal gastric ulcers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They promote cell division.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They neutralize acids that are present in the stomach.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They decrease the amount of acid secreted by stomach cells.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They increase the rate that stomach contents move into the intestinal tract.

questions.rationale: Ulcers do not heal in an acidic environment. Histamine increases acid production by stimulating the stomach’s parietal cells to secrete hydrochloric acid. Histamine (H2) blockers reduce the amount of histamine that is present, which then results in less stimulation of the parietal cells.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 379

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: You are teaching an older adult patient who has been prescribed famotidine (Pepcid) and his or her family about the drug. Which common side effect should you instruct the patient’s family to watch for and report to the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Confusion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anxiety

choices.label: C
choices.text: Depression

choices.label: D
choices.text: Psychosis

questions.rationale: Famotidine is a histamine H2 blocker. The most common side effect of these drugs is confusion. You must teach the patient’s family to monitor for changes in level of consciousness and confusion and to notify the prescriber if this occurs.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 381

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: A patient who has been taking nizatidine (Axid) for the last month has all of the following blood laboratory results. Which result must be reported to the prescriber as soon as possible?

choices.label: A
choices.text: International normalized ratio (INR) of 0.9

choices.label: B
choices.text: Red blood cell count of 2 million/mm3

choices.label: C
choices.text: White blood cell count of 8000/mm3

choices.label: D
choices.text: Platelet count of 150,000/mm3

questions.rationale: Nizatidine is a histamine H2 blocker. This class of drugs can cause anemia. The patient’s red blood cell count is more than 50% below normal.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 380

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: A patient with gastroesophageal reflux disease is prescribed ranitidine (Zantac). Which statement by the patient indicates the need for additional teaching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I will notify my prescriber if I need to use ranitidine for more than 2 weeks.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I will look into a smoking cessation program when I go home.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I will take the ranitidine first thing in the morning so that its effect will last all day and night.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I will not drive until I know how the ranitidine affects me.”

questions.rationale: To prolong the effects of histamine H2 blockers like ranitidine, these drugs should be taken with meals. If a patient takes this drug once a day, giving it at bedtime prolongs the effects when there is no food in the stomach and reflux may be worse.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying (Application) or Higher REF:  p. 379

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed ranitidine (Zantac) reports darkened stools. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hold the dose and notify the prescriber.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document the report as the only action.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reassure the patient that this is a common and harmless side effect.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient if he or she has been using aspirin or any other NSAID drug.

questions.rationale: Ranitidine contains a substance that darkens the tongue and the stools. This is a harmless side effect and does not indicate a health problem. It is not a reason to stop the drug.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 380

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: A patient who has been taking cimetidine (Tagamet) for a year has all of the following symptoms. Which symptom should be reported immediately to the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Darkened urine and white stool

choices.label: B
choices.text: Altered food taste and dry mouth

choices.label: C
choices.text: Weekly constipation with bloating

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduced interest in sexual activity

questions.rationale: Cimetidine can cause drug-induced hepatitis. Dark urine and white stool are symptoms of liver problems and should be investigated immediately before any damage becomes permanent. It is likely the drug will need to be discontinued.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 380

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: You are instructing a pregnant patient about how to manage reflux. Which point about histamine H2 blockers should you include in the teaching plan?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are safe for use during pregnancy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They may be used while breastfeeding.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They should only be taken with a prescriber’s approval.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They can be prescribed instead of lifestyle changes to control reflux.

questions.rationale: The use of histamine H2 blockers has not been studied in pregnant women and safe use has not been established. These drugs pass into breast milk so should be avoided while breastfeeding. During pregnancy, lifestyle changes (e.g., eating more slowly, eating smaller meals) should be tried first to control reflux. Pregnant women must consult with their prescriber before taking any drug for reflux.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 381

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Which precaution is most important to teach an older patient who is starting histamine H2 blockers for gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Do not drink caffeine while taking this drug.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Elevate the head of your bed by 6 to 10 inches.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Take this drug with meals and before going to bed.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Avoid driving until you know how this drug affects you.”

questions.rationale: Older adults are more likely to experience drowsiness and acute confusion when taking these drugs. They should avoid driving or operating dangerous equipment until they know how the drug affects them. Although the drug should be taken with a meal or before bedtime, the safety issue with the confusion and drowsiness has the highest priority. Avoiding caffeine and elevating the head of the bed are helpful for GERD but are not related to drug therapy.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 381

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: A patient who is prescribed omeprazole (Prilosec) asks why the drug should be taken before breakfast. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “The intended action of this drug is best achieved when given before meals, preferably in the morning.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “It is best to take this drug in the morning so that antacids taken later in the day do not interfere with absorption.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “This drug should always be taken on an empty stomach for maximal action and for better absorption.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Taking this drug early in the morning helps to minimize the occurrence of side effects or adverse effects.”

questions.rationale: Omeprazole is a proton pump inhibitor. These drugs work best when given before meals, preferably in the morning. Proton pump inhibitors can be given with antacids, with food, or without food.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 381

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Which precaution is most important to teach a patient who is prescribed rabeprazole (Aciphex)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Wear a hat and sunscreen outdoors to prevent sunburn.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Take this drug 1 hour before or 2 hours after a meal.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Drink at least 3 L of fluid daily to prevent constipation.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Avoid eating raw fruits and vegetables while taking this drug.”

questions.rationale: Rabeprazole increases sun sensitivity and patients can develop severe sunburn if precautions are not taken. This effect also can occur in people who have very dark skin and have never before experienced sunburn. Increased sun sensitivity is present during drug therapy and for many weeks after therapy is completed.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 382

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed rabeprazole (Acipex) develops black, tarry stools. What is your best first action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check the patient’s medication list for prescribed iron supplements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Teach the patient that this is an expected side effect of proton pump inhibitors.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Send a stool sample to the laboratory to check for presence of occult blood.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Keep track of the number of stools the patient has in a 24-hour period.

questions.rationale: Black and tarry stools are never normal; they indicate bleeding and should be reported to the prescriber at once. Sending a stool sample to the laboratory to test for blood helps to confirm the presence of blood in the stool. You would also want to notify the prescriber of this finding.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 382

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: To prevent a common risk among older adults who are prescribed proton pump inhibitors, which safety precaution should you recommend?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Be sure to take a daily multivitamin.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Use handrails when going up or down stairs.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Take this drug with food or a full glass of fluid.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Notify your prescriber if you develop belching or gas.”

questions.rationale: Proton pump inhibitors have been associated with increased hip fracture in older adults because of decreased calcium absorption. Teach patients to use side rails when going up or down stairs.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 382

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Why are older patients who are prescribed proton pump inhibitors more at risk for drug- induced hip fracture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Induced drowsiness increases the risk for falling.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inhibited calcium absorption makes bones more fragile.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Excessive potassium loss reduces muscle strength and balance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Anemia and fatigue prevent participation in exercise and reduce muscle weakness.

questions.rationale: The change in acidity of stomach contents reduces the absorption of calcium through the intestinal tract. As calcium is lost from bones, it is not replaced and the bones become more fragile. More bone density is lost from the hip, making hip fractures more likely, even without a fall or other trauma.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 382

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: A patient with indigestion is prescribed aluminum hydroxide (Amphojel). To prevent the most common side effect of this drug, what should you teach the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Take this drug 30 minutes before each meal.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Do not drink fluids for at least 30 minutes after taking this drug.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Be sure to consume a diet with lots of vegetables and other foods with fiber.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Report any episodes of diarrhea immediately to your prescriber.”

questions.rationale: Aluminum hydroxide is an aluminum-based antacid. The most common side effect of these drugs is constipation. Patients should be taught to consume a diet rich in fiber-containing foods to prevent this side effect.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 383, Table 23-2

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Which precaution is most important to teach a patient who is prescribed an antacid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Avoid using extra pepper on your food while taking this drug.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “If you become constipated, stop taking the antacid completely.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Take this drug at least 2 hours before or after taking any other drug.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Notify your prescriber if heartburn continues after you have taken the drug for 3 days.”

questions.rationale: Antacids interfere with the absorption of other drugs. This can render other drugs completely ineffective. Patients should be taught to never take other drugs with antacids and to separate taking antacids from taking other drugs by 2 hours.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 384

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Why should parents be taught never to give bismuth subsalicylate (Pepto-Bismol) to a child?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It contains aspirin that can cause Reye’s syndrome.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It changes stool color making the identification of GI bleeding more difficult.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It has such powerful antidiarrheal effects that toxic megacolon can occur.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It produces a paradoxical reaction in children that results in worsening of the symptoms.

questions.rationale: Children should not be given bismuth subsalicylate (Pepto-Bismol) because it contains aspirin and can cause Reye’s syndrome, a life-threatening condition that affects the liver and central nervous system.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 387

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: The spouse of an older adult with Alzheimer’s disease tells you that she has been giving him Amphojel (aluminum hydroxide) for heartburn. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the spouse how often the patient is taking the Amphojel.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Suggest that the patient be given AlternaGEL instead.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Teach the spouse that taking this drug can worsen the patient’s condition.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Remind the spouse that giving this drug often can interfere with bowel function.

questions.rationale: Amphojel is an aluminum-based antacid. Older adults with Alzheimer’s disease should not take aluminum-based antacids because these drugs can worsen the patient’s condition. AlternaGEL is not a good alternative because it is also aluminum-based.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 384

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: You are instructing a patient who has been prescribed sucralfate (Carafate) for peptic ulcer disease. Which statement by the patient indicates the need for additional teaching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “This drug coats my ulcer and protects it so that healing can occur.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I should eat foods with lots of fiber while I’m taking this drug.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “If I feel dizzy, I will be sure to get up from bed slowly.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Once I am feeling better I can stop taking the drug.”

questions.rationale: Patients prescribed sucralfate should take the drug exactly as directed by their prescriber. You must teach the patient that this drug is usually prescribed for 4 to 8 weeks and the patient should continue to take it even if feeling better. This is needed because complete healing of the ulcer under good conditions may take 4 to 8 weeks.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 385

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: A patient with gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) asks why metoclopramide (Reglan) has been prescribed. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “The purpose of this drug for GERD is to prevent nausea.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “This drug has been prescribed to help move food along through your GI tract.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Metoclopramide will prevent vomiting and the risk for aspiration.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “This drug causes growth of new cells to heal your esophagus.”

questions.rationale: Metoclopramide increases stomach and small intestine contractions (peristalsis), helping to move food through the GI system. Moving food quickly into the intestinal system decreases the likelihood of backup into the esophagus.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 386

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: What common symptom do you expect to find in a patient with peptic ulcer disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Frequently vomiting bright red blood

choices.label: B
choices.text: Burning gnawing pain between the navel and sternum

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nausea with reflux before and after all meals

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dark coffee-ground textured stools

questions.rationale: The most common symptom of a peptic ulcer is burning, gnawing pain caused by stomach acid coming into contact with the open wound (ulcer). The pain usually occurs somewhere between the navel (umbilicus) and the breastbone (sternum) and may last from a few minutes to many hours.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 375

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Why should a patient with GERD avoid substances such as chocolate, peppermint, alcohol, nicotine, and caffeinated drinks?

choices.label: A
choices.text: These substances increase the pressure in the lower esophageal sphincter and lead to reflux.

choices.label: B
choices.text: These substances decrease the pressure in the lower esophageal sphincter leading to reflux.

choices.label: C
choices.text: These substances increase the pressure in the duodenal sphincter causing reflux and emesis.

choices.label: D
choices.text: These substances decrease the pressure in the duodenal sphincter causing reflux and emesis.

questions.rationale: Patients with GERD should avoid chocolate, peppermint, alcohol, nicotine, and caffeinated drinks because they lower the pressure of the LES and promote reflux.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 377

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: A patient is prescribed omeprazole (Prilosec) 40 mg orally for 6 weeks. For which common side effects will you monitor after giving this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Shortness of breath and increased respiratory rate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Burning and itching with urination attempts

choices.label: C
choices.text: Muscle aches and pains

choices.label: D
choices.text: Diarrhea and gas

questions.rationale: Side effects rarely occur with PPIs. The most common side effects are diarrhea, constipation, belching and gas, abdominal pain, and headaches.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 381

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: An older adult is prescribed lansoprazole (Prevacid) 15 mg orally each day. The patient asks you why a daily multivitamin has also been prescribed. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “As you get older you need vitamin tablets to ensure that you get the minimum daily requirements.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Lansoprazole is a proton pump inhibitor (PPI) and these drugs interfere with absorption of all vitamins from the GI tract.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Taking a daily multivitamin is important because proton pump inhibitor drugs decrease the absorption of vitamin B12 and calcium.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Older adults have more difficulty processing vitamins and minerals from the daily foods consumed.”

questions.rationale: Lansoprazole is a proton pump inhibitor (PPI). Teach older adults who have been prescribed PPIs to take a daily multivitamin because PPIs decrease the absorption of vitamin B12 and calcium.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 382

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Which patient should always avoid taking sodium-containing antacids such as, Alka- Seltzer and Bromo-Seltzer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 55-year-old female with heart failure

choices.label: B
choices.text: 65-year-old male with overactive bladder

choices.label: C
choices.text: 8-year-old child with Crohn’s disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: 25-year-old with leukemia

questions.rationale: Remind patients with heart failure not to take sodium-containing antacids (e.g., Alka-Seltzer, Bromo-Seltzer) because they increase sodium and water retention. This problem causes an increase in the workload of the heart and can worsen heart failure.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF:  p. 384

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Which precaution is important to teach a patient who plans to use over-the-counter cimetidine to help control gastroesophageal reflux disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Watch your stools for changes that indicate bleeding such as bright red blood or a tarry appearance.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Be sure to tell your health care provider that you are taking this drug.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Avoid taking antacids while taking cimetidine.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Always take cimetidine with a full meal.”

questions.rationale: Cimetidine reduces the level of certain liver enzymes that are responsible for drug metabolism and elimination. As a result, the blood levels of many drugs are increased and can lead to toxic effects. This is especially true for antiseizure medications, calcium channel blockers and other antidysrhythmics, warfarin, and benzodiazepine.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 380 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Which foods, activities, and drugs should you teach the patient with peptic ulcer disease to avoid? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alcohol

choices.label: B
choices.text: Caffeine

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dairy products

choices.label: D
choices.text: Exercising

choices.label: E
choices.text: Smoking

choices.label: F
choices.text: Spicy food g Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)

questions.rationale: Alcohol, caffeine, and spicy foods irritate an existing ulcer and promote inflammation. Smoking slows cell division and can delay healing. In addition, smoking promotes acid secretion. NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandin synthesis, which reduces the amount of gel-like mucus in the stomach and increases the likelihood that ulcers will continue to form. Neither dairy products nor exercise promote ulcer formation or delay or reduce healing.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 375

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: What should you teach a patient about smoking and peptic ulcer disease? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Smoking increases acid secretion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Smoking is not a cause of ulcer formation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Smoking slows ulcer healing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Smoking is related to the reoccurrence of ulcers.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Smoking decreases stomach mucus production.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Smoking stimulates scar tissue formation in the stomach.

questions.rationale: When a patient has peptic ulcer disease, smoking is not highly recommended because smoking slows ulcer healing; is related to ulcer recurrence; increases acid secretion; reduces prostaglandin, mucus, and bicarbonate production; and decreases mucosal blood flow.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 375

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Which are risk factors for an individual to develop GERD? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Being underweight

choices.label: B
choices.text: Being pregnant

choices.label: C
choices.text: Taking NSAID drugs

choices.label: D
choices.text: Eating foods high in alkaline content

choices.label: E
choices.text: Drinking caffeinated beverages

choices.label: F
choices.text: Eating spicy foods g Drinking 2 L of water daily.

questions.rationale: Risk factors for development of GERD are listed in Box 23-3 in the text.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 377, Box 23-3

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: Which are intended responses for histamine (H2) blockers? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased gastric acid secretion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased gastric acid secretion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Esophageal reflux is decreased

choices.label: D
choices.text: White blood cells are decreased

choices.label: E
choices.text: Gastric ulcers are healed

choices.label: F
choices.text: Occult bleeding is detected early

questions.rationale: The intended responses for H2 blockers are: secretion of gastric acid is decreased, symptoms of GERD are decreased, and ulcers are healed and prevented.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 380

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: For which occurrences should you teach a patient taking drugs for PUD or GERD to notify the prescriber? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Difficulty swallowing

choices.label: B
choices.text: Persistent belching

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vomiting bright red blood

choices.label: D
choices.text: Brown formed stools

choices.label: E
choices.text: Coffee ground appearing emesis

choices.label: F
choices.text: Bright yellow urine g Dry mouth h Fissured tongue

questions.rationale: Tell patients taking drugs for PUD or GERD to notify their prescriber for difficulty swallowing; persistent abdominal pain; vomiting blood (bright red or coffee grounds–appearing emesis); or black, tarry stools.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 379 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: A 12-year-old child is prescribed a total daily IV dose of ranitidine (Zantac) 4 mg/kg/day. The child weighs 110 lb. The total dose is to be divided into four doses to be given every 6 hours. How many milligrams will you give for each dose?
questions.rationale: 110 lb/2.2 = 50 kg. 4 mg  50 kg = 200 mg/day. 200 mg/4 doses/day = 50 mg/dose IV.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 379

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Gastric Ulcers and Reflux
questions.stem: An adult patient is prescribed esomeprazole magnesium (Nexium) 80 mg orally once daily. The pharmacy has esomeprazole magnesium (Nexium) 40 mg tablets. How many tablets will you teach the patient to take for each dose?
questions.rationale: Have 40 mg/1 tablet, Want 80 mg/X tablets; 40/80 = 2 tablets.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 381 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which type of seizure has a sudden loss of muscle tone for a few seconds followed by confusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Partial seizures

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atonic seizures

choices.label: C
choices.text: Myoclonic seizures

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tonic-clonic seizures

questions.rationale: With an atonic seizure typically there is sudden loss of muscle tone for a few seconds, followed by postictal (after the seizure) confusion.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 404

questions.stem: What is another term for seizure disorder? Epilepsy a.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Enkephalin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Narcolepsy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Neuropathy

questions.rationale: An individual with repeated seizures has a seizure disorder, sometimes called epilepsy.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 401

questions.stem: Which health problem is the most serious possible side effect for status epilepticus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ruptured spinal disks

choices.label: B
choices.text: Brain tumor

choices.label: C
choices.text: Broken bones

choices.label: D
choices.text: Brain damage

questions.rationale: Status epilepticus is a prolonged seizure (usually defined as lasting longer than 30 minutes) or a series of repeated seizures that may occur in almost any type of seizure. Rapid recognition and treatment of this disorder are essential to prevent brain damage.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 404

questions.stem: Which first-line antiseizure drug can only be given by the oral route?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gatifloxacin (Tequin)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Phenytoin (Dilantin)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Carbamazepine (Tegretol)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Valproic acid (Depakote)

questions.rationale: All of these first-line drugs can be given orally and intravenously except carbamazepine.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 407

questions.stem: Which organs can be badly damaged by valproic acid (Depakote)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Eyes and ears

choices.label: B
choices.text: Skeletal muscles

choices.label: C
choices.text: Liver and pancreas

choices.label: D
choices.text: Brain and spinal cord

questions.rationale: Serious adverse effects of valproic acid include damage to the liver (hepatotoxicity) and inflammation of the pancreas (pancreatitis).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 408

questions.stem: Which side effect of phenytoin (Dilantin) commonly occurs among children taking the drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Delayed bone growth

choices.label: B
choices.text: Darkening of the eyes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thickening of the eyelashes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Thickening of the gum tissues

questions.rationale: Phenytoin must be used carefully for children because of extra growth of gums while taking the drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 408

questions.stem: Which side effect is most common among people taking first-line drugs for absence seizures?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Weight loss

choices.label: B
choices.text: Weight gain

choices.label: C
choices.text: Low blood pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: High blood pressure

questions.rationale: Common side effects of first-line drugs for seizures include the GI symptoms of nausea, vomiting, and indigestion. They may also cause loss of appetite (anorexia) and weight loss.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 409

questions.stem: Which second-line drug for seizure control has a potential for physical dependence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Terbinafine (Lamisil)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Primidone (Mysoline)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lamotrigine (Lamictal)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phenobarbital (Luminal)

questions.rationale: Second-line drugs for seizures are alternative drugs for the treatment of seizures. Included in this group are phenobarbital (Luminal) and primidone (Mysoline), which increase the body’s threshold against seizure activity by blocking or slowing the spread of abnormal impulses. The disadvantage of phenobarbital is that it can lead to physical dependence.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 410

questions.stem: Which two conditions are possible complications of status epilepticus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blurred vision and ringing in the ears

choices.label: B
choices.text: Muscle paralysis and weakness

choices.label: C
choices.text: Brain damage and death

choices.label: D
choices.text: Asthma and wheezing

questions.rationale: Without rapid recognition and treatment, status epilepticus can result in brain damage, coma, and death.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 404

questions.stem: Which antiseizure drug increases the availability of the neurotransmitter gamma- aminobutyric acid (GABA)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Phenobarbital (Luminal)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Carbamazepine (Tegretol)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Valproic acid (Depakote)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phenytoin (Dilantin)

questions.rationale: The action of valproic acid may be related to increased availability of the neurotransmitter gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 407 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which temporary condition is considered a brief but expected response immediately after an individual has a seizure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Residual muscle twitches

choices.label: B
choices.text: Low blood pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Unconsciousness

choices.label: D
choices.text: Confusion

questions.rationale: During seizure activity, especially a generalized seizure, the brain is not well-oxygenated and the individual is not aware of his or her surroundings and events. This result in a brief period of confusion after the seizure is over.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 404

questions.stem: A patient with a seizure disorder tells you about training to participate in a marathon run. What is the most important factor to teach the patient at this time?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Your seizure medications prohibit training for this stressful event.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “During times of increased physical stress you are more likely to have a seizure.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Be sure to watch your diet because during training you will need more calories.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Start your exercise program slowly and gradually build up your strength.”

questions.rationale: An individual with a seizure disorder is more likely to have a seizure during times of increased emotional or physical stress (such as training for a marathon). While watching the diet and pacing the exercise program are important, they are not related to the patient’s seizure disorder. Most antiseizure drugs do not require the patient to avoid exercise.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 402

questions.stem: A patient with a seizure disorder asks why bright spots are always seen before experiencing a seizure. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Some people experience a strange sensation called an aura before each seizure.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “This is an unusual occurrence. I will notify your prescriber right away.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Bright spots before a seizure could indicate pressure in your brain from a tumor.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “After the seizure do you feel confused, lethargic, and unable to respond to people?”

questions.rationale: Before a seizure, some people experience an aura, which is a strange sensation. It can be an odor, sound, taste, or visual sensation such as bright spots or flashes. It is a common occurrence and not an indicator of tumors or pressure in the brain. Confusion, lethargy, and an inability to respond are common for the postictal phase of a seizure, but asking about these symptoms does not address the patient’s question.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 402

questions.stem: Which sign should you expect to see when a patient has an absence seizure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rigidity of arm and leg muscles

choices.label: B
choices.text: Automatisms such as lip smacking

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blank staring as if daydreaming

choices.label: D
choices.text: One-sided movement of an extremity

questions.rationale: Absence (petit mal) seizures are more common in children. They involve a brief (few seconds) loss of consciousness and blank staring. A child may appear to be daydreaming. After the seizure the child returns to normal immediately.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 404

questions.stem: Which action is most important to perform for a patient during a simple partial seizure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administering the prescribed antiseizure drug

choices.label: B
choices.text: Record timing the duration of the seizure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inserting a padded tongue blade

choices.label: D
choices.text: Applying oxygen by mask

questions.rationale: A simple, partial seizure does not result in loss of consciousness or respiratory problems. The patient is observed and the duration of the seizure is documented. Administering drugs at this time would not stop the seizure and the attempt could stimulate further seizure activity.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 405

questions.stem: What is the priority intervention for a patient experiencing a tonic-clonic (grand mal) seizure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Place a padded tongue blade in the patient’s mouth to prevent biting the tongue.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Start a saline lock to administer IV antiseizure medication.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Attach pads to the bedside rails to protect the patient from injury.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Monitor for and keep the patient’s airway open.

questions.rationale: The priorities for care of a patient experiencing a seizure include maintaining an airway and providing for safety during the seizure. Forcing a padded tongue blade into a patient’s mouth can damage teeth and lead to aspiration of tooth fragments. Padded side rails are controversial because it is not known if they help maintain safety; they may cause embarrassment to the patient. During a seizure is not the time to place a saline lock.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 405

questions.stem: A pediatric patient is prescribed valproic acid (Depacon) 340 mg intravenously. The available drug solution is 500 mg in 5 mL. How many milliliters should you give?
questions.rationale: Ratio and proportion—340 mg/X mL is equal to 500 mg/5 mL, 1700X/500 = 3.4 mL.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 407

questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed phenytoin (Dilantin) reports gum swelling around the teeth. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tell the patient to document this unexpected change by taking a photograph.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Instruct the patient to stop the drug and notify the prescriber immediately.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Teach the patient to visit the dentist regularly and brush and flow carefully.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Remind the patient to clean teeth gently with dental swabs and avoid flossing.

questions.rationale: Phenytoin (Dilantin) can cause extra growth of gum tissues. Tell patients to visit their dentist regularly and to brush and floss teeth carefully.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 408

questions.stem: A patient who has been taking carbamazepine (Tegretol) for 2 months has all of the following blood laboratory results. Which result should you report to the prescriber immediately?

choices.label: A
choices.text: White blood cell (WBC) count 2200/mm3

choices.label: B
choices.text: Platelet count 300,000/mm3

choices.label: C
choices.text: Potassium 3.6 mEq/L

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sodium 132 mEq/L

questions.rationale: Carbamazepine can suppress bone marrow activity and decrease the number of WBCs. This patient’s WBC count is much lower than normal (normal range is 5000 to 10,000/mm3), greatly increasing his or her risk for infection. The drug must be discontinued as soon as possible (but not abruptly). The blood sodium level is slightly low as well, but it is not an immediate problem.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 407

questions.stem: Which question is most important to ask a patient who is prescribed phenytoin (Dilantin) before?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Do you drink coffee or other caffeinated beverages?”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Have you ever had high blood pressure?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “How many seizures do you have daily?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “What other drugs do you take daily?”

questions.rationale: Phenytoin interacts with many other drugs and changes their activity. When patients who are prescribed phenytoin are also taking other drugs, the dosages of the other drugs may need to be altered, and additional monitoring may be needed.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 408

questions.stem: Which precaution is most important to teach a patient who is prescribed any of the first- line drugs for generalized seizures?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Avoid drinking alcoholic beverages.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Take the drug with food or a snack.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Engage in regular exercise daily.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “See your dentist twice a year.”

questions.rationale: First-line antiseizure drugs enter the brain and often cause drowsiness and dizziness. This effect is worsened by drinking alcohol, increasing the risk for injury and causing the patient to have reduced cognition. The drugs can be taken with food, but do not have to be. Having regular dental checkups is important, but the priority precaution relates to alcohol avoidance.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 406

questions.stem: What is the priority safety measure to implement when a patient has been started on carbamazepine (Tegretol)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the patient about symptoms of nausea/vomiting.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Monitor for and manage seizure activity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assist the patient when getting out of bed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Always give this drug with food.

questions.rationale: Carbamazepine is a first-line drug for partial and generalized seizures. These drugs can cause dizziness and drowsiness. You must teach the patient to ask for assistance when getting out of bed and ensure that the call light is within easy reach.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 406

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed clonazepam (Klonopin). Which adverse effect will you monitor for after giving this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Respiratory depression

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stevens-Johnson syndrome

choices.label: C
choices.text: Angioedema

choices.label: D
choices.text: Epidermal necrolysis

questions.rationale: Clonazepam is a benzodiazepine CNS drug with the life-threatening adverse reaction of respiratory depression.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 412

questions.stem: What is an important teaching point for an older patient prescribed primidone (Mysoline)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: This drug may cause very high fevers.

choices.label: B
choices.text: You may develop a severe rash with this drug.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Your ankles are likely to swell while you are taking this drug.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Primidone can cause development of unusual restlessness and excitement.

questions.rationale: Older adults are also more sensitive to second -line antiseizure drugs and are more likely to develop side effects. They may develop unusual restlessness or excitement with primidone. Ankle swelling is associated with gabapentin (Neurontin) and pregabalin (Lyrica), not with primidone
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 413

questions.stem: A female patient with a seizure disorder who is prescribed valproic acid (Depakote) tells you she wants to have a child. What must be included when teaching this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “This drug is safe to use while breastfeeding.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Valproic acid should be used to prevent seizures during pregnancy.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “This drug may interfere with the ability to become pregnant.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “This drug should be avoided during pregnancy.”

questions.rationale: Valproic acid taken during pregnancy has been associated with developmental defects, low IQ, birth defects, congenital anomalies, and damage to the infant’s liver. This drug also passes into breast milk and should not be taken while a mother is breastfeeding.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: pp. 408-409
