questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed ethosuximide (Zarontin) twice daily (every 12 hours) for absence seizures reports that the morning dose was missed 10 hours ago. What is your best advice?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Skip today’s second dose as well as the dose you already missed.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Take the missed dose now and the regularly scheduled dose in 2 hours.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Take the missed dose and the regularly scheduled second dose immediately.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Take the regularly scheduled second dose now and forget about the morning dose.”

questions.rationale: It is important to try to keep to the dosing schedule to maintain blood levels of antiseizure drugs and prevent seizures. However, it has now been 10 hours since the missed dose. Taking it now and also taking the regularly scheduled second dose could cause blood levels to be too high leading to possible side or adverse effects. The missed dose should not be taken at all. The second dose can be taken now or at its regularly scheduled time and then the patient should return to his or her regular dosing schedule.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 410

questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed ethosuximide (Zarontin) tells you about experiencing an upset stomach after each dose of the drug. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer a dose of an antinausea drug.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Instruct the patient to take the drug with a full glass of water.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give the drug with meals or a snack.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Elevate the head of the bed to avoid aspiration.

questions.rationale: Among the most common side effects of first-line antiseizure drugs such as ethosuximide are GI symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, and indigestion. You should give this drug with food or a snack to minimize or prevent these symptoms. Also teach the patient who has these symptoms to take this drug with meals or a snack when he or she goes home.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 410

questions.stem: What should you teach a patient who is prescribed ethosuximide (Zarontin) about spending time outdoors?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Move slowly to avoid dizziness.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wear dark glasses when the sun is bright.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Be sure to wear long sleeves and a broad-brimmed hat.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Apply a strong sunscreen whenever you will be exposed to sunlight.

questions.rationale: Ethosuximide can make the eyes more sensitive to light. Instruct patients to protect their eyes by wearing dark glasses when going out into bright light. Ethosuximide does not cause skin photosensitivity.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 410

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed lamotrigine for seizure control. The drug received from the pharmacy is Lamisil. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer the drug as prescribed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hold the dose and call the pharmacy immediately.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the patient whether he or she recognizes the tablet.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Notify the prescriber that the usual brand of lamotrigine is not available.

questions.rationale: Lamisil is not a brand of lamotrigine; it is an antifungal drug. This drug is easily confused with the Lamictal brand of lamotrigine. The drug should not be given. The pharmacy needs to be informed about the confusion and asked to send the correct drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 411

questions.stem: Which assessment question is most important to ask a patient who is prescribed clonazepam (Klonopin) before administering the first dose?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Do you smoke cigarettes?”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “How many seizures do you have daily?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Have you ever had high blood pressure?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Do you currently have an open sore or signs of an infection?”

questions.rationale: Cigarette smoking decreases the effectiveness of clonazepam. Smoking is not a contraindication; however, higher dosages of the drug may be needed for smokers.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 413

questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed primidone (Mysoline) also takes warfarin (Coumadin). Which laboratory blood test results indicate a possible drug interaction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hematocrit 32%

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) 21 mg/dL

choices.label: D
choices.text: International normalized ratio (INR) 0.5

questions.rationale: Primidone decreases the effectiveness of anticoagulants, including warfarin. Warfarin effectiveness is measured by the INR. This value should be no less than 1.5 in a patient taking warfarin. With an INR level of only 0.5 the patient is at risk for forming clots and having a stroke, heart attack, pulmonary embolism, or deep vein thrombosis (DVT).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 412

questions.stem: A patient who is prescribed gabapentin (Neurontin) for seizures is also taking aluminum hydroxide (Amphojel) for indigestion. What should you caution the patient about taking these two drugs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Take the gabapentin 1 hour after the aluminum hydroxide.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Schedule at least 2 hours between taking the gabapentin and the aluminum hydroxide.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Take these two drugs together for the best action of both drugs to occur.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “You may need to take a different antacid to avoid affecting the action of gabapentin.”

questions.rationale: Antacids interfere with the absorption of gabapentin. At least 2 hours should be scheduled between doses of gabapentin and any antacid.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 412

questions.stem: A patient who is prescribed gabapentin (Neurontin) is also taking morphine sulfate for severe pain. What dosage adjustment should you expect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased dosage of gabapentin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased dosage of gabapentin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased dosage of morphine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased dosage of morphine

questions.rationale: When a patient takes morphine at the same time as gabapentin, the blood level of gabapentin is increased. You should expect that the ordered dose of gabapentin will be decreased.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 412

questions.stem: A patient is having a prolonged seizure lasting more than 30 minutes. Which drug should you be prepared to administer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Diazepam (Valium) 5 to 10 mg slow IV push

choices.label: B
choices.text: Carbamazepine (Tegretol) 1600 orally as a loading dose

choices.label: C
choices.text: Phenytoin (Dilantin) 15 mg/kg orally as a loading dose

choices.label: D
choices.text: Valproic acid (Depacon IV) 25 mg/kg slow IV push

questions.rationale: A prolonged seizure lasting longer than 30 minutes is status epilepticus. Actions for treating this life-threatening condition include protecting the airway, providing oxygen, establishing intravenous (IV) access to give 5 to 10 mg of diazepam (Valium) by slow IV injection, and determining and treating the cause.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 404

questions.stem: A patient prescribed an antiseizure drug tells you that his favorite morning drink is grapefruit juice. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Call the diet kitchen to order grapefruit juice with the patient’s breakfast tray.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the patient if he would like to have grapefruit juice added to his dinner tray.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Instruct the patient to drink at least 8 ounces of water with each glass of grapefruit juice.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Teach the patient to avoid grapefruit juice because it can increase the effects of these drugs.

questions.rationale: While prescribed and taking any antiseizure drug, patients should be taught to avoid grapefruit and grapefruit juice because they may increase the effects of these drugs.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 406

questions.stem: You are removing an IV catheter from a patient prescribed carbamazepine (Tegretol). What safety technique will you apply while completing this procedure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Place a 2 by 2 dressing over the site with extra tape.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hold pressure on the IV site for at least a minute or until the bleeding stops.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the patient to hold pressure over the IV site for at least 30 seconds.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Put a Band-Aid over the site after to catheter is removed to prevent infection.

questions.rationale: Carbamazepine can also cause thrombocytopenia (low platelet count), increasing a patient’s risk for severe bleeding.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 407

questions.stem: A newly admitted patient with a seizure disorder is prescribed phenytoin (Dilantin) 100 mg orally every 8 hours and phenobarbital (Luminal) 300 mg orally daily. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Remind the prescriber that these two drugs are both types of antiseizure medication.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensure that the phenobarbital is given as early as possible in the morning.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Separate the administration of the two drugs by at least 6 hours.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administer the drugs as prescribed.

questions.rationale: Seizure disorders can be difficult to control and may require more than one type of antiseizure medications. In addition, responses are very individual. The drugs can be taken at the same time. Because phenobarbital can induce sleep, many people prefer to take this drug closer to bedtime.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 405

questions.stem: A 13-year-old boy who has a seizure disorder has grown 6 inches and gained 30 lb in the past 8 months. How do you expect these physical changes to affect his antiseizure drug therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No change will be needed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The dosage of the antiseizure drugs will probably need to be decreased.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The dosage of the antiseizure drugs will probably need to be increased.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The antiseizure drugs can be discontinued because it is likely he has outgrown the disorder.

questions.rationale: Increased growth and hormone changes that occur with puberty can increase the need for higher drug dosages to control seizure disorders. Although some people who have a seizure disorder during childhood may not have the disorder to the same degree in adulthood, adolescents usually need not only to continue the drug therapy to control the seizures but need to have the dosages increased.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 408

questions.stem: Which supplement is needed when a pregnant woman with a seizure disorder is prescribed to take lamotrigine (Lamictal)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ascorbic acid

choices.label: B
choices.text: Folic acid

choices.label: C
choices.text: Citric acid

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nicotinic acid

questions.rationale: Lamotrigine limits the body’s production of folic acid. Folic acid deficiency during pregnancy is associated with a variety of birth defects. To prevent deficiency during pregnancy, a woman taking lamotrigine should take folic acid supplements throughout the pregnancy.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 413

questions.stem: What is the most important action or assessment to perform after giving a patient the first dose of any antiseizure drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check for gingival hyperplasia.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assess any IV sites and skin areas for bleeding.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check the patient’s level of consciousness.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assess the patient’s deep tendon reflexes.

questions.rationale: All drugs for seizure disorders affect the central nervous system and have the potential to alter the level of consciousness shortly after taking them. The development of gingival hyperplasia or bleeding occurs with repeated doses over time. Checking the level of consciousness is more important than assessing deep tendon reflexes.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 406

questions.stem: How does carbamazepine (Tegretol) work to decrease seizure activity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increasing the amount of the neurotransmitter gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) present in the brain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreasing the amount of the neurotransmitter gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) present in the brain

choices.label: C
choices.text: Slowing electrical impulse conduction in the brain by altering sodium channels

choices.label: D
choices.text: Slowing electrical impulse conduction in the brain by altering calcium channels

questions.rationale: Carbamazepine blocks sodium channels in the brain, which slows the transmission of electrical impulses. It does not work by changing the amount of GABA and it does not interfere with calcium channels.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 407

questions.stem: Which precaution should you teach patients prescribed carbamazepine (Tegretol)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Avoid crowds and people who are ill.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Take this drug with an antacid to prevent ulcers.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Restrict your fluid intake to less than 1.5 L daily.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Keep the bottle containing the drug in the refrigerator.

questions.rationale: Carbamazepine can cause a profound reduction in the white blood cell count (neutropenia), which then greatly increases the risk for infection.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 407

questions.stem: A patient who has a seizure disorder and is prescribed valproic acid (Depakote) calls to report that she has been vomiting for 24 hours. What should you advise?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Take the next dose of Depakote with an antacid.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Go to the nearest emergency department to receive the Depakote parenterally.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Take any missed doses and the regularly scheduled doses as soon as the vomiting subsides.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rather than swallowing the next drug dose, place the tablet under the tongue and let it dissolve.

questions.rationale: Depakote and other drugs for seizure control should not be stopped suddenly. This action could lead to severe and prolonged seizure episodes. If a patient is unable to keep the drug down or cannot take the drug orally for any reason, he or she should receive the drug parenterally to ensure seizure control.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 410 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which factors are among the most common causes of seizures in adults? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Emotional distress

choices.label: B
choices.text: Head injury

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stroke

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tumors

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lack of sleep

choices.label: F
choices.text: Fever

questions.rationale: The single most common cause of seizures is not known. However, for adults the most common causes of seizures include head injury, stroke, and tumors.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 402 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: An infant is prescribed phenobarbital 4 mg/kg in an oral suspension. The infant weighs 11 lb. How many milligrams will you give?
questions.rationale: 11 lb/2.2 = 5 kg; 5 kg  4 mg = 20 mg.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 411

questions.stem: A child who weighs 56 lb is prescribed to receive lamotrigine (Lamictal) 0.5 mg/kg as the first dose of the drug. The drug available is lamotrigine 5 mg/scored tablet. How many tablets should you prepare to administer for a correct dose?
questions.rationale: 1 kg = 2.2 lb. Child’s weight in kilogram = 56 lb/2.2, or 25 kg. 25 kg  0.5 mg = 12.5 mg as the correct dose. Want 12.5 mg, Have 5 mg/tablet 12.5/5 = 2.5 tablets.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 411 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: Which statement about Alzheimer’s disease is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The causes of Alzheimer’s disease are known but not treatable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Physical deterioration occurs before intellectual deterioration.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The onset of the disorder is sudden and dramatic.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is the most common form of dementia.

questions.rationale: Alzheimer’s disease is a progressive and incurable condition that destroys brain cells, with gradual loss of intellectual abilities such as memory and extreme changes in personality and behavior. It is the most feared and common form of dementia. Alzheimer’s disease is the most common form of dementia.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 416

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: How do cholinesterase/acetylcholinesterase inhibitors work for treatment of Alzheimer’s disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Protecting the neurons from the changes that occur with Alzheimer’s disease.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blocking the enzyme that breaks down the neurotransmitter at the neuron synapses.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blocking the amino acid glutamate at the N-methyl-D-aspartate receptors in the brain.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increasing the amount of white matter surrounding neurons to prevent the formation of “tangles.”

questions.rationale: Cholinesterase/acetylcholinesterase inhibitors reduce the activity of the enzyme acetylcholinesterase that breaks down acetylcholine in the synapses of neurons. This action keeps levels of acetylcholine higher. These drugs are used for early to moderate stages of Alzheimer’s disease and their effects are temporary.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 418

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: How does memantine (Namenda) work for treatment of Alzheimer’s disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Protecting the neurons from the changes that occur with Alzheimer’s disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blocking the enzyme that breaks down the neurotransmitter at the neuron synapses

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blocking the amino acid glutamate at the N-methyl-D-aspartate receptors in the brain

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increasing the amount of white matter surrounding neurons to prevent the formation of “tangles”

questions.rationale: Memantine (Namenda) blocks the amino acid glutamate at N-methyl-D-aspartate receptors in the brain, preventing overstimulation (overstimulation of these receptors damages neurons and appears to be one cause of Alzheimer’s disease). It can be effective in helping modify dementia (temporarily) in some patients with moderate-to-severe Alzheimer’s disease.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 418

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: Which problem is a common side effect of donepezil (Aricept) for treatment of Alzheimer’s disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Weight gain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypertension

choices.label: C
choices.text: Constipation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nausea/vomiting

questions.rationale: The most common side effects of cholinesterase/acetylcholinesterase inhibitors are nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, stomach cramps, headaches, dizziness, fatigue, weakness, insomnia, and loss of appetite (anorexia).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 419

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: What problem is a possible adverse effect of memantine (Namenda) for Alzheimer’s disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Seizures

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tachycardia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Severe hypertension

choices.label: D
choices.text: Peripheral neuropathy

questions.rationale: Adverse effects of memantine include shortness of breath, seizures, and hallucinations.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 419

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: Which problem is a major symptom of Parkinson’s disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Memory loss

choices.label: B
choices.text: Urinary retention

choices.label: C
choices.text: Abnormal gait

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nausea/vomiting

questions.rationale: The four major symptoms of Parkinson’s disease are tremor at rest, rigidity, bradykinesia (slow movements and difficulty starting to move), and abnormal gait.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 422, Box 26-1

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: Which drug used to treat Parkinson’s disease is a COMT inhibitor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rasagiline (Azilect)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bromocriptine (Parlodel)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Carbidopa (Sinemet)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tolcapone (Tasmar)

questions.rationale: Tolcapone is a COMT inhibitor. Rasagiline is an MAO-B inhibitor while carbidopa and bromocriptine are dopaminergic/dopamine antagonists.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 423

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: Which problem is a common side effect of most drugs used to treat Parkinson’s disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Asthma

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypotension

choices.label: C
choices.text: Constipation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Weight gain

questions.rationale: The most common side effects of drugs to treat Parkinson’s disease are dizziness, nausea, and hypotension.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 424

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: Which drug for Parkinson’s disease is not recommended for breastfeeding mothers because it stops the production of breast milk?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rasagiline (Azilect)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bromocriptine (Parlodel)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Carbidopa (Sinemet)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tolcapone (Tasmar)

questions.rationale: Although Parkinson’s disease is very rare in women of childbearing age, bromocriptine (Parlodel) is usually not recommended during pregnancy or breastfeeding. It stops the production of breast milk.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 427

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: Which symptom is often the earliest to occur in a patient with Alzheimer’s disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Difficulty solving simple math problems

choices.label: B
choices.text: Problems performing simple tasks

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mild forgetfulness

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inability to read

questions.rationale: Alzheimer’s disease symptoms begin very slowly. In the early stage, the first symptom is mild forgetfulness, which is sometimes confused with age-related memory changes.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 417

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: Which common initial symptom suggests that a patient may be developing Parkinson’s disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pill-rolling tremor

choices.label: B
choices.text: Muscle rigidity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stooped posture

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stumble-run walk

questions.rationale: For many patients with Parkinson’s disease, the initial symptom is a coarse, rhythmic tremor of the hand while the hand is at rest, also called pill-rolling tremor.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 422

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: Which drug used to treat Parkinson’s disease is a pure dopamine agonist?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Trihexyphenidyl (Artane)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tolcapone (Tasmar)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Selegiline (Zelapar)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rotigotine (Neupro)

questions.rationale: Trihexyphenidyl is an anticholinergic drug. Tolcapone is a COMT inhibitor. Selegiline is a MAO-B inhibitor. Only rotigotine is a pure dopamine agonist.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 424 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: Which is the common underlying pathophysiology of both Alzheimer’s disease and Parkinson’s disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Both are neurodegenerative diseases.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Both are forms of dementia.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both involve interrupted transmission of nerve impulses.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both are primarily caused and directly related to environmental factors.

questions.rationale: Alzheimer’s disease is a form of dementia. Parkinson’s disease is a neurodegenerative disease. Both illnesses involve interrupted nerve impulse transmissions.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 416

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: For which patient do you watch most closely for symptoms of Alzheimer’s disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 35-year-old with Down syndrome

choices.label: B
choices.text: 50-year-old with a whiplash injury

choices.label: C
choices.text: 60-year-old with hypertension

choices.label: D
choices.text: 75-year-old with a smoking history

questions.rationale: While whiplash and head injuries, hypertension, smoking, and high cholesterol all increase the risk for Alzheimer’s disease, age is the greatest risk factor for development of Alzheimer’s disease. A patient with Down syndrome has an increased risk by age 50 to 60 years.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 417

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: Which statement made by a patient with Alzheimer’s disease indicates the need for additional teaching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “When I have been taking donepezil for 6 weeks I should regain my memories.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “The only way to be sure that I have Alzheimer’s disease is by autopsy after I die.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Even with medication, eventually I will need total care to prevent complications.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “The rivastigmine I am taking may slow down the progression of my symptoms.”

questions.rationale: No drug has been developed that will protect neurons from the changes that occur with Alzheimer’s disease. Drug treatments can temporarily slow the progression of symptoms in some patients. The drugs do not cure the disease and a patient should not expect to regain lost function. The only way to definitively diagnose this illness is by seeing the plaques and tangles in the brain on autopsy after death.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 418

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: A patient with Alzheimer’s disease has been prescribed memantine (Namenda). The patient has developed the symptoms of vomiting, drooling, heart rate of 56 beats/minute, and muscle weakness. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Contact the prescriber and ask for an order for an as-needed antiemetic drug.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Place the patient on a heart monitor and check the heart rate every 2 hours.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hold the drug dose and contact the prescriber immediately.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Give the drug exactly as ordered.

questions.rationale: Symptoms of drug overdose for memantine include upset stomach, vomiting, drooling, sweating, slow heart rate, difficulty breathing, muscle weakness, and seizures. The drug dose should be held and the prescriber notified immediately.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 419

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: Which strategy best supports safe medication administration for patients with Alzheimer’s disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Include the patient’s care provider when teaching about the patient’s drugs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Provide written guidelines about each drug to the patient.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Create a chart listing the drugs, dosages, and times they should be taken.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Suggest that the patient set up the drugs each week in labeled boxes.

questions.rationale: Because patients with Alzheimer’s disease have difficulty with memory and cognition, the individual providing care in the home should be included when doing any teaching about the disease, including drugs and dosages.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 420

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: A patient with Alzheimer’s disease is prescribed rivastigmine (Exelon). What action should you suggest to prevent a common side effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Give the drug with a full glass of water.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer the drug with meals twice a day.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assess the patient’s level of consciousness.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Keep an accurate record of all patient food intake.

questions.rationale: Both rivastigmine and galantamine (Reminyl) commonly cause the side effect of GI upset. Giving these drugs with food twice a day helps to minimize this side effect.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 420

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: What is the most important precaution to teach a patient who is prescribed transdermal rivastigmine (Exelon)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Always remove old patches daily and apply the new patch to a different site.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Report any difficulty starting the urinary stream to your prescriber immediately.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Place the patch on the neck or forehead so that the drug reaches the brain more quickly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: For best drug absorption, hold a warm washcloth over the patch for 5 minutes after applying it.

questions.rationale: As for any drug delivered by transdermal patch, the old patch should be removed before applying the new one to prevent a drug overdose. The patches can irritate the skin and should not be replaced in the same position as the previous patch. Rotating sites prevents skin irritation and breakdown. Applying the patch closer to the brain does not increase brain absorption of the drug.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 425

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: For which patient should the drug donepezil (Aricept) for Alzheimer’s disease be avoided?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 54-year-old woman with early onset Alzheimer’s disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: 67-year-old man who also takes drugs for type 2 diabetes

choices.label: C
choices.text: 72-year-old woman who is 20 lb underweight

choices.label: D
choices.text: 72-year-old man who is unable to sit or stand

questions.rationale: Donepezil suppresses appetite and causes weight loss. In an individual who is underweight, the usual dosage can lead to toxicity. In addition, some Alzheimer’s patients forget to eat and are already underweight. Weighing less than an ideal body weight increases the risk for many complications of old age and poor health.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 420

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: Which statement is true about how drugs for Parkinson’s disease work?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dopaminergic drugs decrease the amount of dopamine activity in the brain.

choices.label: B
choices.text: COMT inhibitors allow a larger amount of levodopa to reach the brain, which lowers the brain’s dopamine levels.

choices.label: C
choices.text: MAO-B inhibitors facilitate the enzyme monoamine oxidase B that breaks down dopamine in the brain.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Anticholinergic drugs block cholinergic nerve impulses to minimize tremors and rigidity.

questions.rationale: Dopaminergic drugs increase the amount of dopamine activity in the brain. COMT (catechol-o- methyltransferase) inhibitors allow a larger amount of levodopa to reach the brain, which increases the brain’s dopamine levels. MAO-B inhibitors inhibit the enzyme monoamine oxidase B that breaks down dopamine in the brain. Anticholinergic drugs are effective against tremors and rigidity by blocking cholinergic nerve impulses that help control the muscles of the arms, legs, and body.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 423

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: A patient with Parkinson’s disease who has been prescribed entacapone (Comtan) tells you he is experiencing muscle aches, weakness, and has dark cola -colored urine. What is yourbest action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Send a urine specimen to the laboratory for urinalysis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Help the patient back to bed and instruct him or her to rest.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tell the patient that these are expected side effects of the drug.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hold the drug and notify the prescriber immediately.

questions.rationale: Entacapone is a COMT inhibitor. These drugs can cause the adverse effect of rhabdomyolysis, a serious and potentially fatal effect involving destruction or degeneration of skeletal muscle. Signs and symptoms of rhabdomyolysis include muscle aches, muscle weakness, and dark cola- colored urine. You should hold the drug and notify the prescriber immediately.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 424

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: Which blood laboratory test result is most important to check before administering the first prescribed dose of carbidopa-levodopa (Sinemet) to a patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: International normalized ratio (INR)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) level

choices.label: C
choices.text: White blood cell (WBC) count

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)

questions.rationale: Carbidopa-levodopa can suppress bone marrow activity and reduce the circulating levels of WBCs (neutropenia), greatly increasing the risk for infection. More patients with Parkinson’s disease are older adults and may already have some age-related decrease in immune function. Neutropenia and infection are more likely when the patient’s WBC count is low before treatment with the drug is started.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 424

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: For which adverse effect should you monitor in a patient with Parkinson’s disease after administering ropinirole (Requip)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome

choices.label: B
choices.text: Depression with suicidal tendencies

choices.label: C
choices.text: Central nervous system depression

choices.label: D
choices.text: Narcolepsy or sleep attacks

questions.rationale: An adverse effect of ropinirole, a dopaminergic/dopamine antagonist drug, is episodes of falling asleep suddenly (narcolepsy). Neuroleptic malignant syndrome is an adverse effect of COMT inhibitors. Depression with suicidal tendencies is an adverse effect of carbidopa-levodopa (Sinemet). Central nervous system depression is an adverse effect of apomorphine (Apokyn) and can include respiratory depression, coma, and cardiac arrest.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 425

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: A patient with Parkinson’s disease informs you that the stiffness and slowness of movements associated with the illness have become worse. What should you teach the patient about this change?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “These changes are normal and expected as your disease progresses.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “You will need to use a walker at all times to avoid falls and accidents.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Whenever your symptoms worsen, contact your prescriber right away.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “If your prescriber increases your drug dosage these functions will improve.”

questions.rationale: Patients should be taught to immediately notify their prescriber if symptoms of Parkinson’s disease (e.g., shaking, stiffness, or slowness of movement) become worse in a relatively short time period.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 426

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: A patient taking entacapone (Comtan) for Parkinson’s disease informs you that his urine is now brownish-orange in color. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Explain that this is an expected side effect and is not harmful.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Collect a urine sample and send it to the laboratory for urinalysis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hold the drug and immediately notify the prescriber.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Instruct the patient to increase his intake of fluids.

questions.rationale: Entacapone may discolor a patient’s urine brownish-orange. This is an expected side effect and is not harmful.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 426

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: What safety precaution should you teach a patient with Parkinson’s disease who is prescribed benztropine (Cogentin)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Always dress warmly and avoid extended exposure to cold weather.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stay indoors in an air-conditioned setting during hot weather.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Change positions slowly to avoid dizziness or light-headedness.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Avoid alcohol intake because drowsiness is associated with this drug.

questions.rationale: Benztropine is an anticholinergic drug. These drugs cause decreased perspiration, leading to increased risk for overheating during hot weather. Patients should be taught to stay indoors, preferably in an air-conditioned environment, to avoid this side effect.
questions.metadata: DIF:  Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 426

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed carbidopa-levodopa (Sinemet) informs you that a dark spot on his arm is getting larger. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Measure the size of the spot and document the finding.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inform the patient that this is an expected side effect of the drug.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer the drug as ordered by the prescriber.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hold the drug and contact the prescriber about this change.

questions.rationale: Carbidopa-levodopa can activate malignant melanoma. Patients should be instructed to report any changes in skin lesions immediately to their prescriber.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 427

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: Why is bromocriptine (Parlodel) not recommended for women who are breastfeeding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It stops the production of milk.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It enters the milk and slows infant growth.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It enters the milk and causes symptoms of Parkinson’s disease in the infant.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is a pregnancy category C drug and has been found to cause birth defects in animals.

questions.rationale: Bromocriptine suppresses the secretion and release of prolactin from the pituitary gland. Without secretion of this important hormone, lactation with milk production does not occur.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 427

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: A patient is prescribed entacapone (Comtan) 800 mg/day. The drug is available in 200 mg tablets. How many tablets should you give?
questions.rationale: Have 200 mg/1 tablet, Want 800 mg/X tablets; 800/200 = 4 tablets.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 426

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: A patient diagnosed with probable Alzheimer’s disease is having his swallowing ability tested. His wife asks you why this is necessary. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “This is a routine assessment that is completed for every patient.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “The prescriber ordered this test to be done to prevent side effects of a new drug for your husband’s disease.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “This test tells us where your husband’s swallowing is now, because it may get worse as his disease progresses.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “We are concerned about whether we will need to place a feeding tube to give his . medications.”

questions.rationale: Assess a patient’s ability to swallow before giving drugs for Alzheimer’s disease because he or she may be at risk for aspiration.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 420

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: A patient is prescribed donepezil (Aricept) for Alzheimer’s disease. What assessment is most important after giving this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blood pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bowel sounds

choices.label: C
choices.text: Peripheral pulses

choices.label: D
choices.text: Signs of bleeding

questions.rationale: Donepezil is an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor. This drug may cause GI bleeding. Monitor the patient carefully for any signs of bleeding. Monitor emesis and stools for blood.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 420

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: A patient prescribed donepezil (Aricept) is to have surgery requiring general anesthesia. Her husband tells you that he read that taking this drug while having surgery can be dangerous. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Because of the action of donepezil, she may awaken more slowly and may need to remain in the postanesthesia care unit for a longer period of time.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Let me contact your surgeon because the danger of taking donepezil and having general anesthesia is that she may have a massive heart attack.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I don’t think there is any danger because if there was your surgeon would certainly have discontinued use of this drug until after the surgery.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “The most dangerous risk is that after the surgery your wife may lose her ability to swallow and aspirate food or medications, ending up with aspiration pneumonia.”

questions.rationale: Donepezil (Aricept) is a cholinesterase inhibitor, therefore likely to increase succinylcholine-type muscle relaxation during anesthesia. If used during surgery, continued respiratory depression may occur due to prolonged neuromuscular blockade. Teach patients and families to discuss the risks associated with these drugs if the patient needs any type of surgery requiring general anesthesia. Those with dementia are at an increased risk for complications following surgery. They may awaken more slowly and are more likely to experience confusion and delirium. This might lead to longer stays in the postsurgical recovery room (PACU) and longer hospitalization.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 419

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: A patient prescribed rasagiline (Azilect) for Parkinson’s disease asks you how this drug will help his disease. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “This drug works by increasing the amount of dopamine activity in the brain and reducing tremor and muscle rigidity to improve movement.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “This drug works by allowing a larger amount of levodopa to reach the brain, which raises dopamine levels in the brain.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “This drug works by blocking cholinergic nerve impulses that help control the muscles of the arms, legs, and body.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “This drug works by helping provide a more stable, constant supply of levodopa, which makes its beneficial effects last longer.”

questions.rationale: Rasagiline is a monoamine oxidase B (MAO-B) inhibitor. These drugs inhibit the enzyme that breaks down dopamine in the brain. As a result, more dopamine is available, and the progression of Parkinson’s disease is slowed.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 422

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: A patient with advanced Parkinson’s disease is prescribed extended-release—50 mg carbidopa/200 mg levodopa capsule twice daily. The patient has difficulty swallowing. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Open the capsule and mix the contents with apple sauce.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Open the capsule and dissolve the contents in water.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Contact the pharmacy to send a liquid form of the drug.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the prescriber to order a different form of the drug.

questions.rationale: The prescriber can order a different form of the drug that is compatible with the patient’s abilities. The pharmacy can send the drug but not without a valid order from the prescriber. The drug ordered is an extended release form and opening the capsule would give the patient too much drug quickly placing him or her at risk for side effects or adverse effects.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 420

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: For which common side effects should you monitor after administering any anti- Parkinson’s drug to a patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dizziness and hypotension

choices.label: B
choices.text: Diarrhea and nausea

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypertension and headache

choices.label: D
choices.text: Body rash and elevated temperature

questions.rationale: The most common side effects of drugs to treat Parkinson’s disease are dizziness, nausea, and hypotension.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 425

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: Which precaution should you teach patients prescribed carbidopa-levodopa (Sinemet)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Avoid crowds and people who are ill.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Take this drug with an antacid to prevent ulcers.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Restrict your fluid intake to less than 1.5 L daily.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Keep the bottle containing the drug in the refrigerator.

questions.rationale: Sinemet can cause a profound reduction in the white blood cell count (neutropenia), which then greatly increases the risk for infection.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 424

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: What is the minimum time period needed before reapplying a rotigotine patch to the same skin site?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 7 days

choices.label: B
choices.text: 14 days

choices.label: C
choices.text: 21 days

choices.label: D
choices.text: 28 days

questions.rationale: The skin site where a rotigotine patch has been removed should be cleansed and not used again for a minimum of 14 days to prevent skin breakdown.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 425

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: What are the major symptoms of Parkinson’s disease? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bradycardia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rigidity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Abnormal gait

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tremor at rest

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lack of facial expression

choices.label: F
choices.text: Facial swelling

questions.rationale: The four major symptoms of Parkinson’s disease are tremor at rest, rigidity, bradykinesia (slow movements and difficulty starting movements), and abnormal gait. Lack of facial expression is a symptom of this illness, but not a major symptom.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 422

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: What are the goals of treatment for Parkinson’s disease? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Help the patient to maintain a high quality of life.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reduce side effects of drug therapy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Regain as much function as possible.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Minimize the patient’s disability.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Prolong the patient’s life span.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Maintain short-term memory

questions.rationale: The goals of treatment for Parkinson’s disease are to minimize disability, reduce possible side effects of drug therapy, and help the patient maintain a high quality of life. There is no cure for this illness, which is progressive. Drugs can help slow the progression and control the symptoms.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 422

questions.chapter_title: Drug Therapy for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases
questions.stem: A patient has been prescribed rasagiline (Azilect). Which foods should you teach the patient to avoid? (select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Green leafy vegetables

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aged cheeses

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sour cream

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fresh apples and pears

choices.label: E
choices.text: Soy sauce

choices.label: F
choices.text: Chicken and beef

questions.rationale: Rasagiline is an MAO-B inhibitor. Patients must be taught to avoid foods with tyramine, an amino acid that can cause a hypertensive crisis when patients are taking these drugs. Examples of such foods are aged cheeses, sour cream, and soy sauce (see Box 23-2 in the text).
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 426, Box 26-2 Workman & LaCharity: Understanding Pharmacology: Essentials for Medication Safety, 3rd Edition BASIC CONCEPTS

questions.stem: What term means to have fixed false beliefs or opinions that are held despite a lack of supporting evidence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anxiety

choices.label: B
choices.text: Delusion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Psychosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obsession

questions.rationale: Common symptoms of psychosis include delusions (false ideas about what is occurring or personal identity). An example of a delusion is when an individual exaggerates his or her sense of self-importance and is convinced that he or she has special powers, talents, or abilities. The individual may believe that he or she is a famous movie star or a saint.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 443

questions.stem: Which statement about psychiatric disorders is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Depression is less common than mania.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Minor mental illnesses are usually caused by prescription drug use.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Psychiatric disorders most often in young adults and decrease with age.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Major mental illnesses are the most common cause of disability in the United States.

questions.rationale: Major mental illnesses are the most common cause of disability in the United States. About 26.2% of adults in the United States experience a clinically diagnosable mental illness each year.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 430

questions.stem: An imbalance of which type of naturally occurring substances in the brain is thought to be responsible for depression?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enzymes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Receptors

choices.label: C
choices.text: Neurotransmitters

choices.label: D
choices.text: Endocrine hormones

questions.rationale: Depression is an illness characterized by feelings of sadness, despair, loss of energy, and difficulty dealing with normal daily life. Research suggests that depression may be caused by an imbalance of brain chemicals called neurotransmitters (e.g., serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine). Communication between neurons in the brain occurs by the movement of these chemicals across a small gap called the synapse. Neurotransmitters are released from one neuron at the presynaptic nerve terminal and then cross the synapse, where they may be accepted by the next neuron at a specialized site called a receptor. When neurotransmitter levels decrease or become imbalanced, the neurons may be less able to communicate with each other, which may lead to depression and other mood changes.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: pp. 433-434

questions.stem: Which antidepressant drug belongs to the tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) category?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Amitriptyline (Elavil)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bupropion (Wellbutrin)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Citalopram (Celexa)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Duloxetine (Cymbalta)

questions.rationale: Amitriptyline (Elavil) is a TCA antidepressant. Bupropion and duloxetine are norepinephrine and dopamine reuptake inhibitors. Citalopram is an SSRI.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 436

questions.stem: Which problem can be a side effect of antidepressants in children and adolescents?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Suicidal thoughts

choices.label: B
choices.text: Profound anemia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Craving for cigarettes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Severe hypertension

questions.rationale: SSRIs, TCAs, and other antidepressant drugs should be used with caution in depressed children and adolescents because the risk for suicidal thoughts or actions may increase while taking these drugs.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 438

questions.stem: Which drug used to treat depression is a combination of a reuptake inhibitor and a receptor blocker?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fluoxetine (Prozac)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sertraline (Zoloft)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tramadol (Rybix)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Trazodone (Desyrel)

questions.rationale: Trazodone is a combined reuptake inhibitor and receptor blocker.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 435

questions.stem: What is the most common side effect of benzodiazepines used to treat anxiety?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Widely dilated pupils

choices.label: B
choices.text: Excessive sweating

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hyperactivity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sleepiness

questions.rationale: The most common side effects of benzodiazepines are related to their CNS effects. They include sedation, sleepiness, depression, lethargy, apathy, fatigue, hypoactivity, light-headedness, memory impairment, disorientation, amnesia, dizziness, delirium, headache, slurred speech, behavioral changes, ataxia, unsteadiness, euphoria, dysarthria, inability to perform complex mental functions, nervousness, irritability, difficulty concentrating, “glassy-eyed” appearance, changes in heart rate and blood pressure, changes in bowel function, and skin rashes.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 443

questions.stem: Which drug used to treat psychosis can cause the urine to turn pink or reddish brown?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alprazolam (Xanax)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Amitriptyline (Elavil)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prochlorperazine (Compazine)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Quetiapine (Seroquel)

questions.rationale: Only prochlorperazine causes the urine to turn pink or reddish brown.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 446

questions.stem: Which side effect is most common to all antianxiety drugs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sedation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Weight gain

choices.label: C
choices.text: Excess excitement

choices.label: D
choices.text: Urinary retention

questions.rationale: All antianxiety drugs suppress central nervous system function to some degree and result in sedation.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 441

questions.stem: What potential problem can occur in an infant being breastfed while the mother takes a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Suicide ideation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acute depression

choices.label: C
choices.text: Excess excitement

choices.label: D
choices.text: Weight loss

questions.rationale: Most selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors are passed into breast milk and cause side effect in the infant. These include drowsiness and decreased feeding, which leads to weight loss.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Remembering REF: p. 438 ADVANCED CONCEPTS

questions.stem: Which statement about depression is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Depression does not affect the way an individual interacts with others.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Men are twice as likely to experience depression as women.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Many older adults with depression are undiagnosed and untreated.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Women are less likely than men to seek treatment for depression.

questions.rationale: Because depression is often mistaken for a normal part of aging, many older adults with depression may be undiagnosed and untreated. Depression affects the way people feel about themselves and how they interact with others. Women are twice as likely as men to experience depression and women are also more likely than men to seek treatment.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 432

questions.stem: Which characteristic is typical of bipolar disorder?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Severe highs and severe lows

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased risk for suicide

choices.label: C
choices.text: Persistently low moods

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lack of pleasure in life

questions.rationale: Bipolar disorder, also called manic-depression, is characterized by cycling moods from severe highs (mania) and severe lows (depression). Increased risk for suicide and lack of pleasure in life are characteristics of major depression. Persistently low moods are characteristic of both major depression and dysthymia.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 433

questions.stem: A patient with depression has all of the following symptoms. For which symptom is he or she likely to receive treatment at an inpatient facility rather than as an outpatient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Expressing anger at a situation or individual

choices.label: B
choices.text: Suggesting a plan to hurt oneself

choices.label: C
choices.text: Crying off and on for several hours or days

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sleeping for 12 hours or longer daily

questions.rationale: Most patients with depression are treated as outpatients; however, when an individual has suicidal thoughts (also called suicidal ideation) and particularly if the individual has a suicide plan, hospitalization may be required.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 433

questions.stem: Which theory about the cause of depression is supported by research?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Heredity or genetics

choices.label: B
choices.text: Altered neuroendocrine function

choices.label: C
choices.text: Psychosocial factors

choices.label: D
choices.text: Neurotransmitter level imbalance

questions.rationale: Research suggests that depression may be caused by an imbalance of brain chemicals called neurotransmitters (e.g., serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine). Communication between neurons in the brain occurs by the movement of these chemicals across a small gap called a synapse. Neurotransmitters are released from one neuron and cross the synapse where they may be accepted by the receptor sites on the next neuron. When neurotransmitter levels decrease or become imbalanced, the neurons may be less able to communicate with each other, which may lead to depression and other mood changes.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 433

questions.stem: A patient who was prescribed citalopram (Celexa) 1 week ago for depression reports feeling no different now from 1 week ago and wants to stop taking the drug. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Treatment for depression is highly individual and this may not be the right drug for you.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Most drugs for depression take at least 2 weeks to start making you feel better.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Are you certain that you are taking the drug exactly the way it was prescribed?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Be sure to stop smoking because cigarette smoking inactivates this drug.”

questions.rationale: Citalopram is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI). SSRIs work by increasing the amount of serotonin in the brain by inhibiting reuptake. This drug begins inhibiting the reuptake of serotonin immediately but the patient’s symptoms often do not respond for 2 to 8 weeks. In order to know whether this is an effective drug for a particular patient, he or she needs to take the drug daily for at least 8 weeks.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 435

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed bupropion (Wellbutrin SR) 100 mg twice daily. It is time for the second dose today and the drug sent up from the pharmacy is Wellbutrin 100 XL. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer the Wellbutrin XL tablet in place of the Wellbutrin SR tablet.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cut the Wellbutrin XL tablet in half and then administer it.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Call the pharmacy and obtain a Wellbutrin SR 100 mg tablet.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hold the dose and notify the prescriber.

questions.rationale: An order for Wellbutrin SR can be confused with Wellbutrin XL. Both drugs are norepinephrine and dopamine reuptake inhibitors used to treat depression. But Wellbutrin SR is a slow-release form of the drug given twice a day, whereas Wellbutrin XL is an extended-release form of the drug given once a day. Because of their absorption and differences in onset of action, these drugs are not interchangeable.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 435

questions.stem: Which statement by a patient with chronic depression who is prescribed fluoxetine (Prozac) indicates the need for additional teaching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “It may take from 2 to 8 weeks for my depression to improve.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “If I have suicidal thoughts, I will contact my prescriber immediately.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I will check my pulse every day and report irregular rhythms to my prescriber.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “When I am feeling better and no longer depressed, I will stop taking the Prozac.”

questions.rationale: When depression is chronic, patients must continue to take antidepressants even when they have no symptoms to keep the depression from returning. Patients should be taught that antidepressants can control the symptoms but will not cure depression.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 437

questions.stem: Which assessment is most important to measure or perform before administering the first dose of amitriptyline (Elavil) to a patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Heart rate and rhythm

choices.label: B
choices.text: Core body temperature

choices.label: C
choices.text: Level of consciousness

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blood pressure in the sitting position

questions.rationale: Amitriptyline is a tricyclic antidepressant that can cause unstable ventricular dysrhythmias. Therefore heart rate and rhythm are most important to assess to establish a baseline and determine possible adverse effects of this drug. Although the drug can also lower blood pressure and increase drowsiness, changes in heart rhythm can be life-threatening.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 437

questions.stem: The parents of a 12-year-old who was prescribed fluoxetine (Prozac) inform you that their child is having trouble sleeping. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “This is a normal expected side effect of fluoxetine.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “We may need to ask the prescriber to order something to help your child sleep.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Children are more sensitive to the effects of this drug and may need a lower dose.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Before bedtime be sure that your child does not eat or drink anything with caffeine.”

questions.rationale: Because children are more sensitive to the effects of fluoxetine, the drug may cause unusual excitement, restlessness, irritability, or trouble sleeping. The prescriber should be notified if these side effects occur because a lower dose or a different drug may be needed to control the child’s depression symptoms.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 438

questions.stem: A patient has been taking mirtazapine (Remeron) for the last 3 months. Which blood laboratory result should be reported to the prescriber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lactate dehydrogenase 122 mg/dL

choices.label: B
choices.text: International normalized ratio (INR) 1.3

choices.label: C
choices.text: White blood cell count (WBC) 2100/mm3

choices.label: D
choices.text: Platelet count 356,000/mm3

questions.rationale: Mirtazapine can suppress bone marrow production of WBCs (neutropenia) which can greatly increase the patient’s risk for infection. The normal WBC range is 5000 to 10,000/mm3. This patient’s WBC count is low. It should be reported to the prescriber because drug dosage adjustment or drug changes are likely needed.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 437

questions.stem: Which precaution is most important to teach a patient who is prescribed any antidepressant drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Avoid drinking grapefruit juice while taking this drug.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Avoid drinking alcoholic beverages while taking this drug.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Be sure to wear sunscreen and a hat when going outdoors.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Drink at least 3 L of fluid every day while taking this drug.”

questions.rationale: Most antidepressants cause some degree of drowsiness. Alcohol intensifies this action and can impair the patient’s physical strength and mental alertness.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 431

questions.stem: Although serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are used more commonly now to treat anxiety disorders than are benzodiazepines, for which patient situation would you expect a benzodiazepine to be prescribed instead of an SSRI?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient is 18 years old.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anxiety has been present daily for 6 months.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient has compulsive repetitive actions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The anxiety attack is severe with hallucinations.

questions.rationale: Benzodiazepines, even when given orally, act within 30 minutes of administration. SSRIs need to build up to a certain blood level and may take as long as 3 to 5 weeks to reduce anxiety. When an anxiety attack is sudden or severe, a benzodiazepine may be most helpful, even if it is just given once.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 441

questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed clonazepam (Klonopin) for the last 3 months tells you that he wants to stop this drug because he constantly feels like he is in a “fog.” What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Quit taking the drug today and I will have the prescriber change your medication to another drug category.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Continue to take this drug as prescribed because most patients eventually get used to the foggy sensation.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “We will give you a final dose of this drug today intravenously and then start you on a different drug.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I will contact the prescriber but it will still be necessary to take this drug at a lower dosage for a while.”

questions.rationale: Clonazepam is a benzodiazepine. Suddenly stopping drugs from this class can cause a potentially life-threatening reaction of withdrawal symptoms. This drug needs to be tapered slowly by gradually reducing the dosage before it can be safely stopped.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 442

questions.stem: Which statement about a common anxiety disorder is accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Panic disorders develop over a period of hours to days.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Generalized anxiety disorder occurs when an individual experiences excessive anxiety daily for 2 months.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Phobic disorders are recurrent persistent fears of certain objects or situations that can cause panic attacks.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Posttraumatic stress disorder is the result of frightening thoughts and dreams.

questions.rationale: Panic disorders develop over a short period of time such as 10 minutes. Generalized anxiety disorder is when an individual experiences excessive anxiety almost daily for more than 6 months. Posttraumatic stress disorder is caused by exposure to death or near-death experiences.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 438

questions.stem: What is the advantage of treating a patient with anxiety with a benzodiazepine drug rather than a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Benzodiazepines are not likely to cause patient dependence when used for an extended period of time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Benzodiazepines have milder side effects and almost no adverse effect when compared with SSRIs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Benzodiazepines control anxiety and allow patients to live a relatively normal lifestyle.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Benzodiazepines act to treat anxiety within 30 minutes and can be given on an as-needed basis.

questions.rationale: Benzodiazepines have stronger side effects than SSRIs and are more likely to cause dependence in a patient who uses them for an extended period of time. Both benzodiazepines and SSRI drugs control anxiety and allow patients to live nearly normal lives. The major benefit of benzodiazepines is that they act within 30 minutes and can be taken as needed.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 441

questions.stem: What type of medication could be prescribed for a patient who experiences anxiety when speaking before a group?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A benzodiazepine drug

choices.label: B
choices.text: An SSRI drug

choices.label: C
choices.text: A beta blocker

choices.label: D
choices.text: A TCA drug

questions.rationale: When a patient is facing an anxiety-producing event such as performing on stage or making a speech, beta blockers can be prescribed to control the symptoms of anxiety. Benzodiazepine drugs may cause dependence. SSRI drugs can take 3 to 5 weeks to control symptoms of anxiety. Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) drugs are used to treat depression, not anxiety.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 441

questions.stem: A patient asks why the prescriber is changing his anxiety medication from lorazepam (Ativan) to sertraline (Zoloft). What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Sertraline is a stronger drug and will do a better job of controlling your anxiety.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Sertraline has milder side effects and a decreased risk for drug dependence.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Sertraline acts much faster to get the symptoms of your anxiety under control.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Sertraline can be taken only when needed to control symptoms of anxiety.”

questions.rationale: Sertraline is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor. This class of drugs has milder side effects and a much lower likelihood of developing drug dependence than benzodiazepines such as lorazepam. However, it takes 3 to 5 weeks for symptoms of anxiety to improve and must be taken on a daily basis.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 441

questions.stem: On admission to the acute care unit a patient tells you that she stopped taking her prescribed clonazepam (Klonopin) 2 days ago. What is your best first action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the patient why she stopped taking the clonazepam.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer a dose of clonazepam immediately.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Document this information as the only action.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Notify the prescriber and watch for seizures.

questions.rationale: Clonazepam is a benzodiazepine drug. Suddenly stopping a drug from this class can cause potentially life-threatening withdrawal symptoms including nervousness, restlessness, tremulousness, weakness, and seizures.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 442

questions.stem: A patient who has been prescribed diazepam (Valium) reports taking the as-needed (PRN) drug more often to keep anxiety symptoms under control and does not want the prescriber to stop prescribing it. What is your best interpretation of this information?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient may be developing signs of drug dependence.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient may be developing signs of drug tolerance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient’s anxiety is becoming more severe.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient should stop taking the drug immediately.

questions.rationale: The signs of developing dependence on benzodiazepines include a strong desire or need to continue taking the drug, a need to increase the dose to feel the effects of the drug, and withdrawal effects after the drug is stopped such as irritability, nervousness, and trouble sleeping. Drug tolerance is a condition that occurs when the body gets used to a medicine so that either more medicine is needed or a different medicine is needed.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 442

questions.stem: A female patient who has been prescribed diazepam (Valium) for anxiety tells you that she and her husband are planning to have a child. What is your best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Before becoming pregnant your dosage of diazepam will need to be decreased.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “You should always discuss any changes in your medications and lifestyle with your prescriber.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Consult with your prescriber because taking diazepam while pregnant has been associated with birth defects.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Diazepam may cause withdrawal effects including miscarriage when taken during pregnancy.”

questions.rationale: Benzodiazepines such as chlordiazepoxide (Librium) and diazepam have caused birth defects when used during the first trimester of pregnancy. Use of these drugs during pregnancy can also cause the fetus to become dependent and experience withdrawal symptoms after birth.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Applying or Higher REF: p. 443

questions.stem: How do the major antipsychotic drugs exert their effects to reduce psychotic episodes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They block dopamine receptors and reduce neuronal impulse transmission.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They redirect nerve impulses away from excitatory areas of the brain and into brain inhibitory areas.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They enhance the breakdown of excitatory neurotransmitter chemicals so that impulse transmission is slower.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They block the reuptake of neurotransmitters so that the concentration of these chemicals is increased in the brain.

questions.rationale: All antipsychotic drugs tend to block dopamine receptors in the dopamine pathways in the brain. The normal effect of releasing the neurotransmitter dopamine is decreased. Transmission of impulses is decreased which in turn decreases the symptoms of hallucinations, illusions, and delusions.
questions.metadata: DIF: Cognitive Level: Understanding REF: p. 444

questions.stem: The family of a patient who was prescribed chlorpromazine (Thorazine) report that the patient continually rolls his tongue and smacks his lips. What is your best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reassure the patient and family that this response is an expected side effect of the drug.
